evidence goes to the validity of the contract rather than a construction of
its terms .
33

The provision of the Code that a memorandum may be "explained "
or " supplemented " by course of dealing or usage of trade may be in con-
flict with Kentucky cases . Kentucky admits evidence of usage to the
following extent : Custom or usage of trade is allowed to explain an
ambiguous or obscure contract . Kendall v. Russell , 35 Ky . ( 5 Dana ) 501 ,
30 Am . Dec. 696 ( 1837 ) ; Thomas v . Charles , 168 Ky . 595, 119 S. W. 752
(1909 ) ; Hawkins v . Midland Flour Milling Co., 236 Ky . 803 , 34 S. W. 2d
439 ( 1930 ) . But a custom cannot enter into a contract , if the parties did
not contract with reference to it. Caldwell v . Dawson , 61 Ky . ( 4 Metc .) 121
(1862 ) ; Castleman v. Southern Mutual Life Insurance Co. , 77 Ky . ( 14 Bush )
197 ( 1878 ) . And evidence of a custom or usage is not allowed to vary
or contradict the terms of a plain , unambiguous contract . National Seed
Co. v . Leavell , 202 Ky . 438, 259 S. W. 1035 ( 1924 ) ; Hawkins v . Midland Flour
Milling Co. , 236 Ky . 803 , 34 S. W. 2d 439 ( 1930 ) ; Huber v . Kerrick , 251 Ky.
439, 65 S. W. 2d 449 ( 1933 ) .
Under paragraph ( b ) consistent additional terms , not reduced to
writing , may be proved unless the court finds that the writing was intended
by both parties as a complete and exclusive statement of all the terms .
The use of parol evidence to "explain " the written agreement is in accord
with past decisions . See Kentucky Annotations to Sec. 238 (a) of the
Restatement , Contracts . The cases seem in conflict on the question whether
the parol evidence may " supplement " the writing. See Kentucky Annota-
tions to Secs . 238, 239 and 240 of the Restatement , Contracts .
Section 2-203 . Seals Inoperative .
Summary
The affixing of a seal to a writing evidencing a contract for sale or
an offer to buy or sell does not make the writing a sealed instrument and
the law as to sealed instruments does not apply to such contract or offer .
KRS 371.010 , 361.030 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Under the Code , a seal has no effect . KRS 371.010 places sealed and
unsealed instruments on the same footing and attaches absolutely no sig-
nificance as to whether or not an instrument is sealed . Bronston v Lakes ,
135 Ky . 173 , 121 S. W. 1021 ( 1909 ) .
The Code provides other devices reaching some of the results of a
seal . Under UCC 2-205 legal effect is given to a written statement by a
merchant that an offer is firm for a limited period , without regard to con-
sideration or the affixing of a seal . See also UCC 2-209 ( 1) on modification
of contracts without consideration .
34

Section 2-204. Formation in General .
Summary
(1) A contract for sale may be made in any manner sufficient to
show agreement, including conduct by both parties which recognizes the
existence of such a contract .
(2) An agreement sufficient to constitute a contract for sale may be
found even though the moment of making is undetermined .
(3) Even though terms are left open a contract does not fall for
indefiniteness if the parties intended to contract and there is a reasonably
certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy .
KRS 361.010 , 361.030 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Manner of Formation . Accord with subsection ( 1 ) : Uniform
Sales Act Sec . 3, KRS 361.030 (contract for sale "may be made in writing
or by word of mouth ... or may be inferred from the contract of the
parties" ) .
(2) Conduct Recognizing Existence . Accord with subsection ( 2) :
Uniform Sales Act , Sec. 3, KRS 361.030 (contract may be "inferred from
the conduct of the parties " ) ; Kellum v . Browning's Adm'r , 231 Ky . 308 ,
21 S. W. 2d 459 ( 1929 ) ; Restatement, Contracts , Secs . 5 and 21 , and Kentucky
Annotations .
(3) Omitted Terms ; Indefiniteness . See annotation to UCC 2-305 ,
Open Price Term , and UCC 2-311 , Options and Cooperation Respecting Per-
formance . Fowler's Bootery v . Selby Shoe Co., 273 Ky . 670, 117 S. W. 2d
931 ( 1938 ) . ( A promise to sell only the shoes of a certain manufacturer
but which does not include a promise to buy any shoes at all is too indefinite
to be a basis for a contract ) . Compare Restatement , Contracts , Secs . 32 , 33 ,
and Kentucky Annotations .
Section 2-205 . Firm Offers .
Summary
An offer by a merchant to buy or sell in a signed writing which by
its terms gives assurance that it will be held open is not revocable for lack
of consideration , during the time stated or if no time is stated for a reason-
able time , but in no event may such period of irrevocability exceed three
months; but any such term of assurance on a form supplied by the offeree
must be separately signed by the offeror .
35

KRS 361.010 , 361.030 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section giving legal effect for a limited time to a "firm" or
" irrevocable " offer signed by a merchant has no counterpart in the Uniform
Sales Act , and would modify present rules as to "consideration ."
The section obviates the necessity for a consideration to support an
irrevocable offer made by a merchant , as "merchant" is defined in Part 1 ,
when the offer is to remain open for a period of not more than three
months .
Commissioners Comment reveals that the time limit of three months
was inserted in order that the procedure would be limited to ordinary
business transactions ; and to prevent its application in lieu of regular long-
term options . The provision that a clause in a form supplied by the offeree
to the offeror must be separately signed by the offeror was intended as a
safeguard against unconscionable advantage being taken of this section.
Kentucky cases hold that a bare promise to keep an offer open is
without legal effect and the offer may be withdrawn . Noble v. Mann, 32
K. L. R. 30 , 105 S. W. 152 ( 1907 ) ; Stamper v . Combs , 164 Ky . 733 , 176 S. W.
178 ( 1915 ) . See Restatment , Contracts , Secs . 35 (1 ) , 47 and 48 , and Ken-
tucky Annotations thereto .
Section 2-206 . Offer and Acceptance in Formation of Contract .
Summary
(1) Unless the contrary is clearly indicated :
(a) an offer invites acceptance in any manner and by any medium
reasonable in the circumstances ;
(b) an order or other offer to buy goods for prompt or current
shipment shall be construed as inviting acceptance either by
a prompt promise to ship or by the prompt or current ship-
ment of conforming a non - conforming goods , but such a
shipment of non - conforming goods does not constitute an
acceptance if the seller seasonably notifies the buyer that the
shipment is offered only as an accommodation to the buyer .
(2 ) Where the beginning of performance is a reasonable mode of
acceptance an offeror may treat the offer as lapsed if not notified within
a reasonable time .
KRS 361.010 , 361.030 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) (a) Manner and Medium of Acceptance . Subsection ( 1) ( a) in
authorizing acceptance by a medium other than that used by the offeror
is similar to the rule of Restatement , Contracts , Sec. 66 ( acceptance may be
36

the means used by the offeror "or customary in similar transactions " ) . See
Kentucky Annotations to Sec. 66 of Restatement , Contracts .
(b) Acceptance by Shipment . On acceptance by action rather
than promise , see Restatement , Contracts , Secs . 29 and 63 and Kentucky
Annotations .
(2) Non -conforming Shipments . On the distinction between ship-
ments which purport to be an acceptance and those offered as an accom-
modation see Restatement , Contracts , Sec. 63 ( a tender of performance
requested "operates as a promise to render complete performance " ) , and
Kentucky Annotations .
(3 ) The provision of subsection (3 ) that "the beginning of a requested
performance can be a reasonable mode of acceptance " follows Sec. 45 of
the Restatement , Contracts , with the addition of a requirement of notice
within a reasonable time . Kentucky cases have applied this principle where
there has been a part performance by the offeree . The explanation is that
since part performance creates reciprocal obligations the offer is thereafter
irrevocable and it is further said that both parties are bound to perform .
Louisville and Nashville R. Co. v . Coyle , 123 Ky . 854, 97 S. W. 772, 99 S. W.
237 ( 1906 ) ( an offer to buy all ties delivered at a certain place within the
next twelve months can not be withdrawn after some ties were delivered
and accepted ) ; Restatement , Contracts , Sec. 54 (notice required where the
offeror "has no adequate means of ascertaining " that performance has been
completed ) . Kentucky decisions as to notice requirement are in conflict .
The question usually arises in connection with guaranties of future credits .
In early cases reasonable notice of advances made under a general letter
of credit was required . Kincheloe v . Holmes , 46 Ky . ( 7 B. Mon. ) 5 ( 1846) .
This case resulted in the requirement that there be notice of the intention
to accept. However , other cases held that notice as to specific advances
was not required , the rationale being that a promise to pay on the default
of the principle is an absolute guaranty and no notice of acceptance is
required . See White Sewing Mach . Co. v . Powell, 25 K. L. R. 97 , 74 S. W.
730 ( 1903 ) ; McGowan v . Wells ' Trustee , 184 Ky . 772 , 213 S. W. 573 ( 1919 ) .
See Kentucky Annotations to Restatement , Contracts , Secs . 45, 54 and 56 .
Section 2-207 . Additional Terms in Acceptance or Confirmation .
Summary
( 1 ) A definite and seasonable expression of acceptance or a written
confirmation which is sent within a reasonable time operates as an accept-
ance even though it states terms additional to or different from those
offered or agreed upon , unless acceptance is expressly made conditional
on assent to the additional or different terms .
(2) The additional terms are to be construed as proposals for addition
to the contract . Between merchants such terms become part of the con-
tract unless :
37

(a) the offer expressly limits acceptance to the terms of the
offer ;
(b) they materially alter it ; or
(c) notification of objection to them has already been given or is
given within a reasonable time after notice of them is received .
(3) Conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of a con-
tract is sufficient to establish a contract for sale although the writings of
the parties do not otherwise establish a contract . In such cases the terms
of the particular contract consist of those terms on which the writings of
the parties agree, together with any supplementary terms incorporated
under any other provisions of this act. This section is an effort to deal with
the situation . The basic rule is that a definite and seasonable expression of
acceptance or a written confirmation sent within a reasonable time operates
as an acceptance even though it contains terms additional to those offered
as previously agreed upon unless acceptance is expressly made conditional
on assent to the additional or different terms . Between merchants , the
additional terms become part of the contract unless the offer expressly
limits acceptance to the terms of the offer ; they materially alter it, or ob-
jection to them has been given or is given within a reasonable time after
notice of them is received .
(4) Contract by Conduct of Parties . Even though the writings of the
parties do not establish a contract , if the parties recognize a contract
between them , then a contract will be established . The terms of the con-
tract will be those agreed upon by the parties , whether in writing or supple-
mentary terms incorporated under other provisions of this Act .
Comment
( 1 ) Additional or Different Terms . Subsection ( 1) of the Code ap-
pears to have changed the orthodox rule that the acceptance of an offer
must be absolute and identical with the offer . See Restatement , Contracts ,
Sec. 59 and Kentucky Annotations . Under the Code the inclusion of
additional or different terms does not serve to destroy the efficacy of a
definite and reasonable expression of acceptance . Subsection ( 2 ) provides
that the additional terms are proposals for additions to the contract . See ,
Restatement, Contracts , Secs . 60 and 62 and Kentucky Annotations . Such
additional or different terms become part of the agreement if they are not
expressly excepted, or do not materially vary the terms of the offer or
reasonable obections are not seasonably made .
(2) The intent of UCC 2-207 is to meet the situation where a part
of the negotiations of the parties consisted of an exchange of business
forms . The old rule that acceptance of an offer had to conform to the
terms of the offer before it would be effective meant that almost any
variance between the terms of the offer and purported acceptance would
render the latter ineffective to form a contract . This rule never worked
too satisfactorily . Quite often a form intended as an acceptance or con-
firmation of a previously established agreement contains certain printed
provisions which are at variance with the terms of a printed form offer .
Everyday experience tells us that often the parties proceed with the agree-
ment notwithstanding these differences and the performance produces an
38

adjustment of the conflict . However , under the present rules , these
variations often compel the conclusion that there was no contract , even
though it was clear that the parties intended a "deal" when the forms
were forwarded . This permits a dissatisfied party to escape the transaction
by pointing to discrepancies in the forms which he probably otherwise
would not have regarded as serious .
Section 2-208. Course of Performance or Practical Construction .
Summary
(1) Where the contract involves repeated occasions for performance
by either party and the other party has knowledge and opportunity to
object , any course of performance accepted or acquiesced in without objec-
tion shall be relevant to determine meaning of the agreement or to show
waiver or modification of any term inconsistent with such course .
(2) The express terms of the agreement and any such course of
performance , as well as any course of dealing and usage of trade , shall be
construed whenever reasonable as consistent with each other ; but when
such construction is unreasonable , express terms shall control course of
performance and course of performance shall control both course of
dealing and usage of trade (UCC 1-205 ) .
(3) Subject to the provisions of the next section on modification
and waiver , such course of performance shall be relevant to show a waiver
or modification of any term inconsistent with such course of performance .
Kentucky Statutes
No such general provision but concept of this section recognized
by terms such as " course of dealing," " the circumstances of the case,"
"the conduct of the parties ," etc. , in Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361 .
Comment
On rendering the failure to object to a course of performance
"relevant ," but not controlling , to determine the meaning of the agree-
ment , see , accord , KRS 361.030 ( contract "may be inferred from the con-
duct of the parties " ) ; KRS 361.710 (implied terms may be negatived or
varied "by the course of dealing " ) ; Myers v . Land , 314 Ky . 514, 235 S. W.
2d 988 ( 1950 ) ; compare Restatement , Contracts , Secs . 72 , 235 ( e) and Ken-
tucky Annotations . As to "waiver " by failure to object , see , accord, KRS
361.490 ( failure to give notice of breach of warranty ) ; Restatement ,
Contracts , Sec. 298, and Kentucky Annotations .
Section 2-209. Modification , Recission and Waiver .
Summary
(1) An agreement modifying a contract within this article needs no
consideration .
(2) A signed agreement which excludes modification or recission
except by a signed writing cannot be otherwise modified or rescinded ,
39

but except as between merchants such a requirement on a form supplied
by the merchant must be separately signed by the other party .
(3 ) The requirements of the Statute of Frauds ( UCC 2-201 ) must be
satisfied if the modified contract is within its provisions .
(4) An attempt at modification or recission can operate as a waiver
even though it does not satisfy subsections ( 2) or ( 3 ) .
(5) A party who has made a waiver affecting an executory portion
of the contract may retract the waiver by reasonable notification received
by the other party that strict performance will be required of any term
waived , unless the retraction would be unjust in view of a material change
of position in reliance on the waiver .
Kentucky Statutes
None.
Comment
The Code presents innovations on the subject of modification , re-
cission and waiver . An agreement to modify a contract is in itself a
contract , hence , under present law it needs a supporting consideration as
much as any other contract . Subsection ( 1) abolishes the requirement
of consideration in agreements modifying sale contracts and the mere
agreement to modify is sufficient , unless the original signed agreement
excludes modification or recission except by another signed writing ,
(subsection 2 ) . Whether a modification of a contract is effective under
the established doctrines of consideration is often a very difficult problem.
See Restatement , Contracts , Secs . 76, 88-90 , and Kentucky Annotations .
Subsection ( 2) also contains a special safeguard when applied between
merchant and nonmerchant . It is provided that when a nonmerchant
subscribes to a contract containing a clause requiring a signed writing
for an effective modification or recission on a form supplied by a merchant ,
a nonmerchant must separately sign the clause in order to make it enforce-
able .
It appears that subsection (2) would overrule Kentucky cases per-
mitting the modification of a written contract by an oral agreement , in
cases where a signed agreement excludes oral modification . Illinois Cent .
R. R. Co. v . Manion , 113 Ky. 7, 67 S. W. 40 ( 1902 ) ; Continental Ins . Co. of
New York v . Simpson , 220 Ky . 167, 294 S. W. 1048 ( 1927 ) ; see Restatement ,
Contracts , Sec. 407 , and Kentucky Annotations . Under subsections (4) and
(5) , an ineffective attempt at modification can operate as a waiver , but
the waiver can be withdrawn if it has not been acted on. Accord , Restate-
ment, Contracts , Secs . 88 (2) , 297, and Kentucky Annotations .
The rule of subsection (3) that the requirements of the Statute of
Frauds must be satisfied if the contract as modified is within its provisions
is substantially the same as that of Secs. 222-224 of the Restatement,
Contracts , Kentucky Annotations to the sections show that no cases were
found . Corbin , in his treatise on Contracts states that it is a fair general
40

statement that the statute of frauds affects agreements to vary or to
rescind a previous contract in the same way that it affects other agreements .
For a detailed discussion on the subject , see Corbin , Contracts , Secs . 301
to 312. See also , Restatement , Contracts , Sec. 407 and Kentucky Annotations .
Section 2-210 . Delegation of Performance ; Assignment of Rights .
Summary
(1) A party may perform through a delegate unless otherwise agreed
or unless the other party has a substantial interest in having the original
promisor perform or control the performance . No delegation relieves a
party of any duty to perform or any liability for breach .
(2) Unless otherwise agreed all rights of either seller or buyer can
be assigned except where the assignment would materially change the
duty of the other party , or increase materially the burden or risk imposed
on him by his contract , or impair materially his chance of obtaining return
performance . A right to damages for breach of the whole contract or a
right arising out of the assignor's due performance of his entire obligation
can be assigned despite agreement otherwise .
(3) Unless the circumstances indicate the contrary , a prohibition of
assignment of "the contract " bars only the delegation of the assignor's
performance .
(4) An assignment of "the contract" or of "all my rights under the
contract " or in similar general terms is an assignment of rights and unless
the language or the circumstances indicate the contrary , it is a delegation
of performance of the assignor's duties and its acceptance by the assignee
is his promise to perform . Either the assignor or other party to the contract
may enforce this promise .
(5) The other party may treat an assignment of performance as
creating grounds for insecurity and may without prejudice to his rights
against the assignor demand assurances from the assignee .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Performance Through a Delegate . Subsection ( 1 ) corresponds
to the rule of Sec. 160 ( 3 ) ( a ) of the Restatement , Contracts that per-
formance may be delegated , unless such performance "would vary mater-
ially from performance by the person named in the contract ..." Cases
cited in the Kentucky Annotations seem to follow this rule.
(2) Asignment of Rights . The rule of subsection (2) appears in
accord with Sec. 151 of the Restatement , Contracts . See Kentucky Annota-
tions , citing cases in accord .
41

(3) Assignment of Contract as Delegation of Performance ; Inter-
pretation . The rule of subsection ( 4) that, unless the circumstances indicate
to the contrary , an assignment of the contract not only assigns rights but
delegates duties , is comparable to that of Sec. 164 (1 ) of the Restatement,
Contracts . See Kentucky Annotations . Subsection (4) is also comparable
to the rule of Sec . 164 (2) of the Restatement , Contracts , that acceptance
of the assignment is interpreted " as a promise to the assignor to assume the
performance of the assignor's duties ." The Code , however , goes beyond
the rule of the restatement by providing the promise is enforceable by
both the assignor and the other party . See 32 Ky. L. J. 216 .
(4) Right to Assurances from Assignee . Subsection ( 5) providing for
"assurances " is new.
Part 3
GENERAL OBLIGATIONS AND CONSTRUCTION OF CONTRACT
Introductory Comment
This part states the general obligations of the parties to the sale
in terms of the provisions of the contract . Since the policy of the Code
is to emphasize the terms of the contract and de-emphasize the title
concept, the rules of construction of the contract are set out with particu-
larity . In addition to general rules , specific rules are given for many
commonly used provisions in order that uniformity may be achieved . The
provisions of this part permit the court to refuse to enforce all or any part
of an "unconscionable " contract . This appears to be a new theory in the
law of sales .
Section 2-301 . General Obligations of Parties .
Summary
The obligation of the seller is to transfer and deliver and that of the
buyer is to accept and pay in accordance with the contract .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.110 , 361.410 .
Comment
UCC 2-301 in substance reiterates Sec . 41 of the Uniform Sales Act ,
KRS 361.410, in stating that the primary obligation of both parties is per-
formance in accordance with the contract . The term "obligation " is used
so as to include the conditional aspects of delivery and payment . The Code
throughout increases the emphasis placed on the contract in sales trans-
actions .
42

Section 2-302 . Unconscionable Contract or Clause .
Summary
(1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause
of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made , the
court may refuse to enforce the contract or it may enforce the remainder
of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the
application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable
result.
(2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or
any clause thereof may be unconscionable , the parties shall be afforded
a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting ,
purpose , and effect to aid the court in making the determination .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section states a theory new to sales law. The section appears
to be intended to carry equity practice into the sales field. See Restatement ,
Contracts , Sec . 367 , and Kentucky Annotations . The Commissioners Com-
ment , citing cases from other states , states that the results intended under
this section have been accomplished by indirection by such means as
interpretation of language . Compare Restatement , Contracts , Sec. 236 and
Kentucky Annotations . For general discussion see Corbin , Contracts , Secs .
128 and 1164 .
Subsection ( 2) is addressed to the court . It states clearly that parties
have a right to a reasonable opportunity to present evidence going to the
question of unconscionability . Under the section , the court , in its discretion ,
may refuse to enforce the contract as a whole if it is permeated by
unconscionability or it may strike any single clause or group of clauses
which are so tainted or which are contrary to the essential purpose of
the agreement .
Section 2-303 . Allocation or Division of Risks .
Summary
Where this article allocates a risk or a burden as between the parties
"unless otherwise agreed ," the agreement may not only shift the allocation
but may also divide the risk or burden .
Kentucky Statutes
None.
Comment
This section is consistent with present law . See Uniform Sales Act ,
Sec . 71 , KRS 361.710 (" any right may be negatived or varied by
43

agreement .. " ) . See also, annotation to UCC 2-719 . Compare also , in
accord , Sec. 22 of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.220 (risk follows title
"unless otherwise agreed " ) .
Section 2-304 . Price Payable in Money , Goods , Realty , or
Otherwise .
Summary
( 1 ) The price may be payable in money or otherwise , and if payable
in goods , the party obligated to deliver goods is a seller with respect to
those goods .
(2 ) When the price is paid by an interest in realty , the seller's obli-
gations are covered by this act, but the transfer of the interest in realty
and the transferor's obligations in connection therewith are not .
Kentucky Statutes
Subsections (2) and (3) of KRS 361.090 .
Comment
(1) The provision that the price can be paid "in money or other-
wise " broadens the coverage of this article beyond that of Sec. 9 ( 2) of
the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.090 , under which the price "may be made
payable in any personal property ." The effect of this change is seen most
clearly under subsection ( 2 ) , below .
(2) Under subsection ( 2 ) , if realty is to be exchanged for goods , the
Code fixes the obligations of the seller with respect to the goods , although
not the realty . This constitutes an expansion beyond the scope of the
Uniform Sales Act ; under Sec. 9 (3 ) , KRS 361.090 (3) , the present act is
inapplicable if " any interest in real estate constitutes the whole or part
of the consideration " for a sale .
Section 2-305 . Open Price Term .
Summary
(1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a sale even though
the price is not settled . In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the
time for delivery if:
(a) nothing is said as to price ; or
(b) the price is left to be agreed on by the parties and they fail
to agree; or
(c) the price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or
standard set by a third person or agency and it is not so set .
(2) A price to be fixed by one of the parties is a price to be fixed
by him in good faith .
44

(3) When a price which is to be fixed otherwise than by agreement
of the parties fails to be so fixed through the fault of one party , the other
may at his option treat the contract as cancelled or himself fix a reasonable
price.
(4) Where the parties do not intend to be bound unless the price
is a fixed or agreed price and the price is not fixed or agreed then there
is no contract . In such a case the buyer must return any goods not used,
the reasonable value of any goods already used, and the seller must return
any portion of the price paid on account.
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.090 , 361.100 .
Comment
The rule under this section that the parties can bind themselves to
a contract for sale without settling the price "if they so intend ," resolves
an ambiguity between Secs . 9 ( 1) and 9 (4) of the Uniform Sales Act,
KRS 361.090 . See Gaines & Sea v . Reynolds Tobacco Co., 163 Ky . 716, 174
S. W. 482 ( 1915 ) , (a price at original cost, plus cost of handling and a " nice"
or reasonable profit was held too indefinite for enforcement ) . Compare
Jenkins v . Richardson , 29 Kу . ( 6 J. J. Marsh ) 441 , 22 Am . Dec. 82 ( 1831 ) ,
wherein it was held that where no price is expressly agreed on by parties
or where seller has not put a specific price on goods when he sends them
to the buyer , it is well settled at common law that the price is to be
determined ordinarily by the market value or reasonable value of the
goods . Compare the cases supra under UCC 2-201 where memorandum
does not state the price . It thus appears that this section of the Code will
probably modify present law to render binding agreements where no price
is stated. See Restatement, Contracts , Sec. 32 and Kentucky Annotations .
(See particularly illustrations 9 and 10.) The buyer may not keep the
goods without compensating the seller for them . Such was the result
under Sec. 9 (4) of the Uniform Sales Act , although no price was stated .
Under this section the parties , if they so intend , may conclude a contract
without settling on the question of price . This would leave the price to be
determined ultimately by the court , by its findings as to what would con-
stitute a reasonable price in the event of disagreement or inability to apply
a standard .
For general discussion on points raised by this section see Corbin ,
Contracts , Secs . 97 through 99. Also 46 Am . Jur., Sales , Secs . 179 and 180 .
Section 2-306 . Output , Requirements and Exclusive Dealings .
Summary
(1) A term which measures the quantity of the goods by the output of
the seller or the requirements of the buyer means such actual output or re-
quirements as may occur in good faith , except that no quantity unreasonably
45

disproportionate to any stated estimate or normal or otherwise comparable
prior output or requirement may be tendered or demanded .
(2 ) A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive
dealing in the kind of goods concerned, imposes unless otherwise agreed an
obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the
buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale .
Kentucky Statutes
None .
Comment
The Code deals with contracts which are based on the "output " of the
seller or on the "requirements " of the buyer . The Uniform Sales Act em-
bodied in KRS Ch . 361 did not deal with this question .
Requirement and output contracts are enforceable in Kentucky. Ross-
Vaughan Tobacco Co. v . Johnson , 182 Ky . 325, 206 S.W. 487 ( 1918 ) . See
Restatement , Contracts , Sec. 32 , Illustration 12 , and Kentucky Annotations .
2 Williston , Sales , Sec. 464A et seq . , 1 Corbin , Contracts , Sec. 156 et seq; 77
C.J.S. , Sales, Sec. 171 and 172. See also Mitchell - Taylor Tie Co. v . Whitaker ,
158 Ky . 651 , 166 S.W. 193 ( 1914) (the number of ties a seller could make
within a year is not too indefinite ) ; Ayer & Lord Tie Co. v . Ο . Τ . Ο'Βαnnor
& Co. , 164 Ky. 34, 174 S.W. 783 ( 1915 ) (all ties vendor could deliver before
a certain date is not too uncertain ) . But compare Springton Coal Co. v .
Bowling , 228 Ky. 317, 14 S.W. 2d 1082 ( 1929 ) , where an output contract
covering all the coal mined was held invalid for lack of mutuality on the
basis that there was no obligation to deliver . ( This decision being against
the decided weight of authority ) .
The Code provision that "output " or " requirement " means "such actual
output or requirements as may occur in good faith " finds support in Ken-
tucky Tobacco Products Co. v. Lucas , 5 F. 2d 723 ( W.D. Ky .) 1925. See
also 1 Williston, Contracts , Sec. 104A .
Subsection ( 1) states that no quantity unreasonably disproportionate to
either a stated estimate or otherwise comparable prior output and require-
ments may be tendered or demanded . It is probable that this part of the
subsection is simply a restatement of the good faith test and not an addi-
tional limitation upon quantitative variations . The comments point out that
variations , even to the point of cessation of requirements , are permitted so
long as in good faith and not for the purpose of avoiding the contract . Thus
in a fixed -price requirements contract , the requirements will perhaps
dwindle to nothing as the market price sinks below the contract price since ,
presumably , the buyer will be forced from the market by lower priced com-
petition . This is not a breach by the buyer , but he is not permitted to shut
down just to avoid the loss which will accrue because of his purchases at a
price above the market . The problem is one of determining what is "un-
reasonably disproportionate ." If this means that any variation which is not
attributable to bad faith is permissible , then the assertion that the section
is a restatement of the good faith test is correct . If it means that under no
46

circumstances can the variation extend beyond what could be termed minor
in view of the past quantities consumed or produced or estimates of those
quantities , then the expressed conclusion is incorrect .
Subsection (2) requires that , in an exclusive dealership contract , the
parties use their best efforts to supply goods or promote their sale. The
Commissioners Comment indicate that the agent is required to use due
diligence in the development of the territory and the principal is expected to
refrain from supplying any other dealer or agent within the exclusive terri-
tory . No Kentucky case has been found which discusses the requirement of
best effort in this type of contract .
Section 2-307 . Delivery in Single Lot or Several Lots .
Summary
Unless otherwise agreed , all goods called for by a contract for sale must
be tendered in a single lot and payment is due only on such tender , but
where the circumstances give either party the right to make or demand de-
livery in several lots , the price may be apportioned and demanded for each
lot.
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.450 ( 1) , 361.420 .
Comment
(1) Presumption of Single Delivery . The presumption that the goods
shall be tendered in a single delivery carries forward the same rule laid
down in Sec. 45 ( 1) of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.450 ( 1) .
(2) Presumption of Separate Payment for Each Lot . The presumption
that where installment delivery is permitted , the buyer shall pay for each
lot separately , applies to this particular situation the general rule of con-
current payment and delivery set forth in Sec. 42 of the Uniform Sales Act ,
KRS 361.420 . Cf. UCC 2-511 ( 1) . See Gilmore & Co. v . W. B. Samuels & Co. ,
135 Ky . 706, 123 S.W. 271 ( 1909 ) ; Collins v . Swan -Day Lumber Co. , 158 Ky .
231 , 164 S.W. 813 ( 1914 ) . The qualification upon this rule that the price be
apportionable was not expressly stated under the Uniform Sales Act , but is
consistent with it, since inability to apportion the price would bear upon
the intent of the parties as to separate payment .
Section 2-308 . Absence of Specified Place for Delivery .
Unless otherwise agreed :
Summary
(a) the place of delivery is the seller's place of business or , if he
has none, his residence; but
47

(b) where identified goods are to the knowledge of both parties
located in another place at the time of contracting then that
place is the place for delivery ;
(c) documents of title may be delivered through customary bank-
ing channels .
Kentucky Statutes
Subsections ( a) and ( b) -KRS 361.430 ( 1) ; Paragraph (c)-none .
Comment
Subsections ( a) and (b) state the same presumption against placing on
the seller the obligation to deliver as was set forth in Sec . 43 ( 1) of the
Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.430 ( 1) . Wilmouth v . Patton , 5 Ky. (2 Bibb )
280 (1811 ) ; Chandler v . Robertson , 39 Ky . ( 9 Dana ) 291 ( 1840 ) ; Sousely v .
Burns ' Adm'r , 73 Ky . ( 10 Bush ) 87 ( 1873 ) . See also 2 Williston , Sales ,
Sec. 450 .
Subsection ( c) provides that delivery of documents of title may be
made through customary banking channels . The Code comment indicates
that notification to the buyer by the bank is all that is necessary , thus
eliminating the necessity of a tender at the buyer's address . No cases have
been found applying such a rule in Kentucky , but it is unquestionably the
rule by business custom .
Section 2-309 . Absence of Specific Time Provisions ; Notice of
Termination .
Summary
(1) The time for shipment or delivery if not specified by this article
or by agreement of the parties , shall be a reasonable time .
(2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is
indefinite in duration , the contract is valid for a reasonable time but unless
otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party .
(3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening
of an agreed event requires that reasonable notice shall be given the other
party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its opera-
tion would be unconscionable .
Kentucky Statutes
Subsection ( 1 ) see KRS 361.430 ( 2 ) , 361.450 ( 2) , 361.480 . Sub-
sections (2 ) and (3) none .
Comment
(1) Performance Within a Reasonable Time . Subsection (1 ) expresses
in somewhat broader terms the requirement of Sec. 43 (2) of the Uniform
Sales Act , KRS 361.430 ( 2 ) . Louisville & N. R. Co. v . Cox , 145 Ky . 667 , 141
S. W. 389 ( 1911 ) ; Crosslan v . Ky. Blue Grass Seed Growers Co - op Assn ., 103
48

F. 2d 565 ( 6th Cir. 1939) ; Fyffe v . Skaggs , 246 Ky . 5 , 54 S.W. 2d 369 ( 1933 ) ;
Fairbanks v . Hooper , 147 Ky . 154, 143 S.W. 1025 ( 1912 ) . See also Williston,
Contracts , Sec. 38. Subsection ( 1) extends the Sales Act provision "to any
other action under a contract " as well as to shipment .
(2) This subsection would substitute a single rule of duration of “a
reasonable time ," subject to termination on notice, in all cases where the
contract fails to specify the duration . Under existing law the period of
duration is determined by reference to the subject matter and the intention
of the parties ascertained from the surrounding circumstances . If no time is
stated , personal service contracts are terminable at will . Hudson v . Cincin-
nati , etc. Ry . Co. , 152 Ky. 711 , 154 S.W. 47 ( 1913 ) ; Hardy v . Myers , 206 Ky .
562, 267 S.W. 1110 ( 1925 ) . See Restatement , Contracts , Secs . 32, 33 , 44 , and
Kentucky Annotations .
(3) Notice of Termination . Subsection ( 3) follows the rule that revo-
cation of an offer must be received by the offeree to be effective . In all
agreements indefinite as to duration , notice of election to terminate is re-
quired , at least after either party has begun performance . Peters Branch of
International Shoe Co. v. Jones , 247 Ky. 193 , 56 S.W. 2d 994 ( 1933 ) ; Elkhorn
Consol . Coal & Coke Co. v. Eaton , Rhodes & Co. 163 Ky . 306, 173 S.W. 798
(1915); Restatement , Contracts , Secs . 41 , 44 , and Kentucky Annotations .
But the provision invalidating "unconscionable " agreements dispensing with
notification changes the law . Compare UCC 1-102 , 1-204 , 2-302 .
Section 2-310 . Open Time for Payment or Running of Credit ;
Authority to Ship Under Reservation .
Unless otherwise agreed :
Summary
(a) payment is due at the time and place at which the buyer is to
receive the goods even though the shipment is the place of
delivery ; and
(b) if the seller is authorized to send the goods , he may ship them
under reservation and may tender the documents , but the
buyer may inspect the goods before payment is due unless
such inspection is inconsistent with the terms of the contract
(UCC 2-513 ) ; and
(c) if delivery is authorized by way of documents otherwise than
by subsection (b) , then payment is due at the place at which
buyer is to receive the documents regardless of where the
goods are received ; and
(d) where the seller is required or authorized to ship the goods
on credit , the credit period will run from the time of shipment
unless the invoice is post-dated or delayed , in which case the
commencement of the credit period will be correspondingly
delayed .
49

Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.420 , 361.470 ( 2) .
Comment
( 1) Time for Payment . The presumption against extension of credit
is consistent with existing law . Uniform Sales Act , Sec. 42 , KRS 361.420
(Payment due at "delivery ," which in some circumstances occurs at point of
shipment . ) Cf. Uniform Sales Act , Sec. 46 , KRS 361.460 ( delivery to carrier
deemed delivery to buyer unless seller to pay freight ) . However , in making
payment due only on " receipt " the Code would delay the time for payment
arising under a technical reading of the present statute .
(2) The provision in subsection (d) is new law, described in part in
the Comment as "common commercial understanding ." The provision for
extension of the credit period when the invoice is delayed seems to follow
the principle of the Restatement , Contracts , Sec. 276, especially Illustrations
4 and 5.
Section 2-311 . Options and Cooperation Respecting Performance .
Summary
(1) An agreement for sale which is otherwise sufficiently definite is
not made invalid by the fact that it leaves particulars of performance to be
specified by one of the parties . Any such specification must be made in good
faith and within limits set by commercial reasonableness .
(2 ) Unless otherwise agreed , specifications relating to the assortment
of goods are at the buyer's option and except as otherwise provided in sub-
sections ( 1 ) ( c ) and ( 3 ) of UCC 2-319 specifications or arrangements re-
lating to the shipment are at the seller's option .
(3) Where such specifications would materially affect the other party's
performance but it is not seasonably made or where one party's cooperation
is necessary to the performance of the other but is not forthcoming , the
other party in addition to all other remedies :
(a)
(b)
is excused for resulting delay in his own performance ; and
may perform in any reasonable manner or after the time for
a material part of his own performance treat the failures to
specify as a breach .
Kentucky Statutes
None.
Comment
(1) Indefiniteness . On subsection ( 1) under which later specification
of details does not render contract indefinite see accord : Restatement , Con-
tracts , Sec . 32 , and Kentucky Annotations .
(2) Specifications . This provision is new law, described in the Code
Comments as a "standard commercial interpretation ."
50

(3) Lack of Cooperation . The Sales Act did not deal with this problem .
See, accord : Restatement , Contracts , Sec . 295 , and Kentucky Annotations ;
United Mercantile Agencies v . Silver Fleet Motor Express , Inc. , 50 F. Supp .
602 ( W.D. Ky . 1943) .
Section 2-312 . Warranty of Title and Against Infringement ;
Buyer's Obligation Against Infringement .
(1)
Summary
Subject to subsection (2) there is in a contract for sale a warranty
by the seller that there is :
(a) good title and rightful transfer ; and
(b) the goods shall be delivered free from any security interest
or other lien or encumbrance of which the buyer at the time
of contracting has no knowledge .
(2) A warranty under subsection (1) will be excluded or modified
only by specific language or by circumstances which give the buyer reason
to know that the person selling does not claim title in himself or that he is
purporting to sell only such rights or title as he or a third person may have .
(3 ) Unless otherwise agreed , a seller who is a merchant regularly deal-
ing in goods of the kind warrants that the goods shall be delivered free of
the rightful claim of any third person by way of infringement or the like ,
but a buyer who furnishes specifications to the seller must hold the seller
harmless against any such claim which arises out of compliance with the
specifications .
KRS 361.130 .
Kentucky Statutes
(1 )
Comment
( a) Title . The warranty of title of subsection ( 1 ) ( a) is com-
parable to that of Sec. 13 (1) of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.130 ( 1) .
However , under the Sales Act the warranty of title was an implied warranty
whereas the Code makes it an absolute statutory warranty . Sego v. Lynch ,
233 Ky . 176 , 25 S.W. 2d 353 ( 1930 ) ; Sandidge v . Stephens, 252 Ky . 620 , 67
S.W. 2d 967 ( 1934) .
(b) Freedom from Liens , Encumbrances and Claims . The warranty
of freedom from liens , encumbrances and claims provided for under sub-
section ( 1) ( b ) is comparable to that of Sec. 13 (2) and (3 ) of the Uniform
Sales Act , KRS 361.130 ( 2 ) ( 3 ) .
(2) Exception Where Title Not Claimed by Seller . Subsection (2 ) re-
phrases Sec. 13 (4) of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.130 (4 ) , freeing a
seller from obligations if the seller does not purport to have title or to be
able to convey title.
51

(3) Buyers Obligation Against Infringement . Subsection ( 3) limits to
merchant sellers the warranty against infringement and makes express pro-
vision for protection to seller against charges of infringement where goods
are manufactured to a buyer's specifications .
Section 2-313 . Express Warranties by Affirmation , Promise ,
Description , Sample .
Summary
(1) Express warranties by the seller are created as follows :
( a) any affirmation of fact or promise in relation to the goods
which becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an
express warranty that the goods will conform ; and
(b) any description which is made part of the basis of the bargain
creates an express warranty that the goods will conform ; and
(c) any sample or model which is made part of the basis of the
bargain creates an express warranty that the goods will
conform .
(2) It is not necessary that formal words of warranty be used to
create the warranty or that the seller have specific intention to create a
warranty but an affirmation merely of value or a statement purporting to
be merely the seller's opinion or commendation does not create a warranty .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.120 , 361.140 and 361.160 .
Comment
(1) Express Warranties ; Scope and Terminology . In addition to in-
cluding affirmations and promises as " express " warranties , the Code treats
as " express " warranties descriptions of the goods and the use of samples or
models . Cf. Uniform Sales Act , Secs . 14 and 16 , KRS 361.140 and 361.160
(termed " implied " warranties ) .
(a) Affirmations and Promises . Subsection ( 1) ( a) , in giving both
affirmations and promises the effect of warranties , is in accord with Sec . 12
of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.120 . Steuerle v . National Bond & Inv . Co. ,
272 Ky . 728, 115 S.W. 2d 283 ( 1938 ) ; Wedding v . Duncan , 310 Ky . 374, 220
S. W. 2d 564 ( 1949 ) ; Balfour -Guthrie v . Du Bois , 245 Ky . 640, 54 S.W. 2d
13 ( 1932 ) ; King v . Ohio Valley Terminix Co., 309 Ky . 35, 214 S.W. 2d 993
(1948) .
(b) Descriptions . Subsection ( 1 ) (b) is comparable to Sec. 14 of the
Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.140, in treating descriptions of the goods as
warranties , although in the Code they are termed " express " rather than
" implied ." Wedding v. Duncan , 310 Ky. 374, 220 S.W. 2d 564 ( 1949 ) .
(c) Sample or Model . Subsection ( 1 ) (c) is comparable to Sec . 16
of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.160, under which , in the case of a sale
52

by sample , there is a warranty that the bulk correspond with the sample .
Balfour-Guthrie &Co. v. L. S. Du Bois Son & Co. , 245 Ky . 640 , 54 S.W. 2d
13 ( 1932 ) ; Frick Co. v. Wiley , 290 Ky . 665, 162 S.W. 2d 190 (1942 ) .
Materiality of Affirmation , Promise , Description , Sample . Under the
Code , the qualification that affirmations , etc. create a warranty if made " as
a basis of" the bargain , appears to be substantially the same as the "reliance "
qualification in Sec. 12 of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.120 . Van Deren
Hardware Co. v . Preston , 224 Ky . 170, 5 S.W. 2d 1052 ( 1928 ) .
(2) Formal Words Not Necessary . Subsection ( 2) rendering formal
words unnecessary is in accord with Sec. 12 of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS
361.120 ( warranties may arise from "any" affirmation or promise ) .
Statements of Value or Opinion . The qualification as to statements of
"value" rephrases but does not appear materially to change Sec. 12 of the
Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.120 . King v . Ohio Valley Terminix Co. , 309 Ky .
35 , 214 S.W. 2d 993 ( 1948 ) ; Wedding v. Duncan , 310 Ky . 374, 220 S.W. 2d
564 ( 1949 ) ; Vandiver v . Wilson , 244 Ky . 601, 51 S.W. 2d 899 ( 1932 ) ; M. F.
Marx Co. v. Beha Laundry Co., 224 Ky . 263, 6 S.W. 2d 245 ( 1928 ) .
Section 2-314. Implied Warranty ; Merchantability ; Usage
of Trade.
Summary
(1) Unless excluded or modified , a warranty of merchantability will
be implied in every contract for sale where the seller is a merchant . Under
this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on
the premises or elsewhere is a sale .
(2 ) Merchantable goods are those which at least :
(a) pass without objection in the trade as described in the con-
tract; and
(b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality ; and
(c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are
used; and
(d) are of even run in quality and quantity ; and
(e) are adequately packaged and labeled ; and
(f) conform to the promises made on the label , if any .
(3) Unless excluded or modified , other warranties may arise from
course of dealing or usage of trade .
KRS 361.150 ( 2) .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Scope . ( a) Sales by Merchant . Special responsibility in sales by
merchants carries forward the warranty of merchantability of Sec . 15 ( 2)
53

of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.150 (2 ) , for goods bought by description
from " a seller who deals in goods of the description ." The Code , however ,
broadens the scope of the warranty by omitting the qualification that the
sale be " by description ."
For decisions applying the warranty of merchantable quality see : Porter
v . Craddock , 84 F. Supp . 704 ( W.D. Ky . 1949 ) ; Finck v . Albers Super Markets ,
136 F. 2d 191 (6th Cir. 1943 ) ; Martin v . Great A. & P. Tea Co. , 301 Ky . 429,
192 S.W. 2d 201 ( 1945 ) ; Nehi Beverage Co. v . Hall , 295 Ky . 353, 174 S.W. 2d
509 ( 1943 ) ; McBridge v . Farmers ' Seed Ass'n , 248 Ky . 514, 58 S.W. 2d 909
(1933 ); Great A. & P. Tea Co. v . Eiseman , 259 Ky . 103 , 81 S.W. 2d 900 ( 1935 ) .
(b) Serving of Food or Drink . The Uniform Sales Act did not specify
whether serving of food or drink would be a "sale" within its scope . The
Code adopts the majority view by declaring the serving of food or drink as
a " sale ." See Anno ., 7 A.L.R. 2d 1027 ( 1949 ) .
(2) Definition of Merchantable Quality . The Uniform Sales Act , unlike
the Code , does not establish tests to be used in determining whether goods
are of "merchantable quality ." Neither have the Kentucky cases expressly
laid down a series of tests to be applied in determining whether goods are
merchantable . But the tests of the Code do not seem to make any change
in the conception of merchantability as that term is used in the cases cited
above. The tests of subsection (2) ( b ) and ( c) which are in terms of "fair
average quality " and " ordinary purposes " are consistent with the majority
view which denies liability under an implied warranty where injury is the
result of the buyer's individual peculiarity .
(3) Warranty by Usage of Trade . Subsection ( 3) is comparable to
Sec. 15 ( 5) of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.150 ( 5 ) . See , North American
Fertilizer Co. v . Combs , 307 Ky. 869 , 212 S.W. 2d 526 ( 1948 ) .
Section 2-315 . Implied Warranty ; Fitness for Particular Purpose .
Summary
Where the seller has reason to know that the goods are required for
a particular purpose and that the buyer is relying on the seller's skill or
judgment , there is, unless excluded or modified , an implied warranty that
the goods are fit for such purpose .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.150 (1) , (4) and (5) .
Comment
This section roughly approximates Sec. 15 ( 2) of the Uniform Sales
Act , KRS 361.150 (2 ) . The Code is somewhat broader than the present
statute in making clear that a warranty may arise if the buyer relies on
the seller either "to select" or " to furnish " suitable goods . Under the Code a
warranty is also imposed where the seller has "reason to know " a buyer's
54

purpose . Contrast the weaker language of the present statute (warranty
arises where the buyer "makes known" his purpose to the seller ) .
The warranty of fitness of purpose under the Uniform Sales Act , KRS
361.150 , has had broad application in the decisions . See, Great A. & P. Tea
Co. v . Eiseman , 259 Ку . 103, 81 S.W. 2d 869 ( 1935 ) ( grocer held to warranty
that chicken he selected was fit for human consumption ) ; Louisville Grind-
ing & Machine Co. v . Southern Oil and Tar Co. , 230 Ky . 39, 188 S.W. 2d 877
(1929 ) (where seller impliedly warranted oil he selected would be reason-
ably suitable for the purpose of hardening a race track ) ; Day Pulverizer Co.
v . Rutledge , 238 Ky . 817, 38 S.W. 2d 949 ( 1931 ) ( where dealer recommended
particular type rock crusher ) ; McBride v . Farmers Seed Ass'n , 248 Ky . 514,
58 S.W. 2d 909 (1933) (sale of chicken feed ) ; Snead v. Waite , 306 Ky . 587,
208 S.W. 2d 749 ( 1948 ) ; Martin v . Great A. & P. Tea Co., 301 Ky . 429 , 192
S.W. 2d 201 ( 1945) ( retailer liable for defect in commodity sold in sealed
container ) ; Frick Co. v . Wiley , 290 Ky . 665, 162 S.W. 2d 190 ( 1942 ) ; R. B.
Tyler Co. v . Hampton Cracker Co. , 265 Ky . 236, 96 S.W. 2d 593 ( 1936 ) ( no
warranty where buyer orders according to specifications ) .
Sales under Patent or Trade Name . The Code contains no provision
comparable to Sec . 15 (4) of the niform Sales Act, KRS 361.150 (4) , under
which the implied warranty of fitness for a particular purpose is made in-
applicable to sales " of a specified article under its patent or other trade
name ." The Kentucky cases have frequently held the implied warranty of
fitness for a particular purpose inapplicable to a sale under a patent or
trade name by virtue of this section. R. B. Tyler Co. v . Hampton Cracker
Co. , 265 Ky . 236 , 96 S.W. 2d 593 ( 1936 ) ; Halterman v . Louisville Bridge &
Iron Co. , Ky . 254 S.W. 2d 493 ( 1953 ) .
The omission of the trade name qualification from the Code makes it
clear that a sale under a patent or a trade name is merely one factor to be
considered on the question of whether the buyer actually relied on the
seller . This omission , however , seems to clarify the present law rather than
change it, since that factor has not been held to be decisive where other
factors show that the buyer relied on the seller , even though the seller
recommended an article with a trade name . Halterman v . Louisville Bridge
& Iron Co. , Ky . 254 S.W. 2d 493 ( 1953 ) .
Section 2-316 . Exclusion or Modification of Warranties .
Summary
(1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty
and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty , shall be construed
wherever reasonable as consistent with each other ; but subject to the pro-
visions of this artcle on parol or extrinsic evidence ( UCC 2-202 ) negation or
limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable .
(2) Subject to subsection ( 3) , to exclude or modify the implied war-
ranty of merchantability or any part of it, the language must mention mer-
chantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous , and to exclude or
55

modify any implied warranty of fitnes the exclusion must be by a writing
and conspicuous . Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is
sufficient if it states , for example , that "There are no warranties which
extend beyond the description on the face hereof ."
(3) Notwithstanding subsection ( 2) :
(a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise , all implied war-
ranties are excluded by language which makes it plain that
there is no implied warranty such as " as is" "with all faults " ;
and
(b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has fully
examined the goods , sample or model or refused to examine
them , there is no implied warranty as to defects which an
examination would have disclosed ; and
(c) an implied warranty may be excluded or modified by course
of dealing or performance or usage of trade .
(4) Remedies for breach of warranties can be limited in accordance
with UCC 2-718 and 2-719 .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Construction of Disclaimer in Light of Express Warranty . Sub-
section ( 1) seems consistent with the general principles of law that a con-
tract must be read as a whole to give effect , if possible , to each provision
and that specific provisions govern more general language . See Restate-
ment , Contracts , Secs . 235 , 236, and Kentucky Annotations .
(2) Disclaimer of Implied Warranty of Merchantability or Fitness .
Subsection ( 2) is consistent with Sec. 15 ( 6 ) of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS
361.150 (6 ) , under which an express warranty does not negative an implied
warranty , unless inconsistent therewith . Frick Co. v . Wiley , 290 Ky . 665, 162
S.W. 2d 190 ( 1942 ) ; J. B. Colt v . Asher , 239 Ky . 235, 39 S.W. 2d 263 ( 1931 ) ;
Day Pulverizer Co. v . Rutledge , 238 Ky . 817, 38 S.W. 2d 949 ( 1931 ) ; Vandi-
ver v . Wilson , 244 Ky . 601, 51 S.W. 2d 899 ( 1932 ) (warranty expressed in a
written contract of sale excludes by implication all inconsistent implied
warranties on the same subject ) ; Compare Graves Ice Cream Co. v . Rudolph
W. Wurlitzer Co. , 267 Ky. 1, 100 S.W. 2d 819 ( 1937 ) ( where seller having
complied with its obligation under contract was not held liable under im-
plied warranty for damage caused by installation of unsatisfactory con-
densing unit .)
Under this section , a disclaimer of the implied warranty of merchant-
ability must be in specific language or in expressions like "as is," "with all
faults ," or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's
attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no
implied warranty . The warranty of fitness is recognized as a special war-
ranty which a buyer should not count on in the face of a disclaimer , even
56

though the disclaimer is broadly comprehensive and unspecific . Therefore ,
the seller is not required to disclaim a warranty of fitness in specific lan-
guage . However , the buyer is afforded an adequate warning of such dis-
claimer by the requirement that the exclusion must be by a writing and
conspicuous .
The provision in subsection (2) that "language to exclude all implied
warranties of fitness is sufficient , etc." finds support in Kentucky decisions .
Sears , Roebuck & Co. v . Lea , C. A. Ky ., 198 F. 2d 1012 (6th Cir. 1952 ) (under
KRS 361.150 , a warranty will not be implied where contract expressly stipu-
lates against its existence or declares no other warranty is made, or may not
be relied upon ) ; Hopkinsville Motor Co. v . Massie , 228 Ky . 569, 15 S.W. 2d
423 ( 1929 ) , (clause stipulated "no guaranty express or implied other than
herein stated is made by the company ." ) ; Vandiver v . Wilson , 244 Ky . 601 ,
51 S.W. 2d 899 ( 1932 ) .
Exceptions . The exceptions of paragraphs ( a) , ( b) and ( c) of subsection
(3) are situations in which the Code deems the circumstances sufficient in
themselves to call the buyer's attention to the exclusion of the implied war-
ranites , and thus equivalent to a specific exclusion in the contract .
(a) The effect given such words as "as is" is in line with the ordinary
commercial usage of these terms . See 1 Williston, Sales, Sec. 239 ( 1948 ) , and
cases there cited ; Dryer- Whitehead & Goedecke v . Land, 309 Ky . 113 , 216
S.W. 2d 413 ( 1949 ) ; Moss v Yount, 296 Ky . 415, 177 S.W. 2d 372 ( 1944 ) .
(b) subsection ( 2) (b ) incorporates Sec . 15 (3) of the Uniform Sales
Act, KRS 361.150 ( 3 ) . Wells v . Ray , 253 S.W. 2d 590 ( 1952 ) . Middlesboro
Black Gem Coal Co. v . Capps , 297 Ky. 600 , 180 S.W. 2d 567 ( 1944 ) (buyer
losses remedy by failure to inspect the goods upon terms permitted ) . Code
makes mention of buyer's refusal to examine the goods whereas Sales Act
did not. Under the Code , the further requirement that the seller demand
that the buyer examine the goods may be necessary .
(c) Courts of dealing and usage of trade are dealt with under annota-
tion to UCC 1-205 , supra .
(3) Limitation on Remedies . As is indicated in subsection ( 4 ) , limi-
tations on remedies are subject to the provisions of UCC 2-718 and 2-719 ,
infra .
Section 2-317 . Cumulation and Conflict of Warranties Express
or Implied.
Summary
All warranties shall be construed as consistent with each other and
cumulative , but where this is unreasonable the intent of the parties shall
determine the dominant warranty . In ascertaining intent :
(a) exact or technical specifications displace inconsistent sample
or model or general language ; and
57

(b) sample displaces inconsistent general language ; and
(c) express warranties displace implied warranties except fitness
for particular purpose .
Kentucky Statutes
On cumulation of warranties see KRS 361.140 , 361.150 , and 361.160 .
Comment
This section incorporates the narrower rule of Sec. 15 (6) of the Uni-
form Sales Act , KRS 361.150 ( 6) , to the effect that an express warranty does
not negative an implied warranty unless inconsistent therewith . See anno-
tation to UCC 2-316 ( 2 ) and cases there cited, supra .
In addition , the Code establishes a set of presumptions for determining
the parties ' intention with respect to which warranty is dominant in the
situation where to construe the warranties as consistent and cumulative
would be unreasonable . These presumptions for reconciling different types
of warranties have no counterpart in the present statute.
However , the emphasis of the section upon construing warranties as
cumulative and of subsection ( a) on exact specifications displacing general
language of description is consistent with the general contract principles re-
ferred to in the annotation to UCC 2-316 ( 1 ) , supra .
Section 2-318 . Third Party Beneficiaries of Warranties Express
or Implied .
Summary
An express or implied warranty extends to any member of the buyer's
family or household or to his guest if it is reasonable to expect such person
may use or consume the goods and is injured in person by the breach . A
seller may not exclude or limit the operation of this section .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The Uniform Sales Act did not specify whether the seller's warranty
obligations ran to persons other than the immediate buyer . In Snead v.
Waite , 306 Ky. 587, 208 S.W. 2d 749 ( 1948 ) ; a retailer of barbecued meat ,
who cut and wrapped meat in buyer's presence and delivered it to buyer for
home consumption , impliedly warranted that the meat was wholesome , and
was liable in damages for illness of buyer and his family arising from
tainted condition of meat. The prohibition against limiting by contract the
responsibility to third persons is new .
Section 2-319 . F.O.B. and F.A.S. Terms .
Summary
(1) Unless otherwise agreed the term F.O.B. ( free on board ) at a
named place is a delivery term under which :
58

(a) F. O. B. place of shipment means the seller at that place ship
the goods in the manner provided in UCC 2-504 and bear the
expense and risk of placing in carrier's possession ;
(b) F. O. B. destination means the seller at his expense and risk
transport the goods , there and tender delivery as provided in
UCC 2-503 ;
(c) When under ( a) or (b) the term is F. O. B. vessel , car or
other vehicle , the seller at his own expense and risk loads the
goods on board . If it is F. O. B. vessel the buyer must name
the vessel and when proper the seller must comply with UCC
2-323 on form of bill of lading .
(2) Unless otherwise agreed , the term F.A.S. vessel (free alongside )
at a named port is a delivery term under which the seller must :
(a) at his own expense and risk deliver the goods alongside the
vessel in the usual manner , or on a dock designated and
provided by the buyer ; and
(b) obtain and tender a receipt in exchange for which the carrier
shall issue a bill of lading .
(3) Unless otherwise agreed in any case under subsection ( 1) ( a ) or
(c) and (2) the buyer must give needed instructions for delivery including
when needed the loading berth of the vessel , its name and sailing date. The
seller may treat failure of instructions as failure of cooperation , and he may
move the goods in any reasonable manner preparatory to delivery or ship-
ment .
(4) Under F.O.B. vessel or F.A.S. the buyer must make payment
against tender of the documents and the seller may not tender nor the buyer
demand delivery of the goods instead of the documents .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1 ) F.O.B. The Uniform Sales Act did not define shipping terms such
as F.O.B. This section of the Code , however , states commercial understand-
ing that the term defines the point at which responsibility for paying trans-
portation expense is the buyer's . See , American Foreign Trade Definitions
(Chamber of Commerce , 1941 ) II A, II B; 2 Williston, Sales ( 1948 ) Sec. 280.
Risk . By placing the risk on the seller until the F.O.B. point , the Code
is consistent with the provisions of Secs . 19 ( Rules 4 and 5 ) , 22 and 46 of
the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.190 , 361.210 and 361.460 ; Troy Refining Corp.
v . Slagter Oil and Grease Co. , 61 F. Supp . 369 ( W. D. Ky. 1945 ) . One ques-
tion not clearly covered by the Code is that of when risk passes to the buyer
in a situation where the seller quotes a delivered price without use of the
term "F.O.B. " Under UCC 2-319 ( 1) ( b ) risk is on the seller until the
destination is reached if the contract is "F.O.B. the place of destination ."
59

But it is not clear that this same result would follow in the former case . See
UCC 2-509 , infra .
( 2) F.A.S. The Code's definition in subsection (2) expresses com-
mercial understanding of the term . 2 Williston , Sales ( 1948) Sec. 280h ;
American Foreign Trade Definitions (Chamber of Commerce , 1941 ) III .
(3) Buyer's Instructions . The rule of subsection (3) imposing on the
buyer the duty to give needed instructions is also consistent with commercial
understanding . See American Foreign Trade Definitions (Chamber of Com-
merce , 1941 ) II-E, III.
(4) Payment against Documents . Subsection ( 4) is new . Compare
UCC 2-513 ( 3 ) .
Section 2-320 . C.I.F. and C. & F. Terms .
Summary
(1) C.I.F. means the price includes costs of goods , insurance and
freight to named destination . C. & F. or C.F. means the price includes cost
and freight to named destination .
(2 ) Unless otherwise agreed and even though used in connection with
a price , the term C.I.F. destination requires the seller at his risk and ex-
pense to:
(a) put the goods in carrier's possession for shipment and obtain
negotiable bill of lading covering entire transportation to
destination ; and
(b) load the goods and obtain a receipt showing freight paid ; and
(c) obtain insurance , including war risk insurance , of a kind and
upon terms then current at port of shipment in the usual
amount , in the currency of the contract , shown to cover the
goods covered by the bill of lading , with payment to buyer's
order or to whom it may concern ; the seller may add the war
risk premium to the price ; and
(d) prepare invoice of goods and any other documents required
for shipment or to comply with the contract ; and
(e) forward and tender with commercial promptness all the
documents in due form and with any indorsement necessary to
perfect the buyer's rights .
(3 ) Unless otherwise agreed, the term C. & F. or equivalent has the
same effect and imposes the same obligations as C.I.F. except as to insur-
ance .
(4) Unless otherwise agreed, under both C.I.F. or C. & F. payment
must be made against tender of the documents and the seller cannot tender ,
nor the buyer demand delivery of the goods in lieu of documents .
60

None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Definitions . The Uniform Sales Act does not specify the effect to
be given these shipping terms . The Code's definition of C.I.F. states com-
mercial understanding that the price quoted by the seller under this term
includes the cost of the goods , the insurance and the freight . See 2 Willis-
ton , Sales ( 1948 ) Sec. 280c ; American Foreign Trade Definitions ( Chamber
of Commerce , 1941 ) V.
The Code also states commercial understanding in defining C. & F. as
meaning that the price quoted includes cost and freight to the named
desination . See 2 Williston, Sales (1948 ) Sec. 280c ; American Foreign Trade
Definitions ( Chamber of Commerce , 1941 ) IV.
(2 ) Seller's Obligations and Risks under C.I.F. Contract . The detailed
obligations listed in subsection (2) reflect commercial understanding . See
2 Williston , Sales ( 1948 ) Sec. 280c ; American Foregin Trade Definitions
(Chamber of Commerce , 1941 ) IV.
The Code , in placing the risk of loss in transit on the buyer , is also
consistent with Commercial understanding . See 2 Williston, Sales ( 1948 )
Sec. 280c ; American Foreign Trade Definitions ( Chamber of Commerce ,
1941) commercial understanding is sufficient to override the presump-
tion of Sec . 19 (Rule 5) of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.190 ( 5 ) , arising
from the seller's responsibility for freight . See 2 Williston , Sales ( 1948 ) Sec .
280d . The Code is also consistent with KRS 361.220 which makes provision as
to parties otherwise agreeing as to the seller's obligations and risk .
(3 ) Seller's Obligations and Risks under C. & F. Contract . Here again
the Code is in line with mercantile understanding , American Foreign Trade
Definitions ( Chamber of Commerce , 1941 ) IV , including the shifting of the
risk of loss in transit to the buyer in spite of the fact that the seller pays
the freight . See 2 Williston , Sales ( 1948 ) Sec. 280h .
(4) Payments Against , and Tender of , Documents Rather Than Goods .
In requiring the buyer to make payment against documents, without await-
ing the arrival of the goods , and the seller to tender documents , and not the
goods, the Code again adopts business usage . See 2 Williston, Sales ( 1948 )
Sec. 280c .
Section 2-321 . C.I.F. or C. & F.; “Net Landed Weights " ; "Payment
on Arrival " ; Warranty of Condition on Arrival .
Summary
Under a C.I.F. or C. & F. contract :
(1) Where price is based on or adjusted to "net landed weights " , "de-
livered weights ", "out turn" quantity or quality or the like , the seller must
reasonably estimate the price . The payment due on tender of documents is
61

the amount estimated, but after final adjustment settlement must be made
with commercial promptness .
(2) An agreement under subsection ( 1) or any warranty of quality or
condition on arrival places the risk of deterioration , shrinkage and the like
in transportation on the seller but has no effect on the place or time of iden-
tification to the contract or delivery or passing a risk of loss .
(3 Unless otherwise agreed, where payment is due on or after arrival
of the goods , preliminary inspection must be allowed before payment ; but if
the goods are lost , delivery of the documents and payment are due when the
goods should have arrived .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1 ) The Uniform Sales Act does not deal with the problem covered
by this section, and commercial practice on this point does not appear to
have been made uniform . American Foreign Trade Definitions (Chamber
of Commerce , 1941 ) does not deal with the problem .
(2) Under subsection ( 2) , an agreement whereby the price is based
on, or is to be adjusted according to, "net landed weights " , etc. , or a warranty
of quality " on arrival" , places the risks of " ordinary " deterioration , shrink-
age , etc. on the seller , while other risks , fall on the buyer . It is not perfectly
clear how some borderline risks , such as sweating , wetting , and shifting of
cargo would be allocated , or whether it would be practicable to determine
on arrival whether a defect in quality resulted from an "ordinary " or ex-
traordinary risk.
(3 ) Subsection ( 3 ) provides that while payment may be postponed
under a C.I.F. contract where the contract documents are to be presented
for payment on or after arrival of the goods , the arrival of the goods is not
a condition precedent to payment which in the case of lost goods , is due
when the goods should have arrived .
Section 2-322 . Delivery " Ex -Ship ” .
Summary
(1) Unless otherwise agreed, a term for delivery " ex-ship" ( meaning
from the carrying vessel) or equivalent is not restricted to a particular ship
and requires delivery from a ship which has reached a place at the named
port where goods of this kind are usually discharged .
(2) Unless otherwise agreed :
(a) the seller must discharge all carriage liens and furnish buyer
with a direction obligating the carrier to deliver the goods ;
and
62

(b) the risk of loss does not pass to buyer until the goods leave the
ship's tackle or are otherwise properly unloaded .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The Uniform Sales Act does not deal with delivery "ex-ship" . With
respect to commercial understanding , compare American Foreign Trade
Definitions (Chamber of Commerce , 1941 ) I (Ex Factory , etc. ) ; VI ( Ex
Dock ) . The provisions of the Code appear to be in accord with British
authority . See 2 Williston , Sales ( 1948 ) Sec. 280g .
Subsection ( 1) , in permitting the seller to deliver by some other ship
than the one named, is consistent with the Code's broader provision in UCC
2-614 on Substituted Performance . No Kentucky decisions were found .
Section 2-323 . Form of Bill of Lading Required in Overseas
Shipment ; " Overseas ” .
Summary
(1) Where an overseas shipment contract contains C.I.F. , or C. & F.
or F.O.B. vessel , unless otherwise agreed , the seller must obtain a negotiable
bill of lading showing the goods have been loaded on board or , in the case
of a term C.I.F. or C. & F. , received for shipment .
(2) Unless otherwise agreed , where under subsection ( 1) a bill of
lading has been issued in parts , if the documents are not to be sent from
abroad the buyer may demand tender of the full set ; otherwise only one
part need be tendered . Even if a full set is expressly required :
(a) tender of a single part is acceptable on cure of improper de-
livery; and
(b) even though a full set is demanded , if the documents are sent
from abroad , the person tendering an incomplete set may
nevertheless require payment upon furnishing an indemnity
which the buyer in good faith deems adequate .
(3) A shipment by water or by air or a contract contemplating such
shipment is " overseas " insofar as by usage of trade or agreement it is
subject to the commercial , financing or shipping practices of international
deep water commerce .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Type of Bill of Lading . The Uniform Sales Act does not deal with
this question . Commercial practice is not uniform . See American Foreign
63

Trade Definitions (Chamber of Commerce , 1941 ) II -A, II-B , II-C, and II-D
(under quotation F.O.B. inland carrier , seller must obtain "clean bill of
lading or other transportation receipt ") ; II-E ( under quotation F.O.B. ves-
sel , seller must provide "clean ship's receipt or on-board bill of lading " ) ;
IV and V ( under quotations C. & F. and C. I. F. , use of "received - for -ship-
ment " or " on-board " bill of lading depends on contract ) . See also Customs
and Practices for Commercial Documentary Credits ( International Cham-
ber of Commerce , 1938 ) Art. 19 ( a) ( in the case of Sea or Ocean Bills of
Lading , "Received for Shipment " or "Along-side" Bills of Lading are ac-
ceptable) .
(2) Bill of Lading Issued in Parts. Subsection ( 2 ) , in allowing tender
of an incomplete set of documents with indemnity bond against loss where
a bill of lading has been issued in a set of parts , codifies Dixon , Irmaos &
Cia . Ltda . v . Chase National Bank , 144 F. 2d 759 (2d Cir. 1944) , cert . denied ,
324 U.S. 850, 89 L. ed. 1410 , 65 S.C. 687 ( 1945 ) . Contrast : Customs and
Practices for Commercial Documentary Credits ( International Chamber of
Commerce , 1938) Art . 15 ( a) ( "Full set" of Sea or Ocean Bills of Lading
required ) .
Section 2-324 . “ No Arrival , No Sale ” Term .
Summary
Under a term " no arrival, no sale" or terms of like meaning unless
otherwise agreed :
(a) The seller must ship conforming goods and if they arrive he
must tender them but he assumes no obligation that they will
arrive unless he has caused the nonarrival ; and
(b) where without fault of the seller the goods are in part lost , or
deteriorated or arrive after the contract time , the buyer may
proceed as if there had been casualty to identified goods .
Kentucky Statutes
None.
Comment
The Uniform Sales Act did not deal with this term . When " no arrival,
no sale" or a similar term is used , the Code obligates the seller to ship the
goods and to tender them ; it gives the buyer the choice of accepting or re-
jecting the goods that arrive if there is a casualty in transit , but relieves
the seller of liability . These provisions seem in accord with the cases out-
side of Kentucky . See 1 Williston, Sales ( 1948 ) Secs . 188, 188a , 188b . In
giving the buyer the unlimited option to accept the goods that arrive , the
Code may be changing the present law . See 1 Williston, Sales ( 1948 ) Sec .
188b ( seller is not obligated to deliver the portion that arrives ) .
64

Section 2-325 . " Letter of Credit " Term ; " Confirmed Credit ."
Summary
(1) Failure of buyer to furnish an agreed letter of credit is a breach
of the sale contract .
(2) The delivery to seller of a proper letter of credit suspends the
buyer's obligation to pay . If the letter of credit is dishonored , the seller may
on seasonable notification to the buyer require payment directly from him .
(3) "Letter of credit " or "banker's credit " in a contract means an ir-
revocable credit issued by a financing agency of good repute , and , if an
overseas shipment is involved , of good international repute . "Confirmed
credit " means that the credit carries the direct obligation of such an agency
doing business in the seller's financial market .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Irrevocable Letter of Credit Required . Subsection (3) appears in accord
with the weight of authority outside Kentucky . See 2 Williston , Sales ( 1948 )
Sec. 469 (e) . But cf. Customs and Practices for Commercial Documentary
Credits (International Chamber of Commerce 1938) Art. 3. (This incon-
sistent rule favoring revocability may however be construed as relating to
relationship between customer and bank .)
The definition of "confirmed credit " in subsection (3 ) is also in line
with banking practice . See 2 Williston , Sales (1948 ) Sec. 469d .
Section 2-326 . Sale on Approval and Sale or Return , Consignment
Sales and Rights of Creditors .
Summary
(1) Unless otherwise agreed, if delivered goods may be returned by
the buyer even though they conform to the contract , the transaction is :
(a) a"sale on approval " if the goods are delivered primarily for
use, and
(b) a "sale or return " if the goods are delivered primarily for
resale .
(2) Except as provided in subsection ( 3) , goods held on approval are
not subject to the claims of the buyer's creditors until acceptance ; goods
held on sale or return are subject to such claims while in the buyer's pos-
session .
(3) Where goods are delivered to a person for sale and such person
maintains a place of business at which he deals in goods of the kind involved ,
under a name other than the name of the person making delivery , then with
65
