respect to claims of creditors of the person conducting the business the goods
are deemed to be on sale or return . The provisions of this subsection are ap-
plicable even though an agreement purports to reserve title in the person
making delivery until payment or resale or uses such words as " on consign-
ment" or " on memorandum . " However , this subsection is not applicable if
the person making delivery :
(a) complies with an applicable law providing for a consignor's
interest or the like to be evidenced by a sign , or
(b) establishes that the person conducting the business is gen-
erally known by his creditors to be substantially engaged in
selling the goods of others , or
(c) complies with the filing provisions of the article on Secured
Transactions (Article 9 ) .
(4) Any " or return " term of a contract for sale is to be treated as a
separate contract for sale within the statute of frauds section of this article
( UCC 2-20 ) and as contradicting the sale aspect of the contract within the
provisions of this article on parol or extrinsic evidence ( UCC 2-202 ) .
KRS 361.190 ( 3 ) .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Transactions Distinguished . The distinction between a " sale or
return " and a " sale on approval " depends on whether goods are delivered for
use or for resale, rather than on the title test of the Uniform Sales Act (Sec .
19 , Rules 3 ( 1) and ( 2) , KRS 361.190 ( 3 ) ( 1) and ( 2 ) . For Kentucky deci-
sions see : Stratton & Terstegge Co. v . Criswell, 290 Ky . 64, 160 S. W. 2d
137 ( 1942 ) ; Porter v. Price , Ky . 128 S. W. 701 , ( 1910 ) ; Jameson v . Gregory's
Exec . 61 Ky . ( 4 Metc .) 363 ( 1863 ) .
(2) Rights of Creditors . Subsection (2) makes explicit the rights
of creditors against a buyer who holds goods on approval or on sale or
return .
(3 ) Subsection (3 ) provides that where the buyer has a place of
business at which he deals in goods of the kind received , words such as
"on consignment " purporting to reserve title to the seller until payment
or resale will not prevent a transaction from being a sale or return , thus
subjecting the goods to levy by the buyer's creditors . Thus , doubts as to
the nature of the transaction are resolved in favor of the general creditors
of the buyer , and the possibility of using the form of a consignment (bail-
ment ) to conceal what is essentially a sale is reduced . Under the present
law , difficult questions of fact as to the nature of the transaction have
arisen . See 2 Williston , Sales ( 1948 ) Sec. 338 .
The Code , therefore , gives general creditors of such buyers protection
comparable to that given bona fide purchasers from agents , consignees and
factors entrusted with merchandise and the authority to sell . See Annotation
to UCC 2-403 ( 2) , infra .
66

An express exception to the above rule is made for the situation in
which the buyers are known to be engaged primarily in selling the goods of
others or the seller has perfected his security interest by filing under
Article 9. See UCC 9-102 , infra .
(4) On the parol evidence provision of subsection (4) , compare
Fannin v . Williams , 231 Ky . 392, 21 S. W. 2d 482 ( 1929 ) ( document not
complete on its face and not integrated , held , parol evidence is admissable .
Section 2-327 . Special Incidents of Sale on Approval and Sale or
Return .
Summary
(1 ) Under a sale on approval , unless otherwise agreed :
(a) although the goods are identified to the contract , risk of loss
and title do not pass until acceptance ; and
(b) use of goods for trial is not acceptance , but failure to season-
ably notify of election to return is acceptance , and if the
goods conform , acceptance of a part is acceptance of the
whole; and
(c) after notice of election to return , return is at seller's risk and
expense, but a merchant buyer must follow reasonable in-
struction .
(2) Under a sale or return , unless otherwise agreed :
(a) the option to return extends to the whole or any commercial
unit of the goods while in substantially their original condi-
tion, but must be exercised seasonably ;
(b) the return is at the buyer's risk and expense .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.190 (3 ) , 361.220 .
Comment
(1) Sale on Approval : (a) Appropriation ; Risk . Subsection ( 1) in
shifting risk and title to the buyer on acceptance or use or failure to give
reasonable notice , follows Secs . 19 Rule 3 (2) , and 22 of the Uniform Sales
Act , KRS 361.190 ( 3 ) ( 2 ) and KRS 361.220 .
(b) Return : Risk and Expense Seller's . The Uniform Sales Act
does not provide whether the risk and expense of return would fall on
buyer or seller . No Kentucky Cases found .
(2) "Sale or Return " : ( a) Option to Return . The Code's provision
concerning a "commercial unit " has not counterpart in the Uniform Sales
Act.
67

(b) "Seasonable" Return . Accord, Jameson v. Gregory's Exec .
61 Ky . (4 Met .) 363 ( 1863 ) ; Stratton & Terstegge Co. v . Criswell, 290 Ky . 64,
160 S. W. 2d 137 ( 1942) .
(c) Return : Risk and Expense Buyer's . The Uniform Sales Act has
no provision as to risk and expense of return . There is a close analogy ,
however , to recission , where the buyer must "return , or offer to return " the
goods . See Uniform Sales Act , Sec. 69 ( 1) (d) , KRS 361.690 ( 1) (d) . Com-
pare , as an analogy to the contrasting rule of subsection ( 1 ) ( c ) , supra ,
Sec. 50 of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.500 , under which if buyer
"refuses to accept" goods he "is not bound to return them to the seller ."
Section 2-328 . Sale by Auction .
Summary
(1) In a sale by auction if goods are put up in lots each lot is the
subject of a separate sale .
(2) A sale by auction is complete when the auctioneer so announces
by the fall of the hammer or other customary way . Where a bid is made
while the hammer is falling on a prior bid, the auctioneer may reopen the
bidding or declare the goods sold under the prior bid .
(3 ) Such a sale is with reserve unless the goods are in explicit terms
put up without reserve . In an auction with reserve , the auctioneer may
withdraw the goods at any time until he announces completion of the
sale. In an auction without reserve , after the auctioneer calls for bids
on an article or lot, that article or lot cannot be withdrawn unless no bid
is made within a reasonable time . In either case a bidder may retract his
bid until the auctioneer's announcement of completion of the sale, but a
bidder's retraction does not revive any previous bid .
(4) If the auctioneer knowingly receives a bid on the seller's behalf
or the seller makes or procures such a bid, and notice has not been given
that liberty for such bidding is reserved , the buyer may at his option avoid
the sale or take the goods at the price of the last good faith bid prior to
the completion of the sale. This subsection shall not apply to any bid
at a forced sale.
KRS 361.210 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Sale in Lots . Subsection (1) is similar to Sec. 21 ( 1) of the
Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.210 ( 1) . See 2 Williston , Sales ( 1948 ) Sec .
295 .
(2) When Sale Complete . The first sentence of subsection ( 2) fol-
lows Sec. 21 (2) of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.210 ( 2 ) . Compare
Becker v . Crabb , 223 Ky . 549, 4 S. W. 2d 370 ( 1928 ) ; Head v . Clark , 88 Ky .
362, 11 S. W. 203 ( 1889 ) (contract incomplete until hammer falls) . The pro
68

vision in the second sentence with respect to bids made while the hammer
is falling is new .
(3) When "With Reserve ." Subsection (3) follows Sec. 21 (2 ) of the
Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.210 ( 2 ) , in presuming that auction sales are
"with reserve" unless explicitly announced to be "without reserve ." It also
follows the Uniform Sales Act and KRS 361.210 ( 2 ) in allowing a bidder
to retract his bid before the hammer falls even in an auction without
reserve . See 2 Willison, Sales ( 1948 ) Sec. 297 .
(4) Bids by Seller . Subsection ( 4 ) follows Sec . 21 (4) of the Uniform
Sales Act, KRS 361.210 (4) , in permitting the buyer to avoid an auction
sale at which the seller or his agent bids , and at which no notice was given
of the right so to bid. Compare Burdon v . Seitz , 206 Ky . 336, 267 S. W. 219
(1924 ) ; Robenson v . Yann , 224 Ky . 56 , 5 S. W. 2d 271 (1924) . But see
Newman v . Woolley , 201 Ky . 139, 255 S. W. 1050 ( 1923 ) (by -bidder em-
ployed who doesn't in fact bid will not invalidate a sale.) In accord as to
forced sale see 50 C.J.S. , Judicial Sales , Sec. 14 .
The Code , however goes further than the present law by giving the
buyer , in addition , the option to take the last bona fide bid.
Part 4
TITLE , CREDITORS AND GOOD FAITH PURCHASERS
Introductory Comment
Although the Uniform Commercial Code abandons the "title " concept
in the law of sales, there are instances outside the law of sales where state
of title is material . This part presents the rules for determining title in
such cases .
Section 2-401 . Passing of Title : Reservation for Security ; Limited
Application of This Section .
Summary
Each provision of this article with regard to the rights , obligations
and remedies of the seller , buyer or other third parties applies irrespective
of title to the goods except where the provision refers to such title . Insofar
as situations are not covered by other provisions and matters concerning
title become material , the following rules apply :
(1) Title to goods cannot pass under a contract for sale prior to their
identification to the contract ( UCC 2-501 ) , and unless otherwise explicitly
agreed, the buyer acquires by their identification a special property as
limited by this act. Any retention or reservation by the seller of the title
69

(property ) in goods shipped or delivered to the buyer is limited in effect
to a reservation of a security interest . Subject to these provisions and to
the provisions of the article on Secured Transactions (Article 9) , title to
goods passes from the seller to the buyer in any manner and on any condi-
tions explicitly agreed on by the parties .
(2) Unless otherwise agreed, title passes to the buyer at the time
and place the seller completes his performance with reference to the
physical delivery of the goods despite any reservation of a security interest
and even though document of title is to be delivered at a different time
or place ; and in particular and despite any reservation of a security interest
by the bill of lading :
(a) if the seller is to send the goods to the buyer but does not
have to deliver them at destination , title passes at the time
and place of shipment ; but
(b) if delivery at destination is required title passes on tender
there .
(3) Unless otherwise agreed, where delivery is to be made without
moving the goods :
(a) if a document of title is to be delivered title passes at the time
and place of such delivery ; or
(b) if the goods are identified at the time of contracting and no
documents are to be delivered title passes at the time and
place of contracting .
(4) A refusal of the buyer to receive or retain the goods , or a justified
revocation of acceptance revests title in the seller . Such revesting occurs
by operation of law and is not a "sale ."
Kentucky Statutes
See generally KRS 361.170 , 361.180 , 361.190 and 361.200 .
Comment
The Code makes specific provision with respect to the various rights
and obligations of seller and buyer , such as risk of loss, action for the price ,
the effect of sale on rights of third persons . Thus , "title " has much less
effect in determining the rights of the parties than under the Uniform
Sales Act . Therefore , in dealing with any problem under the Code , it is
important to ascertain whether any specific provision deals with the prob-
lem; if so , it will override any implication to be drawn from the location
of the title .
( 1) Subsection ( 1) provides that title cannot pass prior to " identifi-
cation" of the goods to the contract . This is in substance the same rule
as is laid down in Sec. 17 of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.170 . As under
Sec. 6 of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.060 , there is a qualification in
UCC 2-105 ( 4 ) , supra , permitting title to pass in shares of fungible goods .
The further provision of subsection ( 1) that , subject to the foregoing
70

limitation , title may pass in any manner " explicitly agreed upon ," is con-
sistent with Sec. 18 of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.180 . But to make
more certain the location of title, the Code requires " explicit " agreement
to vary the effect of the rules stated in the act. Under the Uniform Sales
Act more flexibility based upon implied intent was necessarily allowed
because of the many incidents of locating title.
The further provision of subsection (1 ) that a reservation of title in
the seller after delivery or identification shall be only as security for
payment by buyer , is more detailed , but in general is consistent with the
Uniform Sales Act , Secs . 19 ( 1) , 20 ( 2 ) , KRS 361.190 ( 1) , 361.200 ( 2 ) .
The rule of subsection (2) in passing title when the seller " completes
his performance with respect to the physical delivery of the goods " is dif-
ferently phrased than the rules of Sec. 19 of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS
361.190 . The distinction drawn in subsections (2 ) ( a) and ( 2 ) (b) between
shipment and requirement of delivery is similar to the distinction between
Rules 4 ( 2) and 5 of Sec. 19 of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.190 (4) ( a )
and ( 5) . For possible changes , see annotations to UCC 2-509 , infra .
The rule of subsection 3 ( a) is new . Subsection 3 ( b) is similar to
Rule 1 of Sec. 19 of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.190 ( 1) .
Section 2-402. Rights of Seller's Creditors against Sold Goods .
Summary
(1) Except as provided in subsections ( 2) and ( 3 ) , rights of unse-
cured creditors of the seller with respect to goods which have been identi-
fied to a contract for sale are subject to the buyer's rights to recover the
goods under this article ( UCC 2-502 and 2-716 ) .
(2) A creditor of the seller may treat a sale or an identification of
goods as void if as against him possession by the seller is fraudulent under
any law of the state where the goods are situated , but possession in good
faith and current course of trade by a merchant - seller for a commercially
reasonable time after sale or identification is not fraudulent .
(3) Nothing in this article shall be deemed to impair the rights of
creditors of the seller :
(a) under the provisions of the article on Secured Transactions
(Article 9 ) ; or
(b) where identification to the contract or delivery is made not
in current course of trade but in satisfaction of or as security
for a pre -existing claim for money, security or the like and
is made under circumstances which under any rule of law
of the state where the goods are situated would apart from
this article constitute the transaction a fraudulent transfer
or avoidable preference .
71

Kentucky Statutes
Subsection ( 1) KRS 378.040 ; subsection (2) KRS 361.260 .
Comment
( 1) Subsection ( 1) subordinates the rights of the seller's creditors
to the buyer's possessory remedies . The Code seems to extend the buyer's
rights from those provided in KRS 378.040 whereby the buyer may only
recover if he is a purchaser without notice .
(2) Sold Goods in Seller's Possession . This section of the Code con-
tains the general rule of Sec. 26 of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.260 ,
whereby the local rules of law on transactions in fraud of creditors are
preserved . The exception in subsection ( 2 ) preserving the buyer's interest
in goods left with a merchant - seller for a " commercially reasonable time
in current course of trade" is not found in the Uniform Sales Act . This
provision would appear substantially to modify the Kentucky rule em-
bodied in KRS 378.040, that leaving goods in the seller's possession is deemed
fraudulent as against creditors . Kentucky courts have held , in an unbroken
line of decisions , that an absolute sale of personal property is in judgment
of law per se fraudulent as to purchasers from and creditors of the seller ,
unless the possession of the property accompanies the title. Vanmeter v .
Estill , 78 Ky . 456 ( 1880 ) ; Bourbon Bank v . Porter , 22 K. L. R. 429 , 57 S. W.
609 ( 1900 ) ; Frankfort Chair Co. v . Buchanan , 21 K. L. R. 269 , 51 S. W. 179 ;
(1899 ) ; Ky. Ref . Co. v . Globe Ref. Co., 104 Ky . 559 , 47 S. W. 602 ( 1898 ) ; but
this rule does not apply to the sale of growing crops , or of property not
in a condition to be removed without injury , Robbins v . Oldham , 62 Ky . ( 1
Duv .) 28 ( 1863 ) ; Kenton v . Ratcliffe , 105 Ky . 376 , 49 S. W. 14 ( 1898 ) ; nor to
property sold under execution , Greathouse v . Brown , 21 Ky . ( 5 T. B. Mon. )
280 ( 1827 ) ; or to property exempt from execution , Anthony v . Wade , 64 Ky .
(1 Bush ) 110 (1866 ) .
(3) Preferences . Subsection ( 3 ) is designed to give effect to the
rule of law of the state where the goods are situated governing preferential
identification or delivery not in the current course of trade .
Section 2-403 . Power to Transfer ; Good Faith Purchase of Goods ;
"Entrusting "” .
Summary
(1) A purchaser of goods acquires all title which his transferor had
or had power to transfer except that a purchaser of a limited interest
acquires rights only to the extent of the interest purchased . A person with
voidable title has power to transfer a good title to a good faith purchaser
for value . When goods have been delivered under a transaction of purchase
the purchaser has such power even though :
(a) the transferor was deceived as to the identity of the pur-
chaser; or
72

(b) the delivery was in exchange for a check which is later dis-
honored ; or
(c) it was agreed that the transaction was to be a "cash sale ;" or
(d) the delivery was procured through fraud punishable as
larcenous under the criminal law .
(2) Entrusting of goods to a dealer in that kind of goods gives him
power to transfer all rights of the entruster to a buyer in ordinary course
of business .
(3 ) "Entrusting " includes any delivery and acquiescence in retention
of possession regardless of any condition expressed between the parties
and regardless of whether the procurement of the entrusting or the pos-
sessor's disposition have been such as to be larcenous .
(4) The rights of other purchasers and lien creditors are governed
by the articles of Secured Transactions , Bulk Sales and Documents of Title.
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.200 (4) , 361.230 , 361.240 .
Comment
(1) Power to Transfer . Subsection ( 1) , in giving a purchaser all
title his transferor "has power to transfer ," appears consistent with general
agency principles , such as the doctrine of apparent authority . See Restate-
ment , Agency , Secs . 8, 12, and Kentucky Annotations .
The provision of subsection ( 1) giving good title to a good faith
purchaser for value who purchases from a person with a voidable title
restates with no apparent change Sec. 24 of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS
361.240 . See Dudley v . Lovins , 310 Ky. 491, 220 S. W. 2d 978 ( 1949 ) . Under
this provision , a bona fide purchaser from one who obtained delivery of
goods by a bad check would receive good title . Moreover , by virtue of
UCC 2-401 ( 1 ) (b) , supra, even if the bona fide purchaser's transferor
acquired the goods by a bad check under a "cash sale" agreement, the bona
fide purchaser would apparently still be protected against the "cash sale"
seller . Compare 2 Williston, Sales ( 1948 ) Sec. 346a .
(2) Entrusting to a Merchant Who Deals in Those Goods . The pro-
vision of subsection ( 2) is a departure from the present law . It protects one
who purchases in the ordinary course of business from a merchant , to whom
the goods have been entrusted , even though the merchant was given no
authority to deal with the goods by the entruster . No Kentucky case has
gone this far, and the provision is contra to general principles of agency .
See Restatement , Agency , Sec . 200 .
The protection of subsection ( 2) is limited to a "buyer in the ordinary
course of business ." This term as defined in UCC 1-201 ( 9 ) , supra , is more
restricted than a " good faith purchaser for value ," defined in UCC 1-201
(19 ) . The Code's definition excludes transfers in bulk or as security for
73

or in satisfaction of a debt , and also excludes pawnbrokers and persons
buying goods from farmers .
(3 ) Definition of "Entrusting ." Subsection ( 3 ) defines the term
"entrusting " as including any delivery or acquiescence in retention of pos-
session . Thus the protection given purchasers from a seller in possession
by Sec. 25 of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.250 , is continued . Hartford,
Conn ., v . Collinsworth , 288 Ky . 398, 156 S. W. 2d 157 (1941 ) . By limiting
this protection to buyers in ordinary course , however , this provision , unlike
present law , gives no protection to a mortgagee . The mortgagee must seek
protection from previous sales , if at all , as a creditor under Code , UCC 2-402 .
(4) Rights of Other Purchasers and Lien Creditors . Subsection (4 )
makes cross reference to the other articles in which the rights of purchasers
not covered by UCC 2-403 are dealt with .
Part 5
PERFORMANCE
Introductory Comment
Part 5 covers the duties of the parties in relation to performance . As
in the rest of the Code , the contractual intent of the parties controls .
Section 2-501 . Insurable Interest in Goods ; Manner of Identifica-
tion of Goods .
Summary
(1 ) The buyer obtains a special property and an insurable interest
in the goods upon identification to the contract even though they are
nonconforming and he may return or reject them . The identification may
be made at any time or in any manner explicitly agreed to by the parties .
Absent explicit agreement, identification occurs :
(a) when the contract is made if the goods are already existing
and identified ;
(b) if the sale is of future goods other than those in paragraph
(c) , when the goods are shipped , marked or otherwise desig-
nated by the seller ;
(c) when the young are conceived or the crops are planted or
otherwise become growing crops if the sale is of unborn
74

young to be born within a year or of crops to be harvested
within a year or the next normal harvest season whichever
is longer .
(2) The seller retains an insurable interest so long as title or any
security interest remains in him and where the seller alone makes identifi-
cation he may substitute other goods until default or insolvency or notifi-
cation that the identification is final .
(3) Nothing in this section impairs any insurable interest recognized
under any other statute or rule of law.
KRS 361.170 , 361.190.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Insurable Interest . This appears consistent with existing law .
Phoenix Ins . Co. v. Adams ' Trustee , 8 K. L. R. 532 ( 1886 ) ; Godfrey v . Alcorn ,
215 Ky . 465, 284 S. W. 1094 ( 1926 ) .
(2) Identification . The concept of "identification " is new , but in
substance is close to "appropriation " under Sec. 19, Rule 4 of the Uniform
Sales Act , KRS 361.190 (4) . But whereas "appropriation " related to "title "
with many consequences for both parties , "identification " under the Code
is principally significant for buyer's insurable interest and his right to
recover the goods from the seller under UCC 2-502 , 2-711 (2 ) and 2-716 .
Crops , Unborn Young , Etc. The Code through this section solves a
troublesome problem of identification which was not covered by the Uni-
form Sales Act .
Section 2-502 . Buyer's Right to Goods on Seller's Insolvency .
Summary
(1) Subject to subsection (2) and even though the goods have not
been shipped , a buyer who has paid part or all of the price on goods in
which he has a special property under the provisions of UCC 2-501 may
recover them upon making tender of balance due from the seller who
becomes insolvent within ten days after receipt of the first installment
of their price .
(2) If the identification creating his special property has been made
by the buyer , he may recover the goods only if they conform to the contract .
Kentucky Statutes
Compare KRS 361.170 , 361.180 and 361.190 .
75

Comment
The buyer's remedy here is new . Compare KRS 361.660 basing buyer's
right to obtain the goods on title having passed to him . Although no Ken-
tucky cases have been found supporting the Code's new provision , it does
find strong support in 1 Williston , Sales Secs . 143 and 144 wherein it is
stated :
"Since an insolvent is not precluded by the existing bank-
ruptcy act, or any other, from making a transfer of his assets , or
any part of them, for a return honestly bargained for as an equiv-
alent , there is no objection to the performance by him of a fair
contract wholly executory on both sides, if made in good faith ; ...
but if the insolvent , prior to the performance on his part , has
already received the whole or part of the consideration for his
own promised performance , so that a debt or obligation is due from
him , the situation is different . In that event, insolvency can never
properly be a make -weight for the decision of a court . Ifunder
the facts of the case , apart from the defendant's insolvency , equity
regards the plaintiff as having an interest in the property in ques-
tion , specific enforcement of the obligation to transfer to him that
interest should be granted , and if the defendant becomes bankrupt ,
the court of bankruptcy should recognize the plaintiff's interest in
the property ." ( emphasis added)
..
This section would modify to some extent the rule of Vanmeter v . Estill ,
78 Ky . 456 ( 1880 ) , allowing seller's creditors to attach goods retained by
seller after conveyance to buyer . Compare UCC 2-402 .
As to insolvency after the ten day period , see Article 9 (Secured
Transactions ) .
Section 2-503 . Manner of Seller's Tender of Delivery .
Summary
(1) Tender of delivery requires the seller to put and hold conforming
goods at the buyer's disposition and give the buyer notice to take delivery .
The manner , time and place for tender are determined by the agreement
and this article , and in particular :
(a) tender must be at a reasonable hour and goods must be kept
available for the time necessary for the buyer to take pos-
session ; but
(b) unless otherwise agreed , the buyer must furnish suitable
facilities for the receipt of the goods .
(2) Where the case is within UCC 2-504 tender requires the seller
to comply with that section .
(3 ) Where seller is required to deliver at destination , tender requires
his compliance with subsection ( 1) and tender of documents under sub-
sections ( 4) and ( 5) where necessary .
76

(4) Where bailee has possession of the goods and delivery is to be
made without moving the goods :
(a) tender requires the seller to either tender negotiable docu-
ment of title ; or procure bailee's acknowledgement of buyer's
right to the goods ; but
(b) tender of non - negotiable document or other written direction
to deliver is sufficient unless the buyer objects , and receipt
by bailee of notice of buyer's rights fixes those rights as
against the bailee and all third persons; but risk of loss and
of failure of bailee to honor the non -negotiable document of
title or to obey remains on the seller until buyer has had
sufficient time to present the document or direction , and a
refusal by bailee to honor the document or to obey the di-
rection defeats tender .
(5) If the seller is to deliver documents :
(a) he must tender all documents in correct form except as pro-
vided in UCC 2-323 ( 2) as to bills in set;
(b) tender through bank channels is sufficient and dishonor of
attached draft is nonacceptance or rejection .
Kentucky Statutes
See KRS 361.110 , 361.190 , 361.200 , 361.430 (3) and (4) , 361.460
and 361.510 .
Comment
(1 ) Seller's Obligation to Hold for Buyer's Disposition and Give Rea-
sonable Notice . The Uniform Sales Act did not specifically provide for
notice to the buyer on tender at a reasonable hour . Cf. Sec. 43 (4) of the
Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.430 ( 4 ) .
(2) Goods in Possession of Bailee . Subsection (4) in general follows
Sec. 43 ( 3 ) of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.430 ( 3 ) . The provision that
tender of a non-negotiable document of title is sufficient unless the buyer
seasonably objects is new . Accord , Sec. 49 of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS
361.490 (remedy for breach lost if no reasonable notice ) .
This section of the Code specifies a number of details which are new
but are presumed to express the usual intention of the parties .
Section 2-504 . Shipment by Seller .
Summary
Where the seller is required or authorized to ship the goods but does
not have to deliver them at a particular destination , unless otherwise agreed ,
he must :
(a) put the goods in the possession of a carrier and make a
reasonable contract for their transportation ; and
77

(b) obtain and deliver or tender the necessary documents ; and
(c) notify the buyer of the shipment .
Failure to notify the buyer or to make the proper contract is ground for
rejection only if material delay or loss occurs .
KRS 361.460 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
( 1) Contract for Transportation . In requiring that the goods be put
into the possession of the carrier the Code follows Sec. 46 ( 1) of the Uni-
form Sales Act supplemented by the definition of "delivery " in Sec . 76,
KRS 361.460 ( 1 ) , 361.760 .
(2) Effect of Failure of Notice or Failure to Make a Proper Contract .
The last sentence of UCC 2-504 is comparable to Sec. 46 (2) of the Uniform
Sales Act . There is a slight change in grouping "delay " with loss and
damage, and in specifically providing that the effect must be "material ."
Compare Sec. 19, Rule 4 (2) of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.190 (4) ( 2 ) ,
under which seller appropriates goods to the contract (and thereby shifts
the risk and makes buyer liable for the price ) only if the goods are
delivered "pursuant " to the contract .
Section 2-505 . Seller's Shipment Under Reservation .
Summary
(1) Where the seller has identified the goods by or before shipment :
(a) procurement of a negotiable bill to his order or otherwise
reserves in him a security interest only . Procurement of the
bill to the order of a financing agent or the buyer indicates
only the seller's expectation of transferring that interest to
the person named ;
(b) a non -negotiable bill to himself or nominee reserves posses-
sion of the goods as security , but except in the case of
conditional delivery a non -negotiable bill with the buyer
as consignee reserves no security interest even though the
seller retains possession of the bill .
(2) When shipment reserving a security interest violates the con-
tract , it is an improper contract for transportation under UCC 2-504 but
impairs neither the buyer's rights arising by shipment and identification
nor the seller's power as holder of a negotiable document .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.200 ( 2 ) , ( 3 ) , ( 4) .
Comment
(1) Bills of Lading ; Security Titles . On retaining security title through
78

order bill of lading see accord, Sec. 20 (3) of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS
361.200 ( 3 ) . On retaining security title through a straight bill of lading
by consigning to one other than the buyer see accord , Sec. 20 ( 2 ) of the
Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.200 ( 2 ) .
(2) Improper Shipments . This subsection is new; compare analogy
from Sec . 19 Rule 4 and Sec. 46 of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.190 ( 4 ) ,
361.460 , ( shipment must be "pursuant " to contract ) .
Section 2-506 . Rights of Financing Agency .
Summary
(1) A financing agency by paying or purchasing for value a draft
which relates to a shipment of goods acquires to the extent of the payment
or purchase and in addition to its own rights under the draft and any
document of title securing it any rights of the shipper in the goods including
the right to stop delivery and the shipper's right to have the draft honored
by the buyer .
(2) The right to reimbursement of a financing agency which has in
good faith honored or purchased the draft under commitment or authority
from the buyer is not impaired by subsequent discovery of defects in
relevant documents which were apparently regular on their faces .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
-
This section is new . Details of the relationship between seller or buyer
and financing banks are governed by Article 4 Bank Deposits and Col-
lections , and Article 5 Letters of Credit. The rights given the bank for
making payment against a draft which relates to a shipment appear to be
consistent with general principles of equitable assignment .
Section 2-507 . Effect of Seller's Tender ; Delivery on Condition .
Summary
(1 ) Tender of delivery is a condition to the buyer's duty to accept
and , absent agreement , to pay for the goods . Tender entitles the seller to
acceptance and payment according to the contract .
(2) Where payment is due and demanded on delivery to the buyer ,
his right as against the seller to retain or dispose of the goods is conditional
on his making the payment .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.110 , 361.410 , 361.420 , 361.690 .
79

Comment
The section states general principles consistent with present law. In
making tender a condition of buyer's reciprocal duty the Code is consistent
with Sec. 11 (2) of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.110 ( 2) . Making pay-
ment , if due, a concurrent condition of the duty to tender is consistent with
Sec. 42 of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.420 .
Section 2-508 . Cure by Seller of Improper Tender or Delivery ;
Replacement .
Summary
( 1) Where tender or delivery is rejected as nonconforming and time
for performance has not expired , the seller may notify the buyer of his
intention to cure and then make conforming delivery within the contract
time.
( 2) Where a nonconforming tender is rejected and the seller thought
it would be accepted with or without allowance , if the seller notifies the
buyer he may have a reasonable time to substitute a conforming tender .
Kentucky Statutes
None.
Comment
( 1 ) Subsection ( 1 ) allowing seller to replace a defective tender with
a conforming tender "within the contract time" is new as a statutory pro-
vision , but it may not change prevailing law . A defective tender coupled
with notice of intention to perform fully within the contract time probably
is not an anticipatory breach or repudiation . Restatement , Contracts , Sec .
319 and Kentucky Annotations thereto ( cases cited to the effect that an
anticipatory breach may be withdrawn before "accepted and acted upon " ) .
However , no Kentucky decisions have been found where the repudiation
was later nullified.
(2) Subsection ( 2) which covers replacement of a nonconforming
tender appears to be consistent with the cases cited in the Kentucky
Annotations to the Restatement , Contracts , Sec . 276 .
Section 2-509 . Risk of Loss in the Absence of Breach .
Summary
Where the contract requires or authorizes the seller to ship goods by
carrier :
(a) if it does not require him to deliver them at a particular
destination , the risk of loss passes to the buyer when the
goods are duly delivered to the carrier even though the ship-
ment is under reservation (UCC 2-505 ) ; but
80

(b) if it does not require him to deliver them at a particular
destination and the goods are there duly tendered while in
the possession of the carrier , the risk of loss passes to the
buyer when the goods are there duly so tendered as to
enable the buyer to take delivery .
(2) Where the goods are held by a bailee to be delivered without
being moved, the risk of loss passes to the buyer :
(a) on his receipt of a negotiable document of title covering the
goods; or
(b) on acknowledgement by the bailee of the buyer's right to
possession of the goods ; or
(c) after his receipt of a non -negotiable document or title after
written direction to deliver , as provided in subsection ( 4)
(b) of UCC 2-503 .
(3) In any case not within subsection ( 1) or ( 2) , the risk of loss
passes to the buyer on his receipt of the goods if the seller is a merchant ;
otherwise the risk passes to the buyer on tender of delivery .
(4) The provisions of this section are subject to contrary agreement
of the parties and to the provisions of this article on sale on approval
(UCC 2-327 ) and on effect of breach on risk of loss ( UCC 2-510 ) .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.190 (4) (b) and (5) , 361.220 .
Comment
The rule of subsection (a) in transferring risk to buyer on delivery to
carrier conforms with the result under Sec. 19 , Rules 4 ( 2) and ( 5 ) and
Sec . 22 of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.190 , 361.220, under which risk is
derived from " title ." See accord , Troy Refining Corp. v . Slagter Oil and
Grease Co. , 61 F. Supp . 369 ( W.D. Ky. 1945 ) .
The requirement that the goods be "duly " delivered to transfer risk car-
ries forward the requirement of Sec. 19 , Rule 4 (b) of the Uniform Sales
Act , KRS 361.190 ( 4) (b) , that the shipment be "in pursuance" of the con-
tract . See accord , UCC 2-510 of the Code .
A problem of construction and of comparison with existing law is pre-
sented by subsection ( 1) (b) of this section of the Code. Risk is held on
seller during shipment if the contract "requires him to deliver at a particular
destination ." Under UCC 2-319 ( 1 ) ( b ) risk is on seller if the quotation
is "F.O.B. the place of destination ." But it is not clear whether the same
result follows if the seller quotes a delivered price without use of the term
"F.O.B." If under a delivered price risk is placed on the buyer , the Code
thereby would change existing law under Sec. 19, Rule 5, and Sec. 46 of
the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.190 (5 ) , 361.460. (It would also appear
that the Code would thereby make results turn on an insubstantial differ-
ence in the form of stating the price .)
81

(2) Passage of Risk When Goods "Duly Tendered ." Subsection ( 1 )
(b) , in transferring risk to the buyer when goods are tendered at destination ,
solves the problem under Sec. 19, Rule 5 of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS
361.190 ( 5 ) , of whether "title " ( and therefore risk) passes when goods
have "reached the place agreed upon ." However , under this section of the
Code , although risk may pass , buyer may not be liable to pay the full price ,
and seller consequently may have the responsibility of disposing of the
goods . See UCC 2-709 .
Retention of Documents . Subsection ( 1) ( b ) in providing that risk does
not turn on the time of delivery of documents is in accord with Sec . 22 of
the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.220 .
(3) Goods Held by Bailee-Delivery Without Shipment . Subsection
(2) places the risk on the buyer in certain instances where goods are held
by a bailee to be delivered without being moved . Under KRS 361.190 ( 4)
(b) the seller is presumed to have unconditionally appropriated the goods to
the contract where the goods have been delivered to a bailee for transmis-
sion or holding for a buyer . See In re Shipley Stave & Lumber Co., 29 F.
Supp . 746 (E.D. Ky . 1939 ) .
Risk Other Than in Shipment or Bailee Cases . The rule of subsection
(3 ) placing risk on seller until receipt by buyer if seller is a merchant , or
otherwise until tender of delivery , delays transfer of risk beyond the time
prescribed under Sec. 19, Rule 1 of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.190 ( 1) .
Stimson's Executrix v . Tharp , 284 Ky . 389 , 144 S. W. 2d 1031 ( 1940 ) . Com-
pare also to Sec. 19, Rule 4 (a) Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.190 (4) (a)
(where title passes upon goods being unconditionally appropriated to con-
tract ) . Cf. In re Shipley Stave & Lumber Co., 29 F. Supp . 746 (E.D. Ky .
1939 ) .
Subsection ( 4) , making risk of loss subject to agreement of the parties ,
conforms to KRS 361.220 .
Section 2-510. Effect of Breach on Risk of Loss .
Summary
( 1 ) Where a tender or delivery so fails to conform to the contract as
to give a right of rejection , the risk of loss remains on the seller until cure or
acceptance.
(2) Where the buyer rightfully revokes acceptance he may to the
extent of any deficiency in his effective insurance coverage, treat the risk
of loss as having rested on the seller from the beginning .
(3) Where the buyer as to conforming goods identified to the contract
repudiates or otherwise is in breach before risk of loss has passed , the seller
may to the extent of any deficiency in his insurance coverage, treat the risk
of loss as resting on the buyer for a commercially reasonable time .
Kentucky Statutes
None.
82

Comment
(1) Loss on Non -Conforming Seller . Holding risk during delivery on
a seller who is not conforming to the contract to the extent that the breach
is such as to give a right of rejection carries forward the rule of Sec. 19 ,
Rule 4 (2) of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.190 (4) ( b ) , that the seller
appropriates by delivering goods "in pursuance " to the contract .
(2) Risk After Rightful Revocation of Acceptance . The general rule
of subsection (2 ) placing risk of loss on seller after the buyer revokes ac-
ceptance is in accord with Secs . 22 and 69 ( 1) (d) of the Uniform Sales Act
which gives the buyer power to rescind and thereby transfer risk to seller ,
KRS 361.220, 361.690 ( 1) ( d) . It is however , subject to an insurance pro-
vision discussed below .
(3) Insurance . Subsection (2) and ( 3) as to insurance are new . Sub-
section (3) limits the buyer's risk under that subsection to a "commercially
reasonable time ," thereby preventing the aggrieved seller from holding the
goods indefinitely at the buyer's risk. Compare KRS 361.220 (2) ( risk of
loss for delay in delivery ) , 361.640 ( 1) and ( 2 ) ( damages for breach by
buyer ) and 361.670 (2) and (3) (damages for seller's breach ) .
Section 2-511 . Tender of Payment by Buyer ; Payment by Check .
Summary
(1) Unless otherwise agreed, tender of payment is a condition to the
seller's duty to tender and complete delivery .
(2) Tender of payment is sufficient when made by any means or in
any manner current in the ordinary course of business unless the seller de-
mands legal tender and gives a reasonable extension of time to procure it .
(3) Subject to the provisions of this act on the effect of an instrument
on an obligation ( UCC 3-802 ) payment by check is conditional and is de-
feated as between the parties by dishonor .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.420 .
Comment
Subsection ( 1) , stating that tender of payment and tender of delivery
are presumed to be concurrent conditions follows KRS 361.420 .
Subsection (2 ) is new as a statutory provision , but probably does not
change existing law . See Restatement , Contracts , Sec. 305 and Kentucky
Annotations .
Subsection (3 ) , making payment by check conditional as between the
parties is likewise new , in that KRS Ch. 361 contains no express coverage of
the situation . But the provision seems to be in conformity with the view in
most jurisdictions . See 3 Williston, Contracts , Sec. 732 ; Vold, Sales, 174 .
83

Analogous rules , in Sec . 20 ( 4) of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.200 ( 4) ,
are consistent with this provision . ( Buyer "acquires no added right" by
wrongfully retaining bill of lading without honoring draft.) Apparently the
Code intends to allow seller to recover the goods from the buyer on dishonor
of a check given in payment . This is consistent with prevailing rules allow-
ing rescission for fraud . But it is to be noted that in making payment by
check conditional the Code does not affect the rights of third parties , such
as bona fide purchasers .
Section 2-512 . Payment by Buyer Before Inspection .
Summary
( 1) Where payment is required before inspection nonconformity does
not excuse the buyer from making payment unless :
(a) the nonconformity appears without inspection ; or
(b) despite tender of documents the circumstances would justify
injunction against honor under UCC 5-111 .
(2) Payment under subsection ( 1 ) does not constitute acceptance or
impair the buyer's right to inspect or any of his remedies .
KRS 361.470 , 361.490 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The provision of subsection ( 1 ) requiring payment of price by the
buyer before inspection where the contract requires it has no express coun-
terpart in KRS Ch . 361 ( Unifor mSales Act ) . But compare , KRS 361.470
(3 ) . The requirement seems to be in accord with general principles of con-
tract law.
Preservation of the buyer's rights and remedies in spite of payment
before inspection , in accordance with the contract appears to follow the
policy of KRS 361.490 .
Section 2-513 . Buyer's Right to Inspection of Goods .
Summary
(1) Unless otherwise agreed, and subject to subsection ( 3 ) , where
goods are tendered or delivered or identified to the contract for sale, the
buyer has a right before payment or acceptance to inspect them at any
reasonable place and time and in any reasonable manner . When the seller is
required or authorized to send the goods to the buyer , the inspection may
be after their arrival .
(2) The buyer must bear the inspection expenses , but if the goods are
nonconforming and are rejected he may recover from the seller .
84

(3 ) Unless otherwise agreed and subject to subsection (3 ) , of UCC
2-321 on C.I.F. contracts , the buyer is not entitled to inspect before payment
when the contract provides :
(a) for delivery "C.O.D. " or on like terms ; or
(b) for payment against documents , except when such payment is
due only after the goods are available for inspection .
(4) A place or manner of inspection fixed by the parties is presumed
exclusive but unless otherwise expressly agreed, it does not postpone identi-
fication or shift place of delivery or place for passing the risk of loss . If
compliance is impossible inspection shall be as provided in this section unless
the place or method fixed was clearly intended as an indispensible condi-
tion failure of which was to avoid the contract .
KRS 361.470 ( 2) (3 ) .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
( 1) General Right of Inspection . The general rule of subsection ( 1)
conferring the right of inspection on tender is comparable to Sec. 47 (2 ) of
the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.470 ( 2 ) . Cf. James Grocery Co. v . Birk
Candy Co. , 228 Ky . 11 , 14 S. W. 2d 214 ( 1928 ) .
(2) Expenses of Inspection . Subsection (2) is new as a statutory pro-
vision but seems in accord with the general view . See , 3 Williston , Sales,
Sec . 477 .
(3) Instances Where no Inspection Presumed , C.O.D. In accord with
subsection 3 (a) , Uniform Sales Act Sec. 47 (3) , KRS 361.470 (3) . See also,
3 Williston , Sales, Sec. 479a .
(4) Place or Method of Inspection . Subsection ( 4 ) is new , there being
no corresponding provision appearing in KRS Ch . 361 , the Uniform Sales
Act , treating place or method of inspection . There are no analogous Ken-
tucky cases on the point . Compare 3 Williston , Sales, Sec. 480 .
Section 2-514 . When Documents Deliverable on Acceptance ;
When on Payment .
Summary
Unless otherwise agreed documents against which a draft is drawn are
to be delivered to the drawee on acceptance of the draft if it is payable more
than three days after presentment ; otherwise only on payment .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
It has been decided that if the bill of exchange is payable at a certain
time, but not on demand, the bank or other person to whom the bill of lading
85

and bill of exchange have been sent, without express instructions , may
properly surrender the bill of lading on acceptance (without payment ) of
the bill of exchange . See 2 Williston , Sales , Sec. 290. National Bank of Com-
merce v . Merchants ' National Bank, 91 U.S. 92 ( 1875 ) ; Moore v. Louisiana
Nat. Bank , 44 La. 99 , 10 So. 407 (1892) . If the bill of exchange which goes
forward with the bill of lading is payable on demand , it is equally clear
that the bill of lading should not be surrendered until the bill of exchange
is paid , not merely accepted ; acceptance of a demand bill is not customary .
2 Williston , Sales , Sec. 290 ; Tampico Banking Co. , S.A. , v . Barber , 3 F. 2d
136 ( 5th Cir. 1925 ) ( involved in a sight draft) . Also it is well established
that if a bank , receiving for collection a draft with a bill of lading attached,
under instructions to deliver the latter only upon payment of the draft, sur-
renders the bill of lading without collecting the draft, it is liable for the loss
sustained by reason of such unauthorized action . Shippee v. Pallotti , An-
dretta & Co. , 117 Conn . 472 , 168 Atl . 880 ( 1933 ) .
Attention is directed to the fact that while the cases cited are limited to
bills of lading this section is broader , being designed to cover any document
against which a draft may be drawn whatever may be its form . See also
UCC 3-703 and 5-112 .
Section 2-515 . Preserving Evidence of Goods in Dispute .
Summary
In furtherance of the adjustment of any claim or dispute :
(a) either party on notice to the other and for the purpose of
ascertaining facts and preserving evidence may inspect , test
and sample the goods including those in the possession or
control of the other ; and
(b) the parties may agree to a third party inspection or survey
to determine the conformity or condition of the goods and
may agree that the findings shall be binding upon them in any
subsequent litigation or adjustment .
Kentucky Statutes
None .
Comment
This section is new to the sales law , no corresponding provision appear-
ing in the Uniform Sales Act , KRS Ch. 361. It represents another instance
of integrating the rules of evidence and practice and procedure into the Code
as is pointed up in the provision which entitles either party , upon notice , to
an inspection or testing of the goods in the possession of the other party to
the dispute , for the purpose of establishing evidence in the dispute .
Subsection (b ) conforms to the existing favorable attitude toward the
settlement of disputes by arbitrators . See KRS Ch. 417 .
86

Part 6
BREACH , REPUDIATION AND EXCUSE
Introductory Comment
This part of the Code defines the buyer's rights in the case of improper
delivery and his duties in regard to the exercise of those rights . The ques-
tions of what constitutes an acceptance of goods and the incidents that arise
therefrom are also covered in this part . The part provides for methods of
rightful rejection of the goods , rather than "recission" of the sale , and the
right after acceptance of the goods to "revoke acceptance ." It appears that
the distinction between rescission and rejection as to rights has been abol-
ished . Of very practical significance is the right in the buyer to accept any
commercial unit and reject the rest. The effect of repudiation of the con-
tract by either part , as well as their respective rights in case of casualty to
unique goods or failure of presupposed conditions , are provided in this part .
Section 2-601 . Buyer's Rights on Improper Delivery .
Summary
Subject to the provisions of UCC 2-612 on breach of installment con-
tracts and unless otherwise agreed , under UCC 2-718 and 2-719 on contractual
limitation of remedies , if the goods or tender of delivery fail to conform , the
buyer may :
(a) reject the whole ; or
(b) accept the whole ; or
(c) accept any commercial unit or units and reject the rest .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.110 , 361.440 , 361.690 ( 1) .
Comment
(a) Performance by Seller; Rejection of Whole . The general rule of
subsection ( a) allowing rejection is in accord with Sec. 69 ( 1) (c) of the
Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.690 ( 1) ( c) . The right of rejection is, however ,
limited in other Sections of the Code . See , e.g. , UCC 2-508 ( cure ) ; UCC
2-612 ( installment contracts ) . The Code abandons the distinction between
" rejection " and " recission " under Secs . 69 ( 1 ) ( c) and 69 ( 1 ) (d) of the
Uniform Sales Act . However , roughly comparable to "rescission " is the
power to revoke acceptance under Sec. 2-608 , infra .
(b) Acceptance . Accord, Secs . 49 , 69 ( 1) ( a ) and (b) of the Uniform
Sales Act , KRS 361.490 , 361.690 ( 1 ) ( a) and (b) .
(c) Partial Acceptance . The Uniform Sales Act did not specifically
provide for partial acceptance where seller tenders defective goods , but an
analogy favoring partial acceptance may be drawn from Sec. 44 (3) of the
87

Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.440 ( 3 ) . See : 3 Williston, Sales, Sec. 493b . B. R.
C. Bottle Co. v . Peaslee -Gaulbert Co. , 189 Ky . 28 , 224 S. W. 468 ( 1920 ) ( in
accord with Code as to acceptance of the part conforming and rejection of
remainder .)
The Sales Act uses and defines the phrase "divisible contract " for a
different purpose , and makes divisibility turn on whether the price can be
divided . KRS 361.070 (2 ) ( b ) , 361.080 ( 2 ) ( b ) , 361.760 ( 1) . This section of
the Code , by using the term " commercial unit ," permits partial acceptance
if the goods are commercailly divisible . UCC 2-105 (6) . Compare the parallel
provision on partial revocation of acceptance in UCC 2-608 , infra .
Section 2-602 . Manner and Effect of Rightful Rejection .
Summary
(1) Rejection of goods must be within a reasonable time after their
delivery or tender . It is ineffective without notice to the seller .
(2) Subject to UCC 2-603 and 2-604 on rejected goods :
(a) after rejection any exercise of ownership by the buyer is
wrongful as against the seller ; and
(b) if the buyer before rejection has taken possession of the goods
in which he does not have a security interest under subsection
(3 ) of UCC 2-711 , after rejection he shall hold them with rea-
sonable care at seller's disposition long enough for the seller
to remove them; but
(c) the buyer has no further obligations with regard to goods
rightfully rejected .
(3 ) With respect to goods wrongfully rejected , the seller's rights are
governed by UCC 2-703 on seller's remedies .
KRS 361.480 , 361.500 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
( 1 ) Rejection Within Reasonable Time ; Notice . Subsection ( 1 ) is in
accord with Sec. 48 of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.480 , under which the
buyer is deemed to have "accepted " the goods if he keeps the goods an un-
reasonable time without notice of rejection . Cf. Uniform Sales Act Sec .
69 ( 3 ) , KRS 361.690 (3 ) , cutting off a buyer's right to "rescission " under
such circumstances . See cases under UCC 2-606 and 2-607 , infra .
(2 ) Exercise of Ownership After Rejection ; Duty of Buyer to Hold
With Reasonable Care . The rule of subsection (2) (b ) may impose a broader
responsibility upon a buyer than that under Sec. 50 of the Uniform Sales
Act , KRS 361.500, which provides that "it is sufficient if (buyer ) notifies
seller that he refuses to 'accept' the goods ."
88

Section 2-603 . Merchant Buyer's Duties as to Rightfully Rejected
Goods .
Summary
(1 ) Subject to any security interest in the buyer , when the seller has
no agent or place of busines sat the market of rejection , a merchant buyer's
duty after rejection of goods in his possession or control , is to follow the
reasonable instructions of the seller and absent instructions to make rea-
sonable efforts to sell them for the seller if they are perishable or threaten
to decline in value . Instructions are not reasonable if on demand indemnity
for expenses is not forthcoming .
(2) When the buyer sells goods under subsection ( 1) he is entitled to
reimbursement from the seller or the proceeds of the sale for reasonable ex-
penses, and if no selling commission is included in the expenses , to the usual
commission or to a reasonable sum not to exceed ten percent of the gross
proceeds .
(3) The buyer is held only to good faith and good faith conduct is
neither acceptance nor conversion nor the basis of an action for damages .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Duty to Follow Instructions and Make Reasonable Efforts to Sell.
The Sales Act does not specifically impose any duty upon a buyer who
rightfully rejects to mitigate the seller's loss by selling . By Sec . 50 , KRS
361.500 , where B "refuses to accept " he is "not bound to return them"-and
"it is sufficient if he notifies the seller that he refused to accept them ."
Where "rescission " is involved , Sec. 69 (3) of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS
361.690 ( 3) , requires buyer to "return or offer to return " the goods .
Insofar as this subsection in stated circumstances places a duty on the
buyer to resell , it probably changes existing law . It is comparable , however ,
to the obligation imposed by the Perishable Agricultural Commodities Act,
7 U.S.C. , Sec. 499 (b) ( 3 ) , as to goods shipped in interstate commerce,
which makes it unlawful for any commission merchant to discard , dump or
destroy without reasonable cause .
Also the obligation is probably not much greater than that imposed
upon the buyer in case of rescission where the seller has refused to accept
the buyer's offer to return the goods . By KRS 361.690 ( 5 ) the buyer in such
cases is deemed to hold the goods as bailee for the seller . Automatic Equip-
ment Co. v . Mohney , 295 Ky . 451 , 174 S.W. 2d 716 ( 1943 ) .
(2) Buyer's Reimbursement for Expenses . This specific provision is
new . However , aside from the allowance of a selling commission of ten
percent , it appears to be in accord with general rules . See 29 A.L.R. 61 ,
(1924) . Compare KRS 361.690 ( 5) giving the rescinding buyer the rights
given an unpaid seller by KRS 361.530; Sears, Roebuck & Co. v . Lea , 198 F.
2d 1012 (6th Cir . 1952 ) . Subsection ( 3 ) immunizes the buyer who exercises
in good faith the power given by this section .
89

Section 2-604 . Buyer's Options as to Salvage of Rightfully Rejected
Goods .
Summary
Subject to UCC 2-603 on perishables , if the seller gives no instructions
within a reasonable time after notification the buyer may store the goods
for seller's account or reship them to him or resell for seller's account with
reimbursement . Such action is not acceptance or conversion .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The Sales Act contains no comparable provision , but see , accord 29
A.L.R. 61 ( 1924 ) . Compare KRS 361.690 (5 ) as to rescinding buyer . Under
some circumstances a change in present law may be effected since under
KRS 361.480 a buyer is deemed to have accepted goods by any act in rela-
tion to the goods which is inconsistent with the ownership of the seller .
The Code provision encourages salvage by buyers who have rightfully re-
jected without the danger of incurring liability for the price by acceptance .
Section 2-605 . Waiver of Buyer's Objections by Failure to
Particularize .
Summary
( 1) The buyer's failure to state a defect ascertainable by reasonable
inspection precludes his reliance on the unstated defect to justify rejection
or establish breach :
(2 )
(a) where the seller could have cured it if seasonably stated ; or
(b) between merchants when the seller made a written request for
a full and final written statement of defects on which the
buyer proposes to rely .
Payment against documents without reservation precludes re-
covery of the payment for defects apparent on the face of the documents .
Kentucky Statutes
None .
Comment
( 1) Failure to State Particular Defect in Connection with Rejection .
The Uniform Sales Act has no such provision , but a similar rule is recog-
nized as a matter of contract law . See Restatement , Contracts , Sec. 304 , and
Kentucky Annotations . 46 Am. Jur ., Sales, Sec . 210 ; 77 C.J.S. , Sales , Sec .
202. Compare KRS 361.490 under which notice must "fairly advise the
seller of the alleged defects " if a claim for breach is to be made after ac
90

ceptance . Sears, Roebuck & Co. v . Lea , 198 F. 2d 1012 (6th Cir . 1952 ) ; Fair-
banks v . Hooper , 147 Ky . 154 , 143 S. W. 1025 ( 1912 ) . The provision "between
merchants " in subsection ( 1) ( b ) for a written statement upon written re-
quest by the seller is new .
(2) Payment against Documents . This subsection seems to establish
waiver , as a matter of law , of defects apparent on the face of the documents .
Compare KRS 361.690 ( 3 ) , denying rescission after acceptance of goods with
knowledge of a defect . Under present law, waiver of defects in documents
is a question of fact.
Section 2-606 . What Constitutes Acceptance of Goods .
Summary
(1) Acceptance of goods occurs when the buyer :
(a) after a reasonable opportunity to inspect the goods signifies to
the seller that the goods are conforming or that he will take
or retain them in spite of their non - conformity ;
(b) fails to make an effective rejection , but such acceptance does
not occur until the buyer has had a reasonable opportunity
to inspect them; or
(c) does any act inconsistent with seller's ownership ; but if such
act is wrongful it is an acceptance only if the seller ratifies it.
(2) Acceptance of any part of a commercial unit is acceptance of that
entire unit.
KRS 361.480 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) General Rule . The Uniform Sales Act employs the general concept
of "acceptance ," as in Sec. 49 (buyer's right to an action for breach of war-
ranty in spite of " acceptance " ) and in Sec. 69 (3 ) (buyer's right to rescind
lost if he accepts goods knowing of the defect ) KRS 361.490, 361.690 ( 3 ) .
The term is defined in Sec. 48 of the Uniform Sales Act in language com-
parable to that of subsections ( a ) and (b) . The qualification in subsection
(b) delaying acceptance until after a reasonable opportunity to inspect car-
ries forward the rule of Sec . 47 ( 1 ) of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.470
(1) . See : Young v . Wallace , 201 Ky . 30 , 255 S. W. 856 ( 1923 ) ; Noel v . Kauff-
man Buggy Co. , 32 K.L.R. 576, 106 S. W. 237 ( 1907 ) ; Morgan -Abbott Barker
Co. v . Southwest Cracker Co. , 225 Ky. 418, 9 S. W. 2d 119 ( 1928 ) ; Brown v .
Ellis , 103 Ky . 303 , 45 S. W. 94 ( 1898 ) .
On the period of time allowed for inspection , see Add - Index Corp. v .
Griffith , 224 Ky . 739 , 7 S. W. 2d 207 ( 1928 ) ; South Bend Pulley Co. v . Cald-
well & Co. , 21 K.L.R. 1084 , 54 S. W. 12 ( 1899 ) ; Morgan -Abbott Barker Co.
91

v. Southwest Cracker Co. , 225 Ky . 418 , 9 S. W. 2d 119 ( 1928) . A collateral
question is whether buyer , although accepting , is barred from claim for
damage by delay in giving notice . See UCC 2-607 , infra.
(2) Acceptance of Part of Commercial Unit . The Uniform Sales Act
has no provision comparable to subsection (2 ) . But cases on partial re-
jection (See UCC 2-601 , supra ) refer to the test of "divisibility" defined in
Sec . 76 of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.760 . See Blue Grass Tract Co. v.
Hedges , 139 Ky . 358, 104 S. W. 370 ( 1907 ) .
Section 2-607 . Effect of Acceptance ; Notice of Breach ; Burden of
Establishing Breach After Acceptance ; Notice of
Claim or Litigation to Person Answerable Over .
Summary
(1) The buyer must pay at the contract rate for any goods accepted .
( 2 ) Acceptance of goods precludes their rejection and if made with
knowledge of nonconformity cannot be revoked unless the acceptance was
made on the reasonable assumption the nonconformity would be cured , but
acceptance does not impair any other remedy for nonconformity .
(3) Where a tender has been accepted :
(a) the buyer must within a reasonable time after he discovers or
should have discovered any breach notify the seller or be
barred from any remedy ; and
(b) if claim is for infringement or the like (subsection ( 3) of UCC
2-312 ) and buyer is sued as result of such breach he must
notify the seller of the litigation within a reasonable time
after he receives notice of the litigation or be barred from
any remedy over for liability established by the litigation .
(4) Burden is on buyer to establish any breach with respect to the
goods accepted .
(5) Where buyer receives notice that he is being sued for breach of
warranty or other obligation for which his seller is answerable over :
(a) he may give his seller written notice of the litigation . If no-
tice states that seller may come in and defend and that if
seller does not do so he will be bound in any action against
him by his buyer by any determination of fact common to the
two litigations , then , unless seller after seasonable receipt of
notice does come in and defend he is so bound .
(b) if the claim is for infringement or the like (subsection (3 ) of
UCC 2-312 ) the original seller may demand in writing that
his buyer turn over to him control of the litigation including
settlement or else be barred from any remedy over and if he
92

also agrees to bear all expense and to satisfy any adverse
judgment , then unless the buyer after seasonable receipt of
the demand does turn over control the buyer is so barred .
(6) The provisions of subsections (3 ) , (4) and ( 5) apply to any obli-
gation of a buyer to hold the seller harmless against infringement or the
like ( subsection (3 ) ( b) of UCC 2-312 ) .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.410 , 361.490 , 361.690.
Comment
(1) Payment at Contract Rate . Under Sec . 63 (1) of the Uniform
Sales Act, liability to pay the price principally turns on passage of "title ";
"acceptance" as a primary test is new . See UCC 2-709 , infra . Changing the
test probably narrows seller's right to recover the contract price , since under
present law title can pass on identification or shipment and prior to "ac-
ceptance ."
(2) Effect as to Rejection . The Uniform Sales Act uses different con-
cepts than the Code , but without substantially different effect on this point .
Insofar as this section precludes revocation of acceptance if the buyer has
accepted witht knowledge of an existing nonconformity , it is in accord with
KRS 361.690 (3 ) , which provides that rescission will not be allowed if the
buyer "knew of the breach of warranty when he accepted the goods " . Under
present law the power of "rejection " ends on passage of "title , " rather than
"acceptance." But under both present law and the Code failure of the
buyer to reject within a reasonable time constitutes an "acceptance ." See
UCC 2-606 ( 1) (b) , supra . The provision as to " cure" of the nonconformity
is new . Compare UCC 2-508 , 2-608 .
(3) Remedy Lost by Failure to Give Notice . Subsection ( 3 ) carries
forward the requirement on notice of breach under Sec. 49 of the Uniform
Sales Act , KRS 361.490 ; Sears , Roebuck & Co. v . Lea, 198 F. 2d 1012; see
annotation to UCC 2-605 , supra . See Restatement , Contracts , Sec. 412 and
Kentucky Annotations . Subsection ( 3) (b) is new , but seems in accord with
general contract principles . See 5 Corbin, Contracts , Sec. 1037.
(4) Burden of Proof. Subsection ( 2) is new as a statutory provision
but is the generally accepted view . See 3 Corbin , Contracts , Sec. 709 .
(5) Nothing comparable to subsection ( 5) is found in the Uniform
Sales Act . Subsection ( 5 ) ( a ) codifies for all warranties the practice of
voucher to defend . ( 5) ( b ) gives a warrantor against infringement an
opportunity to defend or compromise third-party claims or be relieved of
his liability .
(6 ) Subsection (6) makes these provisions applicable to the buyer's
liability for infringement under UCC 2-312 .
93

Section 2-608 . Revocation of Acceptance in Whole or in Part .
Summary
(1) The buyer may revoke acceptance of a lot or commercial unit
whose nonconformity substantially impairs its value to him if he has ac-
cepted it:
(a) on the assumption its nonconformity was to be cured and it
was not cured ; or
(b) without discovery of the nonconformity if his acceptance was
induced either by the difficulty of discovery before acceptance
or by the seller's assurances .
(2) Revocation of acceptance must occur within a reasonable time and
before any substantial change in the goods which is not caused by their
own defects . It is not effective until the buyer notifies the seller of it.
(3 ) A buyer who so revokes has the same rights and duties as if he
had rejected the goods .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.690 ( 1 ) ( d) , ( 3 ) , (4 ) , and ( 5) .
Comment
The buyer's power under the Code to "revoke acceptance " performs the
same general functions as rescission under Sec. 69 ( 1) ( d ) of the Uniform
Sales Act , KRS 361.690 ( 1 ) ( d ) . The right is put in different language and
some changes made .
( 1) " Substantial Impairment of Value " Necessary . Under Sec . 69 ( 1)
(d) of the Uniform Sales Act buyer's power to rescind arises " where there is
a breach of warranty by the seller ." No qualification as to "substantial im-
pairment " was set forth . But in particular cases , rescission has been denied
for trivial breach . Louisville & Nashville Railroad Co. v . Fuson , 203 Ky . 708,
262 S. W. 1086 ( 1924 ) . (dictum) . See 5 Corbin , Contracts , Sec. 1176 .
Under Sec . 69 (3) of the Uniform Sales Act, buyer has a power to rescind
unless he knew of the breach when he accepted , KRS 361.690 (3 ) . Subsec-
tion ( 1) of the Code slightly restricts buyer's right to send the goods back
to the seller since nondiscovery must have resulted from "difficulty ."
(2) Revocation within Reasonable Time ; Notice . Accord : KRS 361.690
(3) , Paducah Hosiery Mills v . Proctor & Schwartz , 210 Ky . 806, 276 S. W.
803 ( 1925 ) . See 3 Williston , Sales , Sec. 611. See also , cases cited under UCC
2-606 and 2-607 . The provision on change of condition of the goods also
parallels Sec . 69 (3) of the present act, KRS 361.690 ( 3 ) .
(3) Buyer's Rights and Duties on Revocation . KRS 361.490 ( 3 ) im-
poses a duty on the buyer in cases of rescission to return or offer to return
the goods . By KRS 361.500 the buyer who rejects is not under any duty to
return or offer to return the goods . This is changed by the Code which im-
poses the same duties on the buyer in both types of cases . See UCC 2-602
and 2-603 . Another important change results from allowing buyer to both
"rescind " and recover damages . See UCC 2-711 , infra .
94

Section 2-609 . Right to Adequate Assurance of Performance .
Summary
(1) A contract for sale imposes on each party the obligation that the
other's expectation of performance will not be impaired . When reasonable
grounds for insecurity arise with respect to either party's performance , the
other party may demand , in writing, adequate assurance of performance and
until received , if commercially reasonable , may suspend any performance
for which he has not received the agreed return .
(2 ) Between merchants , the reasonableness of grounds and adequacy
of any assurance is determined by commercial standards .
(3 ) Acceptance of improper delivery or payment does not prejudice
the aggrieved party's right to assurance of future performance .
(4) Failure to provide adequate assurance within a reasonable time not
exceeding 30 days after a justified demand is a repudiation of the contract .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.530 , 361.540 (1) (b) , 361.550 , and 361.630 (3 ) .
Comment
This section considerably expands the right of the aggrieved party to
suspend performance when the other party manifests inability to perform or
intention not to perform . See Restatement , Contracts , Secs . 280 , 287, 306
and Kentucky Annotations . The provisions for demand of " adequate assur-
ance " whenever " reasonable grounds for insecurity arise " is new .
Section 2-610. Anticipatory Repudiation .
Summary
When either party repudiates the contract with respect to a perform-
ance not yet due the loss of which will substantially impair the value of the
contract to the other , the aggrieved party may :
(a) for a commercially reasonable time await performance by the
repudiating party ; or
(b) resort to any remedy for breach ( UCC 2-703 or UCC 2-711 ) ,
even though he has notified the repudiating party that he
would await the latter's performance and has urged retrac-
tion; and
(c) in either case suspend his own performance or proceed in
accordance with the provisions of this article on the seller's
right to identify goods to the contract notwithstanding breach
or to salvage unfinished goods ( UCC 2-704 ) .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.630 (2) , 361.650 .
95

Comment
Giving the injured party the choice of suing or awaiting performance
is consistent with present law . Fidelity & Dep . Co. v . Brown , 230 Ky . 534 ,
20 S. W. 2d 284 ( 1929 ) ; Royster v . Waller , 186 Ky . 476 , 217 S. W. 684 ( 1920 ) .
Restatement , Contracts , Secs . 306, 318, and Kentucky Annotations . On sel-
ler's power to suspend performance see Restatement, Contracts , Sec. 280 and
Kentucky Annotations . There is no comparable provision to this in the
Uniform Sales Act .
Section 2-611 . Retraction of Anticipatory Repudiation .
Summary
(1) Until the repudiating party's next performance is due he can re-
tract his repudiation unless the aggrieved party has cancelled or materially
changed his position or otherwise indicated that he considers the repudiation
final .
(2 ) Retraction may be by any method which clearly indicates that
the party intends to perform , but must include any assurance required under
UCC 2-609 .
(3 ) Retraction reinstates the repudiating party's rights under the con-
tract with due excuse and allowance to the aggrieved party for any delay
occasioned by the repudiation .
Kentucky Statutes
None .
Comment
In giving the repudiating party the right to withdraw that repudiation
under stated circumstances , this section achieves substantially the same re-
sult with respect to the duties of the other party as would be reached under
present Kentucky case law . Paducah Cooperage Co. v . Arkansas Stave Co. ,
193 Ky . 774 , 237 S. W. 412 ( 1922 ) . See generally , 4 Corbin , Contracts , Sec .
980. See also Restatement, Contracts , Secs . 323, 398, and Kentucky Anno-
tations .
Section 2-612 . “ Installment Contract " ; Breach .
Summary
(1) An " installment contract" is one which authorizes or requires the
delivery of goods in separate lots to be separately accepted , even though the
contract contains a clause "each delivery is a separate contract " or its
equivalent .
(2) The buyer may reject any nonconforming installment which sub-
stantially impairs the value of the installment and cannot be cured or if it
is a defect in the required documents, but if the defect does not fall within
96

subsection ( 3) and the seller gives adequate assurance of its cure , the buyer
must accept .
(3) Whenever nonconformity with respect to one or more installments
substantially impairs the value of the whole contract there is a breach of
the whole . But the aggrieved party reinstates the contract if he accepts a
nonconforming installment without seasonable notice of cancellation or if
he brings an action on past installments only or demands performance as to
future installments .
KRS 361.450 ( 2) .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
( 1) Definition . The definition in subsection ( 1) eliminates the require-
ment of separate payment and thus differs from that of Sec. 45 (2) of the
Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.450 ( 2 ) , which applies to contracts to sell goods
"to be delivered by stated installments , which are to be separately paid for."
(2) Power to Reject Nonconforming Installments . This section of
the Code probably changes present law in limiting the right to reject
defective goods unless the nonconformity "substantially impairs the value "
of the installment . Contrast Sec . 69 ( 1 ) ( c ) and (d) of the Uniform Sales
Act, KRS 361.690 ( 1) ( c ) and (d) (right to reject for any breach of
warranty) .
The rule of this section of the Code is different from that of UCC
2-601 , supra , under which if an installment contract is not involved , a buyer
may reject if the goods or tender "fail in any respect to conform to the
contract ." This distinction makes important the definition of "installment
contract " , which is not entirely clear , particularly in the qualification
that the goods be "separately paid for ."
(3) When Breach in Installment Breaks Entire Contract . Accord
though differently stated : KRS 361.450 ( 2 ) ; O'Bryan v . Mengel Company ,
224 Ky . 284, 6 S. W. 2d 249 ( 1928 ) ; Evans v : Barbourville Brick Co. , 205
Ky . 561 , 266 S. W. 46 ( 1924 ) .
Section 2-613 . Casualty to Identified Goods .
Summary
Where the contract requires for its performance goods identified when
the contract is made, and the goods suffer casualty without fault of either
party before the risk of loss passes to the buyer , or in a proper case under
a " no arrival, no sale" term then :
(a) if the loss is total the contract is avoided ; and
(b) if the loss is partial or the goods have so deteriorated as no
longer to conform the buyer may demand inspection and at
his option either treat the contract as avoided or accept the
97

goods with allowance for deterioration or deficiency in
quantity but without further right against the seller .
KRS 361.070 , 361.080 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section deals with the problems treated in Secs . 7 and 8 of the
Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.070 , 361.080 . The Code enlarges the buyer's
rights on destruction or deterioration of specific goods .
Under the Uniform Sales Act , the seller was relieved of responsibility
if the goods destroyed were "specific ." Under the Revised Sales Act,
seller's excuse is narrowed to cases of identified goods which are "irre-
placeable " or treated as " unique ." Another modification is that under the
Uniform Sales Act the buyer was given the choice of receiving goods
remaining after partial loss only on payment of the contract price for all
or for a divisible part of the goods . Under the Code , the buyer may accept
the goods and receive due allowance from the contract price for the de-
terioration or deficiency .
There has been little litigation involving this problem . Compare the
Restatement , Contracts , Secs. 456, 457 , 460 and Kentucky Annotations .
Section 2-614 . Substituted Performance .
Summary
(1) Where without fault of either party the agreed berthing , loading
or unloading facilities fail or an agreed type of carrier becomes unavail-
able or the agreed manner of delivery becomes commercially impracticable ,
but a commercially reasonable substitute is available , such substituted per-
formance must be tendered and accepted .
(2) If the agreed means or manner of payment fails because of
governmental regulations , the seller may withhold or stop delivery unless
the buyer provides a commercially substantial equivalent means or manner .
If delivery has already been taken , payment by the regulated means or
manner discharges the buyer unless the regulation is discriminatory , op-
pressive or predatory .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The Sales Act does not specifically deal with the problem covered by
this section. The Code has provided relief in instances where loading or
unloading facilities or carrier facilities become unavailable or impracticable ,
and also where governmental regulations prevent an agreed manner of
payment . Sec. 69 of the Uniform Sales Act tends towards requiring that
there be strict compliance with the contract , with a possible exception
98

under Sec. 12 if the buyer did not "rely" on the provision , KRS 361.120 ,
361.690 . However , results substantially similar to that required under this
section would be reached under common law principles . See Restatement ,
Contracts , Secs . 462 , 463 , and Kentucky Annotations .
Section 2-615. Excuse by Failure of Presupposed Conditions .
Comment
Except so far as the seller may have assumed a greater obligation and
subject to UCC 2-614 on substituted performance :
(a) delay in delivery or nondelivery by a seller who complies
with paragraphs (b ) and (c) is not a breach if performance
as agreed has been made impracticable by the occurrence of
a contingency the nonoccurrence of which was a basic as-
sumption on which the contract was made , or by good faith
compliance with any applicable governmental regulation or
order whether or not it later proves invalid ;
(b) where the causes mentioned in paragraph ( a) affect only a
part of the seller's capacity to perform , he must allocate pro-
duction and deliveries among his customers but may at his
option include regular customers not then under contract
