as well as his own requirements for further manufacture . He
may so allocate in any manner which is fair and reasonable ;
(c) the seller must notify the buyer seasonably that there will
be delay or nondelivery and, where allocation is required , of
the estimated quota available for the buyer .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The Uniform Sales Act has no comparable provision . The rules of
this section of the Code deal with the general contract rules governing
impossibility and frustration which are incorporated into the Uniform Sales
Act by KRS 361.730 .
(a) Failure of Basic Assumption . Here the Code expressed in dif-
ferent language a test close to present rules of "impossibility " and " im-
practicability " in contract law . See Restatement , Contracts Secs . 454-467
and Kentucky Annotations . Excuse based on "foreign " governmental regu-
lations expands the rule of the Restatement , Contracts , Sec. 548 .
(b) Failure of Part; Allocation . Subsections (b ) and (c) are new .
Section 2-616 . Procedure on Notice Claiming Excuse .
Summary
(1) Where the buyer is notified of a material or indefinite delay or
an allocation under UCC 2-615 , upon written notification to the seller as
99

to any delivery concerned , and where the prospective deficiency sub-
stantially impairs the value of the whole contract under UCC 2-612 relating
to breach of installment contracts , then also as to the whole , he may :
(a) terminate and thereby discharge any unexecuted portion of
the contract ; or
(b) modify his contract by agreeing to take his quota in sub-
stitution .
(2) If, after notice , the buyer fails to modify the contract within a
reasonable time not to exceed 30 days , the contract lapses with respect to
any deliveries affected .
(3 ) The provisions of this section may not be negated by agreement
except as the seller has assumed a greater obligation under UCC 2-615 .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The procedure established by this section implements the rules estab-
lished under UCC 2-614 and 2-615 . No comparable provisions are in the
Uniform Sales Act . As to the buyer's option to cancel when the seller's
failure of performance is excused , see , accord , Restatement , Contracts ,
Secs . 274-276 , and Kentucky Annotations .
Part 7
REMEDIES
Introductory Comment
Part 7 uses the commercial approach rather than the pure legalistic
approach to the subject of remedies . The parties are interested in the
transaction as a business venture in which the seller hopes to dispose
of goods and the buyer hopes to receive those goods . All good faith efforts
to conclude the sale are protected and the parties do not lose their remedies
by such acts .
This part also provides a method for determining market price and
allows the use of published market quotations as evidence of market price .
A new statute of limitations is provided which is intended to give a uniform
statute for sales in all the states .
Section 2-701 . Remedies for Breach of Collateral Contracts Not
Impaired .
Summary
Remedies for breach of any obligation or promise collateral or ancil-
lary to a contract are not impaired by the provisions of this article .
100

None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This is a scope -defining section . The section appears to limit the scope
of the Sales Article to parts of a contract related to the sale of goods , and
requires no annotation .
Section 2-702 . Seller's Remedies on Discovery of Buyer's
Insolvency .
Summary
(1) Where the seller discovers the buyer to be insolvent , he may
refuse delivery except for cash including payment for all goods theretofore
delivered under the contract , and stop delivery under this article .
(2) When the seller discovers that the buyer has received goods
on credit while insolvent , he may reclaim the goods upon demand made
within ten days after the receipt , but if misrepresentation of solvency has
been made to the particular seller in writing within three months before
delivery , the ten-day limit does not apply . Except as provided in this sub-
section, the seller may not base a right to reclaim goods on the buyer's
fraudulent or innocent misrepresentation of solvency or of intent to pay .
(3) The seller's right to reclaim under subsection ( 2) is subject to
the rights of a buyer in ordinary course or other good faith purchaser or
lien creditor under this article ( UCC 2-403 ) . Successful reclamation of
goods excludes all other remedies with respect to them .
Kentucky Statutes
Subsection ( 1) KRS 361.530 ( 1 ) ( b) , 361.540 ( 1) (c) and 361.570 ;
subsection ( 2)-none ; subsection ( 3 ) KRS 361.760 ( 3 ) .
Comment
(1) Seller's Remedies . ( a) Refusal of Delivery . The right on in-
solvency to refuse delivery except for cash , even though credit was called
for , follows Sec. 54 ( 1) ( c) of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.540 ( 1 ) ( c ) .
On stoppage of delivery see UCC 2-705 , infra. The right to assert a lien
on undelivered goods for payment of goods already delivered is new . Cf.
Sec. 55 of the Uniform Sales Act, KRS 361.550 ( presumption against sur-
render of lien on partial delivery ) .
(b) Right of Reclamation . The Uniform Sales Act makes no pro-
vision as to seller's right to reclaim goods . A seller , however , has been
allowed to reclaim goods where the buyer has obtained the goods by
fraudulent representations . See Dietz's assignee v . Sutcliffe , 80 Ky . 650
(1883) . That doctrine has been applied where the buyer obtained goods
with the intention of not paying for them . Kirkpatrick's Exec . v . Rehkoph
Saddlery Co., 144 Ky . 129 , 137 S. W. 862 ( 1911 ) ; Am . German Nat. Bank v .
101

Gray & Dudley Hardware Co. , 129 Ky . 105 , 110 S. W. 393 ( 1908 ) ; Nashville
Grain & Feed Company v . American Cooperative Association , 203 Ky . 458,
262 S. W. 634 ( 1924 ) ; Restatement , Contracts , Sec. 473, and Kentucky Anno-
tations .
Automatic reclamation on insolvency within ten days is new , as is
limitation to reclamation within ten days , or written representation of
solvency made within three months before delivery . A demand within ten
days after receipt may be sufficient even though repossession cannot be
accomplished until later , and subsection ( 2 ) applies to innocent as well
as fraudulent misrepresentation of solvency or intent to pay , and it ex-
cludes any other reclamation for such misrepresentation but not for mis-
representation of other facts . On limiting the time for reclamation , see
Restatement , Contracts , Secs . 480 , 483 , and Kentucky Annotations . Reclam-
ation is made an exclusive remedy because it prefers the seller to other
creditors of the buyer . Such a preference for actual fraud is not inconsistent
with bankruptcy legislation , but there may be difficulty in cases of "pre-
sumed " fraud . See Collier, Bankruptcy Manual , Sec. 70.26 notes , 9, 10
(1948) .
Section 2-703 . Seller's Remedies in General .
Summary
Where the buyer wrongfully rejects or revokes acceptance or fails
to make payment due on or before delivery or repudiates with respect
to a part or the whole then with respect to any goods directly affected
and, if the breach is of the whole contract , then also with respect to the
whole undelivered balance , the aggrieved seller may :
(a) withhold delivery of such goods ;
(b) stop delivery by any bailee as provided in UCC 2-705 ;
(c) proceed under UCC 2-704 respecting goods still unidentified ;
(d)
(e)
resell and recover damages as provided in UCC 2-706 ;
as to goods not resold recover damages for non - acceptance
(UCC 2-708 ) or their price (UCC 2-709 ) ;
(f) cancel .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section sums up the remedies given the seller under other sec-
tions of the article , and requires no separate annotation .
Section 2-704 . Seller's Right to Identify Goods to the Contract Not-
withstanding Breach or to Salvage Unfinished Goods .
(1) An aggrieved seller under the preceding section may :
(a) identify to the contract conforming goods not already identi-
fied , if at the time he learned of the breach they are in his
possession or control ;
102

(b) treat as the subject of resale, goods which have demonstrably
been intended for the particular contract even though those
goods are unfinished .
(2) Where the goods are unfinished an aggrieved seller may in the
exercise of reasonable commercial judgment for the purposes of avoiding
loss and of effective realization either complete the manufacture and
wholly identify the goods to the contract or cease manufacture and resell
for scrap or salvage value or proceed in any other reasonable manner .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.630 (3) and 361.640 (4) .
Comment
This section is introductory to later provisions giving seller the right
to fix buyer's damages by resale (UCC 2-706 ) and, in special circumstances ,
to sue for the price ( UCC 2-709 ) . " Identification " also affects existence of
an insurable interest ( UCC 2-501 ) and is a necessary element in shifting
risk of loss to a party guilty of breach ( UCC 2-510 ) . Explicit provision is
made allowing the seller to resell unfinished goods for scrap or salvage
value . See implementing sections .
Section 2-705 . Seller's Stoppage of Delivery in Transit or
Otherwise .
Summary
(1) The seller may stop delivery of goods in the possession of a carrier
or other bailee when he discovers the buyer to be insolvent (UCC 2-702 )
and may stop delivery of carload , truckload , planeload or larger shipment
when the buyer repudiates or fails to make a payment due before delivery
or if for any other reason , the seller has a right to withhold or reclaim the
goods .
(2) As against such a buyer the seller may stop delivery until :
(a) receipt of the goods by the buyer ; or
(b) acknowledgment to the buyer by any bailee except a carrier
that the bailee holds the goods for the buyer ; or
(c) such acknowledgment to the buyer by a carrier by reshipment
or as a warehouseman ; or
(d) negotiation to the buyer of any negotiable document covering
the goods.
(3 ) To stop delivery the seller must so notify as to :
(a) enable the bailee by reasonable diligence to prevent delivery
of the goods ;
(b) after notice the bailee must hold and deliver the goods ac-
cording to the seller's directions but the seller is liable for
any ensuing charges or damages ;
103

(c) if a negotiable document has been issued on the goods the
bailee is not obliged to obey a notice to stop until surrender
of the documents ;
(d) a carrier who has issued a non-negotiable bill of lading is
not obliged to obey a notification to stop received from a
person other than the consignor .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 358.090 , 358.110 , 358.490 , 361.570 , 361.580 , 361.590 .
Comment
(1) Subsection ( 1) continues the right now conferred under Sec. 57
of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.570 , on buyer's insolvency . The Code,
however , broadens the right to include buyer's repudiation on failure to
make a due payment , or any other instance (such as fraud by the buyer )
where seller would have a right to withhold or reclaim . As to less-than-
carload shipments , the right is limited as under present law to cases of
insolvency .
(2) Subsection 2 ( a) , ending the right of stoppage on receipt of the
goods , corresponds to Secs . 58 ( 1) ( a ) and 58 ( 2 ) (a) of the Uniform Sales
Act , KRS 361.580 ( 1) ( a ) and ( 2 ) ( a ) ( " delivery " ) . Subsection 2 (b ) and
(c) on "acknowledgment " by bailee or carrier is more specific than Sec. 58
(2) (b) of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.580 ( 2 ) ( b ) , and in the case
of carriers apparently prolongs the right of stoppage beyond notification
of arrival to require a separate contract with buyer . Subsections 2 (b) and
3 ( c) under which a negotiable bill of lading overrides the right of
stoppage , follows Sec. 59 (2) of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.590 ( 2 ) .
A substantial change in the law of stoppage in transit is made in UCC
7-303 . By this change , a carrier is made immune from liability for honoring
instructions from the consignor on a non -negotiable bill, even though the
consignor's instructions may constitute an improper stop -order . This pro-
vision , however , does not impair buyer's rights against the seller where
the stop-order is wrongful .
(3) Subsection ( 3 ) ( d) sets forth the rule that strangers to the
contract of carriage have no right of stoppage as against the carrier .
Section 2-706 . Seller's Resale Including Contract for Resale .
Summary
(1) As provided in UCC 2-703 , the seller may resell the goods or
the undelivered portion . Where the resale is made in good faith and in
a commercially reasonable manner the seller may recover the difference
between the resale price and the contract price plus any incidental damages
allowed under UCC 2-710 , but less any expense saved by the buyer's
breach .
104

(2) Except as provided in subsection (3) or as otherwise agreed,
resale may be at public or private sale including sale by way of one or
more contracts to sell or identification to an existing contract of the seller .
Sale may be as a unit or in parcels and at any time and place and on any
terms, but every aspect of the sale must be commercially reasonable. The
resale must be identified as referring to the brokn contract , but th goods
need not be in existence or identified before the breach .
(3) Where the resale is at private sale, the seller must give the buyer
reasonable notice of intention to resell .
(4) Where the resale is at public sale :
(a) only identified goods can be sold except where there is a
recognized market for a public sale of futures in that kind
of goods; and
(b) it must be made at a usual place for public sale if one is
available and except in the case of perishables or goods
threating to decline in value , the seller , must notify the
buyer of the time and place ; and
(c) if the goods are not in view of those attending the sale the
notice must state the place where the goods are located and
provide for their inspection by prospective bidders ; and
the seller may buy .(d)
(5) A good faith purchaser takes the goods free of the original buyer's
rights even though the seller fails to comply with this section .
(6) The seller is not accountable to the buyer for any profit made
on resale . A person in the position of a seller or a buyer who has rightfully
rejected or justifiably revoked acceptance must account for any excess
over the amount of his security interest .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.600 .
Comment
(1) Right of Resale to Fix Damages. Subsection ( 1) allows the seller
to act promptly to resell if buyer defaults ; under Sec. 60 ( 1) of the Uni-
form Sales Act , KRS 361.600 ( 1 ) the right arises only if the goods are
perishable , or the right is expressly reserved , or the buyer has been in
default "an unreasonable time ," Farmers & Depositors Bank v. Taylor, 290
Ky. 774 , 162 S. W. 2d 764 ( 1942) . The Code also provides expressly , as
the Uniform Sales Act did not , that the resale fixes the value of the goods
for the purpose of computing damages . See 5 Williston , Contracts , Sec .
1379A .
(2) Conduct of Sale . Subsection ( 2 ) requires "commercially rea-
sonable" resale, and may give the seller wider latitude than the require-
ment of "reasonable care and judgment " in KRS 361.600 ( 5 ) .
105

(3 ) Notice . Subsections ( 3 ) and (4 ) ( b ) require notice to the buyer
in some circumstances where present law does not . KRS 361.600 ( 3) and
(4) merely make lack of notice of intention to resell relevant on the ques-
tion whether the seller sold too soon . But under the Code , a seller who fails
to give a required notice may apparently recover damages based on market
price under UCC 2-708 . See Clore v. Robinson , 100 Ky . 402 , 38 S. W. 687
(1897) .
(4) The requirements of subsection (4 ) as to the conduct of a public
(auction ) sale are new . Compare KRS 361.600 ( 5 ) .
(5) Subsection ( 5 ) gives greater protection to good faith purchasers
than KRS 361.600 ( 2 ) which gives protection only if resale is " as authorized
in this section ."
(6) Accord, as to a seller , KRS 361.600 ( 1) . The provision making
other parties accountable for profit changes the law. As to a person in
the position of a seller , see KRS 361.520 ( 2) , 361.600 ( 1) , 361.760 (definition
of " seller " ) . As to a rescinding buyer , see KRS 361.590 ( 5 ) .
Section 2-707 . "Person in the Position of a Seller ."
Summary
( 1) A " person in the position of a seller" includes as against a prin-
cipal and agent who has paid or become responsible for the price of goods
on behalf of his principal or anyone who holds a security interest or other
right in goods similar to that of a seller .
(2 ) A person in the position of a seller may , as provided in this article ,
withhold or stop delivery ( UCC 2-705 ) and resell ( UCC 2-706 ) and recover
incidental damages ( UCC 2-710 ) .
KRS 361.520 ( 2 ) .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section slightly expands Sec. 52 ( 2 ) of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS
361.520 ( 2 ) , to include a financing agency with a security interest .
Section 2-708 . Seller's Damages for Non-Acceptance or
Repudiation .
Summary
(1) Subject to subsection (2 ) and to the provisions of this article
with respect to proof of market price ( UCC 2-723 ) , the measure of dam-
ages for non - acceptance or repudiation by the buyer is the difference
between the market price at the time and place for tender and the unpaid
contract price together with any incidental damages provided in this article
(UCC 2-710 ) , but less expenses saved in consequence of the buyer's
breach .
106

(2) If the measure of damages provided in subsection ( 1) is inade-
quate to put the seller in as good a position as performance would have ,
then the measure of damages is the profit ( including reasonable overhead )
which the seller would have made from full performance by the buyer ,
together with any incidental damages provided in this article ( UCC 2-710 ) ,
due allowance for costs reasonably incurred and due credit for payment
or proceeds of resale .
KRS 361.640 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The basic rule of measuring damages approximates that of Sec. 64 (3)
of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.640 ( 3 ) .
Profit . Under present law , loss of profit can also be shown , Fruit
Growers Express Co. v . Citizens Ice & Fuel Co., 271 Ky . 330, 112 S. W. 2d
54 (1937 ) ( loss recoverable by ice company was to be measured by price
express company had agreed to pay for ice and not what ice cost the ice
company ) . See also, Amber Milling Co. v . Kentucky Macaroni Co. , 292 Ky .
831, 168 S. W. 2d 34 ( 1942 ) ; Hawkins v . Midland Flour Milling Co., 236 Ky .
803 , 34 S. W. 2d 439 ( 1931 ) ; In re Independent Distillers of Kentucky , 34 F.
Supp . 708 ( W. D. Ky. 1940 ) .
Section 2-709 . Action for the Price .
Summary
(1) When the buyer fails to pay the price the seller may recover ,
together with incidental damages , the price :
(a) of goods accepted or of conforming goods lost or damaged
within a commercially reasonable time after risk of loss has
passed; and
(b) of goods identified to the contract if the seller is unable to
sell them at a reasonable price or the circumstances indicate
that such effort will be unavailing .
(2) Where the seller sues for the price he must hold for the buyer
any goods which have been identified and are in his control except that
if resale becomes possible he may resell at any time prior to collection
of the judgment . The net proceeds of any resale must be credited to the
buyer and payment of the judgment entitles him to any goods not resold .
(3) After the buyer has wrongfully rejected or revoked acceptance
or has failed to make a payment due or has repudiated , a seller who is
not entitled to the price shall be awarded damages for nonacceptance under
UCC 2-708 .
KRS 361.630 .
Kentucky Statutes
107

Comment
(1) (a) Acceptance . The Code narrows seller's right to the full price ,
making an action for damages the basic remedy . Under this section , liability
for the full price , in the first instance , turns on "acceptance " (as defined in
UCC 2-606 ) instead of "title." As under Sec. 41 of the Uniform Sales Act ,
KRS 361.410 , acceptance is delayed until after a reasonable opportunity to
inspect. But the seller cannot hold the goods at the buyer's risk indefinitely .
A major change in the Code concerning the right to collect the full
price , deals with goods damaged in transit by a carrier , where the buyer
is responsible for the payment of the freight . Under Sec. 19 Rule 4 ( 2) and
Sec. 63 ( 1 ) of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.190 , 361.630, "title" passed
on loading , and buyer became liable for the full price regardless of damage
in transit or the resalability of the goods . Troy Refining Corp. v. Slagter
Oil and Grease Co., 61 F. Supp . 369 ( W. D. Ky. 1945 ) .
The Code also omits the provision of Sec. 63 (2) of the Uniform Sales
Act, KRS 361.630 (2 ) , under which a promise of advance payment is en-
forceable in full, in an action the equivalent of specific performance of a
promise to lend .
(b) Goods Not Resalable . Apparently under the Code , resale is
required , if possible , as a condition precedent to recovery of the seller's
loss, if the buyer does not accept the goods . Subsection ( 1) (b ) roughly
corresponds to Sec. 63 (3 ) of the Sales Act , which gave seller an action
for the price if the goods could not be resold for a reasonable price .
Section 2-710 . Seller's Incidental Damages .
Summary
Incidental damages to an aggrieved seller include any commercially
reasonable charges , expenses or commissions incurred in stopping delivery ,
in the transportation , care and custody of goods after the buyer's breach,
in connection with return or resale of the goods or otherwise resulting from
the breach .
KRS 361.640 , 361.700 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section enumerates the specific things that will be determinative
in arriving at the seller's incidental damages . It is comparable to Secs . 64
and 70 of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.640 and 361.700 .
Section 2-711 . Buyer's Remedies in General ; Buyer's Security
Interest in Rejected Goods .
Summary
(1) Where the seller fails to make delivery or repudiates or the
buyer rightfully rejects or justifiably revokes acceptance , then with respect
108

to any goods involved , and as to the whole if the breach goes to the whole
contract , the buyer may cancel and in addition to recovering any of the
price paid may :
(a) "cover" and have damages under UCC 2-712 as to all the
goods affected whether or not they have been identified ; or
recover damages for nondelivery as provided in UCC 2-713 ,(b)
(2) Where the seller fails to deliver or repudiates the buyer may also :
(a) if the goods have been identified recover them as provided
in UCC 2-502 ; or
(b) in a proper case obtain specific performance or replevy the
goods as provided in UCC 2-716 .
(3) On rightful rejection or justifiable revocation a buyer has a se-
curity interest in goods in his possession or control for any payment made
on their price and any expenses reasonably incurred in their inspection ,
receipt , transportation , care and custody and may hold and resell the goods
as provided in UCC 2-706 .
Kentucky Statutes
No comparable index section ; subsection (3)-KRS 361.690 ( 5) .
Comment
This section, like UCC 2-703 , supra , sums up remedies conferred in other
sections . Subsection ( 1) , permitting the buyer both to revoke acceptance
and to recover damages , is an important innovation . See UCC 2-601 , 2-608 ,
supra . Subsection (3 ) , giving the buyer a lien , extends KRS 361.690 ( 5 ) to
cases of rejection .
Section 2-712 . "Cover " , Buyer's Procurement of Substitute Goods .
Summary
(1) After a breach the buyer may " cover" by making in good faith
and without unreasonable delay any reasonable purchase of or contract to
purchase goods in substitution for those due from the seller .
(2) The buyer may recover as damages the difference between the
cost of cover and the contract price together with any incidental or con-
sequential damages as defined in UCC 2-715 , but less any expense saved
in consequence of the seller's breach .
(3 ) Failure of the buyer to effect cover does not bar him from
any other remedy .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section is designed to give greater weight than under present law
to the price paid for substitute goods in fixing buyer's damages . In effect ,
109

the Code gives the buyer a new right where the seller has failed to supply
the goods or the buyer has rightfully rejected them . This right is called
"cover ," which means the buyer's right to procure substituted goods . The
price paid for the substituted goods is to be accepted as the basis for re-
covery of damages , which is the difference between the contract price
and the cost of the covering goods , together with the buyer's consequential
or incidental damages . Compare UCC 2-706 , supra , ( seller's resale as fixing
buyer's damages ) . Under Sec. 67 ( 1) and ( 3 ) of the Uniform Sales Act ,
KRS 361.670, the basic test is "damages " measured by the difference be-
tween contract price and " market price ... at the time or times when
they ought to have been delivered ." rather than the actual cost of
repurchase . However , under present law, weight is given to the price paid
by buyer on repurchase . See Restatement , Contracts , Secs . 336 and 338 and
Kentucky Annotations . See also 3 Williston, Sales , Sec. 599h .
...
Section 2-713 . Buyer's Damages for Non -Delivery or Repudiation .
Summary
(1) Subject to the provisions of this article with respect to proof of
market price ( UCC 2-723 ) , the measure of damages for nondelivery is the
difference between the market price at the time the buyer learned of the
breach and the contract price together with any incidental and consequential
damages provided in UCC 2-715 , but less expenses saved in consequence
of the seller's breach .
(2) Market price is to be determined as of the place for tender or,
in cases of rejection after arrival or revocation of acceptance , as of the
place of arrival .
KRS 361.670 ( 3 ) .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) In general , subsection ( 1) follows Sec. 67 (3) of the Uniform
Sales Act , KRS 361.670 ( 3 ) . A modification is made, however , in relating
damages to the time buyer "learned of the breach " rather than the time for
performance . Contrast , UCC 2-610 ( b ) which allows a party to "await
performance " by one who has repudiated , and annotations citing Ken-
tucky cases , accord .
(2) Comment 1 indicates that the intention of the section is to use
the market to which buyer has access as the measure of his damages. See
5 Corbin , Contracts , Secs . 1039 and 1044 .
Section 2-714 . Buyer's Damages for Breach in Regard to Accepted
Goods .
Summary
(1) Where the buyer has accepted goods and given notification (UCC
2-607 ( 3 ) ) , he may recover as damages for any nonconformity of tender
110

the loss resulting in ordinary course from the seller's breach as determined
in any reasonable manner .
(2) The measure of damages for breach of warranty is the difference
at the time and place of aceptance between the value of the goods ac-
cepted and the value they would have had if they had been as warranted ,
unless special circumstances show proximate damages of a different amount .
(3) Where proper incidental and consequential damages may also
be recovered .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 361.690 (6) and (7) .
Comment
(1) In allowing action for damages although buyer has accepted , the
Code follows Secs . 49 and 69 ( 1) ( a) and ( b) of the Uniform Sales Act ,
KRS 361.490 , 361.690 . The limitation to damages resulting "in the ordinary
course of events" follows Sec. 69 (6 ) of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.690 .
(2) Subsection (2) follows the general rule for measuring damages
of Sec. 69 (7 ) of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.690 .
Section 2-715 . Buyer's Incidental and Consequential Damages .
Summary
(1) Incidental damages resulting from the seller's breach include
expenses reasonably incurred in inspection , receipt , transportation and care
and custody of the goods rightfully rejected , any commercially reasonable
charges , expenses or commissions in connection with effecting cover and
other reasonable expense incident to the delay or other breach .
(2) Consequential damages resulting from the seller's breach in-
clude:
(a) any loss resulting from general or particular requirements or
needs of which the seller had reason to know at the time of
contracting and which could not be prevented by cover or
otherwise ; and
(b) injury to person or property proximately resulting from any
breach of warranty .
Kentucky Statutes
Subsection (2) (b) -KRS 361.690 (7) , 361.700 .
Comment
( 1 ) Incidental Damages : Components . The specification of expenses
recoverable by the buyer is new . In allowing expenses in making "cover "
(repurchase ) , the section is supported by Coyle v . Hall , 309 Ky . 829, 219
S. W. 2d 48 ( 1949 ) (buyer's expense in trying to repair defective block
in truck before replacing it with new block included .)
111

(2) Consequential Damages . Cf. KRS 361.700 .
(a) Loss Resulting From Requirements or Needs . Subsection ( 2) (a)
is new as a statute. See accord , Riggs Motor Co. v . Archer, Ky . 240 S. W. 2d
75 ( 1951 ) ; Sears, Roebuck and Co. v. Lea , 198 2d 1012 (6th Cir. 1952) ; Moss
v. Yount, 296 Ky . 415, 177 S. W. 2d 372 ( 1944 ) .
(b) Injury to Person or Property . McBride v . Farmers ' Seed Associ-
ation , 248 Ky . 514, 58 S. W. 2d 909 ( 1933 ) . See Williston, Contracts , Sec .
1394 .
Section 2-716 . Buyer's Right to Specific Performance or Replevin .
Summary
(1 ) Specific performance may be decreed where the goods are unique
or in other proper circumstances .
(2) The decree may include such terms as to payment , damages, or
other relief as the court may deem just .
(3 ) The buyer may replevy identified goods if he is unable to effect
cover or if the goods were shipped under reservation and satisfaction of the
security interest has been made or tendered .
KRS 361.680 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Specific Performance . Subsection ( 1) broadens the rule of Sec . 68 of
the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.680 . The Code omits the requirement that
goods be " specific or ascertained .." In actual practice , this limitation seems
to have been overlooked , and specific performance has been liberally
granted . Heidelberg Brewing Co. v . E. F. Prichard Co., 297 Ky . 788 , 180
S. W. 2d 849 ( 1944 ) ; ( contract for sale of ale ) ; Talamini v . Rosa ., 257 Ky .
228, 77 S. W. 2d 627 ( 1934) ( sale of stock) ; Singer v . Campbell , et al., 217
Ky . 830, 290 S. W. 667 ( 1927 ) .
Replevin . Subsection (3 ) abandons the present rule that the action
of replevin depends on the passing of title. Uniform Sales Act Sec. 66 , KRS
361.660 . In some instances the Code would narrow the right of replevin ,
as where title passed to goods available on the market . In other instances
the right of replevin is expanded , where substitute goods are unavailable ,
but the title to the goods is deemed to be in the seller , through a contract
provision requiring seller to take the risk, or deliver , or complete work
on the goods .
Section 2-717 . Deduction of Damages From Price.
Summary
The buyer on notice to the seller may deduct all or any part of the
damages resulting from any breach of the contract from any part of the
price still due under the same contract .
112

KRS 361.690 (1 ) ( a) .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section follows and enlarges upon the provision allowing recoup-
ment in Sec . 69 ( 1 ) ( a) of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.690 . The
requirement of notice of the deduction from the price is new . However , Sec.
49 of the Uniform Sales Act , KRS 361.490 , requires notice of breach of
warranty .
Section 2-718 . Liquidation or Limitation of Damages; Deposits ,
Summary
(1) Damages for breach by either party may be liquidated in the
agreement , but only at an amount which is reasonable under the circum-
stances. A term fixing unreasonably large liquidated damages is void as a
penalty.
(2) Where the seller justifiably withholds delivery of goods because
of the buyer's breach , the buyer is entitled to restitution of any amount
by which the sum of his payments exceeds :
(a) the amount to which the seller is entitled by virtue of terms
liquidating the seller's damages in accordance with subsection
(1) , or
(b) in the absence of such terms , twenty per cent of the value
of the total performance for which the buyer is obligated
under the contract , or $500 , whichever is smaller .
(3) The buyer's right to restitution under subsection ( 2) is subject
to offset to the extent that the seller establishes :
(a) a right to recover damages under the provisions of this article
other than subsection ( 1) , and
(b) the amount or value of any benefits received by the buyer
directly or indirectly by reason of the contract .
(4) Where a seller has received payment in goods , their reasonable
value or the proceeds of their resale shall be treated as payments for the
purposes of subsection ( 2) ; but if the seller has notice of the buyer's breach
before reselling goods received in part performance , his resale is subject to
the conditions laid down in this article on resale by an aggrieved seller
(UCC 2-706 ) .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
( 1 ) Liquidated Damages . There is now no statutory provision . In
general , the test proposed is similar to that set forth in Restatement , Con-
tracts , Sec . 339 , and Kentucky Annotations . See Fidelity & Deposit Co. v .
113

Jones , 256 Ky . 181 , 75 S. W. 2d 1057 ( 1934 ) ; Real Estate Mtge . Co. v. Duke ,
251 Ky . 385 , 65 S. W. 2d 81 ( 1933 ) ; Kilbourne v. Burt & B. Lumber Co., 111
Ky . 693 , 64 S. W. 631 ( 1901 ) .
(2) Commissioners Comment on subsections ( 2 ) , (3 ) and (4 ) :
"The revision makes it clear that a defaulting buyer may be
entitled to restitution under the section, that the section is not
limited to cases of explicit agreement for forfeiture , and that the
buyer's right to restitution is subject to offset the cases set forth
in subsection ( 3) ."
See Restatement , Contracts , Secs . 340, 357 ( 2) , and Kentucky Annota-
tions . See also Robenson v. Yann , 224 Ky . 56, 5 S. W. 2d 271 ( 1928 ) ; Eliza-
bethtown & P. R. R. Co. v . Geoghegan , 72 Ky. 56 ( 1872 ) (dictum ) . See
generally , 5 Corbin , Contracts , Secs . 1073 and 1074. The specified figures
($500 or 20 % ) below which forfeiture may be effective regardless of
amount of damages , are new .
Section 2-719 . Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy .
Summary
(1) Subject to the provisions of subsections (2 ) and (3) and of UCC
2-718:
(a) the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in
substitution for those provided in this article and may limit
or alter the measure of damages recoverable , as by limiting
the buyer to return of the goods and repayment of price or to
repair and replacement of nonconforming goods or parts ;
and
(b) resort to a remedy so provided is optional unless the remedy
is expressed to be exclusive in which case it is the sole
remedy .
(2) Where circumstances cause an exclusive remedy to fail of its
essential purpose , remedy may be had as provided in this act .
(3 ) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless such
limitations is unconscionable . Limitation of consequential damages for per-
sonal injury in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable ,
but where the loss is commercial , it is not .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) The rule of subsection ( 1) , that remedies may be modified by
agreement , is consistent with the general rule of Sec. 71 of the Uniform
Sales Act, KRS 361.710 , that any right, duty or liability under the Act may
114

be modified by agreement . See accord , Graves Ice Cream Co. v . Rudolph
W. Wurlitzer Co. , 267 Ky . 1 , 100 S. W. 2d 819 ( 1937 ) ( remedy for breach of
warranty limited by contract provision that seller would connect unit and
remove it if unsatisfactory ) ; Myers v . Land, 314 Ky . 514, 235 S. W. 2d 988
(1950 ) ; Sears Roebuck & Co. v . Lea, 198 F. 2d 1012 (6th Cir. 1952) ; Dryer-
Whitehead & Goedecke , Inc. v . Land , 309 Ky . 113, 216 S. W. 2d 413 ( 1949 ) .
(2) Subsection (2) is new , and in limiting the effect of the contract
may be inconsistent with the theory of the cases cited above.
(3 ) The refusal to honor contracts limiting consequential damages
if "unconscionable " is inconsistent with the theory of the present law . See
cases , supra ; Tyler v . Moody , 111 Ky . 191 , 63 S. W. 433 ( 1901 ) (damages for
personal injury allowed for breach of warranty that machine could not
explode ) ; contra, Van Deren Hdw . Co. v . Preston & Son , 224 Ky . 170 , 5 S. W.
2d 1052 ( 1928 ) , (consequential damages for loss of profits not allowed as
being too remote) .
Section 2-720 . Effect of "Cancellation " or "Rescission " on Claims
for Antecedent Breach .
Summary
Unless the contrary intention clearly appears , expressions of " can-
cellation " or "rescission " of the contract or the like shall not be construed
as a renunciation or discharge of any claim in damages for an antecedent
breach .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The Commissioners comment makes it clear that the section is designed
to safeguard a person holding a right of action from any unintentional loss
of rights by the ill-advised use of such terms as "cancellation ," "rescission ,"
or the like . Unless the cancellation of a contract expressly declares that it
is "without reservation of rights ," or the like , it cannot be considered to be
a renunciation under this section .
It would appear that this section would change existing Kentucky law.
It has been held that where a buyer has free choice of remedies for breach
of warranty and he elects the remedy of recission , he is thereby precluded
from bringing an action for damages . Piersall v . Huber Mfg . Co. , 159 Ky .
338 , 167 S. W. 144 ( 1914) . This view finds support in 12 Am . Jur. Contracts
Sec. 455:
"Lawful recission puts an end to it for all purposes , not only
to preclude the recovery of the contract price , but also to prevent
recovery for damages for breach of contract ."
12 Am . Jur . Contracts , Sec. 457 states :
"After a contract is rescinded by express or implied agree-
ment, neither party can maintain an action thereon for previous
breach thereof unless a different intent is indicated ."
115

The theory of rescission and cancellation is that the parties are to return
to the status quo and the contract becomes a complete nullity for all pur-
poses . Black Motor Co. v. Green , 258 Ky . 72, 79 S. W. 2d 409 ( 1935 ) .
See generally 3 Williston, Sales , Sec . 612 ; 5 Corbin, Contracts , Secs . 1236 ,
1237; Restatement , Contracts , Secs . 406 , 410 , and Kentucky Annotations .
Section 2-721 . Remedies for Fraud.
Summary
Remedies for material misrepresentation or fraud include all remedies
available under this article for nonfraudulent breach . Neither rescission or
a claim for recission of the contract nor rejection or return of the goods
shall bar or be deemed inconsistent with a claim for damages or other
remedy .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section is designed to insure that an action based on fraud is not
restricted to common -law rules , but is governed by rules at least as liberal
as those applicable where fraud is not present . The Code here would appear
to be contrary to rules set forth in Restatement , Restitution , Sec. 68 , Illus-
tration 6 (partial rescission ) . See also Piersall v . Huber Mfg . Co., 159 Ky.
338, 167 S. W. 144 ( 1914) ( rescission inconsistent with damages ) . This
section would appear to be in accord with the Massachusetts rule that in
an action for deceit, damages are not limited to loss suffered , but the de-
frauded party may recover the benefit which he would have obtained if the
representations had been true . Drake v . Holbrook , 23 K. L. R. 1941 , 66 S. W.
512 ( 1902 ) ( measure of damages for deceit is the difference between what
the plaintiff in fact got and that which he would have gotten if the repre-
sentation had been true ) . C. F. Frick Co. v . Wiley , 290 Ky . 665 , 162 S. W.
2d 190 ( 1942 ) .
Section 2-722 . Who Can Sue Third Parties for Injury to Goods .
Summary
Where a third party so deals with identified goods as to cause action-
able injury to a party to the contract :
(a) a right of action against the third party is in either party who
has title to or a security or a special property or an insurable
interest in the goods ; and if the goods have been destroyed
or converted , a right of action is in the party who either bore
the risk of loss or since the injury has assumed that risk ;
(b) if at the time of injury the party plaintiff did not bear the
risk of loss and there is no arrangement for disposition of the
recovery , his suit or settlement is, subject to his own interest ,
as a fiduciary for the other party to the contract ;
116

(c) either party with the consent of the other may sue for the
benefit of whom it may concern .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Our present sales act does not deal with the problem of "who can
sue third parties for injury to goods "; however , the Code does . It gives
the right exclusively to the buyer where the goods are identified to the
contract and have been accepted ; otherwise the right of action is in the
party who holds title to the goods . Subsection (a) seems to be in accord
with principle giving action to the real party in interest . Kentucky Rules
of Civil Procedure , Rule 17.01 .
(b) Suit as Fiduciary . This would seem to be in accord with the
general rule that where one recovers judgment , any part of which in equity
and in good conscience belongs to another , the one holds the proceeds sub-
ject to the claim of the other .
(c) Kentucky Rules of Civil Procedure , Rule 19.01 requires joinder
of all parties who are united in interest . Therefore , this subsection which
would permit one party to sue as a nominal plaintiff would seem to alter
present law , since under present practice , if both are beneficially interested,
both probably must join in spite of an attempted assignment.
Section 2-723. Proof of Market Price : Time and Place .
Summary
(1) If an action based on anticipatory repudiation is tried before
the time for performance as to some or all of the goods, any damages based
on market price UCC 2-708 or UCC 2-713 shall be determined according to
the price of such goods prevailing at the time the aggrieved party learned
of the repudiation .
(2) If evidence of a price prevailing at the times or places described
in this article is not available the price prevailing within any reasonable
time before or after the time described or at any other place which in
commercial judgment or under usage of trade would serve as a reasonable
substitute may be used, making proper allowance for transporting the
goods to or from such place .
(3) Evidence of a relevant price prevailing at a time or place other
than the one described in this article offered by one party is not admissable
unless and until he has given the other party such notice as the court
finds sufficient to prevent unfair surprise .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1 ) Subsection ( 1 ) is made necessary by UCC 2-610 (b) under which
a party faced with anticipatory repudiation may "await performance ." The
117

choice is consistent with present law; see cases cited UCC 2-610 supra .
There are conflicts in the Kentucky decisions concerning measure of dam-
ages for anticipatory breach . See Restatement , Contracts , Sec. 338 and
Kentucky Annotations .
This subsection provides that the market price will be determined ac-
cording to the price prevailing at the time the aggrieved party learned of
the repudiation where the action comes to trial before the time of perform-
ance . UCC 2-713 , in establishing the proper measure of damages for non-
delivery by a seller , provides that the time of measurement is the time the
buyer learned of the breach . This rule perhaps applies regardless of whether
the breach consists of a repudiation and also regardless of when the action
comes to trial . This subsection does qualify directly what otherwise might
be a contrary implication in UCC 2-708 , which deals with the seller's
measure of damages for nonacceptance.
The rule of this subsection does alter the usual rule of measuring dam-
ages where the trial takes place prior to the date of performance . Generally ,
it is said that damages are measured by what the market price is likely to
be at the time of performance . 3 Williston , Sales , Sec. 587 states :
"If the trial takes place before that time , the jury must estimate
as best they can what future situation is likely to be."
This is probably the Kentucky view , since it almost uniformly measures
the damages as of the time of performance . Acme -Jones Co. v . Ellis Milling
Co. , 200 Ky . 811 , 255 S. W. 829 ( 1923 ) . But see Louisville Packing Co. v.
Crain , 141 Ky . 379, 132 S. W. 575 ( 1910 ) (damages measured as of the time
repudiation "accepted " ) .
(2) Subsection ( 2 ) , allowing the use of a market other than one re-
lated to the contract , is new as a statutory provision . However , it probably
states what has always been understood to be the law where there was no
market prevailing at the time and place of measurement . McCormick ,
Damages , 182 states :
"Rather frequently , however , it happens that the place of de-
livery or the place of the wrong can furnish no market for such
property . Then the property must be valued according to the price
prevailing in the nearest advantageous market with a deduction for
the risk and expense of getting it to that place."
This states the Kentucky view on the matter . Perry v . Reuss , 204 Ky . 359,
264 S. W. 750 ( 1924 ) . See generally 3 Williston , Sales , Sec. 599 ; 5 Williston ,
Contracts , Sec. 1378 .
Section 2-724 . Admissibility of Market Quotations .
Summary
Whenever the prevailing price or value of any goods regularly bought
and sold in any established commodity market is in issue , reports in of-
ficial publications or trade journals or in newspapers or periodicals of gen-
eral circulation published as the reports of such market shall be admissible
in evidence . The circumstances of its preparation may be shown to affect
its weight but not its admissibility .
118

None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The liberal rule of evidence in this section providing that published
market reports are admissible goes beyond existing statutes . Compare Uni-
form Photographic Copies of Business and Public Records as Evidence Act ,
KRS 422.105 (to be admissible under this act reproductions must be of re-
ports made "in the regular course of business to which the entry relates" ) .
No Kentucky decisions on the point were found . Compare Louisville & N.
R. Co. v. Thompson's Adm'r , 217 Ky . 21 , 288 S. W. 761 ( 1926 ) ( published
life or mortality tables admissible .) The Code provision finds support among
the authorities . See Wigmore , Evidence , Sec. 1704. See also 20 Am . Jur .,
Evidence , Sec. 384 wherein it is stated :
"Rule generally recognized that market reports or quotations as
contained in newspapers and trade journals are competent evidence
of the state of the market upon the theory that they are based upon
records from an authentic source."
Section 2-725 . Statute of Limitations in Contracts for Sale.
Summary
(1) An action for breach of contract for sale must be commenced
within 4 years after the cause has accrued . By the original agreement the
parties may reduce this period to not less than one year but may not ex-
tend it.
(2) A cause of action accrues when the breach occurs regardless of
the aggrieved party's lack of knowledge of it. A breach of warranty occurs
when tender of delivery is made, except that where a warranty explicitly
extends to future performance of the goods and discovery of the breach
must await the time of performance , the cause accrues when the breach is
or should have been discovered .
(3) Where an action commenced within the time limited by subsection
(1) is so terminated as to leave available a remedy by another action for
the same breach such other action may be commenced after the expiration
of the time limited and within 6 months after the termination of the first
action unless the termination resulted from voluntary discontinuance or
from dismissal for failure or neglect to prosecute .
(4) This section does not alter the law on tolling the statute of limi-
tations nor does it apply to causes which accrued before the effective date
of this act .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
( 1) Statutory Period . The four -year period under the Code would
change present law . There is now a limitation of 15 years on actions based
119

upon a written contract and a limitation of 5 years on actions based upon
contracts not in writing, express or implied . KRS 413.090 , 413.120 . The
limitation for personal injury actions is one year . KRS 413.140 .
(2) Modification by Contract . This provision does not seem to have
any counterpart in existing statutes. But see accord , Restatement , Contracts ,
Secs . 86 , 558, and Kentucky Annotations .
(3) Lack of Knowledge . The general rule governing the commence-
ment of the running of the Statute is that the statutory period is computed
from the time when the right of action which the plaintiff seeks to enforce
first accrued, that is, ordinarily in an action based on a contract , as soon as
there is a breach of contract . 6 Williston , Contracts , Sec . 2004. Code provision
therefore is in accord with the general rule. The exception made under the
Uniform Sales Act by the implied warranty of quiet possession is not con-
tinued in the Code . KRS 361.130 ( 2 ) ; UCC 2-312 and Comment .

ARTICLE THREE
COMMERCIAL PAPER
Introduction
Article 3 of the Uniform Commercial Code , entitled "Commercial
Paper" is a general revision of the Uniform Negotiable Instruments Law ,
or NIL, enacted in Kentucky as KRS Ch. 356 being KRS 356.001 through
356.193 inclusive . The NIL was adopted in Kentucky in 1904. The KRS
chapter does not include Secs . 190, 195, 196 and 197 of the Uniform Law .
Like other articles of the UCC , Article 3 is broken down into parts ,
in this case eight in number . Each part is broken down into sections which
are indicated by the number three followed by a subsection number of
three digits . The digit numbers for Part 1 are in the 100 series, Part 2
in the 200 series, etc.
The 1952 draft of the UCC contains a table which relates sections of
the UCC to the NIL . Sections of the UCC do not uniformly follow sections
of the NIL . This is due primarily to the breakdown of Article 3 into eight
parts.
The UCC treats in articles other than "Commercial Paper " certain
heads of subject matter covered or affected by the NIL , KRS 356.001
through 356.193 inclusive . Thus , the subject of bank deposits and collections
is dealt with in UCC Article 4; letters of credit are treated separately in
UCC Article 5 ; and bonds , debentures , and other corporate obligations , now
covered at least in part by KRS Ch . 356 are transferred by the UCC to
Article 8, " Investment Securities , " where they are treated in common with
certificates of stock on the theory that this follows the thinking and the
practice of businessmen . Negotiable instruments , insofar as they are
involved in secured transactions , are controlled by UCC Article 9; where
there is any conflict between that article and Article 3 , Article 9 pre-
vails . Similarly , where there is any conflict between the bank collection
provisions of Article 4 and those of Article 3, Article 4 controls .
UCC Article 3 is divided into eight subdivisions as follows :
Part
1.

Short Title , Form and Interpretation .
Transfer and Negotiation .

Presentment , Notice of Dishonor and Protest .
Discharge .
Advice of International Sight Draft.
Miscellaneous .

Part 1
SHORT TITLE , FORM AND INTERPRETATION
Introductory Comment
(1 ) NIL Topics Covered in Part 1. NIL Article 1 , Form and Inter-
pretation , ( "Formal Requisites " ) Secs . 1-9, 11 , 12; and the definition of
bill of exchange ( NIL 126 , 128 ) , the definitions of promissory note and
check (NIL 184, 185) and the rules relating to interpretation of indorse-
ments , effect of blanks , and position of signatures (NIL Secs . 13 , 14, 15 ,
17, 41 , 42, and 68) . The effect of stating on an instrument that it is payable
at a bank (NIL 87) is stated here also .
( 2) NIL Topics Omitted . ( a) NIL Sec. 10 stating that any terms
are sufficient which clearly indicate an intent to conform to the Act .
(b) NIL Sec. 21 , Effect of Signature by procuration , omitted
as obsolete in the United States .
(3 ) New Topics . Sections relating to the effect of a statement that
an instrument is " payable through " a bank , that an instrument is "void if
not presented in ten days" or the like, and the effect of other writings
executed as a part of the same transaction .
(4) NIL Matters Covered Elsewhere . The "Bearer " character of
instruments indorsed in blank , NIL Sec . 9 (5) , is covered in UCC 3-204 , and
the "bearer " character of instruments payable to fictitious payees ( NIL
¡Sec. 9 ( 3 ) and imposters is covered in UCC 3-405 . The rule of NIL Sec .
16 as to the rights of a holder in due course is covered in UCC 3-305 . Secs .
19 , 20 , and 23 are covered in Part 4 on liability of parties .

Section 3-104 . Form of Negotiable Instruments ; " Draft" ;
" Check " ; " Certificate of Deposit " ; "Note" .
Summary
( 1) To be a negotiable instrument , a writing must :
(a) be signed by the maker or drawer ; and
(b) contain an unconditional promise or order to pay a sum
(c)
certain in money and no other promise , order , obligation or
power except as herein authorized by this article ; and
be payable on demand or at a definite time ; and
(d) be payable to order or to bearer .
(2) A writing so complying is a :
(a) "draft" ( "bill of exchange" ) if it is an order ;
(b) "check " if it is a draft drawn on a bank and payable on
demand;
(c) " certificate of deposit" if it is an acknowledgment by a bank
of receipt of money with an engagement to repay ;
(d) "note" if it is a promise other than a certificate of deposit .
(3) As used in other articles of this act, and as the context may
require , the terms "draft," "check," " certificate of deposit" and "note "
may refer to instruments which are not negotiable within this article as
well as to instruments which are so negotiable .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.001 , 356.005 , 356.010 , 356.126, 356.184 , 356.185 .
Comment
(1) (a) Accord , KRS 356.001 ( 1) . KRS 446.060 requires signature
to be at end or close of the writing.
(b) Accord, KRS 356.001 (2 ) . This section also provides that
the writing must not contain any " other promise , order ,
obligation or power given by the maker or drawer except
as authorized by this chapter " . Additional limited obli-
gations or powers which are permissible may be found in
UCC 3-112 .
(c) Accord, KRS 356.001 ( 3 ) . See annotation to UCC 3-109 as
to "definite time ."
(d) Accord, KRS 356.001 (4) .
( 2 ) ( a) , ( b ) and ( d) Accord , KRS 356.126 , 356.184, 356.185.
(c) Accord , Krebs v . Blatz , 134 Ky . 505, 121 S. W. 436 ( 1909 )
(certificate of deposit is in effect a promissory note ) ; Wells
et al. v . Citizens Bank of Albany, 264 Ky . 233, 94 S. W. 2d
608 ( 1936) . See also, Ogden, Negotiable Instruments ( 5th
ed.) Sec. 302 .
(3) Inserted to make clear the same policy as in UCC 3-805 .
Omission . NIL Sec. 10 (KRS 356.010 ) is omitted although such omis-
sion is not intended to indicate a requirement that the instrument contain
the exact words of the statute . See Commissioners Comment .
127

Section 3-105 . When Promise or Order Unconditional .
Summary
( 1) An otherwise unconditional promise or order is not made con-
ditional by the fact that the instrument :
(a) is subject to implied or constructive conditions ; or
(b) states its consideration or the transaction giving rise to the
instrument , or that the promise or order is made or the instru-
ment matures in accordance with or " as per " such transaction ;
or
(c) refers to or states that it arises out of a separate agreement ;
(d)
or
states that it is drawn under a letter of credit ; or
(e) states that it is secured ; or
( f) indicates a particular account to be debited or any other
fund from which reimbursement is expected; or
(g) if issued by a government or governmental agency, is limited
to payment out of a particular fund ; or
(h) is limited to payment out of the entire assets of a partnership ,
unincorporated association , trust or estate by which issued .
(2 ) A promise or order is not unconditional if the instrument states :
(a) it is subject to or governed by any other agreement ; or
(b) it is to be paid out of a particular fund or source except as
provided above.
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.003 .
(1) Not conditional .
Comment
(a) No Kentucky cases found on this specific point . But com-
pare Pulaski County v . Eichstaedt , 110 F. 2d 79 (6th Cir . 1940 )
(where county bonds were on their face unconditional obli-
gations , negotiability was not affected by indication that they
were to be paid out of funds from a certain tax levy ) .
(b) Accord , KRS 356.003 ( 2 ) (transaction giving rise to the in-
strument ) ; Remedial Plan v . Otto , 199 Ky . 161 , 250 S. W. 2d
825 ( 1923 ) (note containing statement of the transaction out
of which it arose and an agreement to return the goods pur-
chased on default is negotiable ) .
(c) No Kentucky cases found . But compare , Fidelity & Columbia
Trust Co. v . Schmidt , 245 Ky . 432 , 53 S. W. 2d 713 (1932 )
(where terms of a collateral instrument are expressly incor-
porated into a bond by reference such bond is non -negoti-
able ) .
(d) No Kentucky cases found on this point .
128

(e) No Kentucky cases found on this point .
(f) Accord, KRS 356.003 ( 1 ) . National Deposit Bank v . Ohio Oil
Co. , 250 Ky . 288, 62 S. W. 2d 1048 ( 1933 ) ; Pulaski County v .
Eichstaedt , 110 F. 2d 79 ( 6th Cir . 1940 ) .
(g) Contra , Pulaski County v . Ben Hur Life Ass'n of Crawfords-
ville , Ind ., 286 Ky . 119 , 149 S. W. 2d 738 ( 1941 ) ; ( municipal
bonds are not negotiable if made payable from a particular
fund derived from designated sources which may prove in-
sufficient ) . Accord , Pulaski County v. Eichstaedt , 110 F. 2d
79 ( 6th Cir. 1940 ) , (where county bonds were on their face
unconditional obligations , negotiability was not affected by
indication that they were to be paid out of funds from a
certain tax levy) .
(h) No Kentucky decisions were found on this subject . But cases
such as Hibbs v. Brown , 190 Ν . Υ . 167 , 82 N. E. 1108 , ( 1907 ) ;
Charles Nelson Co. v . Morton , 106 Cal. App . 144, 288 Pac., 845
(1930) , are in accord ; compare Lorinier v . McGreevy , 229
Mo. App . 970, 84 S. W. 2d 667, ( 1935 ) . Commissioners Com-
ment states : "The provision affects only the negotiability of
the instrument , and is not intended to change the law of
any state as to the liability of a partner , trustee , executor ,
administrator , or any other person on such an instrument ."
(2) Instruments Made Conditional .
(a) Accord, Fidelity & Columbia Trust Co. v . Schmidt , 245 Ky .
432 , 53 S. W. 2d 713 ( 1932 ) , (bonds made subject to terms
of another instrument by reference thereto are non -negoti-
able ) .
(b) Accord , KRS 356.003 ( 2 ) .
Section 3-106 . Sum Certain .
with:
Summary
(1) The sum payable is a sum certain even though it is to be paid
(a) interest or by installments ; or
(b) different rates of interest before and after default or a
(c)
specified time ; or
discount or addition ; or
(d) less exchange ; or
(e) costs of collection or attorney's fees or both .
(2) Nothing in this section shall validate any term which is otherwise
illegal .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.002 , 356.006 ( 5 ) .
129

Comment
(1) (a) Identical to KRS 356.002 ( 1) and ( 2 ) .
(b) No Kentucky cases were found on this point . It is in accord
with the law of Pennsylvania . First National Bank of Miami
v . Bosler , 297 Pa. 353 , 147 Atl . 74 ( 1929 ) . See also , Ogden ,
Negotiable Instruments (5th ed .) Sec. 52 ; 1 Daniel , Negoti-
able Instruments ( 7th ed.) Sec. 58 .
(c) No Kentucky cases were found on this point . The NIL con-
tains no provision as to the negotiability of a note providing
for a discount . Hence the rules of the law merchant have
been applied and many courts have held discount notes
to be non -negotiable . See Waterhouse v. Chouinard , 128 Me .
505, 149 Α. 21 , ( 1930 ) ; Iowa City First National Bank v.
Watson , 56 Okl . 495, 155 P. 1152 ( 1916 ) . The basis for these
decisions has been that the sum payable was at the date
thereof dependent upon the maker's will and that the sum
certain must be ascertainable at the date of the note . How-
ever , other courts have held notes containing similar dis-
count provisions to be negotiable on the ground that the
amount to be paid must rest upon a commercial and not a
mathematical certainty . See , Farmers ' Loan , etc. , Co. v .
Planck, 98 Neb . 225, 152 N. W. 390 ( 1915 ) . See generally ,
1 Daniel , Negotiable Instruments , Sec. 58 .
(d) Identical with KRS 356.002 (4) , except for the addition of
of the words "less exchange."
(e) Identical with KRS 356.002 ( 5 ) , with the addition of the
words "or both " and the substitution of the words "upon
default " for "at maturity ."
(2) No Kentucky decisions were found on this point .
Section 3-107 . Money .
Summary
(1) An instrument is payable in money if the medium of payment
is money , at the time of making the instrument . "Currency " or " current
funds" means money .
(2) A promise or order to pay a sum stated in a foreign currency
is for a sum certain in money and , unless otherwise specified in the instru-
ment , may be paid in dollars at the buying sight rate for that currency on
the day the instrument is payable or day of demand . If a foreign currency
is specified , the instrument is payable in that currency .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.006 (5 ) .
Comment
The definition of "money" in UCC 1-201 (24) rejects the narrow view
of legal tender and also the contention that money includes any medium
130

of exchange in current use whether it be gold dust, salt , or candy bars .
Instead the test adopted is the sanction of government . The provision in
subsection ( 1 ) on "currency " or "current funds " accepts the majority view
that such means payable in money . (No Kentucky decisions found .) See ,
Henderson v . Farmers Sav . Bank, 199 Iowa 496, 202 N. W. 259 ( 1925 ) ;
Merchants Nat. Bank v. Santa Maria Sugar Co., 162 App . Div . 248, 147
N. Y. S. 498 (1914 ) . See generally , 1 Daniel , Negotiable Instruments , Secs .
61 to 65 .
See also, Ashless Coal Co. v . Davis , 183 Ky . 406, 209 S. W. 532 ( 1919 ) ,
holding that if instrument provides for payment other than in money , it
loses its standing as commercial paper under the rules of the law merchant .
Subsection (2) creates the presumption , unless the instrument other-
wise provides , that the instrument may be paid in dollars at the exchange
rate . Even though the exchange rate fluctuates , for purposes of negotia-
bility, such an amount is regarded as a sum certain . No Kentucky de-
cisions were found on this point . However the section states current bank
practice in large commercial states . See, Italian Discount & Trust Co. v .
Herschman , 262 Mass . 362, 160 N. E. 184 ( 1928 ) .
Section 3-108. Payable on Demand .
Summary
Instruments payable on demand include those payable at sight or on
presentation and those in which no time for payment is stated.
KRS 356.007 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Accord, KRS 356.007. However , the last sentence of KRS 356.007 making
overdue instruments payable on demand in certain circumstances is omitted .
See Moriarty v . Howard, 258 Ky . 629 , 80 S. W. 2d 526 ( 1935 ) which held
that a note endorsed after maturity is payable on demand . See also, Reme-
dial Plan v . Ott , 199 Ky. 161 , 250 S. W. 825 ( 1923 ) , wherein a payment clause
in the note read "within ten -," indicating omission of apparently
intended word "months," and it was held to be payable on demand.
Section 3-109 . Definite Time .
Summary
(1 ) An instrument is payable at a definite time if payable :
(a) on or before a stated date or at a fixed period after a stated
date; or
(b) at a fixed period after sight ; or
(c) at a definite time subject to acceleration ; or
131

(d) at a definite time subject to extension at holder's option or
to a further definite time at maker's option or automatically
upon or after a specified act or event.
(2) An instrument payable only upon an act or event uncertain as to
time of occurrence is not payable at a definite time even though the act
or event has occurred .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.004 , 356.017 (3 ) .
Comment
(1) ( a) Accord , KRS 356.004 ( 1 ) , ( 2 ) .
(b) Accord , KRS 356.004 ( 1 ) .
(c) Kentucky cases have generally upheld acceleration clauses .
Finley v . Smith , 165 Ky . 445 , 177 S. W. 262 ( 1915 ) ; Farmers '
Bank & Trust Co. v . Dent , 206 Ky . 405, 267 S. W. 202. But
the phrase " subject to any acceleration " goes beyond the
language of the decisions .
(d) Accord , KRS 356.004 (3 ) ( happening on specified event ) .
As to the effect of extension provisions , no Kentucky de-
cisions were found . See Ogden , Negotiable Instruments , ( 5th
ed .) Sec . 53 ; 1 Daniel , Negotiable Instruments (7th ed .) ,
Sec. 57 ; 7 Am . Jur., Bills and Notes , Sec . 153 .
(2) Subsection ( 2 ) changes the rule as to post-obit bonds expressed
in KRS 356.004 ( 3) . Under the Code an instrument is held not to be payable
at a definite time if it is payable only upon an act or event uncertain as to
time of occurrence , though certain to occur at some time , and even though
the act or event may have occurred .
Section 3-110 . Payable to Order .
Summary
(1) An instrument is payable to order when it is payable to the order
or assigns of a specified person or to him or his order or when designated
as " exchange" and names a payee . It may be payable to the order of :
(a) maker or drawer ; or
(b) drawee ; or
(c) a payee who is not ( a) or ( b ) ; or
(d) two or more payees together or in the alternative ; or
(e)
(f)
(g)
an estate , trust or fund in which case it is payable to the
order of the representative or his successors ; or
an office or officer by his title as such in which case it is
payable to the principal but the incumbent of the office or
his successors may act as if he or they were the holder ; or
a partnership or unincorporated association , in which case it
is payable to the partnership or association and may be in-
dorsed or transferred by any authorized person .
132

(2) An instrument not payable to order is not made so by words
such as "payable upon return of this instrument properly indorsed ."
(3) An instrument payable both to order and bearer is payable to
order unless the bearer words are handwritten or typewritten .
KRS 356.008 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Accord , KRS 356.008 . Except that the word " assigns " now im-
ports negotiability .
(a) Accord, KRS 356.008 ( 2 ) .
(b) Accord , KRS 356.008 ( 3 ) .
(c) Accord , KRS 356.008 ( 1 ) .
(d) Accord, KRS 356.008 (4) and ( 5 ) .
(e) No Kentucky decisions found .
(f) Accord, KRS 356.008 ( 6 ) .
(g) No Kentucky decisions found .
(2) No Kentucky cases were found on this point . There is apparently
a split of authority elsewhere . See Beutel , Brannan's , Negotiable Instru-
ment Law (6th ed.) , p. 127; Ogden, Negotiable Instruments (5th ed .) , Sec.
50.
(3) No Kentucky decisions were found on this point .
Section 3-111 . Payable to Bearer .
Summary
An instrument is payable to bearer when it is payable to :
(a) bearer or order of bearer ; or
(b) a specified person or bearer ; or
(c) "cash " or order of "cash " , or any other indication which does
not designate a specific payee .
KRS 356.009 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) (a) and (b) correspond to KRS 356.009 ( 1) , ( 2) , except for
for addition of "order of bearer ."
(c) corresponds to KRS 356.009 ( 4) except for use of payable to
"cash " as illustration .

Section 3-112 . Terms and Omissions Not Affecting Negotiability .
Summary
(1) Negotiability is not affected by :
(a) omission of consideration or place where instrument is drawn
or payable ; or
(b) provision for collateral with authority to sell ; or
(c) a promise or power to maintain or protect collateral or to
give additional collateral ; or
(d) confession of judgment provision ; or
(e) purported waiver of law for benefit of obligor ; or
(f)
(g)
provision in draft that by indorsing and cashing full satis-
faction is acknowledged ;
a statement in a draft drawn in a set of parts ( UCC 3-801 ) to
the effect that the order is effective only if no other part
has been honored .
(2) Nothing in this section shall validate any term otherwise illegal .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.005 , 356.006 .
Comment
(1) (a) Accord , KRS 356.006 ( 2 ) , ( 3 ) .
(b) Accord , KRS 356.005 ( 1) .
(c) Accord, Finley v . Smith , 165 Ky . 445 , 177 S. W. 262 ( 1915 )
(additional collateral provision held not to affect negotiabil-
ity of note) .
(d) Accord , KRS 356.005 ( 2 ) . The Commissioners Comment
states that paragraph ( d) is intended to mean that a con-
fession of judgment may be authorized only if the instru-
ment is not paid when due, and that otherwise negotiability
is effected . See , Wedding v . First National Bank, 280 Ky .
610 , 133 S. W. 2d 931 ( 1939 ) .
(e) This subsection corresponds to Sec. 5 (3 ) of the NIL. How-
ever, Kentucky omitted subsection ( 3) of this section when
adopting the NIL .
(f) This subsection is new . No Kentucky cases were found on
this point . See Commissioners Comment on UCC 3-802 ( 3 )
for effect of this provision on satisfaction of an obligation .
(g) This subsection is new . It was inserted to insure that the
condition in question will not adversely affect negotiability .
(2) Accord, KRS 356.005 (last sentence ) .
Section 3-113 . Seal.
Summary
An instrument otherwise negotiable is within this article even though
it is under a seal .
134

Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.006 (4) .
Comment
Accord , KRS 356.006 ( 4) .
Section 3-114 . Date , Antedating , Postdating .
Summary
(1) Negotiability is not affected by the fact that an instrument is
undated , antedated or postdated.
(2) The time of payment of an antedated or postdated instrument
is determined by the stated date if it is payable on demand or at a fixed
period after date .
(3) Where the instrument or any signature is dated, the date is
presumed correct .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.006 ( 1) , 356.011 , 356.012 , 356.017 (3 ) .
Comment
(1) The negotiability of an instrument is not affected by the fact
that it is undated , accord , KRS 356.006 ( 1 ) ; or that it is antedated or post-
dated, accord , KRS 356.012 . Republic Life & Accident Ins . Co. v . Hatcher ,
244 Ky . 574, 51 S. W. 2d 922 ( 1932 ) , (fact that insurance premium check
was postdated did not render it invalid , provided it was not dated ahead
for an illegal or fraudulent purpose ) .
(2) Subsection (2) is new . The Commissioners Comment states that
"when the instrument has been dated , this subsection follows decisions
under the original Act in providing that the time of payment is to be
determined from the stated date, even though the instrument is antedated
or postdated ." No Kentucky cases were found on this point .
(3) Accord , KRS 356.011. It should be noted, however , that there is a
slight change in that the Code extends the presumption to all signatures .
Section 3-115 . Incomplete Instruments .
Summary
(1) When a paper intended to be an instrument is signed while in-
complete in any necessary respect it is unenforceable until completed , but
when completed in accordance with authority given , it is effective as com-
pleted.
(2) If the completion is unauthorized , the rules as to material alter-
ation apply , even though the paper was not delivered , but the burden of
establishing unauthorized completion is upon the asserting party .
135

Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.013 , 356.014 , 356.015 .
Comment
(1 ) This section corresponds to the first and portions of the third
sentences of KRS 356.014 with certain changes in phraseology . The fourth
sentence is now found in UCC 4-401 . The second sentence of KRS 356.014
is omitted entirely .
(2 ) Contra as to undelivered paper , KRS 356.015 . Subsection (2)
in effect reverses the rule under KRS 356.015 which provides that where
an incomplete instrument has not been delivered it will not , if completed,
be a valid contract in the hands of any holder as against any person whose
signature was placed thereon before delivery . This results due to the in-
sertion of the language "even though the paper was not delivered " in the
subsection . Since under the Code ( UCC 3-305 and 3-407 ) neither non-
delivery nor unauthorized completion is a defense against a holder in due
course it would be illogical that the two together should invalidate the
instrument in his hands . The theory being that a holder in due course
sees and takes the same paper , whether it was complete when stolen or
completed afterward by the thief , and in each case he relies in good faith
on the maker's signature . Thus the loss should fall upon the party whose
conduct in signing blank paper has made the fraud possible , rather than
upon the innocent purchaser . One Kentucky decision appears to follow the
spirit of the Code provision . In Thompson v. Peck , 217 Ky . 766, 290 S. W.
722 ( 1927 ) the drawer signed a blank check , instructing his agent to draw
it payable to X, have X endorse to Y and forward to Y. The agent drew
it payable to Y and forwarded it to him . The court held there was an
absolute delivery of a completed instrument and drawer was liable thereon .
The language on the burden of establishing unauthorized completion
is substituted for the "prima facie authority " of KRS 356.014 . It follows
the generally accepted rule that the full burden of proof by a preponder-
ance of the evidence is upon the party attacking the completed instrument .
Section 3-116 . Instruments Payable to Two or More Persons .
Summary
An instrument payable to two or more persons :
(a) if in the alternative , is payable to any one of them and may
be negotiated , discharged or enforced by one in possession ;
(b) if not in the alternative , is payable to all and may be negoti-
ated , discharged or enforced only by all of them .
KRS 356.041 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(a) No Kentucky decisions on alternate payees were found , but the
majority view seems in accord : Voris v . Schoonover , 91 Kan . 530 , 138 P.
136

607 (1914 ) ; Page v . Ford, 65 Ore . 450, 131 P. 1013 ( 1913 ) ; Union Bank v .
Spies, 151 Iowa 178, 130 N. W. 928 ( 1911 ) .
(b) Accord, KRS 356.041 . But note the fact that permitting one
partner to sign for the partnership has been dropped from the Code . See
the comment to UCC 3-117 which states that the joint payees need not sign
if one is authorized to sign for the other . This statement it seems is intended
to cover the partnership situation here .
The last sentence of the comment says an instrument payable to "A
and /or B " is payable alternatively or jointly which is in accord with
Glen Falls Indemnity Co. v . Chase Nat'l Bank, 257 N. Y. 441 , 178 N. E. 751
(1931) .
Section 3-117 . Instruments Payable with Words of Description .
Summary
An instrument payable to a named person with words describing him:
(a)
(b)
as officer or agent of a specified person is payable to the
principal but payee may act as if he were the holder ;
as any other fiduciary for a specified person or purpose is
payable to the payee and he may negotiate, discharge or
enforce it;
(c) in any other manner is payable to payee unconditionally and
the additional words are ineffective .
KRS 356.042 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(a) Accord , KRS 356.042 except that the Code extends the rule of
that section to include all agents and officers of principals . See Eades v .
Muhlenberg County Savings Bank , 157 Ky . 416 , 163 S. W. 494, ( 1914 )
where by way of dictum the court stated that it has been uniformly held
by the courts that a note payable to the cashier of a bank is payable to
the bank , and it may sue thereon as payee. (Case held that rule of KRS
356.042 also applies to non - negotiable paper .)
(b) No Kentucky cases were found on this point . The Code pro-
vision appears to follow the majority view on the question which holds
that in such cases the instrument is payable to the individual named, and he
may negotiate it, enforce it or discharge it, but he remains subject to any
liability for breach of his obligation as a fiduciary . For general discussion
see Ogden , Negotiable Instruments ( 5th ed .) , Secs . 150 to 156 .
(c) No Kentucky cases were found on this point .
Section 3-118 . Ambiguous Terms and Rules of Construction .
Summary
The following rules apply to every instrument :
(a) where there is doubt whether an instrument is a note or a
draft the holder may treat it as either . A draft drawn on the
drawer is effective as a note;
137

(b) handwritten terms control typewritten and printed terms,
and typewritten control printed ;
(c) words control figures unless ambiguous then figures control ;
(d) unless otherwise specified , an interest provision means interest
at judgment rate at place of payment from the date of the
instrument or if undated from date of issue ;
(e) unless otherwise specified , two or more persons who sign as
maker , acceptor, drawer or endorser and as part of the same
transaction are liable jointly and severally even though the
instrument is in the singular ;
(f) unless otherwise specified , consent to extension authorizes a
single extension for not longer than the original period . A
consent to extension , expressed in the instrument , is binding
on secondary parties and accommodation makers . A holder
may not exercise his option to extend an instrument over the
objection of a maker or acceptor or other party who in ac-
cordance with UCC 3-604 tenders full payment when the
instrument is due .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.017 , 356.068 .
Comment
(a) Accord, KRS 356.017 ( 5 ) .
(b) Accord, KRS 356.017 ( 4) .
(c) Accord , KRS 356.017 ( 1 ) .
(d) Accord, KRS 356.017 ( 2 ) . See Bassett v . Paine's Adm'r, 264 Ky .
495 , 95 S. W. 2d 8 , ( 1936 ) , (note given in part consideration of deed, pay-
able on death of vendor to vendor's estate , without interest , bore interest
from death of vendor , which was date of maturity , since non -payment at
that time rendered maker in default ) .
(e) Accord , KRS 356.017 ( 7 ) , 356.068 . See Louisville First Nat . Bank
v . Bickel, 143 Ky . 754, 137 S. W. 790 ( 1911 ) (liability as between indorsers
may be shown by parol evidence to be other than successive ) . Query
whether under Code provision parol evidence would be admissible since
inference appears to be that variation must be specified in the instrument
or parties would be jointly and severally liable .
(f) No Kentucky cases found . This provision is new . The Commis-
sioners Comment states that "where consent to extension has been given ,
the subsection provides that unless otherwise specified the consent is to
be construed as authorizing only one extension for not longer than the
original period of the note." It also makes clear that the extension binds
secondary parties and that the holder may not force an extension on a
maker or acceptor who makes due tender .
138

Section 3-119 . Other Writings Affecting Instrument .
Summary
(1) As between the obligor and his immediate obligee or any trans-
feree an instrument may be modified or affected by another written agree-
ment executed as a part of the same transaction , but a holder in due course
