who took without notice is not affected by limitation arising from the sepа-
rate agreement .
(2 ) A separate agreement does not affect the negotiability of an in-
strument .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Accord, Davis v. Logan , 28 Ky . ( 5 J. J. Marsh ) 298 , ( 1831 ) ; Parks
Ex'r v . Cooke , 66 Ky . ( 3 Bush ) 168, ( 1867 ) ; Rommel Bros. v . Clark, 255 Ky .
554, 74 S. W. 2d 933 , ( 1934 ) .
As to rights of holder in due course, accord, Kelly v . Harlan Nat . Bank,
213 Ky . 689 , 281 S. W. 823, ( 1926 ) (purchaser of a negotiable instrument is
unaffected by collateral agreements of which he has had no notice ) ; Crutch-
field v . Robinson , 208 Ky . 354, 270 S. W. 775, ( 1925) ; Gibbs v . Metcalf, 201
Ky . 504 , 256 S. W. 1109 , ( 1923 ) (where bill or note refers to or is accom-
panied by a collateral contemporaneous agreement or the purchaser has
actual knowledge of the same , he takes subject to its contents or conditions ) .
Beattyville Bank v . Roberts , 117 Ky . 689 , 78 S. W. 901 ( 1904 ) .
(2) No Kentucky statutes or decisions have been found which con-
flict with the Code provision that a separate agreement does not affect the
negotiability of an instrument . In order for a separate agreement to affect
negotiability it must be made contemporaneously with the negotiable instru-
ment and be intended to be construed together . See Terrell v . Cheatham ,
200 Ky . 667, 255 S. W. 262 ( 1923 ) ; Fidelity & Columbia Trust Co. v . Schmidt ,
245 Ky. 432, 53 S. W. 2d 713 ( 1932 ) .
Section 3-120 . Instruments " Payable Through " Bank .
Summary
An instrument "payable through " a bank designates that bank as a
collecting bank to make presentment but does not authorize the bank to pay
the instrument .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section states the commercial understanding of a provision " pay-
able" through a named bank which is often used on checks and other nego-
tiable instruments . The bank is not the drawee nor is it ordered or au
139

thorized to pay the instrument . It is merely designated as the collecting
bank through which presentment is properly made to the drawee . See First
National Bank & Trust Co. of Lexington v . First National Bank in Hazard's
Receivers , 260 Ky . 581 , 86 S. W. 2d 325 ( 1935 ) .
Section 3-121 . Instruments Payable at Bank .
Summary
A note or acceptance payable at a bank is equivalent to a draft drawn
on the bank payable when due out of any funds of the maker or acceptor in
current account or otherwise available for such payment .
KRS 356.087 .
Kentucky Statutes
This section adopts the position of Sec. 87 of the NIL which is followed
by New York and surrounding states . Kentucky has adopted Sec . 87 of
NIL . No Kentucky cases appear to be in conflict with this view . Lyons v .
Hager's Adm'r , 278 Ky . 99 , 128 S. W. 2d 196 ( 1939 ) , (where note was made
payable at bank , formal presentment was unnecessary, but notice of dishonor
was required ) .
Section 3-122 . Accrual of Cause of Action .
Summary
( 1) A cause of action accrues against a maker or an acceptor :
on the day after maturity of a time instrument ;(a)
(b) upon its date or if undated , date of issue in case of a demand
instrument .
(2) A cause of action accrues against the obligor of a certificate of
deposit upon demand but a demand cannot be made upon a time certificate
until it matures .
(3) A cause of action accrues against a drawer of a draft or an in-
dorsee of any instrument upon demand following dishonor . Notice of dis-
honor is demand .
(4) Unless otherwise provided , interest at judgment rate runs :
(a)
(b)
in case of a maker of a demand note, from the date of demand ;
in all other cases from the date of accrual of the cause of
action .
Kentucky Statutes
None .
Comment
This is a new section but it follows the general rule that an action may
be brought upon a demand instrument immediately upon issue since no pre
140

sentment is necessary . An exception is made in the case of certificates of
deposit because of their nature . The day after maturity is the accepted date
for time instruments . As to drawers of drafts or indorsees there must be a
demand after dishonor and notice of dishonor is a demand .
Related to the question of accrual of cause of action is the subject of
interest . With the exception of demand notes , the interest runs from the
accrual of the cause of action . On demand notes it runs from the demand .
No Kentucky decisions or statutes were found on these points .
Part 2
TRANSFER AND NEGOTIATION
Introductory Comment
( 1) NIL Topics Covered : Most of Article 3 on Negotiations , KRS
356.030 , 356,034 , 356.035, 356.036 , 356.037, 356.039, 356.040, 356.043 , 356.047 ,
and 356.050 ; KRS 356.022 on effect of indorsement by infant or corporation ,
and parts of KRS 356.058 and 356.059 .
(2) NIL Topics Covered Elsewhere : KRS 356.038 on qualified indorse-
ment is covered in Part 4 on Liability of Parties ; KRS 356.041 , and 356.042
are covered in Part 1 on Form and Interpretation as these sections cover
payees and in part the liability of drawers ; KRS 356.044 is covered in Part 4
on Liability of Parties .
(3) NIL Topics Omitted :
(a) KRS 356.045 and 356.046 . In the absence of evidence, instru-
ments are presumed to be indorsed at the place of dating and
indorsements to have been made before maturity .
(b) KRS 356.047 . An instrument negotiable in its origin continues
negotiable until it has been restrictively indorsed or dis-
charged by payment or otherwise .
(4) New Topics : Provisions governing the rights of a transferee of an
instrument ( UCC 3-201 in part ) ; statement that words of assignment, guar-
anty , condition , waiver , limitation or disclaimer accompanying an indorse-
ment do not affect its sufficiency for transfer ; statement of the right of a
transferee to an unqualified indorsement ; and a statement as to the effect
of an indorsement prohibiting further transfer .
Section 3-201 . Transfer : Right to Indorsement .
Summary
( 1) Transfer of an instrument vests in the transferee such rights as
the transferor has except that a transferee who was a party to any fraud
or illegality affecting the instrument or who as a prior holder had knowl
141

edge of a claim or defense against it cannot improve his position by taking
from a later holder in due course .
(2 ) A transfer of a security interest vests the foregoing rights to the
extent of the interest transferred .
(3 ) Unless otherwise agreed , the transferee of an instrument not then
payable to bearer has an enforceable right to have the unqualified indorse-
ment of his transferor . Negotiation occurs upon such indorsement being
made and until then there is no presumption that the transferee is the
owner .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.027 , 356.049 , 356.058 .
Comment
(1) Accord , KRS 356.049, 356.058 . These rights are transferred even
where no value is given . Lawyers ' Realty Co. v. Bank of Ludlow, 256 Ky.
675, 76 S. W. 2d 920 (1934) .
(2) Accord , KRS 356.027 . Wilkins v . Usher , 123 Ky . 696, 97 S. W. 37
( 1906 ) ; Worden v . Kennedy , 246 Ky . 716 , 56 S. W. 2d 329 ( 1933 ) ; but KRS
356.027 is limited by UCC 3-303 as to a lien acquired by law .
(3 ) Accord , KRS 356.049 . No Kentucky cases were found which de-
termine whether the transferee is entitled to an unqualified indorsement ,
nor when a presumption of ownership arises . Other jurisdictions have held
that unless the parties have agreed to the contrary the transferee is entitled
to an unqualified indorsement . Brannan , Negotiable Instruments , P. 658
(7th ed .) . The cases are in conflict as to whether possession of paper without
indorsement creates a presumption of ownership . Ibid , at 650 .
Section 3-202 . Negotiation .
Summary
(1) Negotiation is the transfer of an instrument in such form that the
transferee becomes a holder . An instrument payable to order is negotiated
by delivery plus necessary indorsement ; one payable to bearer is negotiated
by delivery .
(2) An indorsement must be written by or for the holder and on the
instrument or on a paper so firmly affixed thereto as to become a part
thereof .
(3 ) An indorsement is effective for negotiation only if it conveys the
entire instrument or unpaid residue . If it is less it is a partial assignment .
(4) Words of assignment , condition , waiver , guaranty , limitation or
disclaimer of liability and the like accompanying an indorsement do not
affect its character as an indorsement .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.030 , 356.031 , 356.032 .
142

Comment
(1) Accord, KRS 356.030 . Tany v . Wolfe , 270 Ky. 556, 110 S. W. 2d 277
(1937 ); Foster's Adm'r v . Metcalfe , 144 Ky . 385, 138 S. W. 314 ( 1911 ) ; Com-
merce Union Bank v . Seese , 237 Ky . 384, 35 S. W. 2d 544 ( 1931 ) .
(2) Accord , KRS 356.031, except "by and on behalf of the holder ,"
which is in accord with KRS 356.030 . The new section deletes the phrase
"without additional words " .
( 3 ) KRS 356.032 provides that the indorsement must be to the entire
instrument ; one that purports to transfer a part or to indorsees severally
does not operate as a negotiation . But it may be indorsed as to the residue
where the instrument has been paid in part. In accord , see White v . Sharp ,
275 Ky . 671 , 122 S. W. 2d 474 ( 1938 ) which held that an attempt to transfer
only a part of the unpaid amount of a negotiable note destroys its negotia-
bility , and the transferee is not a holder in due course .
What the effect of the last sentence in subsection (3) will be is dif-
ficult to suggest . At common law the courts attempted to prevent multi-
plicity of suits and refused to permit partial assignments and transfer to
parties severally of an instrument , Martin v . Hayes , 44 N. C. 423 ( 1853 ) . But
there was a view that such partial assignment was not objectionable if the
parties all sued the payor jointly , Flint v . Flint , 6 Allen (Mass .) 34 ( 1863 ) .
Several courts have held that a transfer to several indorsees was an assign-
ment if the parties joined in their action , Goldman v . Blum , 58 Tex . 630
(1883 ) ; King v . King , 73 App . Div . 547, 77 N.Y. Supp . 40 ( 1902 ) ( where
court did not permit assignee of 1/5 part of note to sue ) ; Blake v . Weiden ,
291 Ν . Υ. 134, 51 N.E. 2d 677 ( 1943 ) . At common law the majority rule seems
to be that a partial assignment of the note could not be made . Anno . 63
A.L.R. 499 ( 1929 ) .
(4) Words of assignment , condition , etc.: There is no comparable sec-
tion to subsection (4) in the NIL or the KRS . There is conflict in the cases
of other jurisdictions as to the import of words of assignment or guaranty in
conjunction with the signature of indorser . The majority view appears to
be that such transfer of a negotiable instrument by a holder who signs a
guaranty of payment (or words of assignment) on the back thereof , con-
stitutes a negotiation of the instrument . This is the view of the Code . For
general discussion see Britton , Bills and Notes ( 1943 ) , Secs . 58 and 59 ; 10
C.J.S. , Bills and Notes , Sec. 209.
Section 3-203 . Wrong or Misspelled Name .
Summary
Where the name of the payee is misspelled or is incorrect , he may in-
dorse in that name or his own or both; but the person paying or giving
value may require both .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.043 .
143

Comment
This is a rewording of Sec . 43 of the NIL , KRS 356.043 . No Kentucky
case involving this section could be found . The proposed Code clearly sets
forth the rule that the indorsement by the true owner of the instrument in
a name which varies from that which designates him as payee will pass
such title as he owns . It also adopts the commercially desirable require-
ment that the person buying or paying the instrument may require that both
the name designated as payee an dthe true name be placed by the owner on
the instrument prior to its transfer .
Section 3-204 . Special Indorsement ; Blank Indorsement .
Summary
( 1) A special indorsement specifies the person to whom or to whose
order it makes the instrument payable . A specially indorsed instrument is
payable to the order of the special indorsee whose indorsement is necessary
for further negotiation .
(2) An indorsement in blank specifies no particular indorsee , and may
consist of a mere signature . An order instrument indorsed in blank is pay-
able to bearer and is negotiated by delivery until specially indorsed or in-
dorsed for collection .
(3) The holder may convert a blank indorsement into a special in-
dorsement by writing over the signature of the indorser in blank any con-
tract consistent with the character of the indorsement .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.009 ( 5 ) , 356.033 , 356.034 , 356.035 , 356.036 , 356.037 , 356.040 .
Comment
(1) Accord , KRS 356.034 ; contra KRS 356.040, as to negotiation by
delivery after special indorsement . Code adopts the principle that the
special indorser , as the owner even of a bearer instrument , has the right to
direct the payment and to require the indorsement of his indorsee as evi-
dence of the satisfaction of his own obligation . The special indorsee may of
course make it payable to bearer again by himself indorsing in blank .
(2) Accord , KRS 356.009 ( 5) ; KRS 356.034 .
(3) Accord , KRS 356.035 .
Section 3-205 . Restrictive Indorsement ; Prohibiting Transfer.
Summary
An indorsement is restrictive which either :
(a) is conditional ; or
(b) purports to prohibit further transfer of the instrument ; or
144

(c) includes the words "for collection ," "for deposit," "pay any
bank ," or like terms signifying a purpose of deposit or col-
lection; or
(d) otherwise states that it is for the benefit or use of the indorser
or of another person .
Kentucky Statutes
Accord, KRS 356.036 , 356.037 , 356.039 .
Comment
The purpose of this section is to provide a definition of restrictive in-
dorsements which will include the varieties of indorsement described in NIL
Sec. 36 (KRS 356.036 ) and Sec. 39 (KRS 356.039 ) .
The Commissioners Comment : "Here , however , the separate mention of
conditional indorsements , those prohibiting transfer , indorsements in the
bank deposit of collection process , and other indorsements to a fiduciary ,
permits separate treatment in subsequent sections where policy so requires ."
Section 3-206 . Effect of Restrictive Indorsement .
Summary
(1) No restrictive indorsement prevents further transfer or negotiation
of the instrument .
(2) An intermediary bank , or a payor bank which is not the depository
bank, is neither given notice nor otherwise affected by a restrictive indorse-
ment of any person except the bank's immediate transfer or the person pr-
senting for payment .
(3 ) Except for an intermediary bank , any transferee under an indorse-
ment which is conditional or includes the words "for collection ," "for de-
posit ," "pay any bank , " or like terms (subparagraphs (a) and ( c) of UCC
3-205 ) must pay or apply any value given by him for or on the security of
the instrument consistently with the indorsement and to the extent that he
does so he becomes a holder for value . In addition , such transferee is a
holder in due course if he otherwise complies with the requirements of
UCC 3-302 on what constitutes a holder in due course .
(4) The first taken under an indorsement for the benefit of the in-
dorser or another person (subparagraph (d) of UCC 2-205 ) must pay or
apply any value given by him for or on the security of the instrument con-
sistently with the indorsement and to the extent that he does so he becomes
a holder for value . In addition , such taker is a holder in due course if he
otherwise complies with the requirements of UCC 3-302 on what constitutes
a holder in due course. A later holder for value is neither given notice nor
otherwise affected by such restrictive indorsement unless he has knowledge
that a fiduciary or other person has negotiated the instrument in any trans-
action for his own benefit or otherwise in breach of duty (subsection (2 ) of
UCC 3-304 ) .
145

Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.036 , 356.037 , 356.047 .
Comment
The section eliminates the inference from KRS 356.047 that a restrictive
indorsement destroys negotiability . The section also treats separately paper
indorsed for bank collection , and that indorsed in trust . It also provides that
an indorsement "for deposit ," or the like , has the effect it now has under
KRS 356.037 and KRS 356.047, (with an exception , however , which provides
holder in due course status for an intermediary bank and discharges a payor
bank not a depository bank ) . There is a further purpose-to permit one to
whom an instrument is indorsed in trust to negotiate the instrument , and to
make his transferee a holder in due course . No Kentucky decisions were
found on these points .
Section 3-207 . Negotiation Effective Although It May Be Rescinded .
Summary
( 1) Negotiation is effective although :
(a) made by an infant, a corporation exceeding its powers , or any
other person without capacity ; or
(b) obtained by fraud , duress or mistake ; or
made in breach of duty .
(c) is part of an illegal transaction ; or
(d)
(2) Except as against a subsequent holder in due course such nego-
tiation is in an appropriate case subject to rescission , the declaration of a
constructive trust or any other remedy allowed by law .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.022 , 356.059 .
Comment
(1 ) ( a ) Accord , KRS 356.022, except that the UCC extends the cov-
erage to include negotiation obtained by fraud , duress , mis-
take, illegal transaction , or breach of duty . It also includes
negotiation by insane persons . But one who takes under a
forged instrument is not a holder .
(b) and ( c) Accord , KRS 356.052 and KRS 356.059. Under the
NIL where an indorser obtains the instrument by illegal
means , his title is defective , but it is possible for him to pass
good title to a holder in due course . ( The presumption that
such a transferee was a holder in due course was dropped ,
and the burden is placed on him to prove the elements of
holding in due course , but he will recover if he carries this
burden . ) See such cases as Mackenzie v . Eschmann's Ex'r ,
174 Ky . 450, 192 S. W. 521 ( 1917 ) ; Stevens v . Bailey , 228 Ky.
146

436 , 15 S. W. 2d 263 ( 1929 ) . The Code changes the concept of
the position of the party that obtained the instrument by un-
lawful means to that of a holder , subject to being divested of
the instrument by the rightful party . This does not apply to
lost or stolen instruments in the hands of the finder nor does
it mean that for delivery it is necessary to become a holder .
The presumption that a transferee taking an illegally obtained
instrument is a holder in due course is dropped .
(2) Allows all remedies now afforded to recover an instrument . These
remedies are cut off only by a holder in due course .
Section 3-208 . Reacquisition .
Summary
Where an instrument is returned to or reacquired by a prior party he
may cancel any indorsement not necessary to his title and reissue or fur-
ther negotiate the instrument , but any intervening party is discharged as
against the reacquiring party and subsequent holders not in due course and
if his indorsement has been cancelled is discharged as against subsequent
holders in due course as well .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.048 , 356.050 , 356.121 .
Accord, KRS 356.048 .
Comment
Accord, KRS 356.050, except the words "returned to or reacquired " are
substituted for the words "negotiated back" .
Accord , KRS 356.121 .
Part 3
RIGHTS OF A HOLDER
Introductory Comment
(1 ) NIL Topics Covered . All of Article 4 on Rights of a Holder , KRS
356.051 to 356.059, parts of KRS 356.015 and KRS 356.016 relating to rights
of a holder , and KRS 356.025 to 356.028 as to what is value .
(2) NIL Topics Covered Elsewhere . NIL Sec . 29 on accommodation
parties is covered in UCC 3-415 , and the effect of NIL Secs . 24 and 28, ab-
sence and failure of consideration as a defense , are found in UCC 3-408 .
(3) NIL Topics Omitted . NIL Sec . 26 that where value has at any
time been given for an instrument , the holder is deemed a holder for value ,
is omitted as erroneous . The "shelter provision ," UCC 3-201 , covers one
147

claiming under a holder in due course or a holder for value , and a good faith
donee from a bad faith payor of value should not qualify as a holder in due
course by "tacking " his good faith and the value given by his donor .
(4) New Topics .
(a) A payee may be a holder in due course .
(b) One is not a holder in due course who purchases at judicial
sale or acquires under legal process, or in taking over an
estate or who purchases or otherwise takes in bulk the assets
of a prior holder not in ordinary course of such holder's
business .
(c) Value includes security for an antecedent claim whether or
not due , and the giving up of a negotiable instrument or an
irrevocable commitment to a third person .
(d) A considerable listing of matters which constitute or do not
constitute notice to a purchaser in UCC 3-304 .
(e) Defenses of infancy and any other incapacity rendering the
obligation a nullity are good against a holder in due course .
(f) Defense of a fraud in the factum is good against a holder
in due course .
(g) Discharge in bankruptcy or other insolvency proceedings is
good against a holder in due course, but knowledge of dis-
charge of one party does not preclude a holder from being a
holder in due course as to others .
(h) Signatures are admitted unless denied in the pleadings ; bur-
den is on party claiming under a disputed signature , but he is
aided by a presumption of validity except where action is to
enforce obligation of a purported signer who has died or be-
come incompetent before proof is required .
Section 3-301 . Rights of a Holder .
Summary
The holder of an instrument whether or not he is the owner may trans-
fer or negotiate it and, except as provided in UCC 3-603 , discharge it or en-
force payment in his own name .
KRS 356.051 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Accord , KRS 356.051 . The provision of KRS 356.051 as to discharge by
payment is now covered by UCC 3-603 ( 1 ) . The purpose of this section is to
state in one place all the rights of a holder and to make it clear that all
holders have such rights . The one exception is found in UCC 3-603 on pay-
ment and satisfaction .
There is no question but that the holder of a negotiable instrument prop-
erly indorsed may sue thereon in his own name . Doherty v . Louisville First
148

National Bank, 170 Ky . 810, 186 S. W. 937 ( 1916 ) ; but compare Cumberland
Bank & Trust Co. v . Buchanan , 291 Ky . 300, 164 S. W. 2d 473 (1942 ) , wherein
it was held that "mere possession of a negotiable instrument payable to a
named payee does not constitute the person having such possession a
"holder " within the meaning of this section (KRS 356.051 ) ; that in order to
maintain suit the person having possession must prove that he is the payee
or the transferee by endorsement ."
Section 3-302 . Holder in Due Course .
Summary
(1) A holder in due course is a holder who takes the instrument :
(a) for value ; and
(b) in good faith ; and
(c) without notice that it is overdue or has been dishonored or
of any defense against or claim to it on the part of any person .
(2) A payee may be a holder in due course .
(3) A holder does not become a holder in due course :
(a) by purchase at juidicial sale or by taking under legal process ;
or
(b) by acquiring it in taking over an estate ; or
(c) by purchasing it as part of a bulk transaction not in regular
course of business of the transferor .
(4) A purchaser of a limited interest can be a holder in due course
only to the extent of the interest purchased .
KRS 356.052 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) (a) Accord , KRS 356.052 ( 3 ) .
(b) Accord, KRS 356.052 ( 3 ) .
(c) Compare KRS 356.052, conditions 1, 2 and 4. Under present
law, the instrument must be in fact not overdue for a person
taking it to be a holder in due course . Kentucky cases do not
discuss the point , but the language of the cases speaks strongly
of taking before maturity . See Hughes v . West, 217 Ky . 40 ,
288 S. W. 1011 , ( 1926 ) ; Paintsville National Bank v . Robinson ,
220 Ky . 418, 295 S. W. 412, ( 1927 ) . Under the proposed law
he will be a holder in due course if he took without notice
that it was overdue even if it was so in fact .
The provisions as to previous dishonor and infirmity are in accord
with KRS 356.052 ( 2 ) and (4) . As to "claim against it " see UCC
3-304 and annotations .
(2) A long line of Kentucky decisions have held that a payee is not
a holder in due course . Southern National Life Realty Corp. v . Peoples '
149

Bank , 178 Ky . 80 , 198 S. W. 543 ( 1917 ) ; Fidelity & Columbia Trust Co. v .
Nordeman , 266 Ку . 106 , 98 S. W. 2d 47 ( 1936) ; but in Thomson v. Peck, 217
Ky . 766, 290 S. W. 722 ( 1927 ) , a contra result was reached on grounds of
estoppel , with the court ignoring the problem directly . See also Rider v .
Roberts , 255 Ky . 266, 73 S. W. 2d 17 ( 1934 ) ; 261 Ky . 317, 87 S. W. 2d 611
(1935 ) . Therefore , the Code provision would change the existing Kentucky
law.
(3) ( a )
(b)
Accord , 10 C.J.S. , Bills and Notes , Sec. 307 .
Although no Kentucky cases in point have been found , other
jurisdictions have held that a receiver is not a holder in due
course . See Young v . Victory , 112 Fla . 66 , 150 So. 624 ( 1933 ) ;
Starley v . Dessert Foods , 93 Utah 577, 74 P. 2d 1221 ( 1938 ) .
Also in accord , 10 C.J.S. , Bills and Notes , Sec. 307; 8 Am . Jur.,
Bills and Notes , Sec. 371 .
(c) Accord , as to transfers between banks : 8 Am . Jur., Bills and
Notes , Sec . 372. This entire subsection deals with special cases
of the common law requirement that the transfer take place
"in the usual course of business." This is not an express re-
quirement of the NIL , but has been held to be implicit in
various other requirements . 8 Am. Jur . , Bills and Notes , Sec .
367 ; 10 C.J.S. , Bills and Notes , Sec. 306 .
(4) Accord , KRS 356.027 as to security interests . This follows the
general rule of protecting a pledgee only to the extent of his pledge . It is in
accord with the Kentucky law .
Section 3-303 . Taking for Value .
Summary
A holder takes the instrument for value :
(a) To the extent that the agreed consideration has been performed
or that he acquires a security interest or lien otherwise than by legal
process ; or
(b) When he takes in payment of or as security for an antecedent
claim against any person whether due or not ; or
(c) When he gives a negotiable instrument or makes an irrevocable
commitment to a third person .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.025 . 356.026 , 356.027 , 356.054 .
Comment
(a) As to security interests , Accord , KRS 356.027 . As to payment of
consideration : Contra , KRS 356.025 , which makes any consideration suf-
ficient to support a simple contract "value " . This should include an execu-
tory promise . But compare KRS 356.054 whereby a holder who has not
fully paid the agreed amount is given the rights of a holder in due course
only to the extent of the amount actually paid .
150

(b) "As payment ." Accord , KRS 356.025, Wilkins v . Usher , 123 Ky .
696 , 97 S. W. 37 ( 1906 ) . "As security ." Accord , KRS 356.027 . A long line
of Kentucky decisions hold that where a creditor takes an instrument as
collateral for a debt he takes as a holder for value to the amount of the
indebtedness . Citizens ' Bank v . Bank of Waddy , 126 Ky . 169 , 103 S. W. 249
(1907 ) ; Worden v . Kennedy , 246 Ky . 716, 56 S. W. 2d 329 ( 1933 ) .
(c) Paragraph ( c) is new , but states generally recognized exceptions
to the rule that an executory promise is not value . A negotiable instrument
is value because it carries the possibility of negotiation to a holder in due
course , after which the party who gives it cannot refuse to pay . Greenwade
v. First National Bank, 240 Ky . 60 , 41 S. W. 2d 369 ( 1931 ) . The same rea-
soning as above applies to any irrevocable commitment to a third person,
such as a letter of credit issued when an instrument is taken . However , no
Kentucky cases were found on this point .
Section 3-304 . Notice to Purchaser .
Summary
(1) The purchaser has notice of a claim or defense if:
(a) the instrument is so incomplete , bears visible evidence of
forgery or alteration or is otherwise so irregular as to call
into question its validity, terms or ownership or create an
ambiguity as to the party to pay ; or
(b) he has notice that it is voidable in whole or part as to any
party or that all parties have been discharged .
(2) The purchaser has notice of a claim when he has knowledge that
a fiduciary has negotiated it in payment of or as security for his own debt
or in any transaction for his own benefit or otherwise in breach of duty .
(3) The purchaser has notice that an instrument is overdue if he has
reason to know that :
(a) any of the principal is overdue or that there is an uncured
default in payment of another instrument of the same series ;
(b)
(c)
or
acceleration has been made; or
he is taking a demand instrument after demand or more than
a reasonable time after its issue . For a check drawn and pay-
able within the United States and its territories a reasonable
time is presumed to be thirty days .
(4) Knowledge of the following is not notice :
(a) that the instrument is antedated or postdated ;
(b) that is was issued or negotiated for an executory promise or
accompanied by a separate agreement unless he has notice of
a defense or claim arising therefrom ;
(c) that any party has signed for accommodation ;
(d) that an incomplete instrument has been completed unless he
has notice of improper completion ;
151

(e) except as otherwise provided with respect to restrictive in-
dorsements that any person negotiating the instrument is or
was a fiduciary ;
(f) that there has been a default in interest payment or payment
of any other instrument except one in the same series.
(5 ) The filing or recording of a document is not notice to any one
who would otherwise be a holder in due course .
(6) Notice must be received so as to give a reasonable opportunity to
act upon it .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.045 , 356.052 , 356.053 , 356.055 , 356.056 .
Comment
(1) ( a) Accord, KRS 356.052 ( 1) . The present wording is in terms
stricter than the new , but the cases appear to be in accord
with the Code in requiring that the irregularity be one which
indicates that something is wrong . Karsner v . Cooper , 195
Ky . 8 , 241 S. W. 346 ( 1922 ) ; Citizens ' State Bank v . Johnson
County , 182 Ky. 531 , 207 S. W. 8 ( 1918 ) ; Pensacola State Bank
v . Melton , 210 F. 57 ( W.D. Ky . 1913 ) . See also , 10 C.J.S. ,
Bills and Notes , Sec . 328 ( a ) .
(b) This subsection appears to include notice of such defenses de-
nominated "defective title " under KRS 356.055 , which are
now called " infirmities ." Since defenses which render the
instrument a nullity are good even against a holder in due
course , UCC 3-305 , there was no need to include defenses
other than those rendering the obligation voidable .
(2 ) Compare KRS 356.056. Accord , Kenyon Realty Co. v . National De-
posit Bank , 140 Ky . 133 , 130 S. W. 965 ( 1910 ) ; Dowell v . Bauman , 189 Ky.
136 , 224 S. W .. 641 ( 1920 ) ; Armour v . Lynch Bank , 207 Ky . 203, 268 S. W.
1091 ( 1925 ) .
(3 ) This subsection setting for the criteria for being overdue is
stated in terms of notice , whereas under present law a holder evidently
cannot be in due course if the instrument is in fact overdue . KRS 356.052
(2) . But insofar as these situations constitute dishonor rather than being
overdue , there must be notice under present law to prevent holder from
being in due course . No Kentucky cases have been found on the point of
acceleration constituting notice that an instrument is overdue . Under present
law, if the instrument is negotiated an unreasonable length of time after
issue , the holder is not in due course . KRS 356.053. The only Kentucky
decisions passing on the question of length of time which would be con-
sidered as "reasonable" were Asbury v . Taube , 151 Ку . 142, 151 S. W. 372
(1912) which held that two days was not an unreasonable time for negotia-
tion of a check after its issue , and Fayette Nat. Bank v . Meyers , 211 Ky . 185 ,
277 S. W. 292, ( 1925 ) holding that a nine months ' delay was unreasonable .
There appears to be no conflict insofar as the 30- day presumption set forth
in the Code .
152

(4) (a) Accord , 10 C.J.S. , Bills and Notes , Sec . 336. Compare KRS
356.012. No Kentucky cases found on this point .
(b) Compare , Roberts v . Drover's Nat . Bank , 199 Ky . 439, 251 S. W.
198 , ( 1923 ) where a notation on the face of a check that it
was for three sound mules did not give the bank which cashed
the check notice that it was given in a conditional sale con-
tract . See also, Grimes v . Langevin , 282 Ky . 302, 138 S. W.
2d 353, ( 1940) .
(c) Accord, KRS 356.029.
(d) Accord, KRS 356.013 .
(e) No Kentucky decisions found directly in point . However , it
appears that Kentucky law would be in accord with this sec-
tion . Cases seem to require that holder must have actual
notice of breach of fiduciary duty or should have made inquiry
under the circumstances to ascertain that there was a breach
by the fiduciary . See generally Britton, Bills and Notes , Sec.
115-116 . McDowell v . Bauman , 189 Ky . 136 , 224 S. W. 641
(1920) ; Hardwick v . Cotterill, 221 Ky . 783 , 299 S. W. 958,
(1927 ) .
(f) No Kentucky cases were found on whether knowledge of de-
fault in interest is sufficient to prevent a transferee from being
a holder in due course . Other jurisdictions are split, with New
York holding that it does and Massachusetts that it does not .
See Newell v . Gregg , 51 N.Y. ( Barb ) 263 ( 1868 ) ; National
Bank v . Kirby , 108 Mass . 497 ( 1871 ) . See also , City of New
Port Richey v . Fidelity & Deposit Co. of Maryland , 105 F. 2d
348 (5th Cir. 1939 ) , which held that the nonpayment of in-
terest, though not in law notice of dishonor of negotiable in-
strument , is a fact to be considered with other circumstances
on the question of bona fides of the taking .
(5) No Kentucky cases were found deciding whether recording is such
constructive notice as to prevent a transferee from being a holder in due
course , and cases in general litigating the point are rare . Accord , Foster v .
Augustanna College , 92 Okla. 96 218 Pac . 335 ( 1923 ) . Contra , Murphy v .
Barnard , 162 Mass . 72 ( 1894 ) . Compare , Citizen's State Bank v . Johnson
County , 182 Ky . 531 , 207 S. W. 8 ( 1918 ) .
(6) Accord, Gibson v. First National Bank , 196 Ky . 119, 244 S. W. 290
(1922 ) ; Worden v . Kennedy , 246 Ky . 716, 56 S. W. 2d 329 (1933) , where no-
tice of defense given after holder had taken the instrument was held not
sufficient to prevent him from being holder in due course . No cases found
deciding on how much time is reasonable . Code comments brings out that
notice must be received with a sufficient margi nof time to afford a rea-
sonable opportunity to act on it, and that a notice received by the president
of a bank one minute before the bank's teller cashes a check is not effective
to prevent the bank from becoming a holder in due course .
Section 3-305 . Rights of a Holder in Due Course .
153

Summary
A holder in due course takes free from :
(1) All claims by any person ; and
(2) All defenses of any party with whom he has not dealt except :
(a) infancy , to the extent it is a defense to a simple contract , and
(b) such other incapacity , duress or illegality as renders the obli-
gation a nullity ; and
(c) such misrepresentation as has induced the party to sign with-
out knowledge or opportunity to learn of its character or
essential terms ; and
(d) discharge in insolvency ; and
( e) any other discharge of which he has notice when he takes .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.015 , 356.016 , 356.057 .
Comment
(1) Preamble and subsection ( 1) are in accord with KRS 356.057 , ex-
cept that "takes" is substituted for "holds " to make clear that defenses may
arise against the holder after transfer , and "claims to it on the part of any
person" is substituted for " defect of title of prior parties ," to make explicit
that a holder in due course is protected against set - offs , garnishments , etc.
This includes any claim for rescission of a prior negotiation , in accordance
with the provisions of the section on reacquisition . ( UCC 3-208 ) .
(2 ) Accord , KRS 356.057. As to undelivered instruments , compare
KRS 356.015 and 356.016 . The rule of KRS 356.015 is reversed by this sec-
tion . KRS 356.016 achieves the same result as this section in a different
fashion . Compare UCC 3-306 and 3-307 .
(a) This paragraph is new . It follows the decisions under KRS
356.057 in providing that the defense of infancy may be as-
serted against a holder in due course , even though its effect is
to render the instrument voidable but not void . Universal
Credit Co. v . Hibbard , 273 Ky . 597, 117 S. W. 2d 583 ( 1928 ) ;
Harris v . Shaffer , 197 Ky . 54, 246 S. W. 26 ( 1922 ) ; 10 C.J.S. ,
Bills and Notes , Sec. 506 ( c ) . This applies only to defenses .
The right of rescission of a transfer of an instrument is cut
off . See UCC 3-207 .
(b) Paragraph (b) is also new . It covers mental incompetence,
guardianship , ultra vires acts or lack of corporate capacity to
do business , any remaining incapacity of married women , or
any other incapacity apart from infancy . In accord , Clark
County National Bank v . Allen , 262 Ky . 236 90 S. W. 2d 17
(1935) ( married woman as co - surety ) ; Alexander & Co. v .
Hazelrigg , 123 Ky . 677, 97 S. W. 353 , ( 1906 ) (note given to
satisfy gambling debt is void even in the hands of a holder in
due course ) ; Whitaker et al. v . Smith , 255 Ky . 339, 72 S. W.
1105 ( 1934 ) (Usury : the instrument is valid but the usurious
interest is not due) . Compare , Rath's Committee v . Smith , 180
154

Ky . 326 , 202 S. W. 501 (1918 ) holding that a lunatic's com-
mittee was liable to a holder in due course on a note made
by the lunatic for adequate consideration which could not be
restored , when the holder was ignorant of the lunacy , if it
existed , at the date of execution . See also McVeagh v . Hicks ,
211 Ky . 284, 277 S. W. 280 ( 1925 ) concerning effect of lunacy ;
Davidson v . Falls, 215 Ky . 368 , 285 S. W. 209 ( 1926 ) (that notes
sued on were given for stock sold in violation of Blue Sky
Law , while a good defense against original holder , is not a
good defense against a holder in due course) . First National
Bank v . Utterback , 177 Ky . 76, 197 S. W. 534 ( 1917 ) ( failure to
comply with foreign corporation laws not good defense against
holder in due course ) ; First National Bank v . Combs , 237 Ky .
834 , 36 S. W. 2d 644 (1931 ) ( note given to corrupt election
valid in hands of holder in due course) .
(c) There appear to be no Kentucky cases , but a majority of other
jurisdictions follow this rule . 10 C.J.S. , Bills and Notes , Sec.
499 (b) .
(d) Sec. 14 of Bankruptcy Act , 11 U.S.C. Sec. 14 ( 1946) .
(e) Under the UCC , a holder in due course is not bound by any
discharge of which he has no notice ( UCC 3-602 ) . Under UCC
3-304 ( 1 ) ( b) , notice of discharge of less than all parties does
not prevent a person's becoming a holder in due course . There
appears to be nothing to indicate the position of the present
law in any of these respects , except with regard to a special
form of discharge by "renunciation ," which is not effective
against a subsequent holder in due course without notice .
KRS 356.122 .
Section 3-306. Rights of One Not Holder in Due Course .
Summary
A holder not in due course takes subject to :
(1) All valid claims of any person ; and
(2) All defenses available in an action on a simple contract ; and
(3) The defenses of want or failure of consideration , non -performance
of a condition precedent , non -delivery or delivery for a special purpose
( UCC 3-408 ) ; and
(4) the defense that he or a person through whom he holds the in-
strument acquired it by theft , or that payment or satisfaction to such holder
would be inconsistent with the terms of a restrictive indorsement . The claim
of any third person to the instrument is not otherwise available as a defense
to any party liable thereon unless the third person himself defends the
action for such party .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.016 , 356.028 , 356.058 , 356.059 .
155

Comment
(1) Accord , KRS 356.058, Croach v . United Mercantile Agencies , 299
Ky . 208 , 184 S. W. 2d 574 ( 1945 ) .
(2) Accord, KRS 356.058, Gannon v . Bronston , 246 Ку . 612 , 55 S. W.
2d 358 ( 1933 ) .
(3 ) Accord , KRS 356.028, 356.016. First State Bank v . Morton , 146 Ky .
287 , 142 S. W. 694 ( 1912 ) ; Gibbs v . Metcalf , 201 Ky . 504, 256 S. W. 1109
(1923 ) ; J. I. Case Threshing Mch . Co. v . Barnes , 133 Ky . 321 , 117 S. W. 418
( 1909 ) ; Sparr v. Fulton National Bank, 179 Ky . 755, 201 S. W. 310 ( 1918 ) ;
Owsley County Deposit Bank v. Burns , 196 Ky . 359 , 244 S. W. 755 ( 1922 ) .
(4) Subsection (4) is substituted for the last sentence of KRS 356.059 .
Since the obligation of the obligor is to pay the holder , the claims of others
are not his concern . Often he is not in a poistion to prove or disprove these
claims . The only exception made is the one in case of theft or where pay-
ment would be inconsistent with a restrictive indorsement . Nothing pre-
vents the claimant from intervening and asserting his claim .
Section 3-307 . Burden of Establishing Signatures , Defenses and
Due Course .
Summary
( 1) Unless specifically denied in the pleadings each signature is ad-
mitted . When a signature is put in issue :
(a) the burden of establishing it is on the party claiming under
it; but
(b) the signature is presumed genuine or authorized except where
the signer is dead or incompetent .
(2) When signatures are admitted or established , the holder may
recover upon production of the instrument unless a defense is established.
(3) After it is shown that a defense exists , one claiming rights as
holder in due course has burden of establishing it .
KRS 356.059 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Subsection ( 1) is new but is in accord with Kentucky Civil Rules 8.02
and 8.04 . Its purpose is to require a specific denial of the signature in order
to give plaintiff notice of the defense interposed . The form of the denial will
depend upon the local rule , probably in Kentucky it would be in the answer .
While the burden of establishing any signature is on the person claiming
under it, he is aided by the presumption of genuineness except in the cases
stated . See Menton v . Pensacola Bank and Trust Co., 190 F. 126 (6th Cir .
1911) .
Subsection (2) is substituted for the first clause of KRS 356.059 . Once
a signature is proved or admitted , the holder makes his case by production
of the instrument unless other evidence is in .
156

Subsection (3) is a rephrasing of the second clause of KRS 356.059 as
the need of proving holder in due course status does not arise unless some
defense is introduced .
Part 4
LIABILITY OF PARTIES
Introductory Comment
( 1) NIL Topics Covered : All of NIL Article 4, which includes KRS
356.060 to 356.069 , on Liability of Parties ; KRS 356.127 , 356.128 , 356.132 ,
356.133 , 356.136 to 356.142 on the definition and operation of an acceptance ;
KRS 356.187 and 356.188 on certifications of checks , KRS 356.189 on check
not being an assignment , KRS 356.009 ( 3 ) on fictitious payees ; KRS
356.017 ( b ) , 356.018 to 356.021 and KRS 356.044 on effect of signature by
representatives , in trade names, etc.; KRS 356.029 on accommodation parties ;
KRS 356.024 and 356.028 on consideration and the necessity therefore for
liability ; and KRS 356.023, 356.124 and 356.125 on forging and alteration .
(2) NIL Topics Covered Elsewhere : None of the material found under
this heading in the NIL is covered elsewhere in the Code .
(3) NIL Topics Omitted :
( a ) KRS 356.134 and 356.135 on acceptance by separate instru-
ment and promise to accept bill before it is drawn because
more properly covered in Article on Letters of Credit .
(b) All of NIL Title 2 , Article 5, (KRS 356.161 to 356.170 ) on
acceptance for honor ; and Article 6 (KRS 356.171 to 356.183)
on payment for honor , as obsolete .
(4) New Topics :
(a) Liability in cases of impersonation and extending the fictitious
payee rule ( UCC 3-405 ) .
(b) Provisions , UCC 3-404 (2 ) , as to ratification of forged or un-
authorized signatures .
(c ) UCC 3-406 governing negligent contribution to alteration or
forgery .
( d) UCC 3-417 setting forth warranties of a person obtaining
payment or acceptance .
( e ) UCC 3-416 on the effect of words of guaranty added to a
signature , making a distinction between "payment guaran-
teed," (no legal proceedings against maker necessary ) and
"collection guaranteed" (where legal proceedings against
maker are necessary ) . No notice of dishonor needed .
( f) UCC 3-419 relieving innocent agents or brokers from liability
for conversion where principal is not the owner .
Section 3-401 . Signature .
157

Summary
( 1 ) No person is liable unless his signature is on the instrument .
(2) A signature is made by use of any name, including a trade or
assumed name, or by any word or mark used in lieu of a written signature.
KRS 356.018 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Accord, KRS 356.018 . KRS 356.156 and 356.157 , relating to col-
lateral and virtual acceptances , are not included in the Code .
(2) Accord, KRS 356.018 . Fried v . Holloran , 272 Ky . 323 , 114 S. W.
2d 121 , ( 1938 ) ; National Deposit Bank v . Ohio Oil Co., 250 Ky . 288, 62 S. W.
2d 1048 ( 1933 ) . The reference in subsection (2 ) as to what will be held
to constitute a " signature " under the Code is new but follows the generally
accepted view . See Britton, Bills and Notes , Sec. 8 .
Section 3-402 . Signature in Ambiguous Capacity .
Summary
Unless the instrument clearly indicates that a signature is made in
some other capacity it is an indorsement .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.017 (6 ) , 356.063 .
Comment
Accord , KRS 356.017 ( 6 ) and KRS 356.063 . Williams v . Miller , 258 Ky .
210 , 79 S. W. 2d 707 ( 1935 ) ; Hayland v . National Bank , 137 Ky . 682, 126 S.
W. 356 (1910 ) .
Section 3-403 . Signature by Authorized Representative .
Summary
(1) A signature may be made by a representative whose authority
may be established as in other cases . No particular form of appointment
is necessary .
(2) An authorized representative who signs his own name to an
instrument :
(a) is personally obligated if the instrument neither names the
person represented nor shows that the representative signed
in a representative capacity ;
(b ) except as otherwise established between the immediate par-
ties , is personally obligated if the instrument names the
person represented but does not show that the representative
signed in a representative capacity ;
158

(3) Except as otherwise established the name of an organization
preceded or followed by the name and office of an authorized individual is
a signature made in a representative capacity , or if the instrument does
not name the person represented but does show that the representative
signed in a representative capacity .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.019 , 356.020 and 356.021 .
Comment
(1) The first sentence of subsection ( 1) is in accord with KRS 356.019
which states that the signature of any party may be made by an agent.
However , Kentucky in adopting the NIL modified Sec. 19 and required
that the authority of the representative must be expressed in a written
instrument . Therefore the second sentence of subsection ( 1) would be in
conflict with KRS 356.019 insofar as the requirement for written authori-
zation before an agent may sign on behalf of his principal .
(2) Accord, KRS 356.020 . However , the NIL makes no provision for
cases where the capacity in which a party signed is ambiguous . It has
been held that where the litigation is between the original parties parol
evidence is admissible to resolve it. See Riordan & Co. v . Thornsbury , 178
Ky . 324 , 198 S. W. 920 ( 1917 ) .
KRS 356.021 on signature by " procuration " is omitted . It is doubtful
whether the omission of KRS 356.021 will change the law . However , as
indicated in Comment 4 to this section of the Code , "per procuration " is
often understood to be the equivalent of "by ." See Clinton v . Hibbs , 202 Ky.
304 , 259 S. W. 356 ( 1924 ) .
Section 3-404 . Unauthorized Signatures .
Summary
( 1) An unauthorized signature is wholly inoperative as that of the
person whose name is signed unless he ratifies it or is precluded from
denying it; but it operates as the signature of the signer in favor of any
person who in good faith pays or takes for value .
(2) An unauthorized signature may be ratified but this does not
affect any rights of the person ratifying against the signer .
KRS 356.023 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
An "unauthorized signature " is defined in UCC 1-201 ( 43 ) as a signa-
ture made without actual , implied or apparent authority and includes
forgery .
(1) The first phrase is in accord with KRS 356.023 except for " unless
he ratifies " which is discussed in subsection ( 2 ) .
159

The second phrase " or is otherwise precluded " continues the present
law as to estoppel, laches and other grounds which prevent a person whose
signature is forged from recovering . The same word "precluded " is used
in KRS 356.023 . See Baskett v . Ohio Valley Banking & Trust Co. , 214 Ky .
41 , 281 S. W. 1022 ( 1926 ) ; Day & Night National Bank v . Polley , 257 Ky . 36 ,
77 S. W. 2d 351 (1934) .
(2) Accord , Embry v . Long , 256 Ky . 266, 75 S. W. 2d 1036 , ( 1934 ) ;
Union Central Life Ins . Co. v . Johnson's Adm'x, 25 K. L. R. 682 , 76 S. W.
335 ( 1903 ) .
Section 3-405 . Impostors ; Signature in Name of Payee .
Summary
(1) An indorsement in the payee's name is effective if:
(a) an impostor by use of the mails or otherwise has induced the
issuance to him or his confederate in the name of the payee ;
(b)
or
a person signing as or for a maker or drawer intends the
payee to have no interest ; or
(c) the drawer's or maker's agent has given him the payee's
name intending the payee to have no such interest .
(2) Nothing in this section shall affect the criminal or civil liability
of the person so indorsing .
KRS 356.009 (3 ) .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Subsection ( 1 ) ( a) is new . It rejects the attempted distinction between
imposture by mail and face to face imposture . The distinction resulted in
the loss falling often on a later holder whereas it should fall on the drawer
who thought the impostor and the named payee were one and the same .
Subsection ( 1) ( b ) restates the substance of KRS 356.009 (3) but
limits it to drafts . The test is not the "fictitiousness " of the payee but
whether it is intended that he have any part of the proceeds.
Subsection ( 1) ( c ) is new and extends the rule of KRS 356.009 (3 ) to
cover the "padded payroll " cases .
See generally Mueller & Martin v . Liberty Insurance Bank, 187 Ку . 44,
218 S. W. 465 ( 1920 ) ; Com . v . Farmers ' Deposit Bank , 264 Ky . 839, 95 S. W.
2d 793 ( 1936 ) . The entire section has enlarged KRS 356.009 ( 3 ) . The words
"fictitious or nonexisting person " have been eliminated as misleading , since
the existence or nonexistence of the named payee is not decisive and is
important only as it may bear on the intent that he shall have no interest
in the instrument . The instrument is not made payable to bearer and
indorsements are still necessary to negotiation . The section however recog-
nizes as effective indorsement of the types of paper covered no matter by
whom made .
160

Regarding the changes effected under this Code provision the Com-
missioners Comment states :
"To recapitulate : the instrument does not become bearer paper ;
a purportedly regular chain in indorsements is required , but any
person first thief, second impostor or third murderer can
effectively . indorse in the name of the payee ."
-
Section 3-406 . Negligence Contributing to Alteration or
Unauthorized Signature .
Summary
Any person whose negligence substantially contributes to a material
alteration or to the making of an unauthorized signature is precluded from
asserting these defenses against a holder in due course or any person who
pays the instrument in good faith .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Accord, Citizens' Union National Bank v . Terrell, 244 Ky . 16, 50 S. W.
2d 60 (1932 ) ; Baskett v . Ohio Valley Banking & Trust Co., 214 Ky . 41 , 281
S. W. 1022 ( 1925) ; United States v. Citizens ' National Bank , 40 F. S. 609
(W. D. Ky . 1941 ) .
Section 3-407 . Alteration .
Summary
(1) A material alteration is one which changes the contract of any
party in any respect including any change in:
(a) the number or relations of the parties ; or
(b) an incomplete instrument by unauthorized completion ; or
(c) the writing as signed by addition or removal .
(2) As against any person other than a subsequent holder in due
course:
(a) a fraudulent and material alteration by the holder discharges
any party whose contract is thereby changed unless he assents
or is estopped ;
(b) no other alteration discharges any party and the instrument
is enforceable according to its original tenor , or to the
authority given to complete it.
(3) A subsequent holder in due course may enforce an instrument
according to its original tenor or as completed .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.014 , 356.015 , 356.124 , 356.125 .
161

Comment
( 1) Accord, KRS 356.125 . Commercial Bank v . Arden & Fraley , 177
Ky . 520 , 197 S. W. 951 ( 1917) ( changing principal sum payable ) ; Bernhard
v . Commercial Security Co. , 185 Ky . 357, 215 S. W. 84 ( 1919) (changing
principal sum payable ) ; Jones v . Jones , 254 Ky . 475, 71 S. W. 2d 999 ( 1934 )
(changing interest payable ) ; Kimberley v. Penix, 230 Ky . 91, 18 S. W. 858
(1929 ) ( changing time of payment ) ; Mitchell v . Reed's Ex'r , 23 K. L. R. 683,
106 S. W. 833 , (changing place of payment from one state to another ) ; Tyler
v . First Nat. Bank , 211 Ky . 126 , 277 S. W. 310 ( 1925 ) ( addition of "Pt." for
"president ," to payee's name, was a material alteration ) .
(2) ( a) Accord , KRS 356.124 , except that alteration must be by the
holder , the alteration must be for a fraudulent purpose , the
defense must be raised by the party whose contract is
changed by the alteration , and if the above are not appli-
cable the instrument may be enforced according to
its
original tenor
. The need that the alteration
isdone fraudu-
lently
isnew and
achange from the majority view
,
2Am
.
Jur
., Alteration of Instruments
, Sec
. 16
; but compare
Brannan
, Negotiable Instrument Law Annotated
(7th ed
.)
1198
.
(b) Accord
, KRS 356.124 by implication and KRS 356.014 with
regard
toincomplete instruments
. All Kentucky cases dis-
cussing whether alterations are
"material
" are in accord
,
by implication
. Also see UCC 3-116
as
toincomplete instru-
ment
.
(3) Accord
, KRS 356.014 and 356.124
. Baskett
v. Ohio Valley Banking
& Trust Co.
, 214 Ky
. 41
, 281
S.W. 1022
(1925
); Melton
v. Pensacola Bank
& Trust Co.
, 190 Fed
. 126
(6th Cir
. 1911
). The separate rule
inKRS 356.015
for an instrument that has not been delivered
isreversed
.
Section 3-408
. Consideration
.
Summary
Want or failure of consideration
is
adefense against any person not
having the rights of
aholder
indue course
, except that no consideration
isnecessary for an instrument given in payment of or as security for an
antecedent obligation
. Nothing in this section shall be taken
todisplace
any statute outside this act under which
apromise
isenforceable notwith-
standing lack of failure of consideration
. Partial failure of consideration
is
adefense pro tanto whether or not the failure
isin an ascertained or
liquidated amount
.
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.024
, 356.025
, 356.028
.
Comment
Accord
, KRS 356.028
. However
, the wording
ischanged in order
to
cover persons who are within the
"shelter provision
." See UCC 3-201 and
KRS 356.058
.
162

The rule that want or failure of consideration is a defense only against
one not having the rights of a holder in due course is in accord with
Berryman v . Harris , 196 Ky . 21 , 244 S. W. 68 ( 1922 ) ; Owsley County De-
posit Bank v . Burns , 196 Ky . 359 , 244 S. W. 755 ( 1922 ) ; City of Erlanger v .
Berkemeyer , et al ., 207 F. 2d 832 ( 6th Cir . 1953 ) .
The " except" clause is in accord with Wilkins v . Usher , 123 Ky . 696, 97
S. W. 37 , ( 1906 ) ; Lovelace v . Lovelace , 136 Ky . 452, 124 S. W. 400 ( 1910 ) .
Section 3-409 . Draft Not an Assignment .
Summary
(1) A check or draft is not an assignment of any funds in the hands
of the drawer , and the drawee is not liable until he accepts .
(2) Nothing in this section shall affect any liability arising from
any letter of credit or other obligation which is not an acceptance .
KRS 356.127 , 356.189 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
KRS 356.127 and 356.189 are combined and reworded in this section,
and the rule of the sections retained without change . Accord , Boswell v .
Citizens' Savings Bank , 123 Ky . 485 , 96 S. W. 797 ( 1906 ) ; Ewing v . Citizens '
National Bank, 162 Ky . 551 , 172 S. W. 955 ( 1915 ) ; Kentucky Title Savings
Bank & Trust Co. v . Dunavan , 205 Ky. 801 , 266 S. W. 667 ( 1924 ) .
The language of KRS 356.189 that " the bank is not liable to the holder "
is eliminated as untrue . The drawee is not liable on the instrument until
he accepts but he remains liable to the holder for any other liability . See
UCC 4-302 .
Subsection ( 2) is new and is intended to make it clear that this section
does not affect any other liability that may arise apart from the instrument .
Section 3-410 . Definition and Operation of Acceptance .
Summary
(1) Acceptance is the drawee's signed engagement to honor the draft
as presented . It must be written on the draft , and may be his signature only .
It is operative when completed by delivery or notification .
(2 ) A draft may be accepted although it is unsigned or incomplete ,
or is overdue or dishonored .
(3 ) Where an acceptance of a draft payable at a fixed period after
sight is undated , the holder may supply a date in good faith .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.132 to 356.138 , 356.161 to 356.170 , 356.190 .
163

Comment
Subsection ( 1) by requiring the acceptance to be written upon the
draft eliminates KRS 356.134 and 356.135 providing for collateral accept-
ance by separate writing and virtual acceptance by written promise . The
effect of this requirement is to overturn cases holding that an acceptance
can be made by a separate paper . See Selma Savings Bank v . Webster
County Bank, 182 Ky . 604, 206 S. W. 870 ( 1918 ) ; Roberts v . Drovers' Na-
tional Bank , 199 Ky . 439, 251 S. W. 198 ( 1923 ) .
Even under the Negotiable Instruments Law , the holder could require
that the acceptance be written on the bill , KRS 356.133 . As to the time an
acceptance becomes operative this subsection accords with the definition
of acceptance in KRS 356.190. Most of the value of the concept of "extrinsic "
acceptance is preserved in the article on Documentary Letters of Credit ,
Article 5.
(2) Accord , KRS 356.138 .
(3) This is in accord with UCC 3-116 and KRS 356.014 on incomplete
instruments .
KRS 356.161 to 356.170 providing for acceptance for honor are omitted
as obsolete. The practice arose because of slow communications and has
not been used for a long time . No Kentucky decisions were found on the
subject .
Section 3-411 . Certification of a Check .
Summary
( 1) Certification of a check is acceptance . Certification procured by
a holder discharges the drawer and all prior indorsers .
(2) Unless otherwise agreed, a bank has no obligation to certify a
check .
(3) A bank may certify a check before returning it for lack of proper
indorsement , in which case the drawer is discharged .
KRS 356.187 , 356.188 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Subsection ( 1) continues the rule of KRS 356.187 and 356.188 . Accord ,
First National Bank v . Bank of Ravenswood , 141 Ky . 671, 133 S. W. 581
(1911 ) ; Ewing v . Citizens ' National Bank , 162 Ky . 551, 172 S. W. 955 ( 1915 ) .
Subsection (2) is new but it adopts the generally accepted view . Since
a check is a demand instrument calling for payment rather than acceptance ,
the bank is under no duty to certify . It may be liable for breach of its
agreement to certify but it is not liable on the instrument .
Subsection (3 ) is new . It recognizes the practice of certification of a
check returned for indorsement in order to discharge the drawer . It is
consistent with the policy of acceptance of an incomplete instrument .
Section 3-412 . Acceptance Varying Draft.
164

Summary
( 1 ) When the proffered acceptance varies the draft as presented the
holder may refuse it and treat the draft as dishonored , in which case the
acceptance shall be cancelled .
(2) The terms of the draft are not varied by an acceptance to pay
at any particular bank or place in the continental United States, unless
the acceptance states that the draft is to be paid only at such bank or place .
(3) Where the holder assents to an acceptance varying the terms of
the draft each drawer and indorser who does not affirmatively assent is
discharged .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.139 to 356.142 .
Comment
(1 ) Accord, KRS 356.142. The distinctions between various kinds of
qualified acceptances in KRS 356.141 are eliminated and the rule is made
to apply to any " acceptance that in any manner varies the draft as pre-
sented . " A provision is added that where the holder refuses the conditional
acceptance , the drawee is entitled to have his acceptance cancelled.
(2) Accord, KRS 356.140 .
(3 ) Accord , KRS 356.142 insofar as drawers and indorsers are dis-
charged where the holder assents to an acceptance varying the terms of
the instrument . This section changes KRS 356.142 by requiring affirmative
assent to the acceptance.
Section 3-413 . Contract of Maker , Drawer and Acceptor .
Summary
(1 ) The maker or acceptor engages that he will pay the instrument
according to its tenor at the time of his engagement or as completed pur-
suant to UCC 3-115 on incomplete instruments .
(2 ) The drawer , unless he draws without recourse , engages that he
will pay the draft to the holder or indorser who takes it up .
(3) By making , drawing or accepting , the party admits as against
all subsequent parties the existence of the payee and his then capacity
to indorse .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.060 , 356.061 , 356.062 , 356.013 , 356.014 , 356.015 .
Comment
( 1) Accord, KRS 356.060 and 356.062 . See UCC 3-115 and KRS 356.013 ,
356.014 and 356.015 on incomplete instruments .
(2) Accord, KRS 356.061 .
(3) Accord, KRS 356.060, 356.061 , 356.062 ( 2 ) .
165

The above cited sections are combined and reworded to eliminate
duplication of language without change of substance . This section should
be read with UCC 3-115 , 3-406 , 3-407 , 3-412 and 3-418 .
Section 3-414 . Contract of Indorser ; Order of Liability.
Summary
(1) Unless otherwise specified (such as "without recourse " ) the
indorser engages that upon dishonor and any necessary notice and protest
he will pay the instrument according to its tenor at the time of his indorse-
ment to the holder or any subsequent indorser who takes it up even though
the one who takes it up was not obligated to do so .
(2) Unless otherwise agreed, indorsers are liable to one another in
the order in which they indorse , which is presumed to be the order in
which their signatures appear on the instrument .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.038 , 356.044 , 356.066 , 356.067 and 356.068 .
Comment
(1) Accord, KRS 356.038, 356.044 , 356.066 , 356.067 .
(2 ) Accord , KRS 356.068 except that a presumption is added that the
order in which indorsers indorsed is the order in which their signatures
appear on the instrument .
Section 3-415 . Contract of Accommodation Party .
Summary
(1) An accommodation party is one who signs the instrument in any
capacity for the purpose of lending his name to another party to it.
(2) If the instrument is taken for value before it is due, the accom-
modation party is liable in the capacity in which he signed even though
the taker knows of the accommodation .
(3) Oral proof of accommodation is not admissible against a holder
in due course without notice , but it may be admitted in other cases .
(4) An indorsement not in chain of title is notice of accommodation .
(5) An accommodation party is not liable to the party accommodated ,
and has a right of recourse on the instrument against such party .
KRS 356.029 , 356.064 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Accord, KRS 356.029 ; Mechanics ' & Farmers ' Savings Bank v.
Katterjohn , 137 Ky . 427, 125 S. W. 1071 ( 1910 ) ; Howard v . Southern Na-
166

tional Bank, 204 Ky . 71, 263 S. W. 719 ( 1924 ) ; First National Bank Receiver
v. Boreing's Adm'x, 173 Ky . 327, 190 S. W. 1106 (1917) . It is made clear
that an accommodation party is a surety . See Part 6 on Discharge .
(2) Accord, KRS 356.029 and 356.064 . Shepherd v . Haymond , 291 Ky .
780, 165 S. W. 2d 812 ( 1942 ) .
(3) Accord , KRS 356.029. Compare , Mutual Benefit Life Ins . Co. v .
First National Bank, 160 Ky . 538 , 169 S. W. 1028 ( 1914 ) ; First National
Bank v . Bickel, 143 Ky . 754, 137 S. W. 790 ( 1911 ) .
(4) Contra , KRS 356.063 and Young v . Exchange Bank, 152 Ky . 293,
153 S. W. 444 ( 1913 ) ; Hay v. Roberts , 196 Ky . 831 , 245 S. W. 881 ( 1922 ) .
(5) Accord, KRS 356.064 ( 3 ) .
Section 3-416 . Contract of Guarantor .
Summary
(1 ) "Payment guaranteed " or equivalent means that the signer en-
gages to pay the instrument if not paid when due without resort to any
other party.
(2) " Collection guaranteed" or equivalent means that the signer will
pay the instrument if the holder cannot collect it from the maker or ac-
ceptor .
(3) Words of guaranty which do not otherwise specify guarantee
payment .
(4) Words of guaranty added to the signature of a sole maker or
acceptor do not affect his liability , but such words added to signature of
one or two or more makers or acceptors show a presumption of accommo-
dation .
(5) When words of guaranty are used, presentment , notice and pro-
test are not necessary to charge the user .
(6 ) Any guaranty written on the instrument is enforceable notwith-
standing any statute of frauds .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This is a new section which is intended to state the commercial under-
standing of words of guaranty . An indorser guaranteeing payment waives
not only presentment , notice and protest , but also demand upon the maker
or drawer ; while his indorsement is still effective as such, his liability is
that of a co-maker . A guaranty of collection also waives presentment , notice
and protest , but requires the holder to proceed against the maker or ac-
ceptor first .
The Kentucky Statute of Frauds (KRS 371.010) does not require the
memorandum to state a consideration ; therefore , subsection (6 ) would not
change it.
Section 3-417 . arranties on Presentment and Transfer .
Summary
(1) Any person who obtains payment or acceptance and any prior
167

transferor warrants to a person who in good faith pays or accepts that :
(a) he has a good title to the instrument or is authorized to obtain
payment or acceptance on behalf of one who has a good title ;
and
(b) he has no knowledge that the signature of the maker or
drawer is unauthorized , except that this warranty is not
given by a holder in due course acting in good faith :
1. to a maker with respect to the maker's own signature ; or
2. to a drawer with respect to the drawer's own signature ,
whether or not the drawer is also the drawee ; or
3. to an acceptor of a draft if the holder in due course took
the draft after the acceptance or obtained the acceptance
without knowledge that the drawer's signature was un-
authorized ; and
(c) the instrument has not been materially altered , except that
this warranty is not given by a holder in due course acting in
good faith :
1. to the maker of a note ; or
2. to the drawer of a draft whether or not the drawer is
also the drawee ; or
3. to the acceptor of a draft with respect to an alteration
made prior to the acceptance if the holder in due course
took the draft after the acceptance , even though the
acceptance provided "payable as originally drawn" or
equivalent terms ; or
4. to the acceptor of a draft with respect to an alteration
made after the acceptance .
(2) Any person who transfers an instrument and receives consider-
ation warrants to his transferee and if the transfer is by indorsement to
any subsequent holder who takes the instrument in good faith that :
(a) he has a good title to the instrument or is authorized to obtain
payment or acceptance on behalf of one who has a good title
and the transfer is otherwise rightful ; and
(b) all signatures are genuine or authorized ; and
(c) the instrument has not been materially altered ; and
(d) no defense of either party is good against him ; and
(e) he has no knowledge of any insolvency proceeding instituted
with respect to the maker or acceptor or the drawer of an
unaccepted instrument .
(3) By transferring "without recourse " the transferor limits the obli-
gation stated in subsection ( 2 ) ( d ) to a warranty that he has no knowledge
of such a defense .
(4) A selling agent or broker who does not disclose the fact that
he is acting only as such gives the warranties provided in this section ,
but if he makes such disclosure warrants only his good faith and authority .
KRS 356.065 , 356.069 .
Kentucky Statutes
168

Comment
( 1 ) Warranties on Transfer . (Compare UCC 3-417 (2) with KRS
356.065 .) Under the UCC warranties are given by any person who transfers
an instrument and receives consideration , unless it is otherwise agreed. If
the transfer is by delivery only the warranty runs to the transferee ; if the
transfer is by indorsement then the warranty runs to any subsequent holder
who takes the instrument in good faith. Under KRS 356.066 the warranties
of a general indorser ran only to "subsequent holders in due course ."
Under the UCC the warranties run in favor of holders in due course
who by acquisition of instruments have cut off prior defenses ; such a
holder in due course may go against the defrauding payee instead of the
defrauded maker . See UCC 3-417 , Commissioners Comment , Point 9. The
transferor under the UCC does not warrant against difficulties of collection
or insolvency that are not known to him . See UCC 3-417 , Commissioners
Comment , Point 10. By a transfer without recourse the transferor makes
the same warranties , except that he only warrants that he has no knowledge
of any defense of any party that is good against him . UCC 3-417 ( 3 ) .
A selling agent or broker who does not disclose that he is acting as
such gives the warranties prescribed , but if he makes disclosure he war-
rants only his good faith and authority . Under KRS 356.069 it is necessary ,
to avoid giving warranties , to disclose the name of the principal ; under
UCC 3-417 (4) , disclosure that he is acting as selling agent or broker is
sufficient .
(2) Warranties on Presentment . The UCC introduces a new concept
in order to bring within its compass the doctrine of Price v . Neal, 3 Burr .
354 (1762 ) . The case relates to the avoidance of acceptances given and the
recovery of money paid under a mistake of fact. This doctrine has previ-
ously rested on principles of restitution , and is only partially covered in
KRS 356.062 . The new concept is that the person presenting an instrument
for acceptance and payment makes warranties to the party who accepts
or pays .
A complicating factor in interpreting the UCC provisions is that UCC
3-418 , which according to the Commissioners Comment codifies Price v .
Neal, is subordinated to UCC 3-417 on warranties , and both in turn are
made subject to UCC Article 4 on Bank Deposits and Collections , the rules
of which are subject to variation by agreement. The language of UCC 4-207
in the article on Bank Deposits and Collections is similar to UCC 3-418 and
3-417 , but covers all bank deposits. The warranties of UCC 3-417 and 4-207
operate to permit recovery of money only after "final" payment has been
made for other purposes , although this is not made explicit in the UCC .
Thus , when a person receives cash , as in payment over the counter , UCC
3-417 , 3-418 and 4-207 will be determinative of whether the payor can
recover back the money . When a depositor receives credit in his bank
account , however , this may be revoked without reference to these sections ,
within the time limits and under the conditions prescribed in the sections
on deferred posting and the right of chargeback , UCC 4-301 , 4-213 and
4-212 . Only after these time limits have expired will UCC 3-417 , 3-418 , and
4-207 become effective to determine whether the credit can be revoked
169

or the money recovered if the credit has been withdrawn . Consequently ,
the following discussion is based on the premise that the facts are such
that payment would be final and there can be no recovery back unless it is
authorized under UCC 3-417 , 3-418 , and 4-207 .
(a) Forged Indorsements . UCC 3-417 ( 1 ) ( a) continues existing law
to the effect that money paid under a forged indorsement can be recovered
back . See Restatement, Restitution , Sec. 35 ; Farmers National Bank of
Augusta v . Farmers & Traders Bank of Maysville , 159 Ky . 141 , 166 S. W.
986 , ( 1914 ) . UCC 3-417 , Commissioners Comment , Point 3 states :
"3. Subsection ( 1) (a) retains the generally accepted rule that
the party who accepts or pays does not 'admit' the genuineness of
indorsements , and may recover from the person presenting the in-
strument when they turn out to be forged ."
A similar provision in UCC 4-207 ( a) is intended to have a similar result
and to make unnecessary the practice in bank collections of indorsing "prior
indorsements guaranteed ."
...,
The UCC accomplishes this result by imposing on the presenter and
any prior transferor a warranty " that he has a good title to the instrument ."
A payment or acceptance is final unless this warranty is broken . The
question then arises as to whether the UCC clearly provides that the person
who obtains payment of an instrument bearing a forged indorsement does
not have good title . There is no explicit provision on this point , but UCC
3-404 states that " any unauthorized signature is wholly inoperative as that
of the person whose name is signed but it operates as the signature of
the unauthorized signer in favor of any person who in good faith pays the
instrument or takes it for value ." Under this section it would seem that
the person in possession of an instrument bearing a forged indorsement has
good title only to a piece of paper bearing the signature of the forger . It
may be expected that in interpreting UCC 3-417 ( 1) (a) the intent of the
draftsman will be carried out, but a conclusion that this subsection clearly
states existing law cannot be borne out when UCC 3-404 is taken into con-
sideration .
(b) Forged Signatures of Makers and Drawers .
It is assumed in the following discussion that a person who takes an
instrument with knowledge of a forged or unauthorized signature of the
maker or drawer is not a holder in due course . This assumption , however ,
is the subject of some doubt since UCC 3-404 ( 1) provides that "Any un-
authorized signature is wholly inoperative as that of the person whose
name is signed but it operates as the signature of the unauthorized
signer in favor of any person who in good faith pays the instrument or takes
it for value ." If a person "in good faith " takes such an instrument intending
to hold the unauthorized signer and not the person whose name appears on
the instrument , perhaps he is a holder in due course , since there is no
"defense " against the instrument .
"
The results under the UCC in varying factual situations will be :
(1) Holder in due course who , when he presents and receives accept-
ance or payment , is :
(a) without knowledge of the forged signature . Payment
170

will be final . The presentment warranty has not been broken .
(b) with knowledge of the forged signature . Result is doubt-
ful . A holder in due course acting in good faith does not warrant
that he has no knowledge of forged signatures , except that the
holder in due course of a draft apparently does warrant to an
acceptor that at the same time he obtains the acceptance he has
no knowledge of the drawer's signature being unauthorized .
(2) Holder not in due course, who , when he presents and receives
acceptance or payment , is :
(a) without knowledge of the forged signature . Under
UCC 3-417 ( 1 ) ( b) no warranty has been broken since the warranty
is only against "knowledge of unauthorized signatures ." Yet under
UCC 3-418 the acceptance or payment is not final since the holder is
not a holder in due course . The result , therefore , is in doubt .
(b) with knowledge of the forged signature . Acceptance or
payment not final since the warranty against knowledge of a forged
signature is broken .
The UCC seems to speak of a holder in due course acting in good faith
when he obtains payment of an instrument , although he has knowledge
that the signature of the maker or drawer is forged . If he acts in good faith
he makes no warranty that he has no knowledge of the forgery ; but if he
acts otherwise than in good faith he warrants that has has no knowledge of
the forgery . The section on presentment warranties seems very confused
and to be susceptible to very different interpretations . This is especially
evident when an effort is made to relate the section to UCC 3-418 , which
purports to deal with finality of payment .
(c) Equitable Exceptions to Price v. Neal . As generally applied in
the United States the rule of Price v . Neal carries with it certain equitable
exceptions . If the person receiving payment knows of the mistake of fact
of the payor , that is, that the signature of the drawer or maker is forged
he cannot invoke the rule of Price v . Neal and the payor may recover back
the money . The same is true if the person receiving payment has " reason
to know" ( See Restatement , Restitution , Sec. 34) or if he was negligent .
With reference to these equitable exceptions the Commissioners Com-
ment , UCC 3-418 states :
"4. The section rejects decisions under the original act per-
mitting recovery on the basis of mere negligence of the holder in
taking the instrument . If such negligence amounts to a lack of
good faith .. or to a failure to observe the reasonable commercial
standards applicable to the holder's business or to notice under
the rules relating to notice to a purchaser of an instrument , the
holder is not a holder in due course and is not protected ; but
otherwise the holder's negligence does not affect the finality of the
payment or acceptance ."
The UCC has thus abolished the " equitable exceptions " as such, but has
subsumed some of them under the requirement that the person receiving
payment be a holder in due course . If the person receiving payment should
have purchased with knowledge , or with reason to know , of the forgery ,
171

or if he purchased with such negligence as to constitute a lack of good faith,
the payor can recover back the money . But , if his purchase is only negli-
gent, but does not constitute lack of good faith, the payment cannot be
recovered back .
Under the UCC the status of the person receiving payment becomes
fixed at the time he takes the instrument . Subsequent events cannot
change that status. If, after taking the instrument but before receiving pay-
ment , the holder in due course learns of the forgery , the situation is quite
anomalous . If he reveals his knowledge to the payor , the payor will of
course not pay, and he will have no recourse by right of action against the
