bank
;
203

(b ) "Payor bank " means a bank by which an item is payable as
drawn or accepted ;
(c) " Intermediary bank " means any bank to which an item is
transferred in course of collection except the depository or
payor bank ;
(d) " Collecting bank " means any bank handling the item for
collection except the payor bank ;
(e) "Presenting bank " means any bank presenting an item except
a payor bank ;
(f) "Remitting bank " means any payor or intermediary bank
remitting for an item .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section defines the various types of banks as determined by their
duties in the processing of an item . The terms used to describe the different
banks in the collection chain can best be illustrated by a diagram :
Collecting
Intermediary
Depositary Presenting
Customer Bank -Bank
Bank <
→Bank
-Bank-
Payor
➤Bank
Remitting
From the diagram it will be seen that the bank collection process , when
a bank is the payor , begins with a "Depositary Bank" and ends with a
"Payor Bank." The banks in between are " Intermediary Banks ." The last
bank in the chain before the Payor Bank is the "Presenting Bank ." All
banks prior to the Payor Bank are " Collecting Banks ." When proceeds are
being returned all banks except the Depositary Bank are "Remitting Banks ."
When the item is to be collected from a party other than a bank the
terminology given above shifts to the right to include the last bank in the
chain which will not be a payor bank , but a presenting , intermediary , and
collecting bank .
Section 4-106 . Separate Office of a Bank .
Summary
A branch or separate office of a bank is a separate bank for the pur-
pose of computing the time within which and the place at or to which action
may be taken or notices or orders shall be given under Article Four .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 287.180 .
204

Comment
In Kentucky , state banks cannot establish branches . See , Bruner v . Citi-
zens Bank , 134 Ky . 283, 120 S. W. 345 ( 1909 ) . But banks and trust companies
may establish additional offices in the same city or county for the conveni-
ence of its patrons where the only business transacted is the receipt of de-
posits and the payment of checks . Marvin v . Kentucky Title Trust Co., 218
Ky . 135 , 291 S. W. 17 (1927 ) . However , Kentucky statutes make no dis-
tinction between the main office and the branches . This section makes the
distinction .
Section 4-107 . Time of Receipt of Items .
Summary
(1) Two p . m. or later in the afternoon may be prescribed by a bank
as a cut-off hour for the handling of money .
(2) An item received by a bank after business hours may be treated
as being received at the opening of the next business day .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Kentucky has no statutory provision as provided in subsection ( 1) , but
such a procedure is arbitrarily followed by many banks in the state .
Subsection ( 2) is similar to a provision contained in KRS 357.030 . See ,
Stanley , Banking Law of Kentucky , Sec . 70 .
Section 4-108 . Delays .
Summary
(1) A collecting bank may waive , modify or extend time limits im-
posed by this act for not more than an additional banking day , without the
approval of any person involved , in a good faith effort to secure payment .
( 2) Delay is excused by interruption of communication facilities , by
war , or other emergencies .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 357.060 , 357.050 , 357.080 .
Comment
( 1 ) See KRS 357.060 which sets forth a more rigid time limitation for
forwarding and presenting items according to whether payable in the same
or another city .
( 2) KRS 357.080 provides that a forwarding bank is not responsible
for the loss of an item where it has exercised ordinary care. It would appear
that this subsection would not be contra to Kentucky law .
205

Part 2
COLLECTION OF ITEMS :
DEPOSITARY AND COLLECTING BANKS
Introductory Comment
Part 2 of Article 4 contains provisions governing the process of collection
of items as they pass through the hands of depositary and collecting banks .
In an effort to establish the relative interests of the parties as the items pass
through such process , it is made a rule prima facie that a depositary bank
takes an item for collection regardless of the form of indorsement , thus es-
tablishing the agency rather than ownership of the depositary bank , and
eliminating the many cases which arise concerning such problem of owner-
ship vs. agency. Also , to expedite and hasten the collecting process, banks
are bound only by the instructions of their transferor ; in certain instances
the bank may supply missing indorsements ; and the media of authorized
remittance is extended .
Section 4-201 . Presumption and Duration of Agency Status of
Collecting Banks and Provisional Status of
Credits ; Applicability of Article ; Item Indorsed
"Pay Any Bank ” .
Summary
(1) Unless a contrary intent apears and prior to the time a settlement
given by a collecting bank for an item is or becomes final (subsection ( 3 )
of UCC 4-211 and UCC 4-212 and 4-213 ) , the bank is an agent or sub -agent
of the owner of the item and any settlement given for the item is pro-
visional . This provision applies regardless of the form of indorsement or
lack of indorsement and even though credit given for the item is subject to
immediate withdrawals of right or is in fact withdrawn ; but the continuance
of ownership of an item by its owner and any rights of the owner to pro-
ceeds of the item are subject to rights of a collecting bank such as those re-
sulting from outstanding advances on the item and valid rights of setoff.
When an item is handled by banks for presentment , payment and collection ,
the relevant provisions of this article apply even though action of parties
clearly establishes that a particular bank has purchased the item and is the
owner of it.
(2) After an item has been indorsed with the words "pay any bank " or
the like, only a bank may acquire the rights of a holder :
(a) until the item has been returned to the customer initiating
collection ; or
(b) until the item has been specially indorsed by a bank to a
person who is not a bank .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 357.020 , 357.040 .
206

Comment
( 1) Subsection ( 1) appears to be consistent with prevailing bank prac-
tice under prior law and appears to state workable rules .
The American Banking Association Code (KRS Chapter 357 ) provides
that (1) normally the bank of deposit of a collection item takes it as a
collecting agent for the depositor ; ( 2) an indorsement "for deposit" or "pay
any bank or banker " is restrictive and indicates an agency relationship ;
and ( 3 ) if the item is a bearer item or is indorsed specially or in blank, there
is an agency relationship on the part of the depositary bank , although sub-
sequent holders may presume that the depositary bank is owner of the
item unless the depositary bank takes certain specified action to negative
that presumption . See KRS 357.020 and 357.040 .
Making commercial paper payable at a bank does not of itself con-
stitute such bank the agent of the payee to receive the money, but it is a
mere designation of a place where both paper and funds to take it up will
be on the day it is due ; to make the bank the payee's agent to receive pay-
ment , the paper must be either indorsed to or deposited with it. Caldwell v .
Evans , 68 Ky . 380, 96 Am . Dec. 358 ( 1869 ) . However , a long course of deal-
ing may warrant the conclusion that the bank was appointed an agent for
the purpose of receiving payment . See 9 C.J.S. , Banks and Banking , Sec. 225 .
See also , Bell Nat. Bank v . Green , 258 Ky . 317, 79 S. W. 2d 967 ( 1935 )
holding that a deposit for collection , as distinguishable from a general de-
posit , is a transaction by which a check, note or other instrument providing
for the payment of money is deposited with or received by a bank to act as
agent of the depositor for its collection .
(2) The purpose of subsection (2) is to permit a bank to indorse an
item in such a manner as to "lock" it in bank collection channels . The pro-
visions thus in result follow KRS 357.040 which among other things , pro-
vides that an indorsement "pay any bank or banker " is restrictive . However ,
to accord flexibility to the handling of items in accord with the general
scheme of Article 4, subsection (2) would permit the "unlocking " of an item
so indorsed from banking channels if the item is specially indorsed by a
bank to a nonbank party . Thus if a bank by mistake transfers to another
bank , for value , an instrument so indorsed which is not a collection item
(such as an unmatured time item ) , the mistake may be easily rectified by
the transferee bank or any other bank . Also if an item is returned to the
customer of the depositary bank for any proper reason, he may , in turn ,
transfer the item to any one he pleases .
Section 4-202 . Responsibility for Collection ; When Action
Seasonable .
Summary
(1 ) A collecting bank must use ordinary care in :
(a) presenting an item or sending it for presentment ; and
(b) sending notice of dishoner or non -payment or returning an
an item other than a documentary draft to the bank's trans
207

feror ( or directly to the depositary bank under subsection (2 )
of UCC 4-212 ) (see note to UCC 4-212 ) after learning that the
item has not been paid or accepted , as the case may be ; and
(c) settling for an item when the bank receives final settlement ;
and
(d) making or providing for any necessary protest ; and
( e) notifying its transferor of any loss or delay in transit within
a reasonable time after discovery thereof .
(2 ) A collecting bank acts seasonably if it takes action before its mid-
night deadline following receipt of an item; taking any longer time must be
proved reasonable by the bank .
(3) A bank is not liable for the insolvency , neglect , misconduct , mis-
take or default of another bank or person or for loss or destruction of an
item in transit or in the possession of others .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 357.050 , 357.060 , 357.080 , 356.102 , 356.103 , 356.104 .
Comment
( 1 ) KRS 357.060 is in accord with subdivision ( 1) of the present section
in providing that a collecting bank must use ordinary care in presenting an
item . As to the use of ordinary care in giving notice of dishonor , the present
Kentucky statutes go into greater detail specifying the time within which
notice must be given . ( See KRS 356.102 , 356.103 , 356.104 .) There is no ex-
press provision in the present Kentucky law that ordinary care must be used
in settling for an item or making necessary protest , or in notifying of any
loss or delay in transit , however , the same result would be obtained . 9 C.J.S. ,
Banks and Banking , Sec. 235, states :
"The collecting bank must act in good faith , exercise reasonable skill
in performing its duties , and use due care and diligence in making
prompt presentment , demand , and protest , in giving notice of dis-
honor and in taking whatever steps are necessary to protect its cus-
tomer's rights , or it will be liable for loss ."
See also , Stanley , Banking Law of Kentucky , Sec. 215 ; Second Nat . Bank v .
Merchants Nat . Bank , 111 Ky . 930, 65 S. W. 4 ( 1901 ) .
( 2) Subsection ( 2) allows more flexibility than the standard norm ,
and the time for action has been considerably extended under some circum-
stances . An item received after 2:00 p.m . Monday but before the close of
banking hours , if a cut -off hour has been established under UCC 4-107 ,
need not be forwarded for collection until midnight on Wednesday . Under
KRS 357.060 , which defines ordinary care in terms of taking action not later
than the business day next following the day of receipt , the time for action
would be before the close of business on Tuesday .
(3 ) Subsection ( 3 ) incorporates the "Massachusetts" rule to the effect
that a depositary bank , subject to the duty of selecting properly qualified
intermediary banks and giving proper instructions , is liable only for its own
negligence and not for the negligence or insolvency of a subsequent bank.
This is in accord with Kentucky cases which have followed the Massachu
208

setts rule , as do the majority of the states . See , Farmers Bank & Trust Co.
of Stanford v. Newland , 97 Ky . 464, 31 S. W. 38 ( 1895 ) ; Second Nat. Bank
v. Merchants Nat. Bank, 111 Ky . 930, 65 S. W. 4 ( 1901 ) . In accord with the
provision as to nonliability for loss or destruction of an item in transit or in
the possession of others, see KRS 357.080 .
Section 4-203 . Effect of Instructions .
Summary
Subject to Article 3, provisions concerning conversion of instruments
( UCC 3-419 ) and provisions of Article 3 and 4, concerning restrictive in-
dorsements ( UCC 3-205 , 3-206 , 3-419 and 4-205 ) only a collecting bank's
transferor can give instructions which affect the bank , and for action taken
in following these instructions a collecting bank is not liable to prior parties .
KRS 357.020 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The provision of this section should be compared to KRS 357.020 which
provides that each collecting bank " shall be authorized to follow the instruc-
tions of its immediate forwarding bank " . The Code adopts a chain of com-
mand theory as between banks , under which only a bank's transferor can
give instructions which affect the bank . A bank is not liable to prior parties
for action taken pursuant to the instructions of its transferor or in accord
with any agreement with its transferor , subject only to any special require-
ments relating to restrictive indorsements .
Section 4-204 . Methods of Sending and Presenting ; Sending
Direct to Payor Bank .
Summary
(1) A collecting bank must use reasonably prompt methods of for-
warding , considering relevant instructions , nature of the item , number of
items , cost of collection , and methods generally used for similar items .
(2) A collecting bank may send :
(a) any item direct to the payor bank ;
(b) any item to any non -bank payor if authorized by its transferor ;
and
(c) any item other than documentary drafts to any non -payor , if
authorized by Federal Reserve regulation or operating letter ,
clearing house rule or the like .
KRS 357.050 , 357.060 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Subsection ( 1) prescribes the general standards applicable to
proper sending or forwarding of items . Kentucky has no such express pro
209

vision but a similar result would probably follow . Since KRS 357.050 re-
quires that a collecting bank must use "ordinary care " , proper methods of
collection would naturally be a part thereof . Stanley , Banking Law of Ken-
tucky , Sec. 215, discussing the duty and liability of a bank under KRS
357.050 , states at page 177 :
"This (KRS 357.050) is but a codification of the general law , or
usual decisions of the courts . They have declared that the bank
must exercise ordinary or reasonable diligence with regard to col-
lections , complying with instructions or any special contract , and
taking such steps as are necessary to protect its customer . That
duty requires the exercise of good faith and reasonable skill and
diligence . This includes a proper presentation of the item for ac-
ceptance or demand of payment according to the usual course of
business , and such further action as is necessary to secure and pre-
serve the liabilities of all parties . In this connection , a bank is given
wide discretionary powers in the exercise of the collection , but it is
an implied part of the contract that proper methods shall be used."
(Emphasis added .)
If a collecting bank is negligent in failing to use proper collection methods,
or in performing its duties in any particular , and loss results , the bank is
liable to its customer in damages or must account for the proceeds . Second
Nat . Bank v . Merchants Nat . Bank , 111 Ку . 930, 65 S. W. 4 ( 1901 ) ; National
Bank of Stanford v . Hocker , 3 K.L.R. 535 ( 1882 ) ; Grant County Deposit Bank
v . McCampbell , 194 F. 2d 469 (6th Cir . 1952 ) .
(2) At common law, the practice of direct sending , even to a payor
bank , in the absence of statute, banking custom or agreement , has not found
favor in the courts , and a collecting bank might be held to have acted neg-
ligently in sending the item direct and might be held to assume the risk of
loss , if any , arising by reason of improper handling on the part of the payor
or insolvency of the payor after such payor had received the item but
prior to final settlement thereof . Doing os is deemed unreasonable and op-
posed to the policy of the law because no one can be deemed a suitable legal
agent to enforce in behalf of another a claim against itself . See , 7 Am . Jur .,
Banks , Secs . 674, 676. However , some courts , including Kentucky, consider
a usage or custom of sending commercial paper to be collected directly to
the drawee to be a reasonable one , and hold the collecting bank is not guilty
of negligence in adopting this course pursuant to custom . It was said in
Farmers Bank & Trust Co. of Stanford v . Newland , 97 Ky . 464, 31 S. W. 38
(1895) :
"When a customer deposits with a bank a note , bill of exchange,
certificate of deposit , check, etc. , for collection at a point distant
from the location of the bank , he must know the bank cannot send
one of its officers or agents to such point to make the collection . He
is presumed to know the method employed by banks in making such
collections . He knows that the bank must select some other bank
or agency to aid in accomplishing the undertaking imposed on it.
He has made the bank his agent for that purpose . He has employed
the bank to do through its methods of making collection that which
would cause him much time and money to do himself . When he so
210

engages the bank and makes it his agent to make the collection , he
does so with the implied understanding that the bank will follow
the customary method in making such collections , which necessitates
the selection of agents or correspondents at other points to carry out
the undertaking , and the bank can only be held responsible for the
exercise of due care and diligence in making such selection."
KRS 357.060 , expressly permits direct sending by mail to a payor bank , but
makes no provision for direct sending by a collecting bank to a nonbank
payor . The practice of such direct sending to nonbank payors has developed
increasingly in many areas because such direct sending adds to speed and
efficiency of collection and because sending banks are willing , as a prac-
tical matter , to assume the risk of insolvency or misconduct on the part of
nonbank payors of known good credit standing to which items are regularly
sent.
Subsection (2) would retain the recognized statutory exception to the
strict common law rule by permitting direct sending to a payor bank ; it
would continue the effect of the recognized doctrine that the common law
rule may be varied by agreement, in that it would permit a bank to send an
item to any nonbank payor if authorized by its transferor ; and it would
extend somewhat even the elastic rules provided by UCC 4-103 ( on variation
by agreement) by making conclusive as to the exercise of ordinary care a
clearing house rule ( as well as a Federal Reserve regulation or operating
letter ) authorizing direct sending to a nonbank payor of any item except a
documentary draft.
Under regulations of the Federal Reserve Board , it is permissible for a
federal reserve bank to transmit paper for collection directly to the drawee
or payor . Fderal Reserve Bank v . Malloy , 264 U. S. 160, 68 L. ed. 617 (1924) .
Paragraph (c) of subsection (2) thus expands the banking community ,
under the authority of a clearing house rule , to encompass nonbank payors
whose continuing solvency the banks have heretofore been willing to under-
write in this regard , but with whose solvency thenceforth they would have
no concern as collecting banks , provided they act in good faith .
Section 4-205 . Supplying Missing Indorsement ; No Notice From
Prior Indorsement .
Summary
(1 ) A depositary bank taking an item for collection may supply any
indorsement of the customer which is necessary to title unless the item
contains the words "payee's indorsement required ." In the absence of such a
requirement , a statement placed on the item by the depositary bank to the
effect that the item was deposited by a customer or credited to his account
is effective as the customer's indorsement .
(2) An intermediary bank , or payor bank which is not a depositary
bank , is neither given notice nor otherwise affected by a restrictive indorse-
ment of any person except the banks ' immediate transferor .
211

None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
( 1) Subsection ( 1) is designed to speed up collections by eliminating
the necessity to return to a nonbank depositary any items he may have
failed to indorse . Probably under the section the customer must not only be
in rightful possession of the item before deposit , but also rightfully entitled
to the proceeds . If an instrument were payable to two payees jointly and
deposited by one payee without any indorsement , it would seem that the
bank could only supply the indorsement of the payee who was its customer ,
but not the indorsement of the other payee .
(2) If an owner of an item indorses it "for deposit " or "for collection ,"
he usually does so in the belief such indorsement will guard against further
negotiation of the item to a holder in due course by a finder or a thief . This
belief is reasonably justified if at least one bank in any chain of banks col-
lecting the item has a responsibility to act consistently with the indorse-
ment . The language of subsection ( 2) coupled with UCC 3-205 , 3-206 , 3-304,
3-419 and 4-203 makes depositary banks responsible for acting consistently
with restrictive indorsements , but in the interest of speed and efficiency in
the bank collection process , exempts from this responsibility intermediary
banks and payor banks which are not depositary banks .
Section 4-206 . Transfer Between Banks .
Summary
Any agreed method which identifies the transfer or bank is sufficient
for an item's transfer to another bank .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The word " transfer " as used in this section and throughout Article 4 is
the equivalent of what is sometimes loosely called 'negotiation " between
banks in the collection process . The section would permit agreement cover-
ing the use of an identifying number or symbol such as the A. B. A. transit
number of a bank , as evidence of the transfer . The use of such a simple
device would help solve a troublesome operating problem in collections ; it
is sometimes very difficult, because of the obscurity caused by physically
overlapping bank stamps , to trace back an unpaid item from the evidence on
the item itself .
The legal theory of this section is already given expression in Federal
Reserve Regulations G and J which provide that the mere sending of an item
to a Federal Reserve Bank gives rise to warranties and guaranties , whether
or not the item is indorsed , and whether or not the sending bank's guaranty
of prior indorsements appears thereon . Although there appears to be no
legal reason why equivalent agreements could not be made between banks
under prior law , this section would put the stamp of legislative approval
upon such agreements .
212

Section 4-207 . Warranties of Customer and Collecting Bank on
Transfer or Presentment of Items ;
Time for Claims .
Summary
(1) Each customer or collecting bank who obtains payment or accept-
ance of an item and each prior customer and collecting bank warrants to
the payor bank or other payor who in good faith pays or accepts the item
that:
(a) he has good title or is authorized to act on behalf of one having
good title;
(b) he has no knowledge that the signature is unauthorized , ex-
cept that this warranty is not given by any customer or col-
lecting bank that is a holder in due course and acts in good
faith:
1. to a maker with respect to maker's own signature ;
2. to a drawer with respect to drawer's own signature ,
whether or not drawer is also the drawee ; or
3. to an acceptor of an item if the holder in due course took
the item after the acceptance or obtained the acceptance
without knowledge that the drawer's signature was un-
authorized ; and
(c) the item has not been materially altered , except that this war-
ranty is not given by any customer or collecting bank that is
a holder in due course and acts in good faith :
1. to the maker of a note ; or
2. to the drawer of a draft whether or not the drawer is also
the drawee ; or
3. to the acceptor of an item with respect to an alteration
made prior to the acceptance if the holder in due course
took the item after the acceptance , even though the ac-
ceptance provided "payable as originally drawn " or equiv-
alent terms ; or
4. to the acceptor of an item with respect to an alteration
made after the acceptance .
(2) Each customer and collecting bank who transfers an item and re-
ceives a settlement or other consideration for it warrants to his transferee
and to any subsequent collecting bank who takes the item in good faith that :
(a) he either has good title or is authorized to act for one having
good title and the transfer is otherwise rightful ; and
(b) all signatures are genuine or authorized ; and
(c) item has not been materially altered ; and
(d) no defense is good against him; and
(e) he has no knowledge of insolvency proceedings .
In addition each customer and collecting bank so transferring an item and
receiving a settlement or other consideration engages that upon dishonor
and any necessary notice of dishonor and protest he will take up the item .
(3) These warranties and engagement to honor arise notwithstanding
absence of words of guarantee or warranty . Damages for breach of these
213

warranties and engagement to honor shall not exceed the consideration re-
ceived by the customer or collecting bank responsible plus finance charges
and expenses related to the item , if any .
(4) Claim for breach of warranty must be made within a reasonable
time after person claiming learns of the breach , or the person liable is dis-
charged to the extent of any loss caused by the delay in making the claim.
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 357.040 , 356.062 , 356.065 , 356.066 .
Comment
(1) Subsection (1) is the functional counterpart of UCC 3-417 ( 1) (cov-
ering the warranties on presentment and transfer in the article on Com-
mercial Paper ) .
Subparagraph ( 1) ( a) provides that each customer or collecting bank
and each prior customer and collecting bank give a warranty , running to
the payor bank or other payor , of good title to the instrument or authority
to obtain payment or acceptance on behalf of another party who has good
title. This subparagraph is comparable to KRS 356.065 ( 2 ) . A payor may re-
cover payment made to a collecting bank on an item bearing a forged in-
dorsement under this warranty ; therefore , it will no longer be necessary
for a payor to require a collecting bank to indorse an item in the course of
collection with a legend such as "prior indorsements guaranteed ."
The warranty of title does not change the rule of Price v. Neal, 3 Burr .
1354 ( 1762 ) , incorporated in KRS 356.062, to the effect that the payor of an
item bearing the forged signature of the drawer may not recover the amount
of the item from a person receiving payment therefor who took the item in
good faith and for value . Also , the warranty does not change the rule of
prior law to the effect that a thief or finder of a bearer instrument can pass
good title to a purchaser in good faith and for value , including a collecting
bank which is a holder in due course ( UCC 4-208 and 4-209 .)
Another consideration would be the applicability of subparagraph ( 1)
(a) to the right of a payor to recover against a person receiving payment of
an instrument bearing forgeries of both the drawer's signature and the in-
dorsement of the payee or other indorsee necessary for th negotiation of the
instrument . Under prior law , there was conflict of authority as to whether
a payor paying on such a forged instrument could recover from the person
receiving payment therefor . Most jurisdictions held the payor could not re-
cover , even from a collecting bank on the ground of a guaranty of prior in-
dorsements on the part of the latter . However , recovery by the payor has
been permitted in Kentucky on the ground that the payee's indorsement had
been guaranteed by a guaranty of prior indorsements . Farmers ' National
Bank of Augusta v . Farmers & Traders ' Bank of Maysville , 159 Ку. 141, 166
S. W. 986 , ( 1914 ) . Under subparagraph ( 1) (a) , it would seem that the
payor should recover in such a situation . The result may go the other way ,
however , in any situation where the forger of the signature intended the
named payee to have no interest in the instrument , since the indorsement
would be effective to pass title . See UCC 3-405 ( 1) ( b ) and Commissioners
Comment 3 thereto .
214

Subparagraph ( 1) ( b ) provides that the warranty against knowledge
of the unauthorized signature of the drawer or maker is not given by a
holder in due course , acting in good faith , to a maker or drawer , whether or
not he is also the payor , and is also not given to an acceptor by such a holder
who obtains acceptance without knowledge of an unauthorized signature ,
but learns of such unauthorized signature prior to obtaining payment . The
warranty as set forth continues a reasonable exception to the Price v . Neal
rule . The warranty is fully discussed in the Comments to UCC 3-417 , supra .
The warranty against material alteration set out in subparagraph ( 1 )
(c) is discussed in the Comments to UCC 3-417 , supra .
(2) This subsection contains additional warranties given by each cus-
tomer and collecting bank to the transferee who has given consideration
against an item transferred for collection . The warranties run to the trans-
feree and to any subsequent holder . The engagement to pay the instrument
if it is dishonored and if any necessary protest and notice is given , is analog-
ous to the final sentence of KRS 356.066. A similar provision is contained in
UCC 3-414 ( 1) .
UCC 4-414 ( 1) provides that the engagement to pay the instrument if it
is dishonored and if necessary protest and notice is given , is made only by
indorsers , except those who indorse without recourse ; UCC 4-207 (2) pro-
vides that such engagement is given by each customer and collecting bank ,
and no exception exists in favor of a transferor who does not indorse or who
indorses without recourse . It is conceivable , however , that a " without re-
course" indorsement in the bank collection process would be given effect as
an agreement between the indorser and indorsee . On the other hand , such
indorsement is usually indicative of a sale, pledge or transfer for purpose
other than collection , and the transaction would be governed by UCC 3-414
and 3-417 .
Subparagraphs ( a ) , ( b ) , ( c) and ( d) require no particular comment.
These warranties would be to the effect that the action of the collecting
bank or customer in transferring is proper and that there are no real or per-
sonal defenses available against the transferor .
Presumably , under subparagraph ( 2 ) ( e ) , any customer or collecting
bank against whom recovery is permitted by reason of knowledge of an in-
solvency proceeding instituted with respect to the maker , acceptor or drawer
would retain his claim , if any , on the underlying transaction (or on the
instrument ) against the maker , acceptor or drawer . What constitutes knowl-
edge of an insolvency proceeding ? It appears that the customer or collecting
bank should have actual notice (see UCC 1-202 (25 ) and ( 27 ) ) of an insol-
vency proceeding (as defined in UCC 1-202 (22 ) ) to be chargeable with the
warranty of subparagraph ( 2 ) ( e ) ; mere suspicion of insolvency ( see UCC
1-201 (23 ) ) or knowledge of impending insolvency would not be enough .
(3 ) Subsection (3) would obviate any defense on the part of a collect-
ing bank , which had made remittance in good faith to its prior transferor ,
based on its agency relationship to the depositor . The provision would also
partially negative the effects of UCC 4-201 and 3-418 . The second sentence
of subsection (3) contains a limitation of the amount of damages recoverable
by the transferee for breach of the warranties and engagement to honor
215

under subsections ( 1) and ( 2 ) , limited to the extent of the consideration re-
ceived by the customer or collecting bank "plus finance charges and ex-
penses related to the item." The term "plus finance charges and expenses" is
not defined in the Code . It seems reasonable , however , that the term would
include not only interest and costs , but also other expenses , such as attor-
ney's fees .
(4) Subsection (4) permits discharge of a person otherwise liable under
a UCC 4-207 warranty , where there is an unreasonable delay in making
claim for breach of warranty . The discharge is limited to the extent of any
loss caused by the delay in making claim . UCC 4-406 casts on the drawer or
maker , whose account is charged , the burden of notifying the payor promptly
of forgeries and alterations ; thus no collecting bank should be contingently
liable under UCC 4-207 for a period as long as the period of the statute of
limitations .
Section 4-208. Security Interest of Collecting Bank in Items ,
Accompanying Documents and Proceeds .
Summary
(1) A bank has a security interest in an item and any accompanying
documents or the proceeds of either :
(a) in case of an item deposited in an account to the extent to
which credit given for the item has been withdrawn or applied ;
(b) in case of an item for which it has given credit available for
withdrawal as of right, to the extent of the credit given
whether or not the credit is drawn upon and whether or not
there is a right of charge -back ; or
(c) if it makes an advance on or against the item .
(2 ) When credit is given for several items received at one time or pur-
suant to a single agreement is partially withdrawn , the security interest re-
mains upon all items . For purpose of this section, credits first given are first
withdrawn .
(3 ) Receipt by a collecting bank of a final settlement for an item is a
realization on its security interest in the item , accompanying documents and
proceeds . Until the bank receives final settlement for the item or gives up
possession of it or accompanying documents for purposes other than collec-
tion , the security interest continues and is subject to the provisions of Article
9 except that :
(a) no security agreement is necessary to make the security in-
terest enforceable ( subsection ( 1) ( b ) of UCC 9-203 ) ; and
(b) no filing is required to perfect the security interest ; and
(c) the security interest has priority over conflicting perfected
security interests in the item , accompanying documents or pro-
ceeds .
KRS 357.020 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1 ) Compare KRS 357.020 which provides that where a bank allows
216

withdrawal , it shall have all the rights of an owner to the extent of the
amount withdrawn . National Deposit Bank of Owensboro v . Ohio Oil Co.,
250 Ky . 288 , 62 S. W. 2d 1048 ( 1933 ) . This subsection creates a lien in favor
of the bank rather than creating ownership . Subparagraph ( 1) ( a) would
normally apply where a check is deposited in a bank account for collection ;
subparagraph ( 1 ) ( b ) would normally apply in the case where a bank
purchases or discounts an instrument or receives a documentary draft for
collection and gives credit immediately available for withdrawal . See , Stan-
ley , Banking Law of Kentucky , Sec. 214 .
(2) The rule of subsection ( 2 ) , spreading the security interest pro-
portionally over all items deposited at the same time to the extent of credit
withdrawn by the depositor , is based on practical consideration , since any
attempt to selectively apportion the security interest , where there has been
a partial withdrawal of credit given for a number of items deposited at the
same time, would produce an unworkable rule of law . Britton , Bills and
Notes , p. 397, indicates that it is not clear , under prior law, whether a pos-
sessory lien on the part of the bank would be proportionally spread over all
items deposited at the same time , to the extent of credit withdrawn .
Subsection (2) applies the "first in, first out " rule which is the majority
rule under prior law. Under that rule, if a bank opens a new account for a
depositor receiving for deposit a $ 100 check on Monday , another $ 100 check
on Tuesday, and a third $100 check on Wednesday , and the depositor with-
draws $150 on Thursday , the bank would have a full security interest with
respect to the check received on Monday , a security interest to the extent of
$50 with respect to the check received on Tusday , and no security interest
with respect to the check received on Wednesday . In applying the "first in,
first out" rule, subsection (2) impliedly rejects the holdings of cases to the
effect that the bank has the rights of a holder when it first gives credit for
the item.
( 3 ) Subsection ( 3) is intended to correlate the security interest pro-
vision of UCC 4-208 with Article 9 generally . It precludes any question to
the effect that a bank having a security interest in an item may lose its
rights with respect to the item to a secured creditor who has complied with
filing requirements and other applicable provisions of Article 9. It provides
that a collecting bank may enforce its security interest in an item without
the necessity of entering into a security agreement pursuant to Article 9 , or
filing in accordance with that article , and also that the bank's security in-
terest has priority over conflicting interests in the item perfected pursuant
toArticle 9 .
Section 4-209 . When Bank Gives Value for Purpose of Holder
in Due Course .
Summary
A bank's security interest in an item is value for the purpose of deter-
mining its status as a holder in due course .
KRS 356.027 .
Kentucky Statutes
217

Comment
Accord, KRS 356.027 . National Deposit Bank v. Ohio Oil Co. , 250 Ky .
288, 62 S. W. 2d 1048 ( 1933 ) .
Section 4-210 . Presentment by Notice of Item Not Payable by ,
through or at a Bank ; Liability of Secondary
Parties .
Summary
(1) A collecting bank may present an item not payable by or through
a bank by sending a written notice to the party to accept or pay .
(2) The presenting bank may treat the item as dishonored and charge
a secondary party by sending him notice of the facts if the item is not hon-
ored by the close of business on the day after maturity , or in the case of
demand items, by the close of the third banking day after notice was sent.
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1 ) This section complements UCC 4-204 which permits direct sending
by a collecting bank to the payor bank by mail , express or any other proper
means . Although UCC 4-204 (2) would not permit direct sending to a non-
bank payor , presentment may be made directly to such a payor under UCC
3-504 or , if direct presentment to the payor is inconvenient , presentment by
notice may be made under this section . The nonbank payor receiving pre-
sentment by notice would have the rights to which any payor would be en-
titled under UCC 3-505 , such as exhibition of the item , etc.
(2 ) Subsection ( 2) sets forth a reasonable rule under which a bank
making presentment by notice to a nonbank payor may treat such an item
as dishonored in the event of nonaction by the nonbank payor . No extended
comment with respect to this provision is necessary , although it should be
noted that the presenting bank may charge any secondary party , in the event
of nonaction on the part of the payor to whom presentment by notice has
been made, by sending notice of the facts . Also , the presenting bank , as a
collecting bank , would be obligated to comply with UCC 4-202 (1 ) ( b ) ,
within the time limits set forth in UCC 4-202 ( 2 ) , which time limits would
begin to run at the time specified in this subsection .
Section 4-211 . Media of Remittance ; Provisional and Final
Settlement in Remittance Cases .
Summary
(1) A collecting bank may take in settlement of an item:
(a) a check of the remitting bank or of another bank on any bank
except the remitting bank ; or
(b) a cashier's check or similar primary obligation of a remitting
bank which is a member of or clears through a member of the
same clearing house or group as the collecting bank ; or
218

(c) appropriate authority to charge an account of the remitting
bank or of another bank with the collecting bank ; or
(d) if the item is drawn upon or payable by a person other than a
bank, a cashier's check, certified check or other bank check or
obligation .
(2) If before the midnight deadline , the collecting bank dishonors a
remittance check or authorization to charge on itself or presents or forwards
for collection a remittance instrument of or on another bank which is of a
kind approved by subsection ( 1 ) or has not been authorized by it, the col-
lecting bank is not liable to prior parties in the event of the dishonor of
such check, instrument or authorization .
(3) A settlement for an item by means of a remittance instrument or
authorization to charge is or becomes a final settlement as to both the person
making and the person receiving the settlement :
(a) if the remittance instrument or authorization to charge is of a
kind approved by subsection (1 ) or has not been authorized by
the person receiving the settlement and in either case the per-
son receiving the settlement acts seasonably before its mid-
night deadline , in presenting , forwarding for collection or pay-
ing the instrument or authorization , at the time the remit-
tance instrument or authorization is finally paid by the payor
by which it is payable ;
(b) if the person receiving the settlement authorized remittance by
nonbank check or obligation or by cashier's check or similar
primary obligation of or a check upon the payor or other re-
mitting bank which is not of a kind approved by subsection ( 1 )
(b), at the time of the receipt of such remittance check or
obligation ; or
(c) if in a case not covered by subparagraphs ( a) or (b) the per-
son receiving the settlement fails to seasonably present, for-
ward for collection , pay or return a remittance instrument or
authorization to it to charge before its midnight deadline,-
at such midnight deadline .
KRS 357.090 , 357.100 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Subsection ( 1) lists the forms of remittance that a collecting bank
might receive for an item without becoming liable if the remittance itself is
not paid . One acceptable form of remittance , never before made acceptable
by statute, would be the primary obligation of the remitting bank which is
a member of "or clears through " the same clearing house "or group " set out
in subparagraph ( 1) ( b ) . The practice is justified in Commissioners Com-
ment 3. The use of the words " or clears through " covers the situation of a
remitting bank which is not a member of the same clearing house as the bank
to which remittance is made, but which clears through a clearing house mem-
ber. The words in the subparagraph "or group" would cover two or three
banks in the same area regularly exchanging items on each other but not
organized in a formal clearing house .
219

Subparagraph ( 1) ( d) would permit a collecting bank to take in remit-
tance for an item on a nonbank payor , "a cashier's check , certified check or
other bank check or obligation " without becoming liable if the remittance is
unpaid . The provision is justified on practical grounds , and would permit a
practice now frequently used.
In general , the section follows KRS 357.090 and 357.100 and would nega-
tive the common law rule to the effect that a collecting bank , in the absence
of agreement otherwise , is authorized to receive only cash in payment for
an item . See Federal Reserve Bank of Richmond v . Malloy, 264 U. S. 160,
68 L. ed . 617 , ( 1924 ) ; Stanley , Banking Law of Kentucky , Sec. 218 .
( 2) Subsection ( 2) states certain action required of a collecting bank
if it is to be relieved of responsibility for payment of these forms of pro-
visional remittance .
( 3) Subsection ( 3) states precisely when a final settlement occurs in
remittance cases .
Section 4-212 . Right of Charge-Back or Refund.
Summary
(1) If a collecting bank has made provisional settlement with its cus-
tomer for an item and itself fails because of dishonor , suspension of pay-
ments by a bank or otherwise to receive a settlement for the item which is
or becomes final, the bank may revoke the settlement it gave, charge -back
the amount of credit for the item to its customer's account or obtain refund
from its customer whether or not it is able to return the items , if by its
midnight deadline or within a reasonable time after it learns the facts it
returns the item or sends notification of the facts . These rights terminate if
and when a settlement for the item received by the bank is or becomes
final (subsection ( 3 ) of UCC 4-211 and subsections (2 ) and (3) of UCC
4-213) .
(2) An intermediary or payor bank may return an unpaid item di-
rectly to the depositary bank and obtain reimbursement .
(3) A depositary bank which is also the payor may charge -back the
amount to its customer's account or obtain refund .
(4) The right to charge-back is not affected by:
(a) prior use of credit given ; or
(b) failure of bank to exercise ordinary care.
(5) A failure to charge -back or claim refund does not affect other
rights of the bank against the customer or any other party .
(6) If credit is given in dollars as the equivalent of value of an item
payable in a foreign currency , the dollar amount of any charge -back or
refund shall be calculated on the basis of buying sight rate for the foreign
currency prevailing on the day person learns of dishonor of item .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 357.020 , 357.110 , 357.030 .
Comment
(1) Accord, KRS 357.030 . Thompson v . First State Bank, 216 Ку . 703 ,
220

288 S. W. 702 ( 1926 ) ( Where rights and interests of innocent third persons
are not affected , the bank has the right to charge-back dishonored paper
which has been credited to the depositor's account as cash ) . Compare Ger-
man Nat. Bank v . Grinstead , 21 K. L. R. 674, 52 S. W. 951 ( 1899 ) . See also,
Stanley , Banking Law of Kentucky , Sec. 131 .
It should be noted that subsection (1 ) requires something more on
the part of the bank than merely charging the account of its customer ;
the bank is required to return the item or send notification by its mid-
night deadline (which would be midnight of the day following the day on
which the bank had again received the item or learned of non -payment ) .
The term "within a longer reasonable time after it learns the facts" would
cover occasional situations where the bank would be unable , through no
fault of its own , to act by its midnight deadline . Presumably , the bank
would have the burden of proving that the delay was caused by circum-
stances beyond its control .
(2) Subsection (2) is designed to speed up the return of unpaid items
by permitting the payor bank , or any collecting bank , to return such an
item direct to the depositary bank and to obtain reimbursement by means
of a collection draft on that bank . The depositary bank , if it had received
provisional settlement for the returned item, would be required to pay the
collection draft; and provisional credits for the item would become final
as between banks in the collection chain . See , Clarke & Bailey, Bank De-
posits and Collections (A.L.I. , Committee on Continuing Legal Education )
p. 89 .
(4) Subparagraph ( 4) preserves the right of charge -back , even where
the customer has been permitted to withdraw credit given to him and has
insufficient funds in his account against which a charge-back may be
exercised . Under such circumstances , the bank would have a right to
demand that its customer deposit an amount sufficient to cover the charge-
back .
(3 ) & ( 5 ) . These subsections require no separate discussion .
(6 ) Subsection ( 6) provides , in effect , that the customer of the de-
positary bank bears the risk of fluctuating exchange rates where he
deposits an item payable in a foreign currency and receives a credit in
American dollars , if such item is subsequently returned unpaid . In other
words , the customer would bear any loss resulting from the fluctuating
foreign exchange rates and would be entitled to any profit resulting there-
from , unless the customer and the depositary bank had agreed otherwise .
The bank would normally neither gain nor lose because of fluctuating
exchange rates . The provision appears justified , since the depositary bank
would be an agent for collection ( UCC 4-201 ) , and, as such, should not bear
the risk involved in collecting items payable in a foreign currency .
It should be noted that the measuring date with respect to the ex-
change rate is when "the person entitled to the charge -back " ( i.e. , the
depositary bank ) learns that it will not receive payment in ordinary course,
rather than when the charge -back is actually made . Accordingly , a de-
positary bank learning of non -payment of an item payable in a fluctuating
foreign currency would be well advised to promptly make the charge -back
so that it will not bear any loss resulting from fluctuating exchange rates
221

between the time it learns that it will not receive payment and the time it
makes the charge -back .
Section 4-213 . Final Payment of Item by Payor Bank ; When
Provisional Debits and Credits Become Final ;
When Certain Credits Become Available for
Withdrawal.
Summary
(1) An item is finally paid by a payor bank when it has done any of
the following, whichever happens first :
(a) paid the item in cash ; or
(b) settled for the item without reserving a right to revoke the
settlement and without having such right under statute,
clearing house rule or agreement ; or
(c) completed posting the item to the indicated account of the
drawer , maker , or other person to be charged therewith ; or
(d) made provisional settlement for the item and failed to revoke
the settlement in the time and manner permitted by statute,
clearing house rule or agreement .
Upon a final payment under subparagraphs ( b ) , (c) or (d) the payor bank
shall be accountable for the amount of the item .
(2) All conditional credits become firm at the time the presenting
bank receives final payment in clearing house transactions .
(3) If a collecting bank receives a settlement for an item which is
or becomes final , (subsections (3 ) of UCC 4-211 , subsection (2) of UCC
4-213) the bank is accountable to its customer for the amount of the items
and any provisional credit given for the item in an account with its custo-
mer becomes final .
(4) Subject to any right of the bank to apply the credit to an obli-
gation of the customer , credit given by a bank for an item in its customers '
accounts becomes available for withdrawal as of right:
(a) in any case where the bank has received a provisional settle-
ment for the item,- when the settlement becomes final and
and the bank has had a reasonable time to learn that it is
final;
(b) in any case where the bank is both a depositary bank and a
payor bank and the item is finally paid , - at the opening
of the bank's second banking day following receipt of the
item .
(5) A deposit of money in a bank is final when made, but subject
to any right of the bank to apply the deposit to an obligation of the custo-
mer , the deposit becomes available for withdrawal as of right at the opening
of the bank's next banking day following receipt of the deposit .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section eliminates any question as to when payment becomes
222

final . Although Kentucky has no such special statutory provisions , an
item is ordinarily regarded as paid when a solvent drawee bank charges the
account of its depositor or where the forwarder or depositor for collection
receives a final acceptable credit of proceeds. For general discussion of
section see , Clarke & Bailey, Bank Deposits and Collections (A.L.I. Commit-
tee on Continuing Legal Education ) Chapter 5; Funk , Pennsylvania Banks
and the Uniform Commercial Code , p. 117 .
Section 4-214 . Insolvency and Preference .
Summary
(1) An item in possession of a payor or collecting bank which sus-
pends payment shall be returned to the presenting bank or closed bank's
customer .
(2 ) If a payor bank pays an item but fails to make a final settlement ,
the owner has a preferred claim .
(3) If a payor or collecting bank gives or receives a provisional settle-
ment for an item and thereafter suspends payments , the suspension does not
prevent or interfere with the settlement becoming final if such finality
occurs automatically upon the lapse of certain time or the happening of
certain events (subsection (3) of UCC 4-211 , subsections ( 1) ( d ) , ( 2 ) and
(3) of UCC 4-213 ) .
(4) If a collecting bank receives final settlement from subsequent
parties , but fails to settle with its customer , the owner of the item has a
preferred claim against the collecting bank .
KRS 357.130.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The rules stated in this section are in accord with KRS 357.130 but
abandon any theory of trust . This distinction stems from the fact that the
proposed provisions are not designed to confer preferential claims but to
establish a cut -off time for the completion of the collection process so that
items progressing far enough may continue to finish .
It should be noted that an amendment of the National Bank Act is
necessary to make this section apply to national banks . This is true in
view of the decision in Jenning v . United States Fidelity & Guaranty Co. ,
294 U. S. 216, 79 L. ed. 869 , ( 1935 ) , where , in construing a similar section
of the Bank Collection Code enacted in Indiana , the Supreme Court said
that its provisions were plainly inconsistent with the system of equal dis-
tribution established by federal law .
Part 3
COLLECTION OF ITEMS : PAYOR BANKS
Introductory Comment
Part 3 of Article 4 contains provisions governing the settlement of items
223

by payor banks . A payor bank is made liable for late return of an item;
and as to the order of acceptance , payment or certification of items , the
bank's discretion .
Section 4-301 . Deferred Posting ; Recovery of Payment by
Return of Items ; Time of Dishonor .
Summary
(1) Where a payor bank receives a demand item other than for im-
mediate payment over the counter , it has until midnight of the next day
after receipt thereof to dishonor or refuse payment .
(2) If a demand item is received for credit on its books , it may be
returned or dishonored within time specified above.
(3) An item is dishonored when notice thereof is sent or when the
item is returned for purposes of dishonor .
(4) An item received through a clearing house is returned when
delivered to the presenting or last collecting bank or to the clearing house
or delivered according to its rules ; in other cases , an item is returned when
delivered to bank's customer or transferor .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 357.125 .
Comment
Accord , KRS 357.125 .
Section 4-302 . Payor Bank's Responsibility for Late Return
of Item .
Summary
In the absence of a valid defense such as breach of a presentment war-
ranty ( UCC 4-207 ( 1 ) ) , settlement effected or the like , if an item is pre-
sented on and received by a payor bank the bank is accountable for the
amount of:
(a) a demand item in any case where such bank , not also the
depositary bank , retains the item beyond midnight of the day
of receipt , or regardless of whether the bank is also the
depositary bank , in any case where it retains the item until
after its midnight deadline ; or
(b) any other properly payable item unless within the time
allowed for acceptance or payment of that item the bank
either accepts or pays the item or returns it and accompany-
ing documents .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section states the customer's rights if the payor bank fails to take
action within the prescribed time set out in UCC 4-301 . KRS 356.113,
excuses delay in giving notice of dishonor when such delay is caused by
224

circumstances beyond the control of the holder , whereas the present section
makes no provision for delay .
Section 4-303 . When Items Subject to Notice , Stop- Order , Legal
Process or Setoff ; Order in Which Items May be
Charged or Certified .
Summary
(1) Any knowledge , notice or stop - order received by, legal process
served upon or setoff exercised by a payor bank , whether or not effective
under other rules of law to terminate , suspend or modify the bank's right
or duty to pay an item or to charge its customer's account for the item ,
comes too late to so terminate , suspend or modify such right or duty if
such is received or served and a reasonable time for the bank to act thereon
expires or the setoff is exercised after the bank has done any of the
following :
(a) accepted or certified the item;
(b) paid the item in cash ;
(c) settled for the item without reserving a right to revoke the
settlement and without having such right under statute,
clearing house rule or agreement ;
(d) posted the item to the account of the drawer ;
(e) becomes accountable for the amount of the item because of
late return of items .
(2 ) Otherwise items may be paid in any order convenient to the bank .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
9 C.J.S. , Banks & Banking, Sec . 348 states :
"Checks should be paid in the order of presentation ; mere priority
in the drawing of the check on a bank is not sufficient to give to
the holder of such check a legal right to preference in payment out
of the funds of the drawer over the holder of checks subsequently
drawn."
In accord , Herndon v . Louisville Banking Co., 10 K. L. R. 584 ( 1888 ) ;
Mt. Sterling Nat. Bank v . Green , 99 Ky . 262, 35 S. W. 911 (1896 ) . See gen-
erally , Stanley , Banking Law of Kentucky , Sec. 126.
However , subsection ( 2) of the proposed section allowing the bank to
pay items in an order convenient to itself , would not appear to be in accord
with the rule set forth above, that "checks should be paid in the order of
presentment ." See , Clarke and Bailey, Bank Deposits and Collections
(A.L.I. , Committee on Continuing Legal Education ) p . 74 .
Part 4
RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN PAYOR BANK AND ITS CUSTOMER
Introductory Comment
Part 4, consisting of seven sections , is concerned with the relationship
225

between the payor bank and the customer -drawer arising from the handling
of the latter's items by the payor bank , as affected by the duties and obli-
gations of the customer -drawer with respect to his account .
Section 4-401 . When Bank May Charge Customer's Account .
Summary
(1) A bank may charge against a customer's account any item
properly payable even though it creates an overdraft .
(2) A bank paying in good faith may charge its customer's accounts
according to :
(a) the original tenor of his altered item; or
(b)
None.
the tenor of his completed item , even though the bank knows
the item has been completed unless the bank has notice that
the completion was improper .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) No Kentucky cases found on this point , but it seems to be in
accord with common law. See , Stanley , Banking Law of Kentucky, Secs . 121
and 130 .
(2) ( a) Accord, Commercial Bank of Grayson v. Arden & Fraley ,
177 Ky . 520 , 197 S. W. 951 , ( 1917 ) ; Stanley , Banking Law of Kentucky , Sec.
146.
(b) Accord, KRS 356.014 , Citizens Bank of Morehead v . Nickell ,
277 Ky . 424, 126 S. W. 2d 820 ( 1939 ) ; See Stanley , Banking Law of Kentucky ,
Sec . 148 .
Section 4-402 . Bank's Liability to Customer for Wrongful
Dishonor .
Summary
A payor bank is liable to its customer for damages proximately caused
by the wrongful dishonor of an item . When the dishonor occurs through
mistake , liability is limited to actual damages proved . If so proximately
caused and proved , damages may include damages for an arrest or prose-
cution of the customer or other consequential damages . Whether any con-
sequential damages are proximately caused by the wrongful dishonor is a
question of fact to be determined in each case .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Accord , Berea Bank & Trust Co. , v . Mokwa , 194 Ky . 556, 239 S. W. 1044
(1922); Stanley , Banking Law of Kentucky , Sec. 121 .
226

Section 4-403 . Customer's Right to Stop Payment ; Burden of
Proof of Loss .
Summary
(1) A customer may order his bank to stop payment on any item
provided the order is received in time to give bank opportunity to act
before it has taken action described in UCC 4-303 .
(2) An oral order is binding upon the bank only for 14 days unless
confirmed in writing within that period . A written order is effective for
6 months unless renewed in writing .
(3) Customer must establish fact and amount of loss resulting from
payment of item contrary to a binding stop payment order .
KRS 287.405 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Accord generally : Stanley , Banking Law of Kentucky , Sec. 128 , and
cases therein cited . Limitation as to the binding nature of oral order for
period of 14 days is new .
Section 4-404 . Bank Not Obligated to Pay Check More Than
Six Months Old.
Summary
A bank need not pay a check ( other than a certified check) presented
more than six months after its date but may charge customer's account for
payment made thereafter in good faith.
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
At present , in Kentucky, under the Negotiable Instruments Law , KRS
356.186 , checks must be presented within a " reasonable" time . See , Stanley ,
Banking Law of Kentucky, Sec. 123 .
Section 4-405 . Death or Incompetence of Customer .
Summary
( 1) A payor or collecting bank's authority to accept, pay or collect
an item or to account for proceeds of its collection if otherwise effective ,
is not rendered ineffective by incompetence of a customer of either bank
existing at the time the item is issued or its collection is undertaken if
the bank does not know of an adjudication of incompetence. Neither death
nor incompetence of a customer revokes such authority to accept, pay ,
collect an account until the bank knows of such fact and has reasonable
opportunity to act on it.
(2) Even with knowledge , for 10 days after death, a bank may pay a
check drawn prior to death unless there is a stop payment order .
227

None.
( 1)
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
In accord as to death of depositor revoking the bank's authority
to pay the check, see , Weiand v. State Nat . Bank , 112 Ky . 310, 65 S. W. 617
(1901 ) . See , Stanley , Banking Law of Kentucky , Sec . 133. No Kentucky
cases as to incompetence .
(2 ) New. See , Clarke and Bailey , Bank Deposits and Collections ,
(A. L. I. , Committee on Continuing Legal Education ) p. 135-136 .
Section 4-406 . Customer's Duty to Discover and Report
Unauthorized Signature or alteration .
Summary
(1) When a bank sends to its customer a statement of account , the
customer must exercise reasonable care and promptness to examine the
statement and notify the bank of any unauthorized signature or alteration .
(2 ) If the bank establishes that the customer failed to comply with
the duties imposed on him by subsection ( 1) , the customer is precluded
from asserting against the bank :
(a) his unauthorized signature or any alteration on the item if
the bank also establishes that it suffered loss by reason of
such failure ; and
(b) an unauthorized signature or alteration by the same wrong-
doer on any other item paid in good faith by the bank after
the first item and statement was available to the customer
for a reasonable period not exceeding 14 days , and before the
bank receives notification from the customer of any such
unauthorized signature or alteration .
(3 ) The preclusion under subsection ( 2 ) does not apply if the custo-
mer establishes lack of ordinary care on the part of the bank in paying
the item (s ) .
(4) Without regard to care or lack of care of either the customer
or the bank if such report (as provided in subsection ( 1) ) is not made in
one year , or an unauthorized indorsement is not reported within three
years , the customer is precluded from asserting any rights against the bank.
(5) If under this section a payor bank has a valid defense against a
claim of a customer upon or resulting from payment of an item and waives
or fails upon request to assert the defense, the bank may not assert against
any collecting bank or other prior party presenting or transferring the item
a claim based upon the unauthorized signature or alteration giving rise to
the customer's claim .
KRS 356.023 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The provisions of this section are in accord with the weight of authority .
9 C.J.S. , Banks and Banking , Sec. 356 (d) ( 1 ) :
228

"Weight of authority , and perhaps of reason , supports view that ,
when a depositor's pass book has been written up and returned to
him with the canceled checks which have been charged to his
account , it is his duty to examine such checks within a reasonable
time and to report all forgeries or alterations which he has dis-
covered, failing in which he cannot, if his failure has resulted in
detriment to the bank , dispute the correctness of the payments
shown , and the account as rendered becomes an account stated
between the parties ."
This is the view followed by the Kentucky courts . See , Stanley , Bank-
ing Law of Kentucky , Secs. 144 through 154 .
Kentucky has no statutes setting out specific time limitations as are
found in this section .
Section 4-407 . Payor Bank's Right to Subrogation on
Improper Payment .
Summary
If a payor bank pays an item over a stop payment order or otherwise
under circumstances giving the drawer basis for objection , to the extent
necessary to prevent loss to the bank it is subrogated to the rights :
(a) of any holder in due course on the item against the drawer
or maker ; and
(b) of the payee or other holder against the drawer or maker
either on the item or the transaction out of which the item
arose; and
(c) of the drawer or maker against the payee or other holder
with respect to the transaction out of which the item arose.
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
No Kentucky decisions as to right to subrogation . See generally ,
Stanley , Banking Law of Kentucky , Sec. 128 .
Part 5
COLLECTION OF DOCUMENTARY DRAFTS
Introductory Comment
A common practice in commercial transactions is for the seller to
ship an order of goods to the buyer and to draw a draft on the buyer for the
agreed amount . He then attaches the draft to the documents of title (bill
of lading, etc. ) and the documents are sent through banking channels to
the purchaser . This practice was not separately covered in the NIL. In
229

Part 5, the UCC undertakes to state the duties of a bank in handling docи-
mentary drafts for collection .
The duty of a bank handling a documentary draft for a customer is
stated in UCC 4-501 . Very often , the drafts provide they shall be presented
on arrival of the goods. The bank's position in such cases is covered by
UCC 4-502 . The bank's duty toward the goods in case of dishonor is pro-
vided in UCC 4-503 while UCC 4-504 states when and to what extent the
bank may deal with the goods .
Section 4-501 . Handling of Documentary Drafts ; Duty to Send
for Presentment and to Notify Customer of
Dishonor .
Summary
A bank taking a documentary draft for collection must present it or
send it for presentment and upon dishonor must notify its customer even
though it discounted or bought the draft or extended credit available for
withdrawal as of right.
KRS 357.050 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Accord as to items accepted for collection : KRS 357.050 , Bank Col-
lection Code . The requirement of giving reasons for notice of dishonor
states bank practice . The section contains new law in imposing the duties
of presentment and notice when bank has discounted or bought a draft,
but in practice , the self -interest of a bank will cause it to present an item
so as to receive payment and to give notice so as not to lose the indorser's
liability of its customer .
Section 4-502 . Presentment of " On Arrival " Drafts .
Summary
The collecting bank need not present an "on arrival" draft until a
reasonable time for arrival has expired . Refusal to pay or accept because
the goods have not arrived is not dishonor ; the bank must notify its trans-
feror of the refusal but need not present again until instructed to do so or it
learns of the arrival of the goods .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
No Kentucky cases were found on this point . This section apparently
restates the bank practice of presenting upon receipt of the draft and
again after a reasonable time has elapsed.
230

Section 4-503 . Responsibility of Presenting Bank for Documents
and Goods ; Report of Reasons for Dishonor ;
Referee in Case of Need.
Summary
Unless otherwise instructed and except as provided in Article 5, a
bank presenting a documentary draft :
(a) must deliver the documents to the drawee on acceptance of
the draft if it is payable more than 3 days after presentment ;
otherwise , only on payment ; and
(b) upon dishonor , may seek and follow instructions from any
referee in case of need designated in the draft or it must use
diligence and good faith to learn reason for dishonor , must
inform its transferor , and must request instruction .
But the bank has no duty as to the goods except to follow instructions
seasonably received ; it has a right to reimbursement for expenses and to
prepayment as indemnity for such expenses .
KRS 356.131 (3) .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The reference to Article 5 in the preamble covers drafts drawn under
a letter of credit .
Kentucky has not enacted the Uniform Bills of Lading Act ; therefore
the enactment of the UCC would result in new statutes covering this phase
of commercial transactions .
In accord with subsection (8 ) : KRS 356.131 . This section makes it
clear that the referee in case of need may give instructions . The NIL
(KRS Ch. 356) is silent as to his powers once a holder "resorts " to him .
There do not seem to be any cases in the United States on Referees
in Case of Need . See Brannan , Negotiable Instruments Law Annotated (7th
ed. Beutel 1948 ) p. 1228 .
No Kentucky cases were found discussing a bank's liability or obli-
gations with respect to the goods represented by the documents attached
to a draft , or with respect to the duty to request instructions . It seems
reasonable, however , to require that the draft and documents be kept at
destination pending instructions , and that the bank be not required to take
any action involving further expense without prepayment or indemnity .
Section 4-504 . Privilege of Presenting Bank to Deal With Goods ;
Security Interest for Expenses .
Summary
(1) A presenting bank which does not receive instructions after
requesting them upon dishonor may store, sell or otherwise deal with the
goods in any reasonable manner .
231

(2) The bank has a lien upon the goods or their proceeds for its
reasonable expenses which may be enforced like a seller's lien.
KRS 361.460 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) No Kentucky cases were found on this point . This section is not
intended to impose an obligation on the bank .
(2) No Kentucky cases were found on this point . As to enforcement
of unpaid seller's lien, See UCC 2-706 and Annotations thereto .
232

ARTICLE FIVE
LETTERS OF CREDIT
Introductory Comment
Letters of credit are a highly specialized device for transmission of
monies . An important characteristic is the obligation of the issuer , stated
in the letter , to honor drafts drawn upon it. This undertaking adds the
credit of a bank or other institution of known financial standing to the
obligation of the customer of the issuer in the transaction underlying the
letter of credit .
No body of statutory law deals specifically with letters of credit . This
is true not only in the United States and England but even in civil law
countries which have a long tradition of codification . A number of detailed
rules governing use of letters of credit are stated in the "Uniform Customs
and Practice for Commercial Documentary Credits Fixed by the Thirteenth
Congress of the International Chamber of Commerce " . Other rules , com-
prising the substantial basis of the law of letters of credit in this country ,
rest in the decisional law of contracts , derived in part from the law mer-
chant but absorbed into the common law. The number of decisions in the
field is small in relation to the number and variety of transactions involved
and their economic and financial importance .
Article 5 is not intended to be a comprehensive statute . Instead it
attempts to state basic principles , leaving to custom, agreement and de-
cisional law many details , especially the tests for determining conformity
of documents to the letter of credit prescribing them .
Most of Article 5 is proposed as a statute declaratory of present law.
Other provisions appear to be intended to resolve doubts , to settle conflicts
of authority or of opinion , or to write into "law " what are now contractual
rules embodied in the Uniform Customs and subject in each case to adoption
or rejection by the parties .
Section 5-101 . Short Title .
Summary
Article known and may be cited as Uniform Commercial Code-Letters
of Credit .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Kentucky presently has no statutory provisions governing letters of
credit, except to the extent that the provisions of the Negotiable Instru-
ments Law, KRS Chapter 356 , may affect drafts drawn under such letters .
The applicability of the Negotiable Instruments Law , however , is very
limited . Case law on the subject of letters of credit is practically non
235

existent in Kentucky . Occasional reference is made in these comments to
decisions of courts of jurisdictions other than Kentucky where there is no
controlling Kentucky law .
Section 5-102 . Scope.
(1) This article applies :
Summary
(a) to a credit issued by a bank if the credit requires a docu-
mentary draft or a documentary demand for payment ; and
(b) to a credit issued by a person other than a bank if the credit
requires that the draft or demand for payment be accom-
panied by a document of title ; and
(c) to a credit issued by a bank or other person if the credit is
not within subparagraphs ( a ) or ( b ) but conspicuously states
that it is a letter of credit or is conspicuously so entitled .
(2) Unless the engagement meets the requirements of subsection
( 1) , this article does not apply to engagements to make advances or to
honor drafts or demands for payment , to authorities to pay or purchase ,
to guarantees or to general agreements .
(3 ) This article deals with some but not all of the rules and concepts
of letters of credit as such rules or concepts have developed prior to this
act or may hereafter develop . The fact that this article states a rule does
not by itself require , imply or negate application of the same or a converse
rule to a situation not provided for or to a person not specified by this
article .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
In its simplest form a letter of credit is an engagement by the issuer
(bank ) , made at the request of the customer (buyer ) , to honor the bene-
ficiary's (seller's ) drafts . Subsection ( 1) states precisely the scope of Article
5. All bank letters of credit of a documentary nature are governed by the
article . So far as credits issued by persons other than a bank are concerned ,
only a credit which is in the traditional form , requiring a draft or demand
for payment accompanied by documents of title , falls mandatorily within
the scope of the article . Clean credits those requiring presentation of
drafts only , without documents are included if the document clearly
indicates that it is a letter of credit .
Banking practice with respect to letters of credit , not only in Kentucky
but also throughout the country and to a large extent internationally , has
tended to become unified because of the adherence by banks to a set of
rules promulgated and revised from time to time by the International
Chamber of Commerce . The current regulation , effective since January
1, 1952, is known as the "Uniform Customs and Practice of Commercial
Documentary Credits Fixed by the Thirteenth Congress of the International
Chamber of Commerce ." Originally adopted in 1938 , the Uniform Customs
are effective in controlling relations between banks which have agreed
236

to adhere to them . Frequently , letter of credit forms used by banks incor-
porate the regulations by reference .
Many of the Code provisions adopt rules established by the Uniform
Customs ; however , the draftsmen have not solidified all the details of
banking practice currently set forth in the Customs . Subsection ( 3 ) makes
it clear that this article is not intended to cover the entire range of law
which is applicable to letters of credit and is intended to leave the door
open for continuing case development in this area. The most important
omission in the coverage of Article 5 is detailed provision as to what
constitutes compliance of particular documents which may be called for
under the letter of credit . The existing and developing case law and
banking practice is intended to supply such detailed provision .
Section 5-103 . Definitions .
Summary
(1) In this article unless the context otherwise requires :
(a) " Credit " or "letter of credit " means an engagement by a
bank or other person made at the request of a customer and
of a kind within the scope of this article ( UCC 5-102 ) that
the issuer will honor drafts or other demands for payment
upon compliance with the conditions specified in the credit .
A credit may be either revocable or irrevocable . The engage-
ment may be either an agreement to honor or a statement
that the bank or other person is authorized to honor .
(b) A "documentary draft " or a "documentary demand for pay-
ment" is one , honor of which is conditioned upon the presen-
tation of a document or documents . " Documents " means any
paper including document of title , security , invoice , certificate ,
notice of default and the like.
(c) An " issuer" is a bank or other person issuing a credit .
(d) A "beneficiary " of a credit is a person who is entitled under
its terms to draw or demand payment .
(e) An "advising bank " is a bank which gives notification of the
issuance of a credit by another bank .
(f) A "confirming bank" is a bank which engages either that it
will itself honor a credit already issued by another bank or
that such a credit will be honored by the issuer or a third
bank.
(g) A "customer " is a buyer or other person who causes an issuer
to issue a credit . The term also includes a bank which pro-
cures issuance or confirmation on behalf of that bank's custo-
mer .
(2) Other definitions applying to this article and the sections in which
they appear are :
