Section 5-105 . Consideration .
Summary
No consideration is necessary to establish a credit or to enlarge or
otherwise modify its terms .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Where there is an absence or failure of consideration , the law
has not recognized the enforceability of bills of exchange, checks, or promis-
sory notes . According to KRS 356.024 and 356.028 of the Negotiable Instru-
ments Law, the absence or failure of consideration is a good defense against
any person not a holder in due course, although there is a presumption of
consideration in every negotiable instrument . The absence of consideration
in a letter of credit , however , does not affect validity in the proposed code.
The same was true under the law merchant and there is no evidence that
such law has been substantially changed by the courts in the United States,
but in many instances the courts have found difficulty in seeking to justify
the lack of consideration under common law rules governing contracts . For
a discussion of various legal theories advanced to support the enforceability
of letters of credit , see Doelger v . The Battery Park National Bank , 201 A. D.
515 , 194 N. Y. S. 582 ( 1922 ) ; Lamborn v . The National Park Bank of New
York , 240 N. Y. 520 , 148 Ν . Ε. 664 ( 1925 ) ; Moss v . Old Colony Trust Com-
pany , 246 Mass . 139, 140 N. E. 803 ( 1923 ) ; and text writers : Mead , Docu-
mentary Letters of Credit , 22 Col. L. Rev. 297, 300 ( 1922 ) ( offer and accept-
ance , guaranty , estoppel, equitable assignment , third party beneficiary
contract , contract supported by conventional consideration ) ; 4 Williston ,
Contracts Sec. 1011D ( 1936 ed .) ( "The best view seems to be that the
promise of the issuing bank's customer to reimburse the bank and pay a
commission for issuing the credit is the consideration for the promise made
by that bank or by that bank's correspondent at its request to the bene-
ficiary of the credit . The validity of this form of credit , though unusual
has been sustained in the United States in other classes of cases , that is, it
has been held that consideration for a promise need not proceed from the
promisee" ) . See also McLaughlin , The Letter of Credit Provisions of the
Proposed Uniform Commercial Code , 63 Harv. L. Rev. 1373 , 1379 , ( 1950 ) ;
Trimble , The Law Merchant and the Letter of Credit , 61 Harv . L. Rev. 981 ,
994 ( 1948 ) .
Section 5-106 . Time and Effect of Establishment of Credit .
Summary
(1 ) Unless otherwise agreed a credit is established :
(a) as regards the customer as soon as a letter of credit is sent to
him or the letter of credit or an authorized written advice
of its issuance is sent to the beneficiary ; and
(b) as regards the beneficiary when he receives a letter of credit
or an authorized written advice of its issuance .
239

(2) Unless otherwise agreed once an irrevocable credit is established
as regards the customer it can be modified or revoked only with the con-
sent of the customer and once it is established as regards the beneficiary
it can be modified or revoked only with his consent.
(3 ) Unless otherwise agreed after a revocable credit is established
it may be modified or revoked by the issuer without notice to or consent
from the customer or beneficiary .
(4) Notwithstanding any modification or revocation of a revocable
credit any person authorized to honor or negotiate under the terms of
the original credit is entitled to reimbursement for or honor of any draft
or demand for payment duly honored or negotiated before receipt of notice
of the modification or revocation and the issuer in turn is entitled to reim-
bursement from its customer .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Subsection ( 1) states when a credit is established . Here again , there is
no Kentucky law . However , cases in other states indicate that the general
rule is that a letter of credit need no formal acceptance other than as is
stated therein . Sec . 9 C. J. S., Banks and Banking, Sec . 176 ( a) .
Considering case law as set by decisions in other jurisdictions , no
change is effected by subsections ( 2 ) and ( 3 ) as to the modification of
revocable or irrevocable credits . 9 C. J. S. , Banks & Banking , Sec . 177
states :
"One of the parties to a letter of credit cannot , of its own motion ,
change the terms thereof without the other's consent; nor can it
be cancelled or revoked at the direction of the buyer . A revocable
letter of credit , however , may be modified after its issuance by
annexing conditions thereto which must be complied with or the
issuing bank is not liable thereon . "
In accord with subsection (4) : 6 Michie , Banks and Banking , Ch. 12 ,
Sec . 33b .
Section 5-107 . Advice of Credit ; Confirmation ; Error in
Statement of Terms .
Summary
( 1) Unless otherwise specified an advising bank by advising a credit
issued by another bank does not assume any obligation to honor drafts
drawn or demands for payment made under the credit but it does assume
obligation for the accuracy of its own statement.
(2 ) A confirming bank by confirming a credit becomes directly obli-
gated on the credit to the extent of its confirmation as though it were its
issuer and acquires the rights of an issuer .
(3 ) Even though an advising bank incorrectly advises the terms of a
credit , it is authorized to advice the credit is established as against the
issuer to the extent of its original terms .
240

(4) Unless otherwise specified , the customer bears as against the
issuer all errors in transmission and translation of a message relating to a
credit .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) By imposing upon an advising bank liability for inaccurate notifi-
cation , subsection ( 1) departs from present practice as stated in Article 6
of the Uniform Customs under which an advising bank assumes no respon-
sibility whatsoever . The subsection does not make it clear , however ,
whether the obligation of accurate notification is an obligation owed to
the customer and issuer , as well as to the beneficiary . See Note , 62 Yale
L. J. 227 , 255 ( 1953 ) . Subsection ( 2) sets out the independent obligation
of a confirming bank .
(2) Subsection (3) states the effect of an incorrect advice . Subsection
(4) substantially conforms to the provisions of Article 12 of the Uniform
Customs insofar as it relieves banks of responsibility for errors in trans-
mission , translation , or interpretation of messages relating to credits , and in
addition clearly allocates such responsibility to the customer .
Section 5-108 . " Notation Credit " ; Exhaustion of Credit .
Summary
(1) A credit specifying that any person purchasing or paying drafts
drawn or demands for payment made under it must note the amount of
the draft or demand on the letter or advice of credit is a " notation credit" .
(2) Under a notation credit :
(a) a person paying the beneficiary or purchasing a draft or
demand for payment from him acquires a right to honor only
if the appropriate notation is made and by transferring or
forwarding for honor the documents under the credit such a
person warrants to the issuer that the notation has been made ;
and
(b) unless the credit or a signed statement that an appropriate
notation has been made accompanies the draft or demand for
payment the issuer may delay honor until evidence of notation
has been procured which is satisfactory to it but its obligation
and that of its customer continue for a reasonable time not
exceeding thirty days to obtain such evidence.
(3) If the credit is not a notation credit :
(a) the issuer may honor complying drafts or demands for pay-
ment presented to it in the order in which they are presented
and is discharged pro tanto by honor of any such draft or
demand;
(b) as between competing good faith purchasers of complying
drafts or demands the person first purchasing has priority
241

None.
over a subsequent purchaser even though the later purchased
draft or demand has been first honored .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section defines a notation credit and states the rules which are
applicable to notation and other credits in the event of exhaustion of
credit .
Section 5-109 . Issuer's Obligation to Its Customers .
Summary
(1) An issuer's obligation to its customer includes good faith and ob-
servance of any general banking usage but unless otherwise agreed does not
include liability or responsibility :
(a) for performance of the underlying contract for sale or other
transaction between the customer and the beneficiary ; or
(b) for any act or omission of any person other than itself or its
own branch or for loss or destruction of a draft , demand or
document in transit or in the possession of others ; or
(c) based on knowledge or lack of knowledge of any usage of any
particular trade .
(2) An issuer must examine documents with care so as to ascertain
that on their face they appear to comply with the terms of the credit but
unless otherwise agreed assumes no liability or responsibility for the genu-
ineness , falsification or effect of any document which appears on such ex-
amination to be regular on its face .
(3 ) A non -bank issuer is not bound by any banking usage of which it
has no knowledge .
Kentucky Statutes
None.
Comment
(1) Subsection ( 1) relieves the issuing bank of any responsibility for
acts of its correspondents . This release of liability is frequently effected by
the customer designating all parties involved , other than the issuer , as his
agents rather than as agents of the issuer . Letters issued subject to the
Uniform Customs might automatically incorporate similar releases found in
Articles 11 , 12, 13 and 14 .
(2 ) Subsection ( 2) restates the rule of Article 9 of the Uniform Cus-
toms : "Banks must examine all documents and papers with care so as to
ascertain that on their face they appear to be in order " ; and the rule of
Article 11 of the Uniform Customs :
242

"Banks assume no liability or responsibility for the form , suf-
ficiency , correctness, genuineness, falsification or legal effect of
any documents or papers , ..."
See Kingdom of Sweden v . New York Trust Co., 197 Misc . 431 , 96 N.Y.S.
2d 779 ( 1949) .
(3 ) Subsection ( 3) is inserted to make clear that a non -bank issuer
is not held to knowledge of usage which is not a part of its normal business .
Section 5-110 . Availability of Credit in Portions ; Presenter's
Reservation of Lien or Claim .
Summary
( 1) Unless otherwise specified , a beneficiary may use a credit in such
portions as he desires .
(2) Unless otherwise specified a person presenting a documentary
draft or demand for payment under a credit relinquishes all claim to the
documents , and a person transferring such draft or demand or causing such
presentment authorizes such relinquishment . An explicit reservation of
claim makes the draft or demand non-complying .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Subsection ( 1) codifies current commercial custom . Letter of
credit forms frequently allow use of a credit in portions , and the Uniform
Customs expressly permit honor upon partial shipment ( Uniform Customs ,
Art. 6) .
(2) Subsection ( 2) recognizes the basic purpose of a documentary
letter of credit . It is designed to permit a buyer to obtain goods , while at the
same time assuring a distant seller of prompt payment . If the drafts are
duly honored , its purpose is accomplished , and the buyer is entitled to docu-
ments without reservation of any claim or lien .
Section 5-111 . Warranties on Transfer and Presentment .
Summary
( 1) Unless otherwise agreed , the beneficiary by transferring or pre-
senting a documentary draft or demand for payment warrants that the
necessary conditions of the credit have been complied with in addition to
any warranties arising under Articles 3, 4, 7 and 8 .
( 2 ) Unless otherwise agreed , a negotiating , advising , confirming , col-
lecting or issuing bank presenting or transferring a draft or demand for
payment under a credit warrants only the matters warranted by a collecting
bank under Article 4 and any such bank transferring a document warrants
only the matters warranted by an intermediary under Articles 7 and 8.
Kentucky Statutes
None.
243

Comment
(1) Subsection ( 1) sets out the warranties made by the beneficiary of
a credit .
(2) See warranties made by collecting bank UCC 4-207 , supra , and
comments thereto .
Section 5-112 . Time Allowed for Honor or Rejection ;
Withholding Honor or Rejection by Consent ;
" Presenter " .
Summary
(1) A bank to which a documentary draft or demand for payment is
presented under a credit may without dishonor of the draft, demand or
credit:
(a) defer honor until the close of the third banking day following
receipt of the documents ; and
(b) further defer honor if the presenter consents thereto .
Failure to honor within the time specified constitutes dishonor of the draft
or demand and of the credit .
(2) Upon dishonor the bank may unless otherwise instructed fulfill its
duty to return the draft or demand and the documents by holding them at
the disposal of the presenter and sending him an advice to that effect .
(3 ) "Presenter " means any person presenting a draft or demand for
payment for honor under a credit even though that person is a confirming
bank or other correspondent which is acting under an issuer's authorization .
KRS 356.136 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1 ) Subsection ( 1) extends the time now allowed under the Negotiable
Instruments Law within which the drawee is permitted to consider whether
to dishonor or to accept the draft . KRS 356.136 allows the drawee 24 hours
after presentment in which to decide whether he will accept a bill of ex-
change. This time extension is made only as to drafts under a letter of credit .
It takes time to examine the accompanying documents , additional time which
is not allowed in the case of ordinary drafts . UCC 3-506 requires acceptance
in the latter case by the close of the next business day following present-
ment .
Section 5-113 . Indemnities .
Summary
(1) A bank seeking to obtain (whether for itself or another ) honor ,
negotiation or reimbursement under a credit may give an indemnity to in-
duce such honor , negotiation or reimbursement .
244

(2) An indemnity agreement inducing honor , negotiation or reimburse-
ment :
(a) unless otherwise explicitly agreed, applies to defects in the
documents but not in the goods ; and
(b) unless a longer time is explicitly agreed, expires at the end of
ten business days following receipt of the documents by the
ultimate customer unless notice of objection is sent before such
expiration date. Thte ultimate customer may send notice of
objection to the person from whom he received the documents
and any bank receiving such notice is under a duty to send
notice to its transferor before its midnight deadline .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Ordinarily , a bank is not permitted to act as surety or guarantor of a
paper in which it has no beneficial interest . Nowell v . Equitable Trust Co. ,
249 Mass . 585, 144 Ν.Ε. 749 ( 1924) ; 8 Zollman , Banks and Banking , Sec. 5122 .
However , if a bank's guaranty is a part of, and incident to, the bank's bank-
ing business, the act is not then ultra vires . Subsection ( 1) makes it clear
that a bank seeking payment or negotiation may give indemnity as a proper
part of banking business . Without this provision there might be some ques-
tion as to whether such indemnities were incident to the bank's transaction
of business .
Subsection (2) sets out some of the legal effects of an indemnity agree-
ment .
Section 5-114 . Issuer's Duty and Privilege to Honor ;
Right to Reimbursement .
Summary
(1) An issuer must honor a draft or demand for payment complying
with the terms of the credit regardless of whether the goods or documents
conform to the underlying contract for sale or other contract between the
customer and the beneficiary . The issuer is not excused from honor of such
a draft or demand by reason of an additional general term that all docuи-
ments must be satisfactory to the issuer , but an issuer may require that
specified documents must be satisfactory to it .
(2) Unless otherwise agreed , when documents appear on their face to
comply with the terms of a credit but a required document does not in fact
conform to the warranties made on negotiation or transfer of a document of
title (UCC 7-507 ) or of a security ( UCC 8-306 ) or is forged or fraudulent or
there is fraud in the transaction :
(a) the issuer must honor the draft or demand for payment if
honor is demanded by a negotiating bank or other holder of
the draft or demand which has taken the draft or demand
245

under the credit and under circumstances which would make
it a holder in due course ( UCC 3-302 ) and in an appropriate
case would make it a person to whom a document of title has
been duly negotiated (UCC 7-502 ) or a bona fide purchaser of
a security ( UCC 8-302 ) ; and
(b) in all other cases as against its customer , an issuer acting in
good faith may honor the draft or demand for payment despite
notification from the customer of fraud , forgery or other defect
not apparent on the face of the documents but a court of ap-
propriate jurisdiction may enjoin such honor .
( 3 ) Unless otherwise agreed , an issuer which has duly honored a draft
or demand for payment is entitled to immediate reimbursement of any
payment made under the credit and to be put in effectively available funds
not later than the day before maturity of any acceptance made under the
credit .
None.
(1 )
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The initial clause of subsection ( 1) casts into statutory form the
legal and commercial understanding that non-conformance of goods or docu-
ments to the underlying sales contract is not ground for dishonor . See ,
O'Meara v . National Park Bank , 239 N.Y. 386, 146 Ν.Ε. 636 ( 1925 ) ; Uniform
Customs , Arts . 1, 9 and UCC 5-102 ( 1 ) .
(2) This subsection is in accord with the law as stated in 9 C.J.S. ,
Banks & Banking , Sec. 178b :
"The bank deals in documents , not merchandise ; it undertakes that
it will pay any draft drawn and negotiated pursuant to the terms of
the letter of credit ; its liability on its letter of credit does not de-
pend on the terms of the sales contract which gave rise to the letter
of credit , but it may pay on the required documents , and , in the
absence of an express provision in the letter of credit to that effect ,
it is not required to look beyond the accompanying documents to
ascertain whether the sales contract has actually been complied
with ."
There is some question as to whether this subsection may not incor-
porate a change from present law. Again, turning to C.J.S. , ( supra , p . 390)
in the absence of Kentucky cases in point it is found that if a bank knows
that a document is false or illegal , even though correct in form , it cannot be
called upon to recognize such document as complying with the terms of the
letter of credit . Under the subsection , even if notified , of an alleged forgery
or fraud , the bank is not obligated to endanger its own credit on the basis of
a question of fact which is in any case disputed and difficult of proof. So ,
the bank may honor the claim against itself , in spite of such a notice , and is
entitled to reimbursement for such honor . However , this is subject to the
power of a court to enjoin honor in the circumstances described in the
section.
246

(3) Subsection ( 3) states substantially the existing business practice ,
although no time limitation is indicated by present law. 9 C.J.S. , Banks &
Banking , Sec. 179 , states , "A bank is entitled to reimbursements from its
customer for advances made in good faith and within the terms of a letter
of credit issued at his request ." Note , however , that a time for reimburse-
ment is often included in an application form for a letter of credit . See
Carnegie v . Morrison , 2 Metc . 381, (Mass . 1841 ) .
Section 5-115 . Remedy for Improper Dishonor or Anticipatory
Repudiation .
Summary
(1) When an issuer wrongfully dishonors a draft or demand for pay-
ment presented under a credit the person entitled to honor has with respect
to any documents the rights of a person in the position of a seller ( UCC
2-707 ) and may recover from the issuer the face amount of the draft or de-
mand together with incidental damages under UCC 2-710 on seller's inci-
dental damages and interest but less any amount realized by resale or other
use or disposition of the subject matter of the transaction . In the event no
resale or other utilization is made the documents , goods or other subject
matter involved in the transaction must be turned over to the issuer on pay-
ment of judgment .
(2) When an issuer wrongfully cancels or repudiates a credit before
presentment of a draft or demand for payment drawn under it, the benefici-
ary has the rights of a seller after anticipatory repudiation by the buyer
under UCC 2-610 if he learns of the repudiation in time reasonably to avoid
procurement of the required documents . Otherwise the beneficiary has an
immediate right of action for wrongful dishonor .
Kentucky Statutes
None.
Comment
This section is in accord with present law . 9 C.J.S. , Banks & Banking ,
Sec . 180 provides : "A bank issuing a letter of credit is liable for the pay-
ment of drafts drawn thereunder where the terms of the letter of credit are
complied with ." The provision as to damages which the seller is entitled to
receive is also in accord with present law. See , 9 C.J.S. , Banks & Banking ,
Sec . 183. Kentucky courts recognize an action for anticipatory repudiation
of a contract , therefore subsection ( 2) appears to be consistent with existing
law .
Section 5-116 . Transfer and Assignment .
Summary
(1) The right to draw under a credit can be transferred or assigned
only when the credit is expressly designated as transferable or assignable.
247

(2) Even though the credit specifically states that it is nontransferable
or nonassignable , the beneficiary may before performance of the conditions
of the credit assign his rights to proceeds. Such an assignment is an assign-
ment of a contractual right under Article 9 on Secured Transactions and is
governed by that article except that :
(a) the assignment is ineffective until the letter of credit or advice
of credit is delivered to the assignee which delivery constitutes
perfection of the security interest under Article 9 ; and
(b) the issuer may honor drafts or demands for payment drawn
under the credit until it receives a notification of the assign-
ment signed by the beneficiary which reasonably identifies the
credit involved in the assignment and contains a request to pay
the assignee ; and
(c) after what reasonably appears to be such a notification has
been received , the issuer may without dishonor refuse to accept
or pay even to a person otherwise entitled to honor until the
letter of credit or advice of credit is exhibited to the issuer .
(3) Except where the beneficiary has effectively assigned his right to
draw on his right to proceeds , nothing in this section limits his right to
transfer or negotiate drafts or demands drawn under the credit .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1 ) Subsection ( 1 ) codifies the commercial understanding contained in
Art . 49 of the Uniform Customs , although the Code does not further restrict
transferability to the extent found in the Customs . Art. 49 of the Customs
provides that "... the credit can be transferred once only ..." even when
it is designated as transferable .
(2) Subsection ( 2) states the procedure to be followed and the rules
governing the making of an assignment of the proceeds of a credit .
(3) Subsection ( 3 ) is included to make clear the limited nature of
subsections ( 1) and ( 2) and thus prevent any negative inference from those
subsections .
Section 5-117 . Insolvency of Bank Holding Funds for
Documentary Credit .
Summary
(1) Where an issuer or an advising or confirming bank or a bank which
has for a customer procured issuance of a credit by another bank becomes
insolvent before final payment under the credit and the credit is one to
which this article is made applicable by paragraphs (a) or (b) of UCC
5-102 ( 1 ) on scope , the receipt or allocation of funds or collateral to secure
or meet obligations under the credit shall have the following results :
248

(a) funds turned over specifically for purpose of paying drafts or
demands for payment under a designated credit , are to be used
for such drafts or demands in preference over general creditors
of the issuer or bank ;
(b) if credit expires or beneficiary surrenders it, persons giving
funds thereto are entitled to return of unused portion ;
(c) a charge to an account for paying drafts or demands for pay-
ment falls under the same rules as if the funds had been
drawn out in cash and then turned over with specific instruc-
tions .
(2) After honor or reimbursement under this section, the customer is
entitled to receive the documents involved .
Kentucky Statutes
None .
Comment
Under this section, when insolvency occurs before the letter of credit
transaction is completed , both the outstanding liabilities , the security held
and funds provided to indemnify against those liabilities , and the related
drafts and documents , are regarded as separate from deposit liabilities and
from general assets .
The section is in accordance with the tendency of the case law . Com-
pare Greenough v . Munroe , 53 F. 2d 362 (2d Cir. 1931 ) ; 80 A.L.R. 797 ;
cert . denied, 284 U. S. 672 , 76 L. ed . 568, 52 S. C. 127 ; In re Canal Bank and
Trust Co. , 178 La. 575, 152 So. 297 ( 1933 ) .
249

# ARTICLE SIX
# BULK TRANSFERS

UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN

ARTICLE SIX
BULK TRANSFERS
Introductory Comment
The original Bulk Sales Law was enacted in Kentucky in 1904 and is
now KRS Chapter 377 ( enacted in 1948) . Almost all of the other states have
such a law although there is no uniformity of provisions . The purpose of
bulk statutes is to require the seller to give warning to his creditors that he
intends to dispose of the bulk of his property . The statutes are of three
types: the first, such as the Missouri Statute , requiring notice to creditors ;
the second , such as we have in Kentucky , requiring , in addition to notice,
that the buyer make certain that the proceeds of the sale be applied to the
debts ; and the third, such as the California statute, which uses a public
record as a means of notifying creditors of the impending sale.
The chief objection to a Bulk Sales Law is that it impedes the legitimate
sale of a business . Further, it sometimes serves as a trap for the inexperi-
enced buyer . This article attempts to minimize such objections by requiring
compliance with the Bulk Sales Act only in the case where fraud is more
likely to occur . Generally speaking , this article makes no major change in
the present Kentucky law, with the exception that there is no penalty pro-
vision in the article similar to that found in KRS 377.990 .
Section 6-101 . Short Title .
Summary
This article shall be known and may be cited as Uniform Commercial
Code-Bulk Transfers .
Section 6-102 . “ Bulk Transfer " ; Transfers of Equipment ;
Enterprises Subject to This Article ; Bulk
Transfers Subject to This Article .
Summary
(1) A "bulk transfer " is a transfer in bulk and not in the ordinary
course of business of a major part of the materials , supplies , merchandise or
other inventory of an enterprise subject to this article .
(2) A transfer of a substantial part of the equipment is a bulk transfer
only if made in connection with a bulk transfer of inventory .
(3 ) All enterprises where the principal business is the sale of mer-
chandise from stock , including manufacturers , are subject to this article .
(4) Except as otherwise limited all bulk transfers are subject to this
article .
KRS 377.010 .
Kentucky Statutes
253

Comment
This section sets up definitions which are not incorporated as such in
KRS Chapter 377. Subsection ( 1) identifies the transfers covered by this
article which are further limited by UCC 6-103 , infra. KRS 377.010 uses the
language ". whole or large part of any stock of goods , wares or mer-
chandise of any kind or fixtures in bulk ... otherwise than in the ordinary
course of trade and in the regular and usual prosecution of the seller's
business ." This definition appears to be substantially in accord with the
Code definition . Subsection (2) limits the application of this article in cases
of bulk transfers of equipment only to cases where such transfer is in
connection with a bulk transfer of inventory . There is no similar limitation
in the present Kentucky statute .
Subsection ( 3) defines the business covered in the article . These are the
ones whose principal business is the sale of merchandise and not a service .
This exclusion of businesses selling services is in accord with present Ken-
tucky law. See St. Matthews Motor Co. v. Schnepp , 306 Ку . 823 , 209 S. W.
2d 481 ( 1948 ) holding that the bulk sales law is intended to apply only to
the ordinary merchant and his usual merchandise .
Section 6-103 . Transfers Excepted From This Article .
Summary
The following transfers are not subject to this article :
(1) Transfers for security ;
(2) Assignments for the benefit of creditors ;
(3 ) Transfers in settlement or realization of a lien or security interest ;
(4) Sales by executors , administrators , receivers , trustees in bank-
ruptcy or judicial sales ;
( 5) Sales made in the course of judicial or administrative proceedings
for the dissolution or reorganization of a corporation and of which notice is
sent to the corporation creditors pursuant to order of the court or adminis-
trative agency ;
( 6 ) Transfers to existing business which is bound to pay all debts , give
notice to that effect and is solvent ;
(7 ) Transfers to successor business if notice is given and successor as-
sumes debts and receives nothing except an interest junior to the creditors ;
(8 ) Transfers of exempt property .
KRS 377.010 ( 3) .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) The purpose of this section is to state the transfers which are
excepted from the article even though they might be within the general
definition of UCC 6-102 . Subsection ( 1 ) excludes all security transactions
since under the Code they are covered by Article 9. The Kentucky stat-
254

ute , covering " any sale or transfer ," KRS 377.010 ( 1) , is broad enough to
include as a transfer the creation of a security interest . However , in other
jurisdictions having similar statutes , it is generally held that security trans-
actions are not covered . See Note , 57 A.L.R. 1049 ( 1928 ) .
(2) Accord, KRS 377.010 ( 3 ) .
(3) A sale in enforcement of a lien is apparently not excluded by the
Kentucky statute unless made under order of a court or by a public officer .
KRS 377.010 ( 3 ) .
(4) Accord, KRS 377.010 ( 3 ) .
(5) Accord, KRS 377.010 ( 3) , since exception is specifically limited to
dissolution or reorganization of corporation made pursuant to judicial or
administrative proceedings .
(6) Subsection (6) is new but is in accord with the purpose of bulk
sales law to give seller's creditors a chance to collect . If the buyer assumes
the debts and is solvent after assuming them, there is no reason to subject
the transaction to the delay and bother of this article . However , compare
Riedling v . Ross & Zeitz , 213 Ky . 187 , 280 S. W. 923 ( 1926 ) . There A took
over B's stock of goods, keeping it and its proceeds separately from his own ,
furnished money wherewith to compromise the claims of certain creditors ,
and collected outstanding accounts . This was held to show a bulk sale under
the Kentucky statute .
(7) Subsection (7) is new and covers certain changes in ownership of
a business . Within the prescribed limits the exclusion is probably justified .
The creditor is protected , or at least no worse off, because both the original
debtor and the new enterprise are bound , the property is still subject to the
debt , and the original debtor has taken nothing except an interest junior to
the creditors . See , Gwin Bros. & Co. v . Peoples General Store , 269 Ky . 813 ,
108 S. W. 2d 1001 ( 1937) .
(8) No similar statutory provision in Kentucky.
Section 6-104 . Schedule of Property , List of Creditors .
Summary
(1) Except as to auction sales , a bulk transfer is ineffective against a
creditor unless :
(a) a list of creditors is properly prepared ; and
(b) a schedule of property is prepared ; and
(c) the transferee preserves the list and schedule for six months
and permits inspection and copying by any creditor or files
them in (here designate a public office ) .
(2) The list must be signed and sworn to by the transferor . It must
contain the names and addresses of all creditors together with amount due
if known and also the names of all persons known by the transferee to be
asserting claims even though disputed .
255

(3) The transferor is responsible for the completeness and accuracy of
the list and the transfer is not ineffective because of omissions unless the
transferee had knowledge .
KRS 377.020 , 377.060 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The requirement that the transferee obtain from the transferor a sworn
schedule of the property and a list of creditors is substantially the same as
KRS 377.020 . A new requirement is that the transferee retain the list for
six months or file it in a public office . As under the Kentucky statute , KRS
377.060 , responsibility for completeness and accuracy of the list rests upon
the transferor .
The Code does not impose criminal sanctions for false statements by the
transferor , as does KRS 377.990 .
Section 6-105 . Notice to Creditors .
Summary
A bulk transfer subject to this article , except one made by auction sale
( UCC 6-108 ) , is ineffective against any creditor of the transferor unless the
transferee gives notice of the transfer in the manner and to the persons
hereafter provided (UCC 6-107 ) at least 10 days before he takes possession of
the goods or pays for them .
KRS 377.030 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section requires notice to creditors in all bulk transfers subject to
the article except auction sales . The contents of the notice and other factors
are provided in UCC 6-107 . UCC 6-108 covers auction sales. The notice must
be given ten days in advance, which is in accord with KRS 377.030 .
The giving of notice is one of the main reasons for bulk sales statutes .
Once the creditor has notice he can take appropriate steps to protect himself .
Section 6-106 . Application of the Proceeds .
Summary
In addition to other requirements :
( 1) If a new consideration is paid , it is the duty of the transferee to
assure that such consideration is applied to pay the debts of the transferor
which are on the list (UCC 6-104 ) or filed in writing in the place mentioned
in the notice within thirty days of such notice. This duty runs to all the
holders of such debts and may be enforced by any holder for the benefit
of all.
256

(2) If any debt is in dispute , the necessary sums may be withheld
pending determination of the amount due .
(3) If the consideration is insufficient to pay all , distribution shall be
made pro rata .
KRS 377.040 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section imposes a duty on the transferee similar to that imposed
by the present Kentucky Bulk Sales Law , KRS 377.040 . There is not una-
nimity of opinion as to the feasibility of such provision . Some states , such as
Missouri , and New York , feel that notice to creditors is sufficient . Other
states , including Kentucky and Pennsylvania , impose the greater duty similar
to this section on the transferee .
The purpose of this section is to give the creditors direct protection
against dissipation by the transferor in cases of transfer "for which a new
consideration becomes payable ." Many outright transfers are excluded from
the article by virtue of UCC 6-103 ( 6 ) and ( 7) . In other cases where the
debts are paid as they mature , the buyer is usually safe in disregarding this
section and is protected by UCC 6-109 (2) to the extent of sums honestly paid
to particular creditors .
The buyer may protect himself by any of several methods . He may
withhold payment of consideration or make an escrow or deposit it in court
and interplead the creditors . This obligation runs only to those creditors the
buyer may identify in one of the manners provided in subsection ( 1 ) of
UCC 6-104 .
Section 6-107 . The Notice .
Summary
(1) The notice to creditors shall state :
(a) that a bulk transfer is about to be made ; and
(b) the names and business addresses of the transferor and trans-
feree and all other business names and addresses used by the
tranferor within the last three years if known to the trans-
feree; and
(c) whether all of the debts are to be paid in full as they mature
and if so, the address to which the bills should be sent.
(2) If debts are not to be paid in full , or if the transferee is in doubt
on this, the notice shall state further :
(a) the location and description of the property and the estimated
total of the debts ;
(b) the place where the list of creditors and schedule may be in-
spected ;
(c) whether the transfer is to pay existing debts and if so the
amount and to whom owing ;
257

(d) whether the transfer is for a new consideration and if so
the amount and time and place of payment ; and
(e) if for a new consideration the time and place where creditors
are to file claims .
(3) The notice shall be delivered personally or by registered mail to
all the creditors listed and to all other persons whom the transferee knows
hold or assert claims against the transferor .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 377.050 .
Comment
This section specifies the contents and manner of giving the notice in
all cases specified in UCC 6-105 . If the debts are to be paid in full , a short
form may be used. This facilitates honest and solvent transactions . KRS
377.020 provides for a similar notice .
Section 6-108 . Auction Sales ; " Auctioneer " .
Summary
(1) A bulk transfer by auction sale is subject to this article as provided
in this section.
(2) The transferor shall furnish a list of creditors and prepare a
schedule as provided in UCC 6-104 .
(3) The " auctioneer " is the person who directs , controls or is respon-
sible for the sale other than the transferor . The auctioneer shall :
(a) keep the list of creditors and prepare and keep the schedule
for the period stated in UCC 6-104 ;
(b) give notice of the auction as provided in UCC 6-107 ; and
(c) assure that the proceeds are applied as provided in UCC 6-106 .
(4) Failure of the auctioneer to perform does not invalidate the sale
or affect title of the purchaser , but if the auctioneer knows that the auction
constitutes a bulk transfer such failure makes him liable to the creditors to
the extent of the net proceeds of the auction . If the auctioneer consists of
several persons, their liability is joint and several .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 377.010 , 377.020 , 377.030 , 377.040 .
Comment
Auctioneers are held responsible for compliance with the Kentucky Bulk
Sales Law , KRS 377.010 through 377.990 , but the statute could not be literally
applied to them insofar as it requires advance notice to creditors of the
price to be paid for the goods .
258

Section 6-109 . What Creditors Protected ; Credit for Payment
to Particular Creditors .
Summary
(1) The creditors protected are those whose claims arose before the
bulk transfer , but creditors becoming such after notice is given are not en-
titled to notice .
(2) Against the aggregate obligation imposed by this article , the trans-
feree or auctioneer is entitled to credit for sums paid in good faith to par-
ticular creditors .
KRS 377.020 , 377.040 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Subsection (1 ) identifies the creditors protected by this article . KRS
377.020 is not clear on this point but the decisions are in accord with sub-
section ( 1 ) . Dwiggins Wire Fence Co. v . Patterson , 166 Ky . 278, 179 S. W.
224. (1915) .
Subsection (2) gives the transferee or auctioneer credit for good faith
payments to particular creditors .
Section 6-110 . Subsequent Transfers .
Summary
When the transferee's title is defective because of noncompliance with
this article , then :
(1) a purchaser from him who pays no value or who takes with notice
takes subject to such defect, but
(2) a purchaser for value in good faith without such notice takes free
of the defect .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
There is no corresponding provision in the Kentucky Act, which pro-
vides merely that non -complying transactions are "fraudulent and void " .
(KRS 377.050) . No decisions have been found interpreting this phrase as to
subsequent transferees .
Section 6-111 . Limitation of Actions and Levies .
Summary
No action shall be brought nor levy made more than six months after
the date the transferee took possession unless the transfer was concealed in
which case actions may be brought or levies made within six months after
its discovery .
KRS 377.050 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Under KRS 377.050 the period of limitation was fixed at four months
from the consummation of the sale to the vendee instead of six months as
here proposed . KRS 377.050 had no provision for tolling the statute in the
case of concealed transfers .
!

ARTICLE SEVEN
WAREHOUSE RECEIPTS , BILLS OF LADING AND
OTHER DOCUMENTS OF TITLE
Introduction
Article 7 deals with documents of title. UCC 1-201 ( 15) defines docu-
ments of title as including "bills of lading , dock warrants , dock receipts ,
warehouse receipts or orders for the delivery of goods , and also any other
document which in the regular course of business or financing is treated
as adequately evidencing that the person in possession of it is entitled to
receive , hold and dispose of the document and the goods it covers . To be a
document of title a document must purport to be issued by or addressed to
a bailee and purport to cover goods in the bailee's possession which are
either identified or are fungible portions of an identified mass ."
Article 7 is designed by its sponsors, the National Conference of Com-
missioners on Uniform State Laws and the American Law Institute , to super-
sede two Uniform Acts prepared by the National Conference-the Uniform
Warehouse Receipts Act and the Uniform Bills of Lading Act . Also em-
braced by Article 7 are the provisions of the Uniform Sales Act concerning
negotiation of documents of title . The Uniform Warehouse Receipts Act ,
which was promulgated by the National Conference in 1906 , has been
adopted by every state , Kentucky enacting it in 1938. The Uniform Bills of
Lading Act which was approved by the National Conference in 1909 , has
been adopted in 31 states , but not in Kentucky . The Uniform Sales Act was
approved by the National Conference in 1906 and has been adopted in 34
states , Kentucky enacting it in 1928 .
Attention is directed to the fact that this article is concerned only with
the documents , and not with the business of warehouseman or carriers , which
subjects have been left to local regulatory law . The fact that a warehouse-
man violated some local statute, such as failing to file his annual report ,
should not vitiate the negotiability of an otherwise properly issued ware-
house receipt .
There is a significant omission in Article 7 of material contained in the
two prior uniform acts which it supersedes . Both acts contained provisions
establishing crimes and criminal penalties for certain misconduct in trans-
actions involving documents of title . The Code sponsors considered the
criminal penalty provisions inappropriate to a commercial law code and felt,
further , that they concerned matters upon which there was no commercial
or other necessity requiring uniformity and that , therefore , these questions
were more appropriately left to separate solution by the individual states .
Thus should Kentucky desire to preserve the special criminal provisions em-
bodied in the Warehouse Receipts Act, it will need to draft in the express
263

repealer in enacting the Uniform Commercial Code , that these portions of
the uniform laws are not repealed .
In summary it may be said that the adoption of this article will not
substantially affect present Kentucky law.
Part 1
GENERAL
Introductory Comment
Part 1 of Article 7 is the definitive and delineable part of the article .
Because of the different subject matters of the several sections , no sum-
mary of the part will be attempted . Instead attention is directed to the
comments to the individual sections .
Section 7-101 . Short Title .
Summary
This article shall be known and may be cited as Uniform Commercial
Code-Documents of Title .
Section 7-102 . Definitions and Index of Definitions .
Summary
(1) In this article , unless the context otherwise requires :
( a) "Bailee " means the person who by a warehouse receipt , bill
of lading or other document of title acknowledges possession of
goods and contracts to deliver them ;
(b) "Consignee" means the person named in a bill to whom or to
whose order the bill promises delivery ;
(c) "Consignor " means the person named in a bill as the person
from whom the goods have been received for shipment ;
(d) " Delivery order " means a written order to deliver goods di-
rected to a warehouseman , carrier or other person who in the
ordinary course of business issues warehouse receipts or bills
of lading ;
(e) "Document " means document of title as defined in the gen-
eral definitions in Article 1 ( UCC 1-201 ) ;
(f) "Goods " means al things which are treated as movable for the
purposes of a contract of storage or transportation ;
(g) "Issuer " means a bailee who issues a document except that in
relation to an unaccepted delivery order , it means the person
who orders the possessor of goods to deliver . Issuer includes
any person for whom an agent or employee purports to act in
issuing a document if the agent or employee has real or ap-
parent authority to issue documents , notwithstanding that the
264

( 2)
issuer received no goods or that the goods were misdescribed
or that in any other respect the agent or employee violated
his instructions ;
(h) "Warehouseman " is a person engaged in the business of storing
goods for hire.
Other definitions applying to this article or to specified parts
thereof , and the sections in which they appear are :
"Duly negotiate"
"Person entitled under the document" ..

tions in which they appear are :

"Overseas "
"Receipt " of goods
