UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN

ARTICLE NINE
SECURED TRANSACTIONS ; SALES
OF ACCOUNTS , CONTRACT
RIGHTS AND CHATTEL PAPER
INTRODUCTION
Article 9 covers the entire range of transactions in which debts are
secured by personal property : chattel mortgages , pledges, conditional sales ,
trust receipts , assignments of accounts receivable , factor's liens , field ware-
housing, and the like. The article is intended to supersede the Uniform
Trust Receipts Act and the Uniform Conditional Sales Act , neither of which
have been adopted in Kentucky . Thus the adoption of Article 9 would fill
many gaps in the present Kentucky Law on the subject of secured
transactions .
The scheme of the article is to state rules of law applicable to such
transactions generally , without regard to the type of security device used
by the parties . The Code distinguishes instead between different types of
property put up as security - for example , inventory , industrial and com-
mercial equipment , crops and farm products , intangible property such as
accounts receivable and states special rules where necessary to regulate
the problems which arise in each different situation .
The arrangement of Article 9 begins with an agreement to give a secur-
ity interest and continues through the various steps necessary to the
existence , perfection , performance , and enforcement thereof on a step by
step basis . Terms used are first defined in Part 1, followed in Part 2 by an
explanation of when and under what circumstances a security interest is
created and comes into existence or " attaches " in the language of the
Code . Part 2 requires among other things that there must be a written
agreement to give a security interest and that value must be given and
the debtor have rights in the collateral . After the security interest comes
into existence and attaches under Part 2, it must be perfected under the
provisions of Part 3.
Part 4 of the Code covers filing of the security interest . The pro-
cedural rules for filing are provided by this part to augment the substantive
rules of Part 3 on the effect of filing . Left optional with each state is the
adoption of a central filing system or maintaining local filing in each
county .
Part 5 contains the rights of the parties to the security interest on
default . It governs the sale of the collateral by the secured party and
requires that it be made in a " commercially reasonable " manner . The
secured party is also given the right to accept the collateral as a discharge
of the obligation .
337

ARTICLE 9
SECURED TRANSACTIONS ; SALES
OF ACCOUNTS , CONTRACT
RIGHTS AND CHATTEL PAPER
PART 1
SHORT TITLE , APPLICABILITY AND DEFINITIONS
Introductory Comment
Part 1 acts as a preface to Article 9 prescribing the extent of its ap-
plicability and setting forth definitive sections . In view of the plan of
combining the rules as to all types of security transactions , the majority
of these introductory provisions are innovations as far as Kentucky statu-
tory law is concerned. The one major change evolved is in the field of
conflicts of laws , where , by providing new concepts for the determination
of dominance between laws of interested states , the office of the assignor
or accounts and the chief place of business of a debtor have been made
the determining factors , rather than the laws of the place of contracting
or the law of the place of the location of the chattel .
Section 9-101 . Short Title .
SUMMARY
-This article shall be known as Uniform Commercial Code Secured
Transactions .
Section 9-102 . Policy and Scope of Articles .
Summary
(1) Except as otherwise provided in UCC 9-103 on multiple state
transactions and in UCC 9-104 on excluded transactions , this article applies
so far as concerns any personal property and fixture within the jurisdiction
of this State :
(a) to any transaction (regardless of its form ) which is intended
to create a security interest in personal property or fixtures
including goods , documents , instruments , general intangibles ,
chattel paper , accounts or contract rights ; and also
(b) to any sale of accounts, contract rights or chattel papers .
(2) This article applies to security interests created by contract in-
cluding pledge , assignment , chattel mortgage, chattel trust, trust deed,
factor's lien, equipment trust , conditional sale, trust receipt , other lien or
title retention contract and lease or consignment intended as security . This
article does not apply to statutory liens except as provided in UCC 9-310 .
338

(3) The application of this article to a security interest in a secured
obligation is not affected by the fact that the obligation is itself secured
by a transaction or interest to which this article does not apply .
NOTE : The adoption of this article should be accompanied
by the repeal of existing statutes dealing with conditional
sales , trust receipts , factor's liens where the factor is given
a non -possessory lien , chattel mortgages, crop mortgages,
mortgages on railroad equipment , assignment of accounts
and generally statutes regulating security interests in per-
sonal property .
Where the state has a retail installment selling act or
small loan act, that legislation should be carefully examined
to determine what changes in those acts are needed to con-
form them to this article . This article primarily sets out
rules defining rights of a secured party against persons
dealing with the debtor; it does not prescribe regulations
and controls which may be necessary to curb abuses arising
in the small loan business or the financing of consumer pur-
chases on credit . Accordingly , there is no intention to repeal
existing regulatory acts in those fields . See UCC 9-203 (2)
and the Note thereto .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS Ch . 382, KRS 287.330 , 287.620 , 289.110 , 162.240 , 160.570 ,
164.440 , 341.470 , 381.190 , 382.390 , 382.400 , 382.410 , 382.420 , 376.140 .
(KRS index refers to numerous liens in specific cases and these are
not set forth here for reasons of brevity ) .
Comment
(1) The purpose of this section is to bring under this article all se-
curity interests in personal property , including fixtures , as to which under
UCC 9-313 , it is possible to acquire a security interest in certain kinds of
fixtures which are "in place ." It also includes the category of collateral
denominated "general intangibles " defined in UCC 9-106 . The principal
test whether a transaction comes under this article is: is the transaction
intended to have effect as security ; "personal property " is not defined by
the Code ; the definition is left to the state law. Generally speaking , the
distinction drawn by the common law between real and personal property
is in terms of the difference between rights in land and rights in chattels .
See Brown , Personal Property , Sec . 7 ( 1936 ) . There are no Kentucky
statutes which define either real or personal property . See Trimble v . City
of Mt. Sterling , 11 K. L. R. 727, 12 S. W. 1066 , (1890 ) and Button v . Drake ,
302 Ky . 517 , 195 S. W. 2d 66 , ( 1946 ) for definitions of personal property .
KRS 382.600 ( 1) ( g ) defines personal property for purposes of the chattel
mortgage provisions of KRS Ch . 382 .
(2) Subsection ( 1) ( b ) brings under this article all sales of accounts ,
chattel paper and contract rights , whether made for security or not .
(3) Subsection ( 2 ) negates the idea that a security interest under
Article 9 is a statutory lien.
339

(4) Subsection ( 3 ) makes it clear that Article 9 does apply to a note ,
bond or other instrument which falls within one of the categories of col-
lateral as defined in the article even though the instrument is otherwise
secured by an interest to which the article does not apply , such as a real
estate mortgage .
Section 9-103 . Accounts , Contract Rights , General Intangibles and
Equipment Relating to Another Jurisdiction ; and
Incoming Goods Already Subject to a Security
Interest .
Summary
(1) If the office where the assignor of accounts or contract rights
keeps his records is in this state , the validity of a security interest is gov-
erned by this article ; otherwise by the law (including the conflict of laws
rules ) of the jurisdiction where such office is located .
(2) If the chief place of business of a debtor is in this state, this
article governs the validity and perfection of a security interest and the
possibility and effect of proper filing with regard to general intangibles or
with regard to goods of a type which are normally used in more than one
jurisdiction , if such goods are classified as equipment or classified as
inventory by reason of their being leased by the debtor to others . Other-
wise , the law of the jurisdiction , (including the conflict of laws rules )
where such chief place of business is located shall govern . If the chief
place of business is located in a jurisdiction which does not provide for
perfection of the security interest by filing or recording in that jurisdiction,
then the security interest may be perfected by filing in this state.
(3) If personal property , other than that governed by subsections ( 1)
and ( 2 ) is already subject to a security interest when it is brought into this
state , the validity of the security interest in this state is to be determined
by the law (including the conflict of laws rules ) of the jurisdiction where
the property was when the security interest attached. However , if the
parties to the transaction understood at the time that the securiy interest
attached that the property would be kept in his state and it was brought into
this state within 30 days after the security interest attached for purposes
other than transportation through this state, then the validity of the security
interest in this state is to be determined by the law of this state. If the
security interest was already perfected in the former state when the se-
curity interest attached and before being brought into this state the security
interest continues perfected in this state for four months and also thereafter
if within the four month period it is perfected in this state. If perfected in
this state after the four month period , perfection dates from the time of
perfection in this state . If the security interest was not perfected under the
law of the jurisdiction where the property was when the security interest
attached and before being brought into this state, it may be perfected in
this state ; in such case perfection dates from the time of perfection in this
state.
340

(4) Notwithstanding subsections ( 2 ) and ( 3 ) , if personal property is
covered by a certificate of title issued under a statute of this state or any
other jurisdiction which requires indication on a certificate of title of any
security interest in the property as a condition of perfection , then the per-
fection is governed by the law of the jurisdiction which issued the cer-
tificate .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) Restatement , Conflict of Laws, Secs . 350, 351 , and 352 provide that
the effect of an assignment, the capacity of the assignor , and all formal
requisites are determined by the law of the place of assignment . By reason
of this proposed section, the law of the place where the assignor keeps his
records would become controlling . For a Kentucky case see J. M. Atherton
Co. v . Ives , 20 F. 894 (C. С. А. , Ку . 1884 ) .
(2) The traditional conflict of laws rules speak in terms of the law
of the situs of the property as controlling the validity and perfection of
security interests . Goodrich , Conflict of Laws , Sec. 153 (3rd ed. 1949 ) .
These rules are of uncertain application , however , when the property is of
the highly mobile type which is frequently used in more than one jurisdic-
tion . In order to avoid the problems created by this conflict of laws dogma ,
subsection ( 2) gives controlling effect to the law of the state of the debtor's
principal place of business . The Code selects the location of the chief office
of debtor as the place for filing , even though the highly mobile equipment
is not in this state . If the debtor's chief place of business is in another state
but the equipment is in this state , this subsection will result in a disclaim of
jurisdiction by this state and a reference to the law of the other state .
(3 ) Subsection ( 3 ) sets out the choice of law rule for the situation
created when personal property already subject to a security interest is
brought into the Code state . Under this subsection , Article 9 applies from
the time the collateral comes into this state , except that the validiy of the
security interets is determined by the law of the jurisdiction wherein it
arose , unless the parties understood that the collateral was to be brought
into this state within 30 days after perfection of the security interest . In
such case , validity is to be determined by the applicable principles of sub-
section ( 2 ) . If the security interest was perfected in the other jurisdiction ,
it remains perfected in this state for four months after the collateral is
brought here ; after this four month period , the secured party must comply
with the perfection requirements of Article 9 to continue his perfected
security interest . Subsection (3 ) also provides for the perfection of a se-
curity interest after the expiration of the four month period if it was not
perfected within the four month period . But , in such a case , the perfection
dates from the time of perfection in this state .
(4) Subsection ( 4) avoids the possible necessity of duplicating per-
fection in the case of vehicles subject to a certificate of title law requiring
compliance therewith to perfect security interest . Kentucky does not require
indication on a certificate of title in order to perfect a security interest . See
341

KRS 376.270 , 376.280, and 376.281 for acquiring a lien on motor vehicles ,
boats or aircraft .
Section 9-104 . Transactions Excluded from Article .
This article does not apply :
Summary
(a) to a security interest subject to any statute of the United
States such as the Ship Mortgage Act , 1920, to the extent that
such statute governs the right of parties to and third parties
affected by transactions in particular types of property ; or
(b) to a landlord's lien ; or
(c) to a lien given by statute or other rule of law for services or
materials except as provided in UCC 9-310 on priority of such
liens ; or
(d) to a transfer of a claim for wages , salary or other compensa-
tion of an employee ; or
(e) to an equipment trust covering railway rolling stock; or
(f) to a sale of accounts, contract rights , or chattel paper as part
of a sale of the business out of which they arose, or an assign-
ment of accounts , contract rights , or chattel paper which is
for the purpose of collection only , or a transfer of a contract
right to an assignee who is also to do the performance under
the contract ; or
(g) to a transfer of an interest or claim in or under any policy of
insurance ; or
(h) to a right represented by a judgment ; or
(i) to any right of set-off; or
(j) except to the extent that provision is made for fixtures in
UCC 9-313 , to the creation or transfer of an interest in or lien
on real estate , including a lease or rents thereunder ; or
(k) to a transfer in whole or in part of any of the following ; any
claim arising out of tort; any deposit, savings , passbook or
like account maintained with a bank , savings and loan asso-
ciation , credit union , or like organization .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Subsection ( a) is in accord with the general rule of jurisdiction apply-
ing to state acts when in conflict with federal statutes. The Ship Mortgage
Act of 1920 is specified as an example of particular concern .
Subsections ( b ) , ( c ) , and (d) limit the scope of the coverage of the
article , the types of security interests there mentioned not being within the
class generally contemplated as commercial transactions . KRS 383.070 pro-
vides for a landlord's lien . KRS Ch . 376 sets forth statutory liens including
mechanic's and materialmen's liens .
342

Subsection (e) specifically excludes equipment trusts covering railway
rolling stock. KRS 376.150 allows employees to secure a lien on a railroad .
Subsection (f) points out that Article 9 does not apply to sales of ac-
counts as part of a sale of the business out of which they arose or transfers
of contract rights to an assignee , or assignments of accounts, contract rights
or chattel paper which is for the purpose of collection only .
Subsections ( g ) , ( h ) , ( i ) , (j ) and (k) are other types of transactions
entirely excluded from Article 9.
Section 9-105 . Definitions and Index of Definitions .
Summary
(1) In this article , unless the context otherwise requires :
(a) "Account debtor" means the person who is obligated on an
account , chattel paper, contract right or general intangible ;
(b) "Chattel paper" means a writing or writings which evidence
both a monetary obligation and a security interest in or a
lease of specific goods . When a transaction is evidenced both
by such a security agreement or a lease and by an instrument
or a series of instruments , the group of writings taken to-
gether constitutes chattel paper ;
(c) "Collateral " means the property subject to a security interest ,
and includes accounts, contract rights and chattel paper which
have been sold ;
(d) "Debtor " means the person who owes payment or other per-
formance of the obligation secured , whether or not he owns or
has rights in the collateral , and includes the seller of accounts,
contract rights or chattel paper. When the debtor and the
owner of the collateral are not the same person , the term
"debtor" means the owner of the collateral in any provision
of the article dealing with the collateral , the obligor in any
provision dealing with the obligation , and may include both
where the context so requires ;
(e) "Document " means document of title as defined in the general
definitions of Article 1 (UCC 1-201 ) .
(f) "Goods " includes all things which are movable at the time
the security interest attaches or which are fixtures ( UCC
9-313 ) , but does not include money, documents, instruments ,
accounts , chattel paper , general intangibles , contract rights
and other things in action . " Goods" also includes the unborn
young of animals and growing crops .
(g) "Instrument " means a negotiable instrument (defined in
UCC 3-104 ) , or a security (defined in UCC 8-102 ) or any
other writing which evidences a right to the payment of
money and is not itself a security agreement or lease and is
of a type which is in ordinary course of business transferred
by delivery with any necessary indorsement or assignment;
343

(h) "Security agreement" means an agreement which creates or
provides for a security interest ;
(i ) " Secured party " means a lender , seller or other person in
whose favor there is a security interest , including a person
to whom accounts , contract rights or chattel paper have been
sold .When the holders of obligations issued under an in-
denture of trust , equipment trust agreement or the like are
represented by a trustee or other person , the representative
is the secured party .
(2) Other definitions applying to this article and the sections in which
they appear are :
"Account ."
"Consumer goods ."
"Contract right ."
"Equipment ."
"Farm product ."
"General Intangibles ."
"Inventory ."
"Lien creditor ."
"Proceeds ."
"Purchase money security interest ." .

Section 9-106
. Definitions
:
"Account
";
"Contract Right
";
“General Intangibles
."
Summary
"Account
" means any right to payment for goods sold or leased or for
services rendered which
isnot evidenced by an instrument or chattel paper
.
"Contract right means any right to payment under
acontract not yet earned
344

by performance and not evidenced by an instrument of chattel paper . "Gen-
eral intangibles " means any personal property (including things in action )
other than goods , accounts, contract rights , chattel paper, documents and in-
struments .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) "Account " is the term applied by the Code to the right to pay-
ment for goods sold or leased for services rendered which is already earned,
but not evidenced by an instrument or chattel paper .
(2) " Contract right " is applied to the right of payment under a contract
which is not yet earned nor evidenced by an instrument in writing. These
claims not customarily the subject of commercial financing , such as a right
to wages , salary or other compensation, or a right represented by a judg-
ment are excluded from the above definitions .
(3 ) "General Intangibles " are miscellaneous types of rights which are
personal property and which are used or may become customarily used as
commercial security . Examples are good will, literary rights , copyrights ,
trademarks , patents , rights to perfomance and others , to the extent that
any of same are not governed by statutes of the United States . See UCC
9-104 (a) .
Section 9-107 . Definitions : " Purchase Money Security Interest . "
Summary
it is:
A security interest is a "purchase money security interest " to the extent
(a) taken or retained by the seller of the collateral to secure all
or part of its price ; or
(b) taken by a person who , by making advances or incurring an
obligation , gives value to enable the debtor to acquire rights
in or the use of collateral if such value is in fact so used .
Kentucky Statutes
None.
Comment
Business practice has long recognized a difference between a security
interest created for the purpose of acquiring new assets and security in-
terests of other kinds . The legal recognition of this difference is new .
The section recognizes two types of purchase money security interests :
(1) that of the seller who retains a security interest for all or part of his
selling price , and (2) that of a third party who advances money or incurs an
obligation to enable the purchaser to purchase the collateral from a seller .
This section would probably not affect the present Kentucky law on this
matter .
345

Section 9-108. When After- Acquired Collateral Not Security for
Antecedent Debt .
Summary
Where a secured party makes an advance, incurs an obligation , releases
a perfected security interest , or otherwise gives new value which is to be
secured in whole or in part by after -acquired property , his security interest
in the after -acquired collateral shall be deemed to be taken for new value
and not as security for an antecedent debt if the debtor acquires his rights
in such collateral either in the ordinary course of business or under a con-
tract of purchase made pursuant to the security agreement within a rea-
sonable time after new value is given .
Kentucky Statutes
None .
Comment
This section provides that " new value " given at the time of execution
of a security agreement secured by after - acquired property does not lose its
character as a lien for new value by virtue of the fact that the lien subse-
quently attaches . It is the purpose of this section to make clear that an
after- acquired property interest is not always security for an antecedent
debt . In order for such interest not to be those taken for antecedent debts ,
two tests must be met: ( 1) " new value " must have been given , and ( 2 ) the
collateral which is after -acquired must come in the ordinary course of the
debtor's business or in a manner contemplated when the security agreement
was executed.
Another purpose behind this section is the avoidance of the provisions
under bankruptcy law which make certain transactions for antecedent debt
voidable as preferences . It is intended that this subsection will protect the
creditor against preference when his security increased in the normal course
of business within four months of bankruptcy . However , this has been ques-
tioned by Kripke , The Modernization of Commercial Security Under the
Uniform Commercial Code , 16 Law & Contemp . Problems 183 , 194-195
(1951) .
Section 9-109 . Classification of Goods : " Consumer Goods " ;
" Equipment " ; "Farm Products " ; " Inventory ."
Goods are :
Summary
( 1) "Consumer goods " , if they are used or bought primarily for per-
sonal or household purposes ;
(2 ) "Equipment " , if they are used or bought for use primarily in busi-
ness or by a debtor who is a nonprofit organization or a governmental sub-
division , or if the goods are not included in the three other definitions ;
(3) "Farm products " , if they are crops or livestock used or produced in
farming operations , or if they are products of crops or livestock in their un-
346

manufactured state, and if they are in the possession of a debtor engaged
in farming operations . If goods are farm products , they are neither equip-
ment nor inventory ;
(4) "Inventory " , if they are held by a person who holds them for sale
or lease or to be furnished under contracts of service or if he has so fur-
nished them , or if they are raw materials , work in process or materials used
or consumed in business . Inventory of a person is not to be classified as his
equipment .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
(1) No problem should be encountered in the application of the clas-
sification of "consumer goods " found in subsection ( 1) . Clearly, such items
as personal jewelry, clothing , and household furniture used or bought for
use for personal , family or household purposes would be consumer goods
within this subsection .
(2) The classification "equipment " presented by subsection ( 2) is a
broad category . It should be noted that any goods which are not covered
by one of the other classifications in this section are to be treated as equip-
ment .
(3 ) Goods are "farm products " only if they are in the possession of a
debtor engaged in farming operations and if they are either derived from
or are used in those operations . It includes products in the possession of any
farmer , not merely the farmer who grew or used the products .
(4) The definition of "inventory " includes goods held by a debtor for
lease as well as for sale , and goods which have been furnished under a con-
tract of service or lease. Also , goods consumed or used up in a short period
of time in the production of some end product are inventory under sub-
section (4) .
Subsections ( 3) and ( 4 ) now make it clear that "farm products " are
not inventory , and that inventory of a person who is engaged in the business
of selling or leasing goods is not his equipment .
Section 9-110 . Sufficiency of Description .
Summary
For the purposes of this article any description of personal property or
real estate is sufficient if it reasonably identifies what is described .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The purpose of this section is to provide evidence in the form of a de-
scription of the collateral sufficient to make identification possible .
347

KRS 382.670 which requires the recording of chattel mortgages, requires
that " it shall be sufficient to enter a general description of the property " at
the time of recordation . It seems that this would require that the property
be identified from the record as is required by this section .
Section 9-111 . Application of Bulk Transfer Laws .
Summary
The creation of a security interest is not a bulk transfer under Article 6
(See UCC 6-103 ) .
KRS Ch . 377 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
It is the purpose of UCC 9-111 to remove from the coverage of the
Bulk Sales Act a security iinterest to the extent that it is taken for new
value . The reasoning behind this provision is that it is most desirable for a
debtor to be able to borrow , and when new value is given him his estate is
not depleted . Then so long as the business continues , the creditors are not
injured by the security interest transaction .
Kentucky cases and KRS Ch . 377 seem to be in accord with UCC 9-111
since both the cases (See First National Bank of Louisville v . Walling, Ky .
264 S. W. 2d 876 ( 1954) and the statutes indicate that the Bulk Sales Act
applies to sales and not to security transactions .
Section 9-112 . Where Collateral is Not Owned by Debtor .
Summary
Unless otherwise agreed, when a secured party knows that collateral is
owned by a person who is not the debtor , the owner of the collateral is en-
titled to receive from the secured party any surplus under UCC 9-502 ( 2 )
or under UCC 9-504 ( 1) , and is not liable for the debt or for any deficiency
after resale, and he has the same right as the debtor :
(a) to receive statements under UCC 9-208 ;
(b) to receive notice of and to object to a secured party's proposal
to retain the collateral in satisfaction of the indebtedness
under UCC 9-505 ;
(c) to redeem the collateral under UCC 9-506 ;
(d) to obtain injunctive or other relief under UCC 9-507 ( 1 ) ; and
(e) to recover losses caused to him under UCC 9-208 ( 2 ) .
Kentucky Statutes
None.
Comment
This section relates to the situation where a third -party owner has al-
lowed the debtor to use the third -party owner's property as collateral , and
348

states rules designed to clarify the respective rights of the debtor and the
owner between themselves and against the secured party in five specified
situations .
In the situations governed by UCC 9-502 ( 2) or UCC 9-504 ( 1) , return
of surplus following sale of collateral after default , this section makes the
third -party owner entitled to the surplus rather than the debtor .
Section 9-113 . Security Interests Arising Under Article on Sales .
Summary
A security interest arising solely under the article on Sales (Article 2 )
is subject to the provisions of this article except that to the extent that and
so long as the debtor does not have or does not lawfully obtain possession
of the goods :
(a) no security agreement is necessary to make the security in-
terest enforceable ; and
(b) no filing is required to perfect the security interest ; and
(c) the rights of the secured party on default by the debtor are
governed by the article on Sales (Article 2 ) .
Kentucky Statutes
None.
Comment
This section clarifies the application of Article 9 to security interests
created by Article 2 on Sales . While Article 9 applies to security interests
arising under Article 2, if the debtor has not obtained or is not in possession
of the goods the formal requisites of Article 9 as to a written security agree-
ment and as to filing are obviated and on default by the debtor the secured
party's rights are governed by Article 2 on Sales and not by Part 5 of
Article 9. Of course , if the debtor obtains or has possession of the goods , then
all of the provisions of Article 9 are applicable .
PART 2
VALIDITY OF SECURITY AGREEMENT AND RIGHTS
Introductory Comment
Part 2 of Article 9 prescribes the rules relating to the validity of a
security interest in any type of collateral and also the rules which govern
the rights of the parties to a security agreement. The changes effected by
this part included the dimunition in the importance of the location of "title"
to collateral ; the reduction of formal requisites in security agreements to a
minimum ; the complete validation of after -acquired property clauses (with
limitations only in the cases of consumer goods and crops) ; the moderation
349

of the rule that a bona fide security transaction becomes fraudulent and
void against general creditors if the secured party relaxes his control on the
collateral in any manner .
Section 9-201 . General Validity of Security Agreement .
Summary
Except as otherwise provided by this act, a security agreement is effec-
tive according to its terms between the parties , against purchasers of the
collateral and against creditors . Nothing in this article validates any illegal
practice under any statute governing usury , small loans or the like , or ex-
tends the application of any such law to any transaction not otherwise
subject thereto .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The first sentence recognizes the general principle of freedom of con-
tract . Thus a security agreement as made by the parties to it is valid and
effective as between them , as against purchasers of the collateral and as
against creditors , unless otherwise provided by a specific provision of the
UCC .
The second sentence merely amplifies and particularizes the qualifi-
cations contained in the first sentence . It makes clear that the purpose of
Article 9 is to disturb as little as possible the present state law concerning
the validity and effectiveness of security agreements .
Examples of Kentucky statutes of the type referred to in the second
sentence are KRS 360.020 , 288.150, 289.185, and 290.200 . These statutes pro-
hibit usurious interest rates.
Section 9-202 . Title to Collateral Immaterial .
Summary
Provisions of this article as to rights , obligations , and remedies apply
whether title to the collateral is in the secured party or in the debtor .
Kentucky Statutes
None.
Comment
This section provides that Article 9 applies to a security transaction
without regard to whether title to the collateral is in the secured party or in
the debtor . This section applies only to security transactions and does not
purport to affect other situations where the applicability of some other rule
of law depends upon who has title .
350

See KRS Ch . 382 as to rights of parties where a chattel mortgage is in-
volved . Also see Rule 17 of the Kentucky Rules of Civil Procedure per-
taining to real parties in interest .
Section 9-203 . Enforceability of Security Interest ; Proceeds ,
Formal Requisites .
Summary
(1 ) Subject to the provisions of UCC 4-208 on the security interest of
a collecting bank and UCC 9-113 on a security interest arising under the
Article on Sales , a security interest is not enforceable against the debtor or
third parties unless :
(a) the collateral is in the possession of the secured party ; or
(b) the debtor has signed a security agreement which contains a
description of the collateral and in addition , when the security
interest covers crops or oil , gas or minerals to be extracted or
timber to be cut , a description of the land concerned . In de-
scribing collateral , the word "proceeds " is sufficient without
further description to cover proceeds of any character .
(2) A transaction , although subject to this article , is also subject to
all applicable Kentucky statutes (e.g. any statute regulating small loans ,
retail installment sales and the like) and in the case of conflict between the
provisions of this article and any such statute, the provisions of such statute
controls . Failure to comply with any applicable statute has only the effect
which is specified therein .
NOTE : In the italicised part of subsection ( 2 ) insert refer-
ence to any local statute regulating small loans, retail in-
stallment sales and the like .
The foregoing subsection (2) is designed to make it clear
that certain transactions , although subject to this article ,
must also comply with other applicable legislation .
This article is designed to regulate all the "security " aspects
of transactions within its scope . There is , however , much
regulatory legislation , particularly in the consumer field,
which supplements this article and should not be repealed
by its enactment. Examples are small loan acts , retail in-
stallment selling acts and the like. Such acts may provide
for licensing and rate regulation and may prescribe par-
ticular forms of contract . Such provisions should remain in
force despite the enactment of this article . On the other
hand if a Retail Installment Selling Act contains provisions
on filing , rights on default , etc. , such provisions should be
repealed as inconsistent with this article .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
351

Comment
Subsection ( 1) (b) states the formal requisites for an enforceable se-
curity agreement . There are three requirements : ( 1) a writing, (2) the
debtor's signature and ( 3) a description of the collateral . When the security
iinterest covers crops or oil , gas or minerals to be extracted or timber to be
cut, a description of the land is required . The present Kentucky law has
no requirement in the statutes requiring a description of the land in in-
stances where crops , minerals or timber thereon are mortgaged, however ,
KRS 382.670 (4) requires a general description of the property mortgaged
and such a description would probably describe the land .
Subsection (2) is designed to make it clear that certain transactions , al-
though subject to this article , must also comply witih any applicable state
legislation , dealing with small loans , retail installment sales and the like.
When the bill is submitted to the legislature , all applicable Kentucky statutes
will be specifically enumerated in this subsection .
Section 9-204 . When Security Interest Attaches ; After-Acquired
Property; Future Advances .
Summary
(1 ) A security agreement cannot attach until there is agreement that
(subsection ( 3) of UCC 1-201 ) it attach , value is given , and the debtor has
rights in the collateral . It attaches as soon as all of the events in the pre-
ceding sentence have taken place unless explicit agreement postpones the
time of attaching .
(2) For the purposes of this section , the debtor has no rights :
(a) in crops until they are planted , in the young of livestock until
they are conceived ;
(b) in fish until caught , in oil , gas or minerals until extracted , in
timber until cut;
(c) in a contract right until the contract is made;
(d) In an account until it comes into existence .
(3 ) A security agreement may provide that collateral , whenever re-
quired , shall secure all obligations covered by the security agreement except
as provided in the next subsection .
(4) No security interest attaches under an after -acquired property
clause :
(a) to crops which became such more than one year after the
security agreement is executed except that a security interest
in crops which is given in conjunction with a lease or land
purchase or improvement transaction evidenced by a contract ,
mortgage or deed of trust may if so agreed attach to crops to
be grown on the land concerned during the period of such
real estate transaction ;
352

(b) to consumer goods other than accessions ( UCC 9-314 ) when
given as additional security unless the debtor acquires rights
in them within 10 days after the secured party gives value .
(5) Obligations covered by security agreement may include future
advances or other value whether or not the advances or value are given
pursuant to commitment .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 382.610 , (1) (b) , 382.620 , 382.670 (4) .
Comment
The function of this section is to prescribe when a security interest
attaches .
It may be helpful in understanding this section to set out the customary
time-table in the evolution of a security interest . The time sequence is
ordinarily as follows : ( 1) the debtor acquires title or other interest in the
personal property which will be used as collateral , ( 2) the debtor and
creditor make a security agreement, ( 3) the creditor , immediately there-
after , makes available the money or credit to the debtor , and finally , ( 4)
the written security agreement is filed.
Subsection ( 1) states three basic prerequisites to the existence of a
security interest-agreement, value , and rights in the collateral . When these
three co -exist a security interest may, in the terminology adopted in this
article , attach . Obviously if no agreement to extend a security interest has
been made, none can attach (other than those which exist by operation of
law) . The requirement that value be given limits the operation of subsection
(5) and requires that something in the form of value be given by the se-
cured party before the security interest may attach . The requirement that
the debtor have rights in the collateral states the obvious that the debtor
may not grant a security interest in goods in which he has no interest .
Accord, Scoggan v . Dillan , Ky ., 252 S. W. 2d 35 ( 1952 ) . The last clause in
subsection ( 1) makes clear that upon fulfillment of the prerequisites of the
subsection the security interest attaches automatically , unless explicit agree-
ment to postpone its attaching is made between the debtor and the secured
party . Subsection ( 1) will work no change in the Kentucky law since this
subsection deals only with the existence of a security interest , not with its
perfection . In order for the security interest to be "perfected " , KRS 382.630
requires that the instrument be recorded .
Subsections ( 2 ) , (3 ) and (4) deal with after - acquired property situa-
tions . A security agreement may be executed and value given before the
debtor acquires rights ; the security interest will then attach as to after-
acquired property when he acquires these rights .
Subsection ( 2 ) ( c ) and (d) provide that the debtor has rights in ac-
counts and contract rights when they come into existence.
Subsection ( 3) serves to validate "cross -security " clauses under which
collateral acquired at any time may secure advances whenever made . Under
353

present Kentucky law, this after - acquired collateral would have to be
definite enough at the time of recordation to sufficiently describe it in order
for the security interest to be perfected (KRS 382.670 ( 4) ) .
Subsection ( 4) ( a) limits long -term mortgages on future crops . KRS
382.610 ( 1 ) ( b) is in accord as to the one-year limitation , but the provision
pertaining to a security interest given in conjunction with a lease is new .
There is no such limitation in Kentucky law as is provided in sub-
section ( 4) ( b ) wherein it is stated that no security interest in consumer
goods can be claimed under an after -acquired property clause more than 10
days after the secured party has given value .
Under subsection ( 5) collateral may secure future as well as present
advances when the security agreement so provides . KRS 382.620 presently
limits future advances to advances made within one year after execution of
the mortgage and the aggregate amount must not exceed the amount stated
in the mortgage . Thus it will be necessary to broaden KRS 382.620 .
Section 9-205 . Use or Disposition of Collateral Without Accounting
Permissible .
Summary
A security interest is not invalid or fraudulent against creditors by
reason of liberty in the debtor to use, commingle or dispose of all or part
of the collateral (including returned or repossessed goods ) or to collect or
compromise accounts, contract rights or chattel paper , or to accept the re-
turn of goods or make repossessions , or to use , commingle or dispose of pro-
ceeds , or by reason of the failure of the secured party to require the debtor
to account for proceeds or replace collateral . This section does not relax the
requirements of possession where perfection of a security interest depends
upon possession of the collateral by the secured party or by a bailee .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section provides that a security interest is not invalid or fraudulent
because the debtor has considerable liberty in dealing with the collateral .
It rejects the rule of Benedict v . Retner , 268 U. S. 353 , 455 S. Ct . 566 ( 1925 ) ,
which held the security interest void where the secured party remained in
possession of the collateral and retained considerable power to deal with it .
The Kentucky law on this matter seems unsettled . Devan v . Naunheim ,
7 K.L.R. 220 ( 1885 ), held that the fact that a mortgagor of a stock of mer-
chandise was allowed to remain in possession and sell the mortgaged goods
in the usual course of trade did not render the transaction fraudulent . But
Sandy Valley Grocery Co. v . Patrick , 267 Ky . 768, 103 S. W. 2d 307 ( 1937 )
held a provision in a mortgage invalid as against creditor who became such
after execution of the mortgage where it permitted the mortgagor to retain
354

possession and sell the mortgaged merchandise , and further provided that
goods purchased to replace those sold should become subject to the mort-
gage . In Jackson v . Coons , 285 Ky . 154, 147 S. W. 2d 45, ( 1941 ) , the court
said , "Ordinarily, when mortgaged property is sold by a mortgagor with
the mortgagee's consent, a lien does not attach to the proceeds of the sale to
the prejudice of an attaching creditor , and a landlord's lien may be likewise
lost." But the court then held that the lessor to whom lessee executed a
mortgage covering growing tobacco, did not lose his lien by entering into
an agreement that the lessee might sell the tobacco , even though the tobacco
had been attached by a creditor of the lessee .
Section 9-206. Agreement Not to Assert Defenses Against Assignee ;
Modification of Sales Warranties Where Security
Agreement Exists .
Summary
(1) Subject to any statute or decision which establishes a different
rule for buyers of consumer goods an agreement by a buyer that he will not
assert against an assignee any claim or defense arising out of the sale is
enforceable by an assignee who takes for value in good faith without notice
of a claim or defense, except a defense of a type which may be asserted
against a holder in due course of a negotiable instrument under Article 3.
A buyer who as part of one transaction signs both a negotiable instrument
and a security agreement makes such an agreement.
(2 ) When a seller retains a purchase money security interest in goods ,
Article 2 governs the sale and any disclaimer , limitation or modification of
the seller's warranties .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Subsection ( 1 ) allows clauses by the buyer that he will not assert de-
fenses against an assignee to be enforced by an assignee taking the assign-
ment for value , in good faith and without notice of any defense (except as
to real defenses of the type under Article 3 which may be asserted ) . Such
clauses are only validated outside the consumer field .
Subsection (2) makes clear that purchase money security transactions
are sales , and warranty rules for sales are applicable . This serves to warn
the buyer that warranties have been made by the sales arrangement and
that he should not abandon them by inadvertantly allowing a "no war-
ranties" clause to be in the security agreement .
Section 9-207 . Rights and Duties When Collateral is in Secured
Party's Possession .
Summary
( 1) A secured party must use reasonable care in the preservation of
collateral in his possession . In the case of an instrument or chattel paper
355

reasonable care includes taking necessary steps to preserve rights against
prior parties unless otherwise agreed.
(2) Unless otherwise agreed , and subject to Part 5 after default , when
collateral is in the secured party's possession :
(a) reasonable expenses incurred in the custody of the collateral
are chargeable to the debtor and secured by the collateral ;
(b) the risk of accidental loss or damage is on the debtor to extent
not covered by effective insurance ;
(c) the secured party may hold as additional security any increase
or profits received from the collateral , but money so received ,
unless remitted to the debtor , shall be applied in reduction of
the secured obligation ;
(d) only fungible collateral may be commingled ;
(e) the secured party may not make use of the collateral except
in the exercise of preservation , but may repledge it.
(3 ) A secured party is liable for loss caused by his failure to meet any
obligation imposed above but does not lose his security interest .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section states the rules governing the situation where the secured
party is in possession of the collateral either before default , as in the case
of a pledge , or when he has taken possession of the collateral after default.
Restatement , Security , Sec. 17 , provides :
"The pledgee owes to the pledgor the duty of reasonable care of the
pledged chattel except where the chattel is in the possession of a
third person designated by the pledgor or is in the possession of the
pledgor himself ."
Restatement , Security , Sec. 18, provides :
"Where instruments representing claims of the pledgor against third
persons are pledged, the pledgee has the duty of using reasonable
diligence to preserve and collect the claims or to enable the pledgor
to undertake such preservation and collection ."
Kentucky collection cases are in accord, Hudson & Co. v. Wood , 183
Ky . 16 , 208 S. W. 2d ( 1919 ) Erlanger Citizens Bank v . Williams , 286 Ky . 492,
151 S. W. 2d 381 ( 1941 ) .
It is stipulated under Sec. 14 of the Restatement of Security that the
borrower and lender may modify the normal incidents of a pledge relating
to their respective privileges and duties . This conforms with subsection
(2 ) of the UCC .
Section 25 and 26 of the Restatement of Security provide that reasonable
expenses are chargeable to the borrower and are a prior charge on the
pledged chattel . Subsection ( 2 ) ( a ) conforms .
356

Section 3 of the Restatement of Security agrees with subsection ( 2)
(c) of the UCC , in providing that , "In the absence of an agreement to the
contrary ..., a pledgee has a pledge interest in the increase or profits of a
pledged chattel ."
Subsection ( 2 ) ( e) prohibits use of the pledgee without authorization
by the pledgor . Shindler v . Hayden's Adm'r , 8 K. L. R. 859 ( 1887 ) held
that the pledgee of negotiable notes or choses in action could not sell them
in the absence of a special agreement to do so . Of course if the pledgor
defaults in payment , the pledgee may sell the property . Byland v. Miller ,
13 F. Supp . 137. ( E. D. Ky. 1936 ) .
Subsection (3 ) , in stating that the secured party is liable for any loss
caused by his failure to meet any obligation imposed by subsection ( 1)
and ( 2) but does not lose his security interest , proposes no change in the
Kentucky law . The purpose of this subsection is to make clear that the
secured party does not suffer some form of forfeiture because of his fault.
This forfeiture theory , which is rejected by the UCC , does not seem to
have been advanced in any of the Kentucky cases , the usual situation being
that the debtor seeks to set -off the value of the collateral lost or con-
verted by the secured party against the suit on the principal debt . Charles
I. Hudson & Co. v . Wood , 183 Ky . 16 , 208 S. W. 2d (1949) .
Section 9-208 . Request for Statement of Account or List of
Collateral .
Summary
(1) A debtor may sign a statement indicating what he believes is
the amount of unpaid indebtedness as of a specified date and send it to
the secured party to be approved and returned . A debtor may also request
the secured party to approve a list of collateral .
(2) The secured party must comply with such request within two
weeks by sending a written correction or approval . An itemized list of
collateral is unnecessary if the secured party claims interest in all of a
particular type of collateral owned by the debtor . If the secured party
fails to comply , he is liable for any loss caused to the debtor thereby ; and
if in the list a good faith statement is included , the security party may
claim a security interest only as shown in the statement against persons
misled by his failure to comply . If he has no interest when the request is
received he must disclose name and address of successor and he is liable
for any loss to debtor for failure to disclose . A successor is subject to this
section when a request is received .
(3) A debtor is entitled to such a statement once every six months
without charge . A charge not exceeding $ 10 may be required for each
additional statement .
Kentucky Statutes
None.
357

Comment
The scheme established by this section has no counterpart in the
present Kentucky statutory or case law. However , UCC 9-208 seems to
be no more than a codification of a sound business practice .
Subsection ( 3) is designed to act as a curb upon the indiscriminate
use of the right extended to the debtor , which conceivably could be used
by a belligerent debtor merely to harass his creditor .
PART 3
RIGHTS OF THIRD PARTIES ; PERFECTED
AND UNPERFECTED SECURITY INTERESTS ;
RULES OF PRIORITY
Introductory Comment
Part 3 of Article 9 imposes the rules relating to the rights of third
parties , including rights between perfected and unperfected security in-
terests , and priorities between conflicting security interests . The premise
that has brought about the major changes in the rules , is the importance
attached to the diversity in the type of goods subject to the security interest
rather than to the variation in the type of security interest employed .
Section 9-301 . Persons Who Take Priority Over Unperfected
Security Interests ; " Lien Creditor . "
Summary
(1 ) Except as otherwise provided in subsection ( 2 ) , an unperfected
security interest is subordinate to the right of:
(a) persons entitled to priority under UCC 9-312 ;
(b) a person who becomes a lien creditor without knowledge of
the security interest and before it is perfected ;
(c) in the case of goods , instruments , documents, and chattel
paper , a person who is not a secured party and who is a
transferee in bulk or other buyer not in ordinary course of
business to the extent that he gives value and receives de-
livery of the collateral without knowledge of the security
interest and before it is perfected ;
(d) in the case of accounts , contract rights , and general intan-
gibles , a person who is not a secured party and who is a
transferee to the extent that he gives value without knowl-
edge of the security interest and before it is perfected .
(2) If the secured party files with respect to a purchase -money
security , before or within 10 days after he gives value , he takes priority
over a transferee in bulk or a lien creditor which arises between the time
the security interest attaches and the time of filing .
358

(3) A "lien creditor " is one who has acquired a lien on property by
attachment , levy or the like, and includes an assignee for benefit of
creditors from the time of assignment , and a trustee in bankruptcy from
the date of filing the petition , or a receiver in equity from the time of
appointment. Unless all the creditors represented had knowledge of the
interest , a representative is without knowledge even though he personally
knows of the interest .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS Ch . 376 , 382.270 , 382.630 , 425.270 , 426.383 .
Comment
This section lists the four classes of persons who take priority over an
unperfected security interest .
Subsection ( 1) ( a ) transfers all questions of priority to UCC 9-312 .
Subsection ( 1) ( b ) subordinates the unperfected security interest to
a lien creditor who becomes such without knowledge of and before per-
fection of the earlier interest . C. T. T. Corporation v . Studebaker Sales of
Ky ., 251 Ky . 349 , 65 S. W. 2d 84 ( 1933 ) held that a creditor cannot obtain
by attachment a lien superior to a prior mortgage on property attached,
if he has legal notice thereof . See also Hart County Deposit Bank v . Hat-
field , 236 Ky . 725, 33 S. W. 2d 660 ( 1930 ) which held that one holding an
unrecorded chattel mortgage would prevail over a subsequent mortgage
with notice of the earlier mortgage. This is in accord with subsection
(1) (b) .
Subsection ( 1) ( c ) gives unsecured transferees in bulk and buyers
not in the ordinary course of business priority over an earlier unperfected
security interest to the extent that the transferee gives value and takes
delivery without knowledge of the earlier interest . The Kentucky Bulk
Sales Act ( KRS Ch . 377) sets up a detailed procedure for bulk transfers
and places the burden on the purchaser to attempt to ascertain the sellor's
creditors , and if notification to the creditors is not given , the sale will be
deemed fraudulent and void and operate as a general assignment for the
benefit of creditors (KRS 377.050 ) .
Subsection ( 2) provides a 10 day "grace period " in a purchase money
security interest situation . This would change Kentucky law since Ken-
tucky has a " race " type recording act. KRS 382.270 and 382.630 .
Subsection (3) , insofar as it defines "lien creditor " as a creditor who
has acquired a lien on the property by attachment , levy , or the like, is
in accord with Kentucky law (KRS 425.270 , 426.383 ) . Apparently Kentucky
has never decided whether assignees for benefit of creditors , or receivers
in equity are lien creditors . The Code makes them such. Since the 1950
amendment to the Federal Bankruptcy Act , trustees in bankruptcy have
been so considered as of the date of bankruptcy , as to all property of the
bankrupt at the date of bankruptcy . 64 Stat . 26 , 11 U. S. C. A. , Sec . 110 с
(Supp . 1951 ) . The Code provision giving a creditor his status as a lien
creditor from issuance of process which within a reasonable time results
359

in attachment , levy or the like is contrary to Kentucky law . At present ,
levy , rather than issuance of process, is the critical point in Kentucky .
KRS 425.270 , 426.383.
Section 9-302 . When Filing is Required to Perfect Security In-
terest ; Security Interests to Which Filing Provisions
of this Article do not Apply.
Summary
(1) A financing statement must be filed to perfect all security
interests except the following :
(a) a security interest in collateral in possession of the secured
party under UCC 9-305 ;
(b) a security interest temporarily perfected in instruments or
documents without delivery under UCC 9-304 or in proceeds
for a 10 day period under UCC 9-306 ;
(c) a purchase money security interest in farm equipment hav-
ing a purchase price not in excess of $2500 ; but filing is
required for a fixture under UCC 9-313 or for a motor ve-
hicle required to be licensed ;
(d) a purchase money security interest in consumer goods; but
filing is required for a fixture under UCC 9-313 for a motor
vehicle required to be licensed ;
(e) an assignment of accounts or contract rights which does not
alone or in conjunction with other assignments to the same
assignee transfer a significant part of the outstanding ac-
counts or contract rights of the assignor ;
(f) a security interest of a collecting bank ( UCC 4-208 ) or arising
under the Article on Sales ( UCC 9-313 ) or covered by sub-
section ( 3 ) of this section .
(2) If a secured party assigns a perfected security interest , no filing
under this article is required in order to continue the perfected status of
the security interest against creditors of and transferees from the original
debtor .
(3 ) The filing provisions of this article do not apply to a security
interest in property subject to a statute :
(a) of the United States which provides for a national registration
or filing of all security interests in such property ; or
(b) of this state which provides for central filing of, or which
requires indication on a certificate of title of, such security
interests in such property .
(4) A security interest in property covered by a statute described in
subsection ( 3 ) can be perfected only by registration or filing under that
statute or by indication of the security interest on a certificate of title or
a duplicate thereof by a public official .
360

Kentucky Statutes
KRS 382.270 , 382.290 , 382.630 .
Comment
(1) Under present Kentucky law in order for a security interest to
be good against a purchase without notice thereof , creditors of the mort-
gagor , and any and all third parties , the security interest must be filed
for record (KRS 382.270 and 382.630) . The present Kentucky law does not
require the filing of a financing statement but it does require chattel
mortgages and conditional sales contracts (which are treated as chattel
mortgages for purposes of recordation ) to be recorded in order to be
effective against purchasers without notice , creditors and other third
parties .
(2) The effect of subsections ( 1) ( a) through ( 1) (d) on the present
Kentucky law will be discussed in the sections referred to in these sub-
sections .
(3 ) An assignment must be recorded under KRS 382.290 in order
to be effective against bona fide purchasers and creditors , Hick's Com-
mittee v . Smith , 158 Ky . 752, 166 S. W. 248 ( 1914 ) .
(4) Under present Kentucky law, it seems that a security interest
of a collecting bank would have to be filed for record in order to be good
against bona fide purchasers and creditors .
(5) Subsection ( 2) provides that a perfected security interest need
not be filed by the assignee . Under Kentucky law, the assignee's security
interest would not be perfected since a bona fide purchaser from the
assignor or creditors of the assignor could prevail against the assignee
until the assignment was recorded . See paragraph ( 3) above.
(6) Compliance with the federal statutes ( as provided for in sub-
section ( 3) (a) ) is equivalent to filing under Article 9.
(7) Subsection (3 ) ( b ) exempts a security interest in property sub-
ject to any Kentucky statute which requires the indication on a certifica-
tion of title .
The present Kentucky law does not require that a notation be made
on the bill of sale concerning a security interest on a motor vehicle , but
under KRS 382.270 and 382.630 , a recorded chattel mortgage would be
needed in order to perfect the security interest .
Section 9-303 . When Security Interest is Perfected ; Continuity of
Perfection .
Summary
(1 ) A security interest is perfected when it has attached and when
all of the applicable steps required for perfection have been taken . Such
steps are specified in UCC 9-302 , 9-304 , 9-305 , and 9-306 . If such steps are
361

taken before the security interest attaches , it is perfected at the time when it
attaches .
(2) If a security interest is originally perfected in any way permitted
under this article and is subsequently perfected in some other way under
this article , without an intermediate period when it was unperfected , the
security interest shall be deemed to be perfected continuously for the pur-
poses of this article .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 382.270 , 382.390 , 382.630 .
Comment
Kentucky chattel mortgage legislation , which by definition covers all
security devices (KRS 382.600 ( b ) ) , poses no problem with respect to
whether a security interest may be perfected before the instrument is filed,
since filing is the only method of perfection . The last sentence of subsection
(1) provides that if the filing for record is done before the security interest
attaches then perfection dates from the time the interest attaches .
Section 9-304. Perfection of Security Interest in Instruments ; Doc-
uments and Goods Covered by Documents ; Perfec-
tion by Permissive Filing ; Temporary Perfection
Without Filing or Transfer of Possession .
Summary
(1) A security interest in chattel paper or negotiable documents may
be perfected by filing . A security interest in instruments (other than instru-
ments which constitute part of chattel paper ) can be perfected only by
the secured party's taking possession , except as provided in subsections
(4) and ( 5 ) .
(2) During the period that goods are in the possession of the issuer
of a negotiable document thereof, a security interest in the goods is per-
fected by perfecting a security interest in the document , and any security
interest in the goods otherwise perfected during such period is subject
thereto .
(3 ) A security interest in goods in the possession of a bailee other
than one who has issued a negotiable document therefor is perfected by
issuance of a document in the name of the secured party or by the bailee's
receipt of notification of the secured party's interest or by filing as to the
goods .
(4) A security interest in instruments or negotiable documents is
perfected without filing or the taking of possession for a period of 21 days
from the time it attaches to the extent that it arises for new value given
under a written security agreement.
(5) A security interest remains perfected for a period of 21 days
without filing when a secured party having a perfected security interest in
362

an instrument , a negotiable document or goods in possession of a bailee or
other than one who has issued a negotiable document therefor :
(a) makes available to the debtor the goods or documents repre-
senting the goods for the purpose of ultimate sale or exchange
or for the purpose of loading , unloading , storing , shipping ,
transhipping , manufacturing , processing or otherwise dealing
with them in a manner preliminary to their sale or exchange ;
or
(b) delivers the instrument to the debtor for the purpose of ulti-
mate sale or exchange or of presentation , collection , renewal
or registration of transfer .
(6) After the 21 day period in subsections (4) and ( 5) perfection
depends upon compliance with applicable provisions of this article .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.027 , 356.052 , 382.630 .
Comment
Kentucky has no provision for filing for protection of a security interest
in negotiable instruments or documents .
Under present Kentucky law , where a holder has a lien on an instru-
ment, he is deemed to be a holder for value to the extent of his lien ( KRS
356.027 ) . If he meets the other requirements needed to be a holder in due
course to the value of his lien (KRS 356.052 ) then it is not necessary for
him to file notice of the security interest in order for the lien to be per-
fected. Being a holder in due course and having possession of the negotiable
instrument will be sufficient to allow him to prevail against all but "real "
defenses .
Under subsection ( 2 ) a security interest in goods may be perfected by
perfecting a security interest in a negotiable document . Under the present
Kentucky law , recording of a chattel mortgage would be necessary in order
to perfect the security interest in the goods (KRS 382.630 ) .
Subsection (3) would seemingly change the present Kentucky law
since notification to the bailee of the secured party's interest would not
under the present Kentucky law perfect the secured party's interest . Recor-
dation is now necessary in order to protect the secured party's interest
against a bona fide purchaser or subsequent creditor of the debtor .
The 21 day provision in subsections (4) and (5) is new and will change
present Kentucky law.
Section 9-305 . When Possession by Secured Party Perfects Security
Interest Without Filing .
Summary
A security interest in letters of credit and advices of credit (subsection
(2 ) ( a) of UCC 5-116 ) , goods , instruments , negotiable documents or chattel
363

paper may be perfected by the secured party's taking possession of the
collateral . If such collateral other than goods covered by a negotiable
document is held by a bailee , the secured party is deemed to have possession
from the time the bailee receives notification of the secured party's interest .
A security interest is perfected by possession from the time possession is
taken without relation back and continues only so long as possession is
retained , unless otherwise specified in this article . The security interest
may be otherwise perfected as provided in this article before or after the
period of possession by the secured party .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 356.027 , 356.052 , 382.630 .
Comment
This section enables the secured party to perfect his security interest
by taking possession of the collateral . This differs from the present Ken-
tucky law which requires a recording in order to perfect the security in
personal property (KRS 382.630 ) , except when the security interest is in a
negotiable instrument and then possession by the holder may be perfected
as to all except " real " defenses if he qualifies as a holder in due course
(KRS 356.052) . If he is not a holder in due course , but is a holder for value
(KRS 356.027) , he will be subject to "personal " defenses as well as those
defenses classified as " real ."
The present Kentucky law is that if a subsequent purchaser or creditor
has notice of the fact that the collateral is held as security , he cannot pre-
vail even though the security interest is not recorded . Eastern Construction
Co. v . Carson Construction Co. Trustee , 242 Ky . 648 , 47 S. W. 2d 67 ( 1932 ) .
Possession of the property will operate as notice to purchasers and creditors .
Warden v . Addington , 131 Ky . 296 , 115 S. W. 241 ( 1909 ) ; Ashland Grocery
Co. v . Martin, 267 Ky . 677, 103 S. W. 2d 72 ( 1937 ) .
Section 9-306 . " Proceeds " ; Secured Party's Rights on Disposition
of Collateral .
Summary
(1) "Proceeds " includes whatever is received when collateral or pro-
ceeds is sold , exchanged, collected or otherwise disposed of. The term
also includes the account arising when the right to payment is earned
under a contract right. Money , checks and the like are "cash proceeds ."
All other proceeds are "non - cash proceeds ."
(2) Except where this article otherwise provides , a security interest
continues in collateral notwithstanding sale , exchange or other disposition
thereof by the debtor unless his action was authorized by the secured party
in the security agreement or otherwise , and also continues in any identi-
fiable proceeds including collection received by the debtor .
(3 ) The security interest in proceeds is a continuously perfected
security interest if the interest in the original collateral was perfected , but
364

it ceases to be perfected security interest and becomes unperfected 10 days
after receipt of the proceeds by the debtor unless :
(a) a filed financing statement covering the original collateral
also covers proceeds ; or
(b) the security interest in the proceeds is perfected before the
expiration of the 10 day period .
(4) In the event of insolvency proceedings instituted by or against
a debtor, a secured party with a perfected security interest in proceeds has
a perfected security interest :
(a) in identifiable non -cash proceeds ;
(b) in identifiable cash proceeds in the form of money which
is not commingled with other money or deposited in a bank
account prior to the insolvency proceedings ; and
(c) in identifiable cash proceeds in the form of checks and the
like which are not deposited in a bank account prior to the
insolvency proceedings ; and
(d) in all cash and bank accounts of the debtor , if other cash
proceeds have been commingled or deposited in a bank ac-
count , but the perfected security interest under this para-
graph ( d ) is
1. subject to any right of set- off; and
2. limited to an amount not greater than the amount of any
cash proceeds received by the debtor within 10 days
before the institution of the insolvency proceedings and
commingled or deposited in a bank account prior to the
insolvency proceedings less the amount of cash proceeds
received by the debtor and paid over to the secured party
during the 10 day period .
(5) If a sale of goods results in an account or chattel paper which
is transferred by the seller to a secured party , and if the goods are returned
to or are repossessed by the seller or the secured party , the following rules
determine priorities :
(a) If the goods were collateral at the time of sale for an indebted-
ness of the seller which is still unpaid , the original security
interest attaches again to the goods and continues as a per-
fected security interest if it was perfected at the time when
the goods were sold . If the security interest was originally
perfected by a filing which is still effective , nothing further is
required to continue the perfected status ; in any other case ,
the secured party must take possession of the returned or
repossessed goods or must file .
(b) An unpaid transferee of the chattel paper has a security
interest in the goods against the transferor . Such security
interest is prior to a security interest asserted under para-
graph ( a) to the extent that the transferee of the chattel
paper was entitled to priority under UCC 9-308 .
365

(c) An unpaid transferee of the account has a security interest
in the goods against the transferor . Such security interest is
subordinate to a security interest asserted under paragraph
(a) .
(d) A security interest of an unpaid transferee asserted under
paragraph (b) or ( c) must be perfected for protection against
creditors of the transferor and purchasers of the returned or
repossessed goods .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Kentucky does not have a statutory definition of "proceeds ." KRS
382.730 ( 3 ) uses the word "proceeds" but does not define it. The definition
expressed in this section will probably not affect the Kentucky law .
Under present Kentucky law, a security interest will only continue in
property sold by the debtor when ( 1 ) the secured party had perfected his
interest by recording or (2 ) the purchaser had notice of the unrecorded
security interest . Thus subsection ( 2) would change the Kentucky law
on this point unless that subsection really means that a "perfected " security
interest continues notwithstanding sale, etc.
Subsection (3 ) says a secured party loses his perfected security interest
10 days after the debtor sells the collateral unless the filed financing state-
ment also covers the proceeds, or he perfects his security interest in the
proceeds within 10 days . Under the present Kentucky law, a perfected
security interest would not be lost by the debtor's sale of the collateral since
KRS 382.630 provides that a recorded chattel mortgage prevails against
subsequent purchases . Also , upon default in payment of the chattel mort-
gage , the secured party could go against the debtor even though the col-
lateral had been sold by the debtor .
Subsection ( 4) set forth the four classes of proceeds in which a secured
party has a perfected interest when insolvency proceedings are instituted
by or against a debtor . It permits the retention of a security interest in
identifiable cash proceeds and gives a security interest in all unidentifiable
cash and bank accounts received by the debtor , but this interest in the un-
identifiable bank accounts and cash is limited to the net amount of cash
proceeds received and retained by the debtor within 10 days prior to the
insolvency proceedings .
Subsection ( 5) ( a) would not change the present Kentucky law since
a perfected security interest remains effective even though the debtor sells
the collateral .
Subsection ( 5 ) ( b ) provides that an unpaid transferee of chattel paper
who gave new value will have priority over the original security interest.
Subsection ( 5) ( c) gives an unpaid transferee of an account a security
interest , but it is subordinate to the original security interest provided for
in subsection (5) ( a) .
366

Subsection ( 5) ( d) provides that the unpaid transferee must perfect
his security interest in order to be protected against creditors of the trans-
feror and purchasers of the repossessed or returned goods .
Section 9-307 . Protection of Buyers of Goods .
Summary
(1 ) A buyer in ordinary course of business (subsection ( 9 ) of UCC
1-201 ) other than a person buying farm products from a person engaged
in farming operations takes free of a security interest created by his seller
even though the security interest is perfected and even though the buyer
knows of its existence .
(2) In the case of consumer goods and in the case of farm equipment
having an original purchase price not in excess of $ 2500 (other than fix-
tures , see UCC 9-313 ) , a buyer takes free of a security interest even though
perfected if he buys without knowledge of the security interest , for value
and for his own personal , family or household purposes or his own farming
operations , unless prior to the purchase the secured party has filed a
financing statement covering such goods .
KRS 382.630 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Subsection ( 1) permits a buyer in the ordinary course of business to
take free of a perfected security interest , even though he knows of its terms .
This is limited to inventory cases . A security interest is perfected in Ken-
tucky by the filing of a chattel mortgage. Normally a mortgage duly
recorded is notice to all the world and a party purchasing the mortgaged
property from the mortgagor is liable to the mortgagee . Hughes v . Graves ,
11 Ky . ( 1 Litt .) 317 ( 1822 ) . But if the mortgagee authorizes the sale of the
mortgaged goods , then he cannot recover against one purchasing in good
faith . Penick v . White & Beauchamp , 264 Ку . 172 , 94 S. W. 2d 338 ( 1936 ) .
Thus it seems that this subsection would change the Kentucky law since
even though the sale is authorized , the purchaser in order to prevent
recovery must be a good faith purchaser . Whereas , under this subsection ,
he prevails even though he has notice of the perfected security interest .
On its face, subsection ( 2 ) appears to require a buyer of a washing
machine or a plow , for example , to check the filing records prior to his
purchase . Reference to the Code definition of "Buyer in the Ordinary
Course of Business ", (subsection ( 9 ) of UCC 1-201 ) shows otherwise . A
buyer of consumer goods of that kind would be protected by the pro-
visions of subsection ( 1 ) since he would be purchasing " inventory " and
not " consumer goods ." While subsection ( 1) deals with sales of goods
by one whose business is that of selling such goods , subsection ( 2) deals
with sales of goods by one not engaged in the business of selling such goods .
Subsection ( 2 ) will work no appreciable change in Kentucky law since a
367

secured party can prevail over a subsequent purchaser only if he recorded
his chattel mortgage (KRS 382.630 ) . ( Under the Code it is called a financing
statement. ) Or even though the chattel mortgage is not recorded , the pur-
chaser had knowledge of the security interest . Hart County Deposit Bank
v . Hatfield 236 , Ky . 725, 33 S. W. 2d 660 ( 1930 ) .
Section 9-308 . Purchase of Chattel Paper and Non -Negotiable
Instruments .
Summary
A purchaser of chattel paper or a non-negotiable instrument who gives
new value and makes possession of it in the ordinary course of his business
and without knowledge that the specific paper or instrument is subject
to a security interest has priority over a security interest which is perfected
under UCC 9-304 ( permissive filing and temporary perfection ) . A pur-
chaser of chattel paper who gives new value and takes possession of it in
the ordinary course of his business has priority over a security interest in
chattel paper which is claimed merely as proceeds of inventory subject to a
security interest (UCC 9-306 ) , even though he knows that the specific
paper is subject to the security interest .
KRS 382.630 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The first sentence makes clear that an interest in chattel paper or
non - negotiable instruments based on possession will have priority over
security interests based on filing , or on temporary perfection under UCC
9-304 without filing . Kentucky law is contra since under KRS 382.630 a
security interest perfected by recording prevails over subsequent pur-
chasers . The present Kentucky law does not provide for temporary per-
fection without filing and thus a security interest not recorded will be
subordinate to a purchaser without knowledge .
The second sentence makes clear that a security interest in chattel
paper for new value outranks a security interest based merely on a claim
to proceeds of inventory which had been subject to the security interest ,
even though the purchaser knows of the security interest . This is contra
to the Kentucky law since knowledge of the security interest will prevent
him from prevailing even though the mortgage is not recorded .
Section 9-309 . Protection of Purchasers of Instruments and
Documents .
Summary
Nothing in this article limits the rights of a holder in due course
of a negotiable instrument ( UCC 3-302 ) or a holder to whom a negotiable
368

document of title has been duly negotiated (UCC 7-501 ) , or a bona fide
