purchaser of a security ( UCC8-301 ) and such holders or purchasers take
priority over an earlier security interest even though perfected . Filing
under this article does not constitute notice of the security interest to such
holdrs or purchasers .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section is in accord with the common law and the N. I. L. whereby
a holder in due course holds free from rights available to prior parties , and
makes explicit a rule universally recognized . As Kentucky has no pro-
vision for filing negotiable instruments , no change in Kentucky law is
effected here .
Section 9-310. Priority of Certain Liens Arising by Operation of
Law .
Summary
A person in the ordinary course of business who furnishes services
or materials with respect to goods subject to a security interest , has a lien
upon goods in the possession of such person superior to a perfected security
interest unless the lien is statutory and the statute expressly provides
otherwise .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS Ch . 376 , KRS 376.010 ( 2) .
Comment
This section subordinates a security interest in goods , though perfected ,
to liens arising from the subsequent furnishing of services or materials
with respect to the goods . However , the rule is otherwise if the latter
lien is statutory and the statute expressly subordinates the lien to prior
security interests .
Many statutory liens for the furnishing of services or materials are
set forth in KRS Ch . 376, and in general these liens are subordinated to
prior perfected security interests in the goods . KRS 376.010 ( 2 ) . The
Commissioners Comment points out that many statutes creating "mechanics '
liens " expressly make the lien subordinate to a prior security interest and
it is not the intention of this section to repeal such statutory provisions .
Thus this section would not change present Kentucky law on the subject .
Section 9-311 . Alienability of Debtor's Rights : Judicial Process .
Summary
The debtor's rights in collateral may be voluntarily or involuntarily
transferred (by way of sale, creation of a security interest , attachment ,
369

levy , garnishment or other judicial process) notwithstanding a provision in
the security agreement prohibiting any transfer or making the transfer
constitute a default .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section recognizes the alienability of a debtor's rights in collateral
even though the security agreement makes such alienation without con-
sent of the secured party a default .
Kentucky cases : Cooper v . McKee, 121 Ky . 287 , 89 S. W. 203 , ( 1905 )
held that where a chattel mortgage authorized the mortgagor to exchange
any horse covered by the mortgage , for another horse or horses , and pro-
viding that any horse so received in exchange should be subject to the
mortgage, did not authorize the mortgagor to sell the horses . But Perros v .
Stone's Adm'r , 305 Ky . 142 , 202 S. W. 2d 160 ( 1947) held that a provision
in a chattel mortgage that mortgager would not sell or remove from the
premises any of the tangible personalty covered by the mortgage without
written consent of the mortgagee was not violated by sale of personalty
to a buyer who assumed the mortgage and left the personalty on the
premises .
Section 9-312 . Priorities Among Conflicting Security Interests in
the Same Collateral .
Summary
(1) The rules of priority stated in the following sections shall gov-
ern where applicable : UCC 4-208 with respect to the security interest of
collecting banks in items being collected , accompanying documents and
proceeds ; UCC 9-301 on certain priorities ; UCC 9-304 on goods covered
by documents ; UCC 9-306 on proceeds and repossessing ; UCC 9-307 on
buyers of goods ; UCC 9-308 on possessory against non -possessory interests
in chattel paper or non -negotiable instruments ; UCC 9-309 on security
interests in negotiable instruments , documents or securities ; UCC 9-313 on
security interests in fixtures as against interests in real estate ; UCC 9-314
on security interests in accessions as against interests in goods ; UCC 9-315
on conflicting security interests where goods lose their identity or become
part of a product ; and UCC 9-316 on contractural subordination .
(2) A perfected security interest in crops for new value given to
enable the debtor to produce the crops during the production season and
given not more than three months before the crops become growing crops
by planting or otherwise takes priority over an earlier perfected security
interest to the extent that such earlier interest secures obligations due
more than six months before the crops become growing crops by planting
or otherwise , even though the person giving new value had knowledge of
the earlier security interest .
(3) A purchase money security interest in inventory collateral has
priority over a conflicting security interest in the same collateral if
370

(a) the purchase money security interest is perfected at the time
the debtor receives possession of the collateral ; and
(b) any secured party whose security interest is known to the
holder of the purchase money security interest or who has
previously filed a financing statement covering the same
items or type of inventory , has received notification of the
purchase money security interest before the debtor receives
possession of the collateral covered by the purchase money
security interest ; and
(c) such notification states that the person giving the notice has
or expects to acquire a purchase money security interest in
inventory of the debtor , describing such inventory by item
or type .
(4) A purchase money security interest in collateral other than in-
ventory has priority over a conflicting security interest in the same col-
lateral if the purchase money security interest is perfected at the time the
debtor receives possession of the collateral .
(5) In all cases not governed by other rules stated in this section,
priority between conflicting security interests in the same collateral shall
be determined as follows :
(a) in the order of filing if both are perfected by filing , regard-
less of which security interest attached first under UCC
9-204 ( 1) and whether it attached before or after filing ;
(b) in the order of perfection unless both are perfected by filing
regardless of which security interest attached first under
UCC 9-204 ( 1) and , in the case of a filed security interest ,
whether it attached before or after filing ; and
(c) in the order of attachment under UCC 9-204 ( 1) so long as
neither is perfected .
(6) For the purpose of the priority rules of the immediately preced-
ing subsection , a continuously perfected security interest shall be treated
at all times as if perfected by filing if it was originally so perfected and it
shall be treated at all times as if perfected otherwise than by filing if it
was originally perfected otherwise than by filing .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 382.610 ( 2 ) (b ) , 382.630 .
Comment
Subsection ( 1) merely points out the sections where the rules govern-
ing priorities are stated .
Subsection ( 2) gives a party who gives new value to enable a debtor
to produce crops , a priority secured interest over an earlier perfected
security interest , if the new value was given not more than three months
before the crops became growing , and the earlier perfected interest secured
obligations due more than six months before the crops bcame growing
371

crops. This priority exists even though the party giving new value had
knowledge of the security interest . This subsection differs from the present
Kentucky law since under KRS 382.610 (2) ( b ) crops to be planted within
one year may be mortgaged and under KRS 382.630 , if the chattel mortgage
is recorded , it will take priority . Further Kentucky cases hold that unre-
corded mortgages are valid against purchasers and creditors with notice .
Eastern Const . Co. v . Carson Construction Co.'s Trustee , 242 Ky . 648 , 47
S. W. 2d 67 (1932) .
Subsection ( 3 ) protects a purchase money security interest in inventory
where the security interest is perfected at the time the debtor receives
possession of the collateral , and any other party seeking a security interest
in the same property has notice of the purchase money security interest or
intention to acquire a purchase money security interest . Kentucky seems
to be in accord , except notification to a subsequent creditor of expectation
to acquire a purchase money security interest would probably not be
sufficient to allow the notifying party to prevail over the subsequent
creditor who perfects his security interest first .
Subsection (4) protects one having a purchase money security interest
in collateral other than inventory if it is perfected at the time the debtor
receives possession of the collateral . Under the present Kentucky law ,
perfection of the interest so as to protect the purchase money security
interest would be needed prior to delivery of possession if the debtor was
in a position to transfer title to a bona fide purchaser or receive credit
or give a security interest in the collateral . KRS 382.630 .
Subsection ( 5) sets forth the priority of conflicting interests in all
other cases not covered by this section, at the present time , a security
interest may only be perfected in Kentucky by filing .
Section 9-313 . Priority of Security Interests in Fixtures .
Summary
(1) The rules of this section do not apply to goods incorporated
into a structure in the manner of lumber , bricks , tile, cement, glass , metal
work and the like and no security interest in them exists under this article
unless the structure remains personal property under applicable law . The
law of this state other than this act determines whether and when other
goods becomes fixtures . This act does not prevent creation of an encum-
brance upon fixtures or real estate pursuant to the law applicable to real
estate.
(2) A security interest which attaches to goods before they become
fixtures takes priority as to the goods over the claims of all persons who
have an interest in the real estate except as stated in subsection (4) .
(3 ) A security interest which attaches to goods after they become fix-
tures is valid against all persons subsequently acquiring interests in the real
estate except as stated in subsection (4) but is invalid against any person
with an interest in the real estate at the time the security interest attaches
372

to the goods who has not in writing consented to the security interest or
disclaimed an interest in the goods as fixtures .
(4) The security interests described in subsections ( 2) and (3 ) do not
take priority over
(a) a subsequent purchaser for value of any interest in the real
estate; or
(b) a creditor with a lien on the real estate subsequently obtained
by judicial proceedings ; or
(c) a creditor with a prior encumbrance of record on the real
estate to the extent that he makes subsequent advances .
If the subsequent purchase is made, the lien by judicial proceedings is ob-
tained, or the subsequent advance under the prior encumbrance is made or
contracted for without knowledge of the security interest and before it is
perfected . A purchaser of the real estate at a foreclosure sale other than
an encumbrancer purchasing at his own foreclosure sale is a subsequent
purchaser within this section .
(5) When under subsections (2) or (3) and ( 4 ) a secured party has
priority over the claims of all persons who have interests in the real estate ,
he may on default , subject to the provisions of Part 5, remove his collateral
from the real estate but he must reimburse any encumbrancer or owner of
the real estate who is not the debtor and who has not otherwise agreed for
the cost of repair of any physical injury , but not for any diminution in
value of the estate caused by the absence of the goods removed or by any
necessity for replacing them. A person entitled to reimbursement may refuse
permission to remove until the secured party gives adequate security for
the performance of this obligation .
KRS 382.630 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Subsection ( 1) makes clear that no security interest in the goods as
chattels may be retained in goods which become an integral part of real
estate . Under KRS 382.620, it does not seem that one with a perfected se-
curity interest would lose it by a chance of the character of the property .
Subsection (2) states that a secured party claiming an interest in goods
as personal property is entitled to priority over a person claiming an interest
in the same goods by reason of their having become realty under the law of
fixtures , leaving it to case law to determine when such goods become realty
by affixation . Kentucky law: Perfection by recording (KRS 382.630 ) , or
knowledge of the security interest by the party seeking a security interest
in the real estate will prevent a party from prevailing . Perry v . Trimble , 25
K. L. R. 725 , 76 S. W. 343 ( 1903 ) . But, subsection ( 2) refers to a security
interest "attaching " , not to its "being perfected ." Thus , this subsection
may change the Kentucky law since the present law requires perfection by
recording , rather than the mere attaching of an interest , in order to prevail
against bona fide purchasers or subsequent creditors without notice of the
prior security interest .
373

Subsection (3) permits creation of a chattel interest in goods even after
they become fixtures , with the consent of all parties then interested in the
real estate .
Subsection ( 4) (a) is a restatement of Kentucky law (KRS 382.630 ) .
But if the purchaser for value has notice of the prior security interest , then
he will not prevail . Eastern Const . Co. v . Carson Const . Co.'s Trustee , 242
Ky . 648, 47 S. W. 2d 67 , ( 1932 ) .
Subsection ( 4) ( b ) is a restatement of Kentucky law also . The claim
of a subsequent judgment creditor with a lien on the real estate will prevail
over a chattel interest provided the lien creditor becomes such without
knowledge of the chattel interest and before its perfection .
Subsection ( 4) (c) is in accord with Kentucky law. See KRS 382.270
and 376.010 ( 2 ) . There is no statutory provision for a mortgage to secure
future advances on real property , but there is such a provision for chattel
mortgages (KRS 382.620 ) .
Subsection ( 5) permits removal of the affixed goods from the whole
where the security interest in the affixed goods has priority over all claim-
ants in the real estate . This would change Kentucky law, since, at present ,
such removal cannot be made if the removal will cause an injury to the
estate . Bank of Louisville v . Bannister , 87 Ky . 6, 7 S. W. 170 ( 1888) . How-
ever , subsection ( 5) imposes a duty upon the remover to reimburse or in-
demnify the subordinated claimants , not the debtor , for physical injury to
the whole .
Section 9-314 . Accessions .
Summary
(1 ) A security interest in goods which attaches before they are in-
stalled in or affixed to other goods takes priority as to the goods installed
or affixed (called in this section "accessions " ) over the claims of all persons
to the whole except as stated in subsection ( 3) and subject to UCC 9-315 ( 1 ) .
(2) A security interest which attaches to goods after they become part
of a whole is valid against all persons subsequently acquiring interests in
the whole except as stated in subsection (3) but is invalid against any person
with an interest in the whole at the time the security interest attaches to
the goods who has not in writing consented to the security interest or dis-
claimed an interest in the goods as part of the whole .
(3 ) The security interest described in subsections (1) and (2) do not
take priority over :
(a) a subsequent purchaser for value of any interest in the whole ;
or
(b) a creditor with a lien on the whole subsequently obtained by
judicial proceedings ; or
(c) a creditor with a prior perfected security interest in the
whole to the extent that he makes subsequent advances ;
374

if the subsequent purchase is made, the lien by judicial proceedings ob-
tained or the subsequent advance under the prior perfected security interest
is made or contracted for without knowledge of the security interest and
before it is perfected . A purchaser of the whole at a foreclosure sale other
than the holder of a perfected security interest purchasing at his own fore-
closure sale is a subsequent purchaser within this section .
(4) When under subsections ( 1) or ( 2 ) and ( 3 ) a secured party has an
interest in accessions which have priority over the claims of all persons who
have interests in the whole , he may on default subject to the provisions of
Part 5 remove his collateral from the whole , but he must reimburse any
encumbrancer or owner of the whole who is not the debtor and who has
not otherwise agreed for the cost of repair of any physical injury but not
for any diminution in value of the whole caused by the absence of the
goods removed or by any necessity for replacing them . A person entitled
to reimbursement may refuse permission to remove until the secured party
gives adequate security for the performance of this obligation .
KRS 382.630 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section conforms as closely as possible to the structure and
language of UCC 9-313 . This section generally states that a secured party
claiming an interest in goods installed in or affixed to other goods is en-
titled to priority over a party with a security interest in the whole .
For a discussion of the present Kentucky law , see comment following
UCC 9-313 .
Section 9-315 . Priority When Goods Are Commingled or Processed .
Summary
( 1 ) If a security interest in goods was perfected and subsequently
the goods or a party thereof have become part of a product or mass , the
security interest continues in the product or mass if :
(a) the goods are so manufactured , processed , assembled or com-
mingled that their identity is lost in the product or mass ; or
a financing statement covering the original goods also covers
the product into which the goods have been manufactured ,
processed or assembled .
(b)
In a case to which paragraph ( b) applies , no separate security interest in
that part of the original goods which has been manufactured , processed ,
or assembled into the product may be claimed under UCC 9-314 .
(2 ) When under subsection (1) more than one security interest attaches
to the product or mass , they rank equally according to the ratio that the
cost of the goods to which each interest originally attached bears to the
cost of the total product or mass .
375

None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Subsection ( 1 ) allows a perfected security interest in goods which have
become commingled so as to lose their identity to prevail . No Kentucky
cases directly in point could be found but Bank of Louisville v . Baumeister ,
87 Ky . 6, 7. S. W. 170 ( 1888 ) , a case involving fixtures , indicates that Ken-
tucky would hold in accord with this subsection . Also see Well v . Silver-
stone , 69 Ky . ( 6 Bush ) 698 ( 1869 ) , a case involving fraudulent confusion of
goods .
Subsection ( 1 ) ( b) makes this section applicable to situations of small
components and parts built into a unit, like a radio , even though their
identity may not be lost . In such a case UCC 9-315 rather than UCC 9-314
on accessions will be applicable , if the financing statement claims an interest
in the product .
Subsection ( 2 ) provides equal priority for all secured claims entitled
to priority under subsection ( 1 ) . They share rateably .
Section 9-316 . Priority Subject to Subordination .
Summary
Nothing in this article prevents subordination by agreement by any
person entitled to priority .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section is inserted to make clear that persons entitled to priorities
may enter into subordinate agreements .
Section 9-317 . Secured Party Not Obligated on Contract of Debtor .
Summary
The mere existence of a security interest or authority given to the
debtor to dispose of or use collateral does not impose contract or tort liability
upon the secured party for the debtor's acts or omissions .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section provides a rule where none now exists in Kentucky . Its
effect is that personal liability of a secured party does not result from the
376

mere fact of the security interest nor from the fact that the secured party
has authorized the debtor to dispose of or use the collateral .
Section 9-318 . Defenses Against Assignee ; Modification of Contract
After Notification of Assignment ; Term Prohibiting
Assignment Ineffective ; Identification and Proof of
Assignment .
Summary
(1) Unless an account debtor has made an enforceable agreement not
to assert defenses or claims arising out of a sale as provided in UCC 9-206
the rights of an assignee are subject to:
(a) all the terms of the contract between the account debtor and
assignor and any defense or claim arising therefrom ; and
(b) any other defense or claim of the account debtor against the
assignor which occurs before the account debtor receives no-
tification of the assignment .
(2) So far as the right to payment under an assigned contract right
has not already become an account , and not withstanding notification of
the assignment , any modification of or substitution for the contract made
in good faith and in accordance with reasonable commercial standards is
effective against an assignee unless the account debtor has otherwise
agreed but the assignee acquires corresponding rights under the modified
or substituted contract . The assignment may provide that such modification
or substitution is a breach by the assignor .
(3) The account debtor is authorized to pay the assignor until the
account debtor receives notification that the account has been assigned
and that payment is to be made to the assignee . A notification which does
not reasonably identify the rights assigned is ineffective . If requested by
the account debtor , the assignee must seasonably furnish reasonable proof
that the assignment has been made and unless he does so the account debtor
may pay the assignor .
(4) A term in any contract between an account debtor and an assignor
which prohibits assignment of an account or contract right to which they
are parties is ineffective .
KRS 371.040 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
No apparent change in Kentucky law is made by subsection (1 ) . An
assignee in Kentucky is subject to defenses and claims based on facts
arising before the account debtor is notified of the assignment (KRS 371.040 ) .
Subsection (2) deals with contract rights as distinguished from accounts.
It makes good faith modification or substitution by assignor and account
debtor after notice to account debtor of the assignment effective even
377

without the assignee's consent, if in accordance with reasonable commercial
standards .
Subsection (3 ) authorizes the account debtor to pay the assignor until
he receives notice of the assignment and notice that he is to pay the assignee .
The notification must reasonably identify the rights assigned . Reasonable
proof of the assignment may be required . Kentucky is in accord as to notice ,
Wallins Creek Collieries Co. v . Saylor , 214 Ky . 206, 282 S. W. 1095 ( 1926 ) ,
but there are no statutory provisions or cases prescribing what degree of
proof of assignment is required , and probably any payment made to the
assignor while awaiting proof of the assignment would be made at the
debtor's peril .
Subsection (4) would make all accounts and contracts rights freely
assignable by making a term prohibiting assignment ineffective .
PART 4
FILING
Introductory Comment
Part 4 of Article 9 prescribes the rules relating to the filing of security
agreements in the public records . The simplified filing procedure herein
set forth eliminates for the perfection of a security interest all intricacies
of acknowledgements , affidavits of good faith , verification by witnesses
and other formalities .
Part 4 will greatly change the present Kentucky law relating to
recording of chattel mortgages (KRS 382.600 through 382.990 ) . It will not
affect the present law relating to filing of mechanics' and material -men's
liens (KRS Ch . 376 ) .
Section 9-401 . Place of Filing ; Erroneous Filing ; Removal of
Collateral .
Summary
(1) The proper place to file in order to perfect a security interest is as
follows :
(a) In the office of the County Clerk in the county of the debtor's
residence or if the resident is not a resident of this state then
in the office of the County Clerk in the county where the
goods are kept ;
(b) when the collateral is goods which at the time the security
interest attaches are or are to become fixtures, then in the
office where a mortgage on the real estate concerned would
be filed or recorded ;
378

NOTE : The Kentucky State Bar Association Committee
on the Uniform Commercial Code recommends that this
section be adopted as submitted herein . They have elimi-
nated all reference to central and duplicate filing which
were optional provisions under the Official Draft to the
Code; thereby continuing the present Kentucky law cover-
ing filing .
(2) A filing which is made in good faith in an improper place or not
in all of the places required by this section is nevertheless effective with
regard to any collateral as to which the filing complied with the require-
ments of this article and is also effective with regard to collateral covered
by the financing statement against any person who has knowledge of the
contents of such financing statement.
(3) A filing which is made in the proper place in this state continues
effective even though the debtor's residence or place of business or the
location of the collateral or its use , whichever controlled the original
filing, is thereafter changed .
(4) If collateral is brought into this state from another jurisdiction ,
the rules stated in UCC 9-103 determine whether filing is necessary in this
state.
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 382.110 , 382.270 , 382.610 (4) , 382.630 and 382.670 .
Comment
The present Kentucky law requires filing of security interests with
the County Clerk in order for it to be perfected ( KRS 382.110, 382.270,
382.610 (4 ) , 382.630 , and 382.670 ) . Filing with the Secretary of State is not
required .
Paragraph ( a) is in accord with the present Kentucky law ( KRS
382.670 ( 1 ) ) .
Subsection ( 2) makes it clear that filing to be effective with respect to
a particular item of collateral must have been filed in both places in those
states requiring dual filing . Kentucky does not require dual filing . Under
subsection (2 ) filing in only one of two required places will not be effective
against any person , except one with actual knowledge of the interest . Actual
notice will keep one from prevailing in Kentucky . Hart County Deposit
Bank v . Hatfield, 236 Ky . 725, 33 S. W. 2d 660 ( 1930 ) .
Subsection ( 3) states that a proper filing remains effective , although
the debtor's residence or place of business or the location of the collateral is
thereafter changed. Kentucky law seems to be in accord , see American Loan
Co. v . See , 298 Ky . 180 , 182 S. W. 2d 644 ( 1944 ) , Denkins Motor Co. v . Hum-
phreys , 310 Ky . 344, 220 S. W. 2d 847 ( 1949 ) .
Subsection (4) is a cross - reference to UCC 9-103 . For Kentucky cases ,
see American Loan Co. v . See , supra , and Denkins Motor Co. v . Humphreys ,
supra .
379

Section 9-402 . Formal Requisites of Financing Statement ;
Amendments .
Summary
( 1) A financing statement is sufficient if it is signed by the debtor and
the secured party , gives an address of the secured party from which in-
formation concerning the security interest may be obtained, gives a mailing
address of the debtor and contains a statement indicating the types , or
describing the items , of collateral . A financing statement may be filed
before a security agreement is made or a security interest otherwise at-
taches. When the financing statement covers crops growing or to be grown
or goods which are or are to become fixtures the statement must also contain
a description of the real estate concerned . A copy of the security agreement
is sufficient as a financing statement if it contains the above information and
is signed by both parties .
(2 ) A financing statement which otherwise complies with subsection
(1) is sufficient although it is signed only by the secured party when it is
filed to perfect a security interest in:
(a) collateral already subject to a security interest in another
jurisdiction when it is brought into this state. Such a financing
statement must state that the collateral was brought into this
state under such circumstances .
(b) proceeds under UCC 9-306 if the security interest in the
original collateral was perfected . Such a financing statement
must describe the original collateral .
(3) A form substantially as follows is sufficient to comply with
subsection ( 1) :
Name of debtor (or assignor )
Address
Name of secured party
(or assignee )
Address
1. This financing statement covers the following types (or items )
of property :
(Describe )
2. (If collateral is crops ) The above described crops are growing
or are to be grown on :
(Describe real estate )
3. (If collateral is goods which are or are to be fixtures ) The
above described goods are affixed or to be affixed to :
(Describe real estate )
4. (If proceeds or products of collateral are claimed ) Proceeds
Products of the collateral are also covered .
Signature of Debtor (or Assignor )
Signature of Secured Party (or Assignee )
-
380

(4) The term "financing statement" as used in this article means the
original financing statement and any amendments but if any amendment
adds collateral it is effective as to the added collateral only from the filing
date of the amendment .
(5) A financing statement substantially complying with the require-
ments of this section is effective even though it contains minor errors which
are not seriously misleading .
KRS 382.650 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section describes the financing statement which is filed where
filing is required to perfect a security interest . The simple formal requisites
for the financing statement are: (1 ) the signatures and addresses of both
parties and (2) a description of the collateral . When the collateral is crops
or fixtures, the statement must also contain a description of the land in-
volved .
Subsection (2) allows filing by the secured party of a statement signed
only by himself in two situations : ( 1) when the financing statement states
that the collateral was brought in from another state , while under a security
interest , and (2) if the financing statement is filed merely to protect the
proceeds of collateral disposed of by the debtor when the original security
interest in the collateral was perfected under UCC 9-306 .
The present Kentucky law requires , in the case of chattel mortgages, the
instrument to be deposited with the county clerk (KRS 382.670) . KRS
382.650 requires that the chattel mortgage be executed by the mortgagor or
his duly authorized agent and the instrument must be witnessed or ac-
knowledged as provided in KRS 382.130 to 382.150 . For filing requirements
for mechanic's liens , see KRS 376.080 ( 1) , 376.230 ( 1 ) , 376.440 (1 ) .
Subsection (3) merely sets out a suggested form .
Subsection (4) provides additional flexibility through the use of amend-
ing statements .
Subsection (5) makes it clear that an immaterial error will not make
the instrument ineffective .
Section 9-403 . What Constitutes Filing ; Duration of Filing ; Effect
of Lapsed Filing ; Duties of Filing Officer .
Summary
(1) Presentation for filing of a financing statement and tender of the
filing fee or acceptance of the statement by the filing officer constitutes
filing under this article .
(2) A filed financing statement which states a maturity date of the
obligation secured for five years or less is effective until such maturity date
381

and thereafter for a period of 60 days . Any other filed financing statement
is effective for a period of five years from the date of filing . The effective-
ness of a filed financing statement lapses on the expiration of such 60 day
period after a stated maturity date or on the expiration of such five year
period , as the case may be, unless a continuation statement is filed prior to
the lapse . Upon such lapse the security interest becomes unperfected .
(3 ) A continuation statement may be filed by the second party (a)
within six months before and 60 days after a stated maturity date of five
years or less, and (b) otherwise within six months prior to the expiration
of the five year period specified in subsection (2 ) . Any such continuation
statement must be signed by the secured party , identify the original state-
ment by file number and state that the original statement is still effective.
Upon timely filing of the continuation statement, the effectiveness of the
original statesment is continued for five years after the last date to which
the filing was effective whereupon it lapses in the same manner as provided
in subsection (2 ) unless another continuation statement is filed prior to such
lapse. Succeeding continuation statements may be filed in the same manner
to continue the effectiveness of the original statement. Unless a statute on
disposition of public records provides otherwise , the filing officer may
remove a lapsed statement from the files and destroy it .
(4) A filing officer shall mark each statement with a consecutive
file number and with the date and hour of filing and shall hold the state-
ment for public inspection . In addition the filing officer shall index the
statements according to the name of the debtor and shall note in the index
the file number and the address of the debtor given in the statement .
(5 ) The uniform fee for filing , indexing and furnishing filing data
for an original or a continuation statement shall be $ 1.00.
KRS 382.700 , 382.720 .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
Kentucky is in accord with subsection ( 1) , Webb v . Austin, 22 K. L. R.
764, 58 S. W. 808 ( 1900 ) .
Subsection (2 ) provides that once a financing statement is filed which
is for a period of five years or less , it remains effective for the life of the
agreement plus 60 days thereafter . The maximum period of effectiveness
for a filed financing statement not specifying a maturity date is five years .
Under present Kentucky law ( KRS 382.720 ) , a chattel mortgage is invalid
against third parties after three years unless a statement is filed, and such
statement extends the mortgage for an additional three years .
Subsection ( 3 ) provides for a continuation statement . This will change
KRS 382.720.
Subsection (4) is in accord with KRS 382.670 ( 5 ) .
Subsection (5) sets forth the recording fee. KRS 382.700 provides that
the county clerk may charge $ 1.00 for filing, indexing and abstracting a
382

chattel mortgage . For filing , indexing , abstracting or noting a release or
assignment , on a separate instrument , the fee is $ .50, and for noting an
assignment or release in the minute book , the fee is $ .25 .
Section 9-404 . Termination Statement .
Summary
(1) Whenever there is no outstanding secured obligation and no com-
mitment to make advances, incur obligations or otherwise give value ,
the secured party must on written demand by the debtor send the debtor
a statement that he no longer claims a security interest under the financing
statement, which shall be identified by file number . A termination statement
signed by a person other than the secured party of record must include
or be accompanied by the assignment or a statement by the secured party
of record that he has assigned the security interest to the signer of the
termination statement. The uniform fee for filing and indexing such an
assignment or statement thereof shall be $ .50. If the affected secured
party fails to send such a termination statement within 10 days after
proper demand therefor , he shall be liable to the debtor for $ 100, and in
addition for any loss caused to the debtor by such failure .
(2) On presentation to the filing officer of such a termination state-
ment he must note it in the index . Unless a statute on disposition of public
records provides otherwise , the filing officer shall remove the financing
statement from the files , mark it "terminated " and send or deliver the
financing statement to the secured party .
(3 ) The uniform fee for filing and indexing a termination statement
and for sending or delivering the financing statement shall be $ .50 .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 382.670 ( 6) , 382.680 , and 382.700 .
Comment
Subsection ( 1) provides that the secured party or his assignee must
furnish the debtor a termination statement on demand where a security
interest is no longer claimed . KRS 382.680 is very similar to this subsection ,
except it does not provide for a penalty if the demand ( also not mentioned
in the Kentucky statute) is not complied with within 10 days . KRS 382.700
sets forth the fees to be charged for releases and assignments.
Subsection (2) sets forth the duties of the filing officers when a ter-
mination statement is filed. KRS 382.680 is very similar .
Section 9-405 . Filing of Statement of Assignment of a Secured
Party's Interest ; Duties of Filing Officer ; Fees .
Summary
A secured party may assign of record all or a part of his rights under
a financing statement by indication therein of the name and address of the
383

assignee or by the filing of a separate written statement of assignment
signed by the secured party and setting forth the name of the secured party
and the debtor , the file number and the date of filing of the financing
statement and the name and address of the assignee , and containing a de-
scription of the collateral assigned. A copy of the assignment is sufficient
as a separate statement if it complies with the preceding sentence . On
presentation to the filing officer of such a financing statement or such a
separate statement, the filing officer shall mark such financing statement
as provided in UCC 9-403 (4) or mark such separate statement with the
date and hour of filing . He shall note the assignment on the margin of
the index of the filing of the financing statement . The uniform fee for filing ,
indexing and furnishing filing data about such a separate statement of as-
signment shall be $.50 .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 382.680 , 382.700 .
Comment
This section permits but does not require filing of an assignment of a
security interest perfected by filing . The present Kentucky law is very
similar (KRS 382.680 ) . For fee charge , see KRS 382.700 .
Section 9-406 . Release of Collateral ; Duties of Filing Officer , Fees .
Summary
A secured party of record may by his signed statement release all or a
part of any collateral described in a filed financing statement. The state-
ment of release is sufficient if it contains a description of the collateral
being released , the name and address of the debtor , the name and address of
the secured party , and the file number of the financing statement . Upon
presentation of such a statement to the filing officer he shall mark the state-
ment with the hour and date of filing and shall note the same upon the
margin of the index of the filing of the financing statement . The uniform fee
for filing and noting such a statement of release shall be $ .50.
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 382.670 , 382.680 , 382.700 .
Comment
This section provides for the filing of a statement of release by the
secured party (KRS 382.680 ) . The fee for such filing is provided for in
KRS 382.700 at the present time .
384

PART 5
DEFAULT
Introductory Comment
The secured party's right to exact his due from the collateral upon
default by the debtor is a primary reason for security arrangements . Like-
wise the protection of the debtor from sharp practices of the secured party
has always been recognized as one of the duties of the court , particularly in
equity . The Code attempts to do right by both parties as well as other
creditors and interested persons .
The primary change made in the present Kentucky law by this part
is a general liberalization of the procedure for realization on i.e. disposition
of the collateral . From the debtor's standpoint , he is protected by being
authorized either to force or restrain disposition of the collateral .
Section 9-501 . Default ; Procedure When Security Agreement
Covers Both Real and Personal Property .
Summary
(1) When a debtor is in default under a security agreement , a secured
party has the remedies provided by this part, which are cumulative . In
addition , he may reduce his claim to judgment , foreclose the security in-
terest by any available judicial procedure , or both . If the collateral is docu-
ments the secured party may proceed either as to the documents or as to
the goods covered thereby .
(2 ) After default , the debtor has the rights provided in this part,
and a secured party in possession has the rights and duties provided in
UCC 9-207 .
(3) The rules stated in this part which give rights to the debtor and
impose duties on the secured party may be waived or varied only as pro-
vided in this part but the parties may by agreement determine the stand-
ards by which the fulfillment of these rights and duties is to be measured
if such standards are not manifestly unreasonable .
(4) If the security agreement covers both real and personal property ,
the secured party may proceed under this part as to the personal property
or he may proceed as to both the real and the personal property under the
law relating to foreclosure of real estate mortgages. If the secured party
proceeds as to both under the real estate mortgage law , the provisions of
this part do not apply .
(5) When a secured party has reduced his claim to judgment the lein
of any levy which may be made upon his collateral by virtue of any exe-
cution based upon the judgment shall relate back to the date of the per-
fection of the security interest in such collateral . A judicial sale, pursuant
to such execution , is a foreclosure of the security interest by judicial pro
385

cedure within the meaning of this section, and the secured party may
purchase at the sale and thereafter hold the collateral free of any other
requirements of this article .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 426.005 .
Comment
Subsection (1 ) gives a secured party the rights provided under Part 5
and also the right to reduce his claim to judgment where the debtor defaults .
KRS 426.005 presently provides that judgment may be rendered in an
action to enforce a mortgage or lien .
Subsection ( 2 ) is a cross - reference to UCC 9-207 as to the rights and
duties to which the secured party is subject when in possession of the
collateral .
Subsection (3) does not permit a waiver or variation of rights and
duties but permits the parties to agree as to standards by which the fulfill-
ment of the rights and duties is to be measured if the standard is not
manifestly unreasonable .
Subsection ( 4) permits proceedings under this part as to the personalty
if the agreement covers both real and personal property , but if the secured
party proceeds under the real estate mortgage law, as to both , this part does
not apply . Under present Kentucky law, a secured party may proceed as
to both real and personal property under KRS 426.005 , 426.130 and 426.140
which require that personal property be sold first , and if it is not sufficient
to satisfy the judgment , then real property may be sold and the proceeds
used to satisfy the judgment .
Subsection (5) makes it clear that a judicial sale on a judgment is a
foreclosure of a security interest to which the requirements of Part 5 do not
apply . Kentucky seems to be in accord , but KRS 426.200 and 426.530 give
the debtor a redemptive right for one year after the sale where the col-
lateral is land and does not sell for two -thirds of its appraisal value . KRS
426.525 provides that foreclosure of a mortgage is forbidden . But Mon-
tenegro - Riehm Music Co. v . Beuris , 160 Ky . 557, 196 S. W. 986 (1914) held
that a sale of a mortgaged chattel by the mortgagee under a power of sale
was a mere enforcing of the mortgagee's lien and did not violate the sec-
tion . The court also held that the mortgagor's right of redemption was
barred forever , and that the title to the chattel forfeited without a sale .
See also Farmers and Deposior's Bank v . Taylor, 290 Ky . 774, 162 S. W. 2d
764 ( 1942 ) .
Section 9-502 . Collection Rights of Secured Party .
Summary
(1) When so agreed and in any event on default the secured party is
entitled to notify an account debtor or the obligor on an instrument to make
386

payment to him whether or not the assignor was theretofore making col-
lections on the collateral , and also to take control of any proceeds to which
he is entitled under UCC 9-306 .
(2) A secured party who by agreement is entitled to charge back
uncollected collateral or otherwise to full or limited recourse against the
debtor and who undertakes to collect from the account debtors or obligors
must proceed in a commercially reasonable manner and may deduct his
reasonable expense of realization from the collection . If the security agree-
ment secures an indebtedness, the secured party must account to the debtor
for any surplus , and unless otherwise agreed, the debtor is liable for any
deficiency . But if the underlying transactions were a sale of accounts,
contract rights , or chattel paper , the debtor is entitled to any surplus or is
liable for any deficiency only if the security agreement so provides .
Kentucky Statutes
None.
Comment
Subsection ( 1) permits the secured party to take the necessary steps to
take over collection of the accounts upon the debtor's default-notifying
the account debtors to make payments directly to the assignee . KRS 371.040
impliedly is in accord . See Van Winkle v . Kayes , 7 Ky . Opin . 585 ( 1874) .
Subsection (2 ) imposes on the secured party a standard of "commercial
reasonableness " where he takes control of collections and proceeds to
realize upon the collateral . The secured party may deduct , in addition to
the amount of the secured claim , the reasonable amount of expenses in-
curred in realization from the collections . The assignor remains liable for
any deficiency and is entitled to any surplus from the realization by the
secured party . But if the transaction was a sale, then the assignor is not
liable for deficiency or entitled to surplus in the absence of an express agree-
ment . See White v . General Motors Acceptance Corp. , 2 F. Supp . 406 (E.D.
Ky ., 1932 ) , for a case requiring payment of surplus to mortgagor .
Section 9-503 . Secured Party's Right to Take Possession After
Default .
Summary
Unless otherwise agreed , a secured party has on default the right to
take possession of the collateral . In taking possession a secured party may
proceed without judicial process if this can be done without breach of the
peace or may proceed by action . If the security agreement so provides the
secured party may require the debtor to assemble the collateral and make
it available to the secured party at a place to be designated by the secured
party which is reasonably convenient to both parties . Without removal a se-
cured party may render equipment unusable , and may dispose of collateral
on the debtor's premises under UCC 9-504 .
387

None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The chattel mortgagee has a right to possession of the collateral upon
default . This possession may be by judicial process or by self-help if it can
be done without breach of the peace . Kentucky is in accord , Brown v.
Phillips , 66 Ky . (3 Bush ) 656 ( 1868 ) , White v . General Motors Acceptance
Corp. , 2 F. Supp . 406 (E. D. Ky . 1932 ) . But see Commercial Credit Co. v.
Cooper , 246 Ky . 513, 55 S. W. 2d 381 ( 1932 ) , which held that the mortgagor
was guilty of conversion where the peaceably repossessed automobile was
not sold within a reasonable time for the balance due . For a general dis-
cussion and cases , see 14 C.J.S., Chattel Mortgages , Sec. 185 .
The provisions as to disabling equipment appears novel , but it is prob-
ably a very practical solution in the case of heavy machinery .
Section 9-504 . Secured Party's Rights to Dispose of Collateral After
Default ; Effect of Disposition .
Summary
(1) A secured party after default may sell , lease or otherwise dispose
of any or all of the collateral in its then condition or following any com-
mercially reasonable preparation or processing . Any sale of goods is subject
to the Article on Sales (Article 2) . The proceeds of disposition shall be
applied in the order following to :
(a) the reasonable expenses of retaking , holding , preparing for
sale , selling and the like and , to the extent provided for in
the agreement and not prohibited by law, the reasonable at-
torneys' fees and legal expenses incurred by the secured party;
(b) the satisfaction of indebtedness secured by the security in-
terest under which the disposition is made;
(c) the satisfaction of indebtedness secured by any subordinate
security interest in the collateral if written notification of
demand therefor is received before distribution of the pro-
ceeds is completed . If requested by the secured party, the
holder of a subordinate security interest must seasonably fur-
nish reasonable proof of his interest , and unless he does so ,
the secured party need not comply with his demand .
(2) If the security interest secures an indebtedness , the secured party
must account to the debtor for any surplus , and, unless otherwise agreed ,
the debtor is liable for any deficiency . But if the underlying transaction was
a sale of accounts , contract rights , or chattel paper , the debtor is entitled to
any surplus or is liable for any deficiency only if the security agreement so
provides .
(3) Disposition of the collateral may be by public or private proceed-
ings and may be made by way of one or more contracts . Sale or other dis-
388

position may be as a unit or in parcels and at any time and place and on any
terms but every aspect of the disposition including the method, manner ,
time, place and terms must be commercially reasonable . Unless collateral
is perishable or threatens to decline speedily in value or is of a type cus-
tomarily sold on a recognized market , reasonable notification of the time
and place of any public sale or reasonable notification of the time after which
any private sale or other intended disposition is to be made shall be sent by
the secured party to the debtor , and except in the case of consumer goods to
any other person who has a security interest in the collateral and who has
duly filed a financing statement indexed in the name of the debtor in this
state or who is known by the secured party to have a security interest in the
collateral . The secured party may buy at any public sale and if the collateral
is of a type customarily sold in a recognized market or is of a type which
is the subject of widely distributed standard price quotations he may buy
at a private sale .
(4) When collateral is disposed of by a secured party after default ,
the disposition transfers to a purchaser for value all of the debtor's rights
therein , discharges the security interest under which it is made and any
security interest or lien subordinate thereto . The purchaser takes free of
all such rights and interests even though the secured party fails to comply
with the requirements of this part or of any judicial proceedings :
(a) in the case of a public sale; if the purchaser has no knowledge
of any defects in the sale and if he does not buy in collusion
with the secured party , other bidders or the person conducting
the sale; or
(b) in any other case , if the purchaser acts in good faith .
(5) A person who is liable to a secured party under a guaranty , in-
dorsement , repurchase agreement or the like and who receives a transfer of
collateral from the secured party or is subrogated to his rights has thereafter
the rights and duties of the secured party . Such a transfer of collateral is
not a sale or disposition of the collateral under this article .
Kentucky Statutes
KRS 426.160 , 426.200 , 426.560 , 426.530 .
Comment
The provision allowing the "lease or other disposition " of the collateral
is new as at present the only disposition the secured party may make is a
sale. White v . General Motors Acceptance Corp. , 2 F. Supp . 406 (E. D. Ky .
1932 ) ; Cartwright v . C. I. T. Corp. , 253 Ky . 690, 70 S. W. 2d 388 ( 1934 ) .
Subsection (2) is designed to take care of risk financing and financing
sales . See White v . General Motors Acceptance Corp. , 2 F. Supp . 406 (E. D.
Ky . 1932 ) , for a case requiring payment of surplus to mortgagee where the
transaction was a true security agreement.
Section ( 3 ) imposes the test of commercial reasonableness upon the
exercise of the right of sale . Commercial Credit Co. v . Cooper , 246 Ky . 513 ,
55 S. W. 2d 381 ( 1932) requires the collateral to be sold at a fair sale on
389

adequate notice to the debtor after default . KRS 426.160 sets forth the re-
quirements for a sale of personal property and KRS 426.200 provides for
the sale of land, but both of these statute sections concern sales based upon
a judgment followed by execution and levy thereon . Provisions of KRS
426.500 must also be complied with. See KRS 361.600 on right of seller to
resell perishable goods .
Subsection (4) makes clear that a good faith purchaser from the secured
party who meets the test of this subsection gets a clear title even though the
secured party fails to comply with the requirements of Part 5. Under the
present Kentucky law (KRS 426.320 ) an execution sale will be set aside if
it is shown that the sale was fraudulent or collusive . Further , an execution
purchaser does not acquire title but merely acquires a lien subject to en-
cumbrances which were on the property at the time of the levy. Likens v .
Pate , 160 Ky . 319, 169 S. W. 734 ( 1914 ) . This case differs from the provision
of subsection (4) discharging liens which were subordinate to the security
interest under which the property was sold . Under Likens v . Pate , supra , if
the subordinate interest is in being at the time of the levy , then a purchaser
at the sale only secures a lien for the value of the purchase price . KRS
426.530 gives a party whose property is sold at a judicial sale a right of re-
demption for one year , and Messer v . American Eagle Fire Ins. Co. , 227 Ky .
3, 12 S. W. 2d 358 (1928) held that the title does not pass to the purchaser
until the year allowed for redemption had expired .
Subsection ( 5 ) states definitely the status of a third party stepping into
the shoes of a secured party .
Section 9-505 . Compulsory Disposition of Collateral ; Acceptance of
the Collateral as Discharge of Obligation .
Summary
(1) If the debtor has paid 60 percent of the cash price in the care of
a purchase money security interest in consumer goods or 60 percent of the
loan in the case of another security interest in consumer goods, and has not
signed after default a statement renouncing or modifying his rights under
this part a secured party who has taken possession of collateral must dispose
of it under UCC 9-504 and if he fails to do so within 90 days after he takes
possession the debtor at his option may recover in conversion or under
UCC 9-507 ( 1) on secured party's liability .
(2) In any other case involving consumer goods or any other col-
lateral a secured party in possession may , after default , propose to retain
the collateral in satisfaction of the obligation . Written notice of such pro-
posal shall be sent to the debtor and except in the case of consumer goods
to any other secured party who has a security interest in the collateral and
who has duly filed a financing statement indexed in the name of the debtor
in this state or is known by the secured party in the possession to have a
security interest in it. If the debtor or other person entitled to receive no-
tification objects in writing within 30 days from the receipt of the notifica-
tion or if any other secured party objects in writing within 30 days after the
secured party obtains possession the secured party must dispose of the col
390

lateral under UCC 9-504 . In the absence of such written objection the se-
cured party may retain the collateral in satisfaction of the debtor's obliga-
tion.
Kentucky Statutes
None.
Comment
Kentucky does not have a statute similar to subsection ( 1 ) . However ,
White v . General Motors Acceptance Corp. , 2 F. Supp . 406 (E. D. Ky . 1932 )
and Cartwright v . C. I. T. Corp. , 253 Ky . 690, 70 S. W. 2d 388 ( 1934 ) hold
that a chattel mortgagee who takes possession after default must sell chat-
tels , within a reasonable time, and pay mortgagor any excess over the debt,
if the chattels are not within a reasonable time redeemed . If the chattels are
not so sold, the mortgagee will be guilty of conversion . Commercial Credit
Co. v. Cooper , 246 Ky . 513, 55 S. W. 2d 381 ( 1932 ) .
Subsection ( 2) is a provision allowing the secured party in possession
to retain the collateral in satisfaction . While the cases cited in the para-
graph above insist on a sale of the collateral , earlier cases held that after
default in the conditions of a chattel mortgage, the absolute right of pos-
session was in the mortgagee . Swigert v . Thomas , 37 Ky . ( 7 Dana ) 220
(1838 ) , McIsaacs v . Hobbs , 38 Ky . ( 8 Dana ) 268 ( 1839 ) and Brown v.
Phillips , 66 Ky . ( 3 Bush ) 656 ( 1868 ) .
Section 9-506 . Debtor's Right to Redeem Collateral .
Summary
At any time before the secured party has disposed of collateral , or en-
tered into a contract for its disposition under UCC 9-504 , or before the ob-
ligation has been discharged under UCC 9-505 (2) the debtor or any other
secured party may unless otherwise agreed in writing after default redeem
the collateral by tendering fulfillment of all obligations secured by the
collateral as well as the expenses reasonably incurred by the secured
party in retaking , holding and preparing the collateral for disposition , in
arranging for the sale , and to the extent provided in the agreement and not
prohibited by law, his reasonable attorneys ' fees and legal expenses .
Kentucky Statutes
None.
Comment
There are Kentucky cases allowing a chattel mortgagor a right of re-
demption . Miles v . Blanton , 33 Ky . ( 3 Dana ) 525 ( 1835 ) , allowed a pur-
chaser from the mortgagor to redeem; Thompson's Adm'r v. Campbell's
Adm'r, 22 Kу . ( 6 T. B. Monroe ) 120 ( 1827 ) , on redemption the mortgagor
must pay sum advanced on mortgage with legal interest , less the hire of
the property while the mortgagee was in possession .
391

KRS 426.220 and 426.530 gives a debtor a right of redemption in real
property where it does not bring two -thirds of its appraised value at a sale
in pursuance of a judgment or order of a court . This right must be exer-
cised within one year after the sale.
Section 9-507 . Secured Party's Liability for Failure to Comply
With This Part .
Summary
(1) If it is established that the secured party is not proceeding in
accordance with the provisions of this part disposition may be ordered or
restrained on appropriate terms and conditions . If the disposition has oc-
curred the debtor or any person entitled to notification or whose security
interest has been made known to the secured party prior to the disposition
has a right to recover from the secured party any loss caused by a failure
to comply with the provisions of this part. If the collateral is consumer
goods , the debtor has a right to recover in any event an amount not less than
the credit service charge plus 10 percent of the principal amount of the
debt or the time price differential plus 10 percent of the cash price .
(2) The fact that a better price could have been obtained by a sale
at a different time or in a different method from that selected by the
secured party is not of itself sufficient to establish that the sale was not
made in a commercially reasonable manner . If the secured party either
sells the collateral in the usual manner in any recognized market therefor
or if he sells at the price current in such market at the time of his sale or if
he has otherwise sold in conformity with reasonable commercial practices
among dealers in the type of property sold he has sold in a commercially
reasonable manner . The principles stated in the two preceding sentences
with respect to sales also apply as may be appropriate to other types of
disposition . A disposition which has been approved in any judicial pro-
ceeding or by any bona fide Creditor's Committee or representative of
creditors shall conclusively be deemed to be commercially reasonable , but
this sentence does not indicate that any such approval must be obtained in
any case nor does it indicate that any disposition not so approved is com-
merically reasonable .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
The liability of the secured party for improper disposition is recog-
nized in the present law. Montenegro -Riehm Music Co. v . Beuris, 160 Ky .
557 , 169 S. W. 986 ( 1914 ) .
Possibly the most important feature of subsection (1) is the right of
the debtor to force or restrain disposition .
Subsection ( 2) is essentially a definition of "commercially reasonable "
which together with "good faith " ( see UCC 1-203 ) are the criteria for acts
under the Commercial Code .
392

ARTICLE TEN
EFFECTIVE DATE AND REPEALER
Introductory Comment
The sections in this article supply certain formal requisites necessary
to make the Code operative as law. As the provisions of each section are
largely self -explanatory , no further introductory comment is required .
Section 10-101 . Effective Date .
Summary
This act shall become effective at 12:01 A. M. CST on July 1 , 1960. It
applies to transactions entered into and events occurring after that date .
Comment
Sec. 55 of the Kentucky Constitution provides that no law shall become
effective until 90 days after adjournment of the session at which it was
passed , except emergency measures and appropriations . The purpose of such
a provision is to allow time for all to become familiar with the new law.
Ordinarily , 90 days is sufficient time, however , with an act as important as
the Code , there should be more time allowed .
The Code , as submitted by the Kentucky Bar Association Committee ,
provides 27 months , assuming its adoption at the 1958 session . In Pennsyl-
vania , the Code was adopted on April 6 , 1953, and became effective on July
1, 1954. In Massachusetts , the Code was adopted in September , 1957, and
will become effective in August, 1958 .
The Kentucky Bar Association Committee on the Uniform Commercial
Code recommends that should Kentucky adopt the Code at the 1958 session ,
it should be given an effective date of July 1, 1960. This would allow suf-
ficient time for the business community to become familiar with the Code
provisions and would insure a gradual change - over from the existing com-
mercial law to the Code . Also, it would give a subsequent legislature an
opportunity to make any modifications in the Code which they deem desir-
able in the light of action which will be taken in other states during the
interim period between its adoption in Kentucky and the suggested effective
date.
Section 10-102 . Specific Repealer .
Summary
The following acts and all other acts and parts of acts inconsistent here-
with are repealed :
(Here are listed various uniform acts together with other types of
statutes which are superseded by the Code . For tables of Kentucky
statutes affected , see Appendices I and II) .
395

Section 10-103 . General Repealer .
Summary
Except as provided in UCC 10-104 , all acts and parts of acts inconsistent
with this act are hereby repealed .
None .
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section is a general repealer . The policy in this State is not to use
such sections as they are essentially nothing but the statement of the gen-
eral rule of statutory construction .
The generally accepted view of such sections is well stated in Crawford,
Statutory Construction , Sec . 307, from which the following is extracted :
"... express repeals occasionally create problems almost as difficult
as those created by implied repeals . For example , where a statute
contains the common expression 'all acts and parts of acts in con-
flict with the provisions of this act are hereby repealed ,' the ques-
tion obviously arises as to the extent or scope of the repeal .
"... But the vast majority of the problems due to repeals by im-
plications , so far as express repealing acts are concerned , grow out
of the use of the expression that 'all laws and parts of laws in
conflict herewith are expressly repealed ' , there is considerable
justification for regarding a repeal caused by such a statutory pro-
vision as a repeal by implication . Provisions of this character leave
the question open as to what laws are inconsistent . This is equally
true , even where inconsistent statutes are enacted. In either case ,
whether the repealing act contains a provision that all inconsistent
acts are repealed or not , all pre-existing inconsistent acts are neces-
sarily abrogated ."
Section 10-104 . Laws Not Repealed .
Summary
...
Article 7, on Documents of Title , does not repeal or modify any laws
prescribing the form or contents of documents of title or the services or
facilities to be afforded by bailees, or otherwise regulating bailees ' busi-
nesses in respects not specifically dealt with herein ; but the fact that such
laws are violated does not affect the status of a document which otherwise
complies with UCC 1-201 .
None.
Kentucky Statutes
Comment
This section emphasizes that the Code does not alter laws which pre-
scribe regulations of local police concern or which prescribe duties of care
in certain cases . KRS 359.050 , 359.060 and 359.160 relating to the licensing
and bonding of grain and oil warehousemen are representative of the type
of law with which this section is concerned .
396

APPENDIX I
Acts to be Repealed on Adoption of Code
The sections listed here are the Kentucky Statutes corresponding to
the acts listed in UCC 10-102 . Many of them are uniform acts . All are
covered thoroughly by the new Code and should be repealed as inconsistent
therewith :
1. Uniform Stock Transfer
KRS Chapter 274
2. Uniform Negotiable Instruments
KRS Chapter 356
3. Uniform Warehouse Receipts
KRS Chapter 358
4. Uniform Sales
KRS Chapter 361
5. Bank Collection
KRS Chapter 357
6. Bulk Sales
KRS Chapter 377
APPENDIX II
The statutes in this part of the Appendix are representative of those
which are affected in varying degree by the adoption of the Uniform Com-
mercial Code . The common feature of these statutes is that they regulate a
phase that is also touched by the Code . Some of these statutes should be
repealed , some amended and others left unchanged .
1. KRS Chapter 251 , covering Grain Warehouses .
2. KRS Chapter 292, covering Securities ( Blue Sky Laws ) , also 278.300
on issuance of securities by utilities subject to Public Service Commission
regulation .
3. KRS Chapter 376, covering Statutory Liens .
4. KRS Chapter 382, covering Conveyances and Encumbrances ,
(checked to delete reference to personal property which is intended to be
covered under Article 9 of Code ) .
5. KRS Chapter 426, covering Enforcement of Judgments , ( checked
for inconsistencies insofar as Article 9 of Code ) .
The following statutes should be studied for possible conflicts and
duplications :
1. KRS 287.180, covering State Banks and branches thereof .
2. KRS 395.230, covering Stock Transfer under court order . (Code
provides for limited duty of inquiry prior to registration .)
3. KRS 382.600 through 382.730 , covering Chattel Mortgages (should
probably be repealed in toto ) .

4. KRS 162.240 , 160.570, 146.440 , 287.330 , 287.620 , 289.110 , 341.470 ,
376.140 , 381.190 , 382.390, 382.410 , 382.420 . ( Covering special situations such

9 of the Code .)

400

Administrative Analyst on the Commission staff for the excellent study of
the Commercial Code which he had the primary responsibility to prepare
and we feel sure that his work will be of great assistance to both the legis-
lature and the bench and bar of Kentucky .
JBY:jt
Very truly yours ,
James Boswell Young , Chairman
Uniform Commercial Code Committee
Kentucky State Bar Association
APPENDIX IV
December 5, 1957
Lt. Governor Harry Lee Waterfield
Chairman and Director
Legislative Research Commission
Frankfort, Kentucky
Dear Governor Waterfield :
The Uniform Commercial Code Committee of the Kentucky State Bar
Association working in close cooperation with the Legislative Research
Commission has completed its study of the Uniform Commercial Code as
directed by Senate Resolution No. 16 , Regular Session , 1956 General
Assembly .
Its study culminated in the unanimous Committee adoption of a resolu-
tion recommending to the Board of Bar Commissioners that the Uniform
Commercial Code be approved and supported for adoption by the 1958 Ken-
tucky General Assembly to be effective July 1, 1960. This resolution was
submitted to the Board of Bar Commissioners of the Kentucky State Bar
Association on Thursday , November 7, 1957 .
I am pleased to report that the Board of Bar Commissioners has voted
unanimously to accept the report of the Uniform Commercial Code Com-
mittee and has gone on record as supporting 1958 adoption of the Uniform
Commercial Code by the Commonwealth of Kentucky , with an effective date
of July 1 , 1960 .
We feel sure that the adoption of the Code would be in the best interests
of Kentucky . The major benefit would be (1) codification of the entire body
of commercial law, ( 2 ) supply of specific statutes to cover areas of the
commercial law where presently there is no established law, and ( 3) uni-
formity throughout the State and Nation in the field of commercial law.
On behalf of the Kentucky State Bar Association , we would like to
express our sincere appreciation to the staff of the Legislative Research
Commission for the fine assistance and cooperation rendered to the Bar
Association Committee in the preparation of this study .
Sincerely ,
/s/ D. BERNARD COUGHLIN
President
Kentucky State Bar Association
